topics.title: Basic Concepts: Agents, Environments, Rewards, and Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts: Agents, Environments, Rewards, and Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Four Pillars of Reinforcement Learning

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is built upon four fundamental interacting entities. Understanding these four concepts is the **bedrock** upon which every advanced RL algorithm (Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, PPO) is constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The agent–environment interface is the central abstraction of RL. Every KTU examination question in this module will be anchored to the precise definitions of these four entities.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)

#### **A) Agent**
The **Agent** is the learner and decision-maker. At each time step $t$, it observes the state of the environment and selects an action according to its policy. The goal of the agent is to maximize the cumulative reward it receives over the long run.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Sutton & Barto Framework):** The agent is any entity that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators. In KTU notation, the agent at time $t$ is the decision process $\mathcal{A}_t$ producing action $A_t$.

**Mathematical Symbol:** $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$ — the action taken at time $t$ from the action space $\mathcal{A}$.

#### **B) Environment**
The **Environment** is everything external to the agent that the agent cannot arbitrarily control. The environment accepts the agent's action and transitions to a new state, emitting a reward signal as feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** The environment is a stochastic, interactive system characterized by a state-transition probability function $p(s' \mid s, a)$ and a reward function $r(s, a, s')$. It responds to each action with a new state $S_{t+1} \in \mathcal{S}$ and reward $R_{t+1} \in \mathcal{R}$.

**Mathematical Symbol:** $p(s', r \mid s, a) = \Pr\{S_{t+1} = s', R_{t+1} = r \mid S_t = s, A_t = a\}$.

#### **C) Reward**
The **Reward** $R_t$ is a scalar numerical feedback signal that indicates how well the agent is performing at time $t$. The agent's sole objective is to maximize the **total cumulative reward** it receives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reward Hypothesis (Core KTU Concept):** All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a scalar reward signal. This hypothesis is **central** to KTU Module 1 theory questions.

**Mathematical Symbol:** $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ — a real-valued scalar.

#### **D) Policy**
The **Policy** $\pi$ defines the agent's behavior at a given time. It is a mapping from perceived states of the environment to probabilities of selecting each possible action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A policy $\pi$ is a probability distribution over actions given states:
> $$\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr\{A_t = a \mid S_t = s\}$$

**Two Types of Policies (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
1. **Deterministic Policy:** $a = \pi(s)$ — a fixed action for each state.
2. **Stochastic Policy:** $a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$ — an action is sampled from a probability distribution.

---

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy: Training a Pet Dog

To make these concepts **instantly intuitive**, consider training a pet dog to fetch a ball:

| RL Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Maps |
|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Agent** | The dog | The learner making decisions |
| **Environment** | The house, garden, and the ball's location | Everything outside the dog's control |
| **Reward** | Treats, praise, scolding | Scalar feedback for good/bad actions |
| **Policy** | The dog's learned behavior pattern | "When ball is thrown, run, pick up, return" |

The dog (agent) does not know the rules in advance. It experiments with actions, observes outcomes (rewards), and gradually builds a strategy (policy) that maximizes the number of treats it receives.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When answering KTU 2-mark definition questions, always pair the formal mathematical notation with a one-sentence real-world analogy. Examiners reward conceptual clarity.

---

### 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics and Constants

- **Discount Factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1]$: A real-valued scalar (most often between **0.9 and 0.99** in practice) that weights future rewards relative to immediate ones.
- **Time Step** $t \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$: Discrete integer index.
- **State Space** $\mathcal{S}$: The set of all possible states.
- **Action Space** $\mathcal{A}$: The set of all possible actions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The entire framework of Reinforcement Learning is governed by a continuous feedback loop. The operational sequence is as follows:

1. **Initialization:** The environment starts in some initial state $S_0 \sim d_0(s)$ where $d_0$ is the initial state distribution.
2. **Observation:** The agent observes the current state $S_t$.
3. **Action Selection:** The agent selects an action $A_t$ from its policy: $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$.
4. **Environment Response:** The environment processes the action and emits:
   - A new state $S_{t+1} \sim p(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$
   - A scalar reward $R_{t+1} = r(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$
5. **Learning Update:** The agent updates its policy $\pi$ based on the new tuple $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$.
6. **Iteration:** Set $t \leftarrow t + 1$ and return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The boundary between agent and environment is drawn **arbitrarily** but **consistently**. Anything the agent cannot change arbitrarily is considered part of the environment. This demarcation is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

### 2.2 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Foundation

The complete mathematical framework underlying RL is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, p, r, \gamma)$.

**The Markov Property:** The future is conditionally independent of the past given the present:
$$\Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t, A_t, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots, S_0, A_0\} = \Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t, A_t\}$$

This is **why** the state $S_t$ is sufficient statistic for predicting the next state and reward.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every high-yield formula for Module 1 KTU examinations.** Memorize the column headers and the *form*, not exact values.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Domain / Range** |
|-------------|------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Return (Undiscounted)** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}$ | Total reward from time $t$ until episode end | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Return (Discounted)** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Total discounted future reward | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Discount Factor** | $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ | Trade-off between immediate and future reward | Real scalar |
| **State-Value Function** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | Expected return starting from $s$ under policy $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Action-Value Function** | $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Expected return after taking $a$ in $s$ under $\pi$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Policy (Stochastic)** | $\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ | Probability of action $a$ in state $s$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Policy (Deterministic)** | $a = \pi(s)$ | A specific action for each state | $a \in \mathcal{A}$ |
| **Transition Probability** | $p(s' \mid s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$ | Probability of next state | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Reward Function** | $r(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']$ | Expected reward for a transition | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| **Bellman Expectation (V)** | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a)[r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$ | Recursive decomposition of $V$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note for Students:** The vertical bar $\mid$ in conditional probability $\Pr(A \mid B)$ is **NOT** the same as the markdown table column separator $\vert$. In KTU answer sheets, always use a clean vertical stroke or the word "given" to avoid ambiguity.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

| **Field** | **Agent** | **Environment** | **Application** |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Robotics | Robotic arm controller | Physical workspace | Manipulation, locomotion |
| Game AI | Game-playing bot | Video game engine | AlphaGo, Atari agents |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Self-driving controller | Road, traffic, weather | Lane keeping, navigation |
| Industrial Control | Process controller | Manufacturing plant | HVAC, chemical processes |
| Finance | Trading algorithm | Stock market | Portfolio optimization |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Strategy:** When asked to identify the agent and environment in a real-world scenario, ask yourself: *"What is learning, and what is everything else?"* The entity that **learns and changes its strategy** is the agent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Recursive Return Equation

The **return** $G_t$ is the discounted sum of future rewards. We can express it recursively, which is the foundation of nearly every RL algorithm.

**Step 1: Write the definition of the discounted return from time $t$.**
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \gamma^{3} R_{t+4} + \ldots$$

**Step 2: Factor out the first term to expose the remaining structure.**
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \ldots \right)$$

**Step 3: Recognize that the bracketed term is exactly $G_{t+1}$.**

By definition, the return from time $t+1$ is:
$$G_{t+1} = R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \ldots$$

**Step 4: Substitute $G_{t+1}$ back into the expression.**
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most important recursive identity in all of Reinforcement Learning.** It is a guaranteed KTU Part A (3-mark) or Part B (sub-part) question every exam cycle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The State-Value Function from First Principles

The state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ measures "how good" it is to be in state $s$ when following policy $\pi$.

**Step 1: Start with the formal definition.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 2: Substitute the recursive form of the return** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 3: Apply linearity of expectation.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 4: Condition on the next action and next state** using the law of total expectation.

For the first term, the agent picks $a$ from $\pi(a \mid s)$, and the environment produces $r$ and $s'$:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \, r$$

For the second term, once we know $s'$, the expected return is $V^{\pi}(s')$:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 5: Combine and write the complete Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}$.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the Bellman Expectation Equation.** It is the *defining recursive equation* of the state-value function. It appears in KTU Module 1, Module 2 (Dynamic Programming), and Module 3 (Bellman optimality).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Action-Value Function and Its Decomposition

**Step 1: Define the action-value function.**
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

**Step 2: Substitute the recursive form $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.**
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

**Step 3: Apply the law of total expectation, conditioning on the next state $s'$.**
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \right]$$

**Step 4: Recognize that the expected return from the next state is the value of the next state** (averaged over what action the policy picks next).
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^{\pi}(s', a')$$

**Step 5: Write the final Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^{\pi}$.**
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \right]$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Four Core Concepts

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix as a quick reference for KTU Part A definition questions.**

| **Aspect** | **Agent** | **Environment** | **Reward** | **Policy** |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| **Role in RL** | Learner / Decision-maker | Reactive system | Feedback signal | Behavior strategy |
| **Type** | Active entity | Passive responder | Scalar real number | Probability distribution |
| **Updated during training?** | Yes (improves policy) | No (fixed dynamics) | No (fixed by task) | Yes (continuously) |
| **Mathematical form** | Decision function $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$ | Transition kernel $p(s' \mid s, a)$ | $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$ | $\pi(a \mid s)$ or $a = \pi(s)$ |
| **Engineering example** | Robot arm controller | Factory floor with obstacles | $+1$ for success, $-1$ for collision | Pick-up algorithm |
| **What changes over time?** | Internal parameters of $\pi$ | Nothing (assumed stationary) | Nothing (task-defined) | Yes, this *is* what changes |
| **Key relation** | $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$ | $S_{t+1} \sim p(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$ | Maximized by $\pi$ | $\pi^{*} = \arg\max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop (Core RL Diagram)

This is the **single most important diagram** in the entire KTU Reinforcement Learning course. You will be expected to draw and label it in every module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["AGENT"] -->|Action A_t| nodeB["ENVIRONMENT"]
    nodeB -->|Reward R_t+1| nodeA
    nodeB -->|New State S_t+1| nodeA
    nodeA -->|Updates Policy pi| nodeA
    nodeC["State S_t"] --> nodeA
    nodeD["Policy pi a given s"] --> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The agent receives **state** $S_t$ and **reward** $R_{t+1}$ from the environment. The agent outputs **action** $A_t$ to the environment. The agent internally maintains and updates its **policy** $\pi$.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the RL System

This diagram breaks down the *internal architecture* of the agent and the environment into functional sub-modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph subA["AGENT SUBSYSTEM"]
        nodeP["Policy Module pi a given s"]
        nodeL["Learning Module Q and V updater"]
        nodeM["Memory Buffer past experience"]
        nodeP --> nodeM
        nodeM --> nodeL
        nodeL -->|Improved pi| nodeP
    end
    subgraph subB["ENVIRONMENT SUBSYSTEM"]
        nodeT["Transition Kernel p s prime given s a"]
        nodeR["Reward Generator r s a s prime"]
        nodeS["State Register S_t"]
    end
    nodeS -->|S_t| nodeP
    nodeP -->|A_t| nodeT
    nodeT -->|S_t+1| nodeS
    nodeT -->|to reward function| nodeR
    nodeR -->|R_t+1| nodeL
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: The Reinforcement Learning Feedback Cycle (Time-Indexed)

This diagram shows the **temporal sequence** of how state, action, and reward propagate across consecutive time steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["t=0: S_0"] --> nodeA0["A_0 sampled from pi"]
    nodeA0 --> nodeT1["t=1: S_1, R_1"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeA1["A_1 sampled from pi"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeT2["t=2: S_2, R_2"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeA2["A_2 sampled from pi"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeT3["t=k: S_k, R_k"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain the RL process with a diagram," always include the **temporal index** ($t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$). Examiners award bonus marks for clear time-step annotation.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Policy Types — Deterministic vs Stochastic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1["State s_1"] -->|deterministic policy| nodeD1["Action a_3 always"]
    nodeS1 -->|stochastic policy| nodeP1["pi a_1 given s_1 = 0.6"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeP2["pi a_2 given s_1 = 0.3"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeP3["pi a_3 given s_1 = 0.1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the stochastic policy diagram:** In state $s_1$, the agent chooses action $a_1$ with probability **0.6**, $a_2$ with probability **0.3**, and $a_3$ with probability **0.1**. The probabilities sum to **1.0**, which is a required property of any valid stochastic policy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1]**

**Q1. Define the following with respect to Reinforcement Learning: (i) Agent (ii) Environment (iii) Reward (iv) Policy.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Agent:** The agent is the decision-making entity that interacts with the environment by perceiving the state and taking actions according to its policy, with the goal of maximizing cumulative reward.

**(ii) Environment:** The environment is the external system the agent interacts with. It responds to the agent's action by transitioning to a new state and producing a scalar reward signal, governed by the transition probability $p(s' \mid s, a)$.

**(iii) Reward:** A reward $R_t$ is a scalar numerical feedback signal from the environment that indicates how well the agent is performing at time $t$. The agent's objective is to maximize the cumulative reward over time.

**(iv) Policy:** A policy $\pi$ is a mapping from states to probabilities of selecting each possible action, defining the agent's behavior. Formally, $\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$. A policy may be **deterministic** ($a = \pi(s)$) or **stochastic** ($a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)$).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q2]**

**Q2. Explain the Markov property in the context of Reinforcement Learning. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Markov property** states that the future evolution of the environment depends only on the current state and action, not on the entire history of past states and actions. Mathematically:

$$\Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t, A_t\} = \Pr\{S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_0, A_0, S_1, A_1, \ldots, S_t, A_t\}$$

**Importance:**
1. It allows the state $S_t$ to act as a **sufficient statistic** for predicting the future, meaning we do not need to store the entire history.
2. It enables the formulation of the agent–environment problem as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, which is the theoretical foundation of almost all RL algorithms.
3. It makes value functions $V^{\pi}(s)$ and $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ well-defined recursive quantities that can be computed using the Bellman equations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q3A]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the agent–environment interaction in Reinforcement Learning. Clearly define the agent, environment, state, action, and reward. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the recursive form of the discounted return $G_t$ and explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$. What happens to the agent's behavior when $\gamma = 0$ and when $\gamma = 1$? (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Agent–Environment Interaction (7 Marks):**

The agent–environment interaction in Reinforcement Learning is a continuous feedback loop. The block diagram representation is as follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["AGENT: contains policy pi"] -->|A_t action| nE["ENVIRONMENT: contains transition p and reward r"]
    nE -->|S_t+1 new state| nA
    nE -->|R_t+1 reward| nA
```

**Definitions:**

- **State $S_t$:** A numerical representation of the environment's situation at time $t$. It encodes all information needed for future decisions.
- **Action $A_t$:** The choice made by the agent at time $t$, drawn from the action space $\mathcal{A}$. The set of all possible actions available in state $S_t$ is $\mathcal{A}(S_t)$.
- **Reward $R_{t+1}$:** A scalar feedback signal emitted by the environment immediately after action $A_t$ is taken. It is computed via the reward function $r(s, a, s')$.
- **Policy $\pi$:** The agent's behavior strategy; a mapping from states to action probabilities.
- **Transition Kernel $p$:** The environment's internal dynamics; the probability of moving to state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$.

**Interaction Sequence:** At each time step $t$: (1) the agent observes $S_t$, (2) selects $A_t \sim \pi(\cdot \mid S_t)$, (3) the environment responds with $S_{t+1} \sim p(\cdot \mid S_t, A_t)$ and $R_{t+1} = r(S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})$, and (4) the agent uses this experience to improve its policy.

---

**Part (b) — Recursive Return and Discount Factor (7 Marks):**

**Derivation:** Starting from the definition of the discounted return:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \gamma^{3} R_{t+4} + \ldots$$

Factor out $\gamma$ from the second term onward:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+4} + \ldots \right)$$

The bracketed expression is exactly $G_{t+1}$. Hence:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**Role of Discount Factor $\gamma$:**
- $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ weights the importance of future rewards relative to immediate ones.
- It ensures the infinite sum converges mathematically when reward sequences are bounded.
- It encodes the agent's preference: **myopic** (low $\gamma$) versus **far-sighted** (high $\gamma$) decision-making.

**Boundary Cases:**
- **When $\gamma = 0$:** The agent is **completely myopic**. It maximizes only the immediate reward $R_{t+1}$ and ignores all future rewards. The return collapses to $G_t = R_{t+1}$.
- **When $\gamma = 1$:** The agent is **completely far-sighted**. It treats all future rewards equally with the immediate one. This is the **undiscounted** setting, and convergence is guaranteed only for episodic tasks with finite total reward.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q3B]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Define and distinguish between a deterministic policy and a stochastic policy. Provide a mathematical representation of each with one example. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ from first principles, starting from its definition. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Deterministic vs Stochastic Policy (7 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Deterministic Policy** | **Stochastic Policy** |
|------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Mathematical form** | $a = \pi(s)$ | $\pi(a \mid s) = \Pr(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ |
| **Output for a given state** | A single, specific action | A probability distribution over actions |
| **Choice process** | Direct mapping (lookup) | Sampling from a distribution |
| **Use case** | Deterministic environments | Stochastic / partially observable environments |
| **Example** | In Gridworld, $\pi(s) = \text{Right}$ for all $s$ on the right edge | In rock-paper-scissors, $\pi(\text{Rock} \mid s) = \pi(\text{Paper} \mid s) = \pi(\text{Scissors} \mid s) = 1/3$ |

**Mathematical Representation:**

- **Deterministic:** $A_t = \pi(S_t)$ — a function from $\mathcal{S}$ to $\mathcal{A}$.
- **Stochastic:** $\pi : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow [0, 1]$ such that $\sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Key Distinction:** A stochastic policy can represent the *same* behavior as a deterministic one (e.g., $\pi(a^* \mid s) = 1$ and zero elsewhere), but it can also represent **exploration**, where the agent occasionally selects non-greedy actions to gather information about the environment.

---

**Part (b) — Bellman Expectation Equation Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Begin with the definition of the state-value function.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 2: Apply the recursive return identity** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 3: Use linearity of expectation.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 4: Condition on the action $a$ and the resulting next state $s'$ and reward $r$.**

For the reward expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \, r$$

For the next-step return expectation:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} p(s' \mid s, a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 5: Combine the two terms** to obtain the final Bellman Expectation Equation.
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Equation Summary:** This recursive equation expresses the value of being in state $s$ in terms of the immediate expected reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state. It is the cornerstone of **Dynamic Programming**, **Monte Carlo**, and **Temporal Difference** methods covered in later modules.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing reward with return:** Reward $R_t$ is *immediate*; return $G_t$ is *cumulative*. Marks are routinely docked when students interchange these.
> 2. **Forgetting to sum over the policy:** The Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$ must include $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)$. Many students omit this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up $V^{\pi}(s)$ and $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$:** $V$ is for states; $Q$ is for state–action pairs. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 4. **Writing the Markov property as $S_{t+1}$ depends only on $S_t$ (not $S_t, A_t$):** This is incomplete. The state alone, without the action, is generally insufficient. The *correct* statement must include both $S_t$ and $A_t$.
> 5. **Forgetting the boundary condition for valid policies:** Any stochastic policy must satisfy $\sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$ for all $s$. Writing a distribution that does not sum to 1 is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

- **Agent:** The learner/decision-maker. Modifies its own policy to maximize reward.
- **Environment:** Everything outside the agent. Governed by transition probabilities $p(s' \mid s, a)$ and reward function $r(s, a, s')$.
- **Reward $R_{t+1}$:** A scalar real number emitted by the environment after each action. It is the **only** feedback signal.
- **Policy $\pi$:** Behavior strategy. **Deterministic** form: $a = \pi(s)$. **Stochastic** form: $\pi(a \mid s) \in [0, 1]$ with $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) = 1$.
- **Return $G_t$:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$. **Recursive form:** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
- **Discount Factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$:** $\gamma = 0$ means myopic; $\gamma = 1$ means far-sighted. Typical value: $\gamma \approx 0.9$ to $0.99$.
- **Markov Property:** The next state depends only on the current state and action — not the history.
- **State-Value Function:** $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.
- **Action-Value Function:** $Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$.
- **Bellman Expectation Equation (V):** $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) [r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- **MDP Tuple:** $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, p, r, \gamma)$.
- **The Boundary Rule:** "Anything the agent cannot arbitrarily control is part of the environment."
- **The Reward Hypothesis:** "All goals can be described as the maximization of expected cumulative scalar reward."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Stage Decision Making Problems: N-Arm Bandit Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single-Stage Decision Making Problems: N-Arm Bandit Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **k-Armed Bandit Problem** (also called the **N-Armed Bandit Problem**) is the foundational formalization of **single-stage decision making under uncertainty** in reinforcement learning. It was originally described by Herbert Robbins in 1952 as a statistical decision-making model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Sutton & Barto, RL Textbook Standard):**
> *You are faced repeatedly with a choice among $k$ different options, or actions. After each choice, you receive a numerical reward chosen from a stationary probability distribution that depends on the action you selected. Your objective is to maximize the expected total reward over some time horizon (e.g., 1000 action selections, or time steps).*

Mathematically, the problem consists of the following elements:

- A set of $k$ actions $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}$
- For each action $a$, a stationary reward distribution $R(a)$ with **unknown** expectation $q_*(a)$
- At each time step $t$, the agent selects an action $A_t \in \mathcal{A}$
- It receives a stochastic reward $R_t \sim R(A_t)$
- The goal is to find a policy that maximizes $E\left[\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_t\right]$

The **true value** (expected reward) of an action $a$ is defined as:

$$
q_*(a) \doteq E\left[R_t \mid A_t = a\right]
$$

If you knew $q_*(a)$ for every $a$, solving the problem would be trivial — you would always select the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a q_*(a)$. The entire difficulty of the bandit problem arises because the action values are **unknown** and must be learned from sampled rewards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slot Machine" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Casino Slot Machine**
>
> Imagine walking into a casino with **N slot machines** (one-armed bandits) lined up in a row. Each machine has a different, **unknown payout rate**. You have a fixed budget of **T pulls**. After each pull, the machine spits out a random coin reward drawn from its own hidden distribution.
>
> - **Exploit:** Keep pulling the machine that has given you the most money so far.
> - **Explore:** Try a different machine occasionally — perhaps it pays out even more.
>
> The dilemma is fundamental: *Do you exploit the best-known option, or explore to find a potentially better one?*

This dilemma is the **Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off**, the central theme of all reinforcement learning. Each pull is a **single-stage decision** because the reward of one pull does not influence the dynamics of future pulls (no state transition, no Markov chain). It is *not* a sequential multi-stage problem.

## 1.3 Engineering Utility and Relevance

The bandit framework is the building block for:

| Application Domain | Bandit Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **Clinical Trials** | Each arm = a treatment; reward = patient outcome |
| **A/B Testing (Web)** | Each arm = a webpage variant; reward = click-through |
| **Adaptive Routing (Networks)** | Each arm = a route; reward = latency |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Each arm = a recommended item; reward = user engagement |
| **Hyperparameter Tuning (ML)** | Each arm = a hyperparameter config; reward = validation score |

## 1.4 Why "Single-Stage" Matters in KTU 2024 Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, the N-Arm Bandit Problem is positioned *before* the full Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework. This is because the bandit problem is the **simplest possible RL setting**:
> - **Single state** (no state transitions).
> - **Single decision per episode** (one action, one reward).
> - **Stationary** reward distributions (initially).
>
> Mastering the bandit trains the student to think in terms of **estimates, uncertainty, and action selection** — skills that directly scale up to MDPs in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Decision Framework

At each time step $t$, the learning agent performs two coupled operations:

1. **Estimation Step:** Maintain an estimate $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ of each action's true value $q_*(a)$.
2. **Action Selection Step:** Use these estimates (and possibly uncertainty measures) to pick the next action $A_t$.

### 2.1.1 Estimating Action Values (Sample-Average Method)

The simplest estimator is the **sample-average** of observed rewards:

$$
\hat{Q}_t(a) \doteq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbb{1}\{A_i = a\}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbb{1}\{A_i = a\}}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function. Let $N_t(a)$ denote the number of times action $a$ has been chosen up to time $t-1$. Then:

$$
\hat{Q}_t(a) = \frac{\text{sum of rewards when } a \text{ was taken}}{\text{number of times } a \text{ was taken}}
$$

If $N_t(a) = 0$, we define $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ to be some default (e.g., 0).

### 2.1.2 The Greedy Action and Optimality

The action with the highest estimated value at time $t$ is the **greedy action**:

$$
A_t^* \doteq \arg\max_{a} \hat{Q}_t(a)
$$

Selecting $A_t^*$ is called **exploitation**. Selecting any non-greedy action is called **exploration**.

### 2.1.3 The Greedy Algorithm Failure Mode

A purely greedy algorithm that initializes $\hat{Q}_0(a) = 0$ will lock onto the first action that gives a non-zero reward and never explore again. If that initial lucky action is suboptimal, total reward suffers dramatically. This motivates stochastic exploration strategies.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are the core of any KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 question on bandits.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True action value | $q_*(a) = E[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ | Defines the unknown target of learning |
| 2 | Sample-average estimate | $\hat{Q}_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbb{1}\{A_i = a\}}{N_t(a)}$ | Converts observations to estimates |
| 3 | Incremental update rule | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n \left[R_n - Q_n\right]$ | Memory-efficient online update |
| 4 | Step-size (sample average) | $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ | Converges for stationary rewards |
| 5 | $\varepsilon$-greedy selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ w.p. $1-\varepsilon$; random w.p. $\varepsilon$ | Simplest exploration strategy |
| 6 | UCB selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a \left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$ | Optimism under uncertainty |
| 7 | Gradient bandit preference | $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ | Updates softmax preferences |
| 8 | Softmax probability | $\Pr\{A_t = a\} = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{H_t(b)}}$ | Gradient bandit action prob. |
| 9 | 10-armed testbed regret | $\Delta_a = q_*(a^*) - q_*(a)$ | Gap between optimal and suboptimal |
| 10 | Incremental implementation | $Q_{n+1} = (1-\alpha_n) Q_n + \alpha_n R_n$ | Memory- and compute-efficient form |

### 2.2.1 Critical Distinction: Stationary vs. Non-Stationary Bandits

- **Stationary:** reward distributions $R(a)$ do not change over time → use $\alpha_n = 1/n$.
- **Non-stationary:** reward distributions drift → use a **constant** step-size $\alpha \in (0,1]$, giving exponentially decaying weights:

$$
Q_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha (1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i
$$

This is **KTU-high-yield** because exam questions often ask students to compare or justify the choice of $\alpha$.

## 2.3 The Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off (Detailed)

The trade-off can be formalized through the notion of **regret**:

$$
\text{Regret}(T) \doteq \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[q_*(A^*) - q_*(A_t)\right] = \sum_{a} \Delta_a \cdot E[N_t(a)]
$$

where $\Delta_a$ is the gap between the optimal action's value and action $a$'s value. A good algorithm ensures **sublinear regret** ($\text{Regret}(T) = o(T)$), meaning the average per-step regret tends to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a clinical trial setting, "regret" corresponds to patients receiving suboptimal treatments. Minimizing regret is an ethical and statistical imperative.

## 2.4 Why the Incremental Update Rule Matters

The naive sample-average computation is $O(N_t)$ per update — costly as $t$ grows. The **incremental update rule** achieves $O(1)$ per step:

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n \left[R_n - Q_n\right]
$$

### Derivation of the Incremental Rule

Starting from the sample-average definition and writing $R_n$ as the most recent reward after $a$ has been chosen $n$ times:

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \frac{1}{n}\left(R_n + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i\right)
$$

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\left(R_n + (n-1) \cdot Q_n\right) = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\left(R_n - Q_n\right)
$$

This identity shows $\alpha_n = 1/n$ emerges **naturally** from the sample-average definition, and the same form generalizes to any step-size $\alpha_n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sample-Average to Incremental Update Rule

**Given:** We want a memory-efficient way to maintain $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ after observing the $n$-th reward $R_n$ for action $a$.

**Step 1 — Write the sample average explicitly for action $a$ chosen $n$ times:**

$$
Q_{n} \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{n-1}}{n-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $Q_{n+1}$ in terms of the previous sum plus the new sample $R_n$:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \frac{1}{n}\left(R_n + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Factor $(n-1)$ out of the historical sum, recognizing $Q_n = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i$:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\left(R_n + (n-1) \cdot Q_n\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the $1/n$:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{R_n}{n} + \frac{n-1}{n} Q_n = \frac{R_n}{n} + Q_n - \frac{Q_n}{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Group $Q_n$ terms and isolate the update difference:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{R_n - Q_n}{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to a generic step-size $\alpha_n$:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n \left[R_n - Q_n\right] = (1 - \alpha_n) Q_n + \alpha_n R_n
$$

**Interpretation:** Each new estimate is a **convex combination** of the old estimate and the new sample. The quantity $(R_n - Q_n)$ is the **innovation** or **prediction error** — the surprise signal that drives learning.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $\varepsilon$-Greedy Action Selection Probability Analysis

**Given:** At time $t$, there are $k$ actions. The agent selects the greedy action with probability $1-\varepsilon$ and a uniformly random non-greedy action with probability $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Probability of selecting the optimal action $a^*$:**

If ties are broken randomly among $m_t$ greedy actions (where $m_t \geq 1$):

$$
\Pr(A_t = a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{1}{k}
$$

But this assumes $a^*$ is uniquely greedy. In general, with $m_t$ greedy candidates:

$$
\Pr(A_t = a^* \mid a^* \text{ is greedy}) = \frac{1-\varepsilon}{m_t} + \frac{\varepsilon}{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of suboptimal selections in $T$ steps:**

$$
E[N_T(\text{suboptimal})] = \varepsilon \cdot T \cdot \frac{k-1}{k} \approx \varepsilon T
$$

(for large $k$).

**Step 3 — Implication:** Even small $\varepsilon$ yields **linear regret** because exploration never decays. Greedy with $\varepsilon > 0$ is simple but asymptotically suboptimal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) Action Selection

**Motivation:** $\varepsilon$-greedy explores non-greedy actions without preference for *uncertain* ones. UCB instead explores actions whose estimates have **high uncertainty**, because they might actually be optimal.

**Step 1 — The Hoeffding inequality for bounded rewards:** If rewards are in $[0,1]$, the probability that the sample average $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ deviates from $q_*(a)$ by more than $u$ decreases as:

$$
\Pr\left(\vert \hat{Q}_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert > u\right) \leq 2 e^{-2 N_t(a) u^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Bound $u$ such that this probability is at most $t^{-4}$ (a chosen small value), then sum across actions using the union bound.**

Choose $u = \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{N_t(a)}}$. Then:

$$
\Pr\left(\vert \hat{Q}_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right) \geq 1 - \frac{2}{t^4}
$$

**Step 3 — The UCB action selection rule:**

$$
A_t \doteq \arg\max_a \left[ Q_t(a) + c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]
$$

where $c > 0$ controls the confidence level.

**Step 4 — Engineering intuition:** $Q_t(a)$ is the **exploitation** term; $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ is the **exploration** term. It is large when an action has been tried rarely and shrinks as it is tried more. Action selection is "optimistic in the face of uncertainty."

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Gradient Bandit Algorithm (Softmax Preference)

**Motivation:** $\varepsilon$-greedy and UCB use no relative ranking of actions. The gradient bandit learns a numerical **preference** $H_t(a)$ for each action and samples proportionally via softmax.

**Step 1 — Softmax (Boltzmann) action probability:**

$$
\Pr\{A_t = a\} \doteq \pi_t(a) = \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{H_t(b)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Stochastic gradient ascent on expected reward.** The expected reward under policy $\pi_t$ is:

$$
E[R_t] = \sum_{a} \pi_t(a) \cdot q_*(a)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient of $\ln \pi_t(a)$ w.r.t. $H_t(a)$ (a standard softmax derivative):**

$$
\frac{\partial \pi_t(b)}{\partial H_t(a)} = \pi_t(b) \left[\mathbb{1}_{a=b} - \pi_t(a)\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the policy-gradient (REINFORCE) update with baseline $\bar{R}_t$:**

$$
H_{t+1}(a) \doteq H_t(a) + \alpha \left(R_t - \bar{R}_t\right)\left(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a)\right)
$$

where $\bar{R}_t$ is the **average of all rewards seen so far** (used as a baseline to reduce variance).

**Step 5 — Interpretation of terms:**
- If $A_t = a$ and $R_t > \bar{R}_t$ → $H_{t+1}(a)$ increases → $a$ becomes more likely.
- If $A_t = a$ and $R_t < \bar{R}_t$ → $H_{t+1}(a)$ decreases → $a$ becomes less likely.
- If $A_t \neq a$ → the term $-\pi_t(a)$ slightly decreases $H_{t+1}(a)$ for the unchosen action when reward is above baseline.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Bandit Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **exam-critical** under the KTU 2024 syllabus. Memorize it.

| Algorithm | Exploration Mechanism | Stationary Behavior | Non-Stationary Behavior | Asymptotic Regret |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Greedy** | None | Stuck on first lucky action | Stuck | Linear |
| **$\varepsilon$-Greedy** | Uniform random | Slow improvement | Better than greedy | Linear (constant $\varepsilon$) |
| **Decaying $\varepsilon$-Greedy** | $\varepsilon_t \to 0$ | Eventually greedy | Less adaptive | Sublinear if decay is tuned |
| **UCB** | Optimism-based confidence | Strong in stationary | Less effective (needs tuning) | $O(\ln T)$ (theoretically strong) |
| **Gradient Bandit** | Softmax over preferences | Good | Adapts via baseline | Competitive empirically |
| **Optimistic Initialization** | Initial $Q_0(a) = $ high | Strong early exploration | Decays naturally | Depends on init gap |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Sample-Average Estimator)

**Problem:** Consider a 3-armed bandit. The following rewards were observed:

| Time $t$ | Action $A_t$ | Reward $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_2$ | 4 |
| 2 | $a_1$ | 1 |
| 3 | $a_2$ | 2 |
| 4 | $a_3$ | 5 |
| 5 | $a_1$ | 3 |

**Compute $\hat{Q}_6(a)$ for all actions using sample-average.**

**Solution:**

After $t = 5$:
- $a_1$: chosen 2 times, rewards $\{1, 3\}$, so $\hat{Q}_6(a_1) = (1+3)/2 = 2$.
- $a_2$: chosen 2 times, rewards $\{4, 2\}$, so $\hat{Q}_6(a_2) = (4+2)/2 = 3$.
- $a_3$: chosen 1 time, rewards $\{5\}$, so $\hat{Q}_6(a_3) = 5/1 = 5$.

Greedy action at $t = 6$: $\arg\max_a \hat{Q}_6(a) = a_3$ (value 5). Exploration would discover that $a_3$ may be a fluke (only 1 sample) — this is exactly where UCB or $\varepsilon$-greedy helps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The N-Armed Bandit Decision Loop (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Agent at time t"] --> B["Observe current estimates Q-hat of each arm"]
    B --> C{"Decision Policy"}
    C -->|"Greedy"| D["Pick A_t = argmax Q-hat"]
    C -->|"Epsilon Greedy"| E["With prob epsilon: pick random arm; else greedy"]
    C -->|"UCB"| F["Pick A_t = argmax Q-hat + c*sqrt(ln t / N)"]
    D --> G["Environment returns stochastic reward R_t"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Update estimate for arm A_t using R_t"]
    H --> I["Increment N for arm A_t"]
    I --> J["t = t + 1"]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXPLOIT["EXPLOITATION SIDE"]
        E1["Choose best-known arm"] --> E2["Get expected high reward"]
        E2 --> E3["Risk: miss better undiscovered arm"]
    end
    subgraph EXPLORE["EXPLORATION SIDE"]
        X1["Try less-known arm"] --> X2["Get possibly lower immediate reward"]
        X2 --> X3["Gain information: refine estimate"]
    end
    E3 -.->|Trade-off tension| X1
    X3 -.->|Informs future exploits| E1
```

## 4.3 Incremental Update Rule — Internal Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Step n: receive reward R_n for chosen action"] --> P["Compute prediction error: delta = R_n minus Q_n"]
    P --> M["Multiply by step-size alpha_n"]
    M --> U["Add to old estimate: Q_new = Q_old + alpha_n times delta"]
    U --> N["Store Q_new; increment step counter for that action"]
    N --> S
```

## 4.4 Taxonomy of Bandit Solution Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Bandit Problem Solving Strategies"]
    R --> A1["Action Value Methods"]
    R --> A2["Gradient Based Methods"]
    A1 --> B1["Greedy Selection"]
    A1 --> B2["Epsilon Greedy"]
    A1 --> B3["Upper Confidence Bound UCB"]
    A2 --> C1["Gradient Bandit with Softmax"]
    A2 --> C2["REINFORCE with Baseline"]
    B1 --> D1["Stationary assumption"]
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D2["Uses uncertainty term"]
    C1 --> E1["Numerical preferences H_t a"]
    C2 --> E1
```

## 4.5 Regret and Learning Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Time t along horizontal axis"] --> Y["Average reward along vertical axis"]
    Y --> Z["Curve rises as agent learns"]
    Z --> W["Greedy plateaus at suboptimal level"]
    Z --> V["Epsilon Greedy climbs slowly toward optimum"]
    Z --> U["UCB climbs fastest early then plateaus near optimum"]
    Z --> T["Gradient Bandit climbs competitively"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the k-Armed Bandit Problem. Explain the Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **k-Armed Bandit Problem** is a single-stage sequential decision-making problem in which a learning agent must repeatedly choose one of $k$ available actions. Each action $a$ has an unknown stationary reward distribution $R(a)$ with expected value $q_*(a) = E[R_t \mid A_t = a]$. After selecting action $A_t$, the agent receives a stochastic reward $R_t$. The objective is to maximize the expected cumulative reward over $T$ time steps.

The **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma** arises because the agent must balance:

- **Exploitation:** selecting the action currently believed to have the highest value to maximize immediate reward.
- **Exploration:** selecting less-known actions to gather information that may reveal a better long-term option.

A purely greedy strategy exploits but risks locking onto a suboptimal arm; a purely exploratory strategy wastes rewards on poorly performing arms. Effective algorithms (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy, UCB) manage this trade-off.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between sample-average action-value estimation and the constant step-size update. State one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Sample-Average | Constant Step-Size |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha [R_n - Q_n]$ with $\alpha \in (0,1]$ |
| Weights | Equal weight to all past rewards | Exponentially decaying weights, recent rewards dominate |
| Stationary behavior | Converges to true $q_*(a)$ | Converges only to a value influenced by recent rewards |
| Non-stationary behavior | Fails to track drift | **Adapts** to changing distributions |
| Memory / compute | Can be implemented incrementally | Same $O(1)$ memory via incremental update |

**Sample-average advantage:** Theoretically converges to the true expected reward (unbiased) as $n \to \infty$ in stationary environments.

**Constant step-size advantage:** Adapts to non-stationary reward distributions automatically; no need to store a count $n$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Action-Value Methods in Stationary Bandits `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Explain the sample-average method of estimating action values. Derive the incremental update rule and show it requires only constant memory per action.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sample-average estimator definition.**

After observing rewards $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_{n-1}$ for an action, the sample-average estimate is:

$$
Q_n \doteq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i}{n-1}
$$

with the convention $Q_1 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Incremental form derivation.**

Starting from the definition of $Q_{n+1}$:

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \frac{1}{n}\left( R_n + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i \right)
$$

Using the identity $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i = (n-1) Q_n$:

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\left( R_n + (n-1) Q_n \right) = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\left( R_n - Q_n \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Generalization.** Replacing $\frac{1}{n}$ with a general step-size $\alpha_n$:

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n \left( R_n - Q_n \right) = (1 - \alpha_n) Q_n + \alpha_n R_n
$$

**Step 4 — Memory analysis.** The algorithm stores only the running sum (or running $Q_n$ and count $n$) and updates in $O(1)$ time per reward, regardless of $n$. Memory per action is **constant**.

**Step 5 — Convergence condition.** For stationary rewards, the convergence guarantee is:

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^2 < \infty
$$

Sample-average satisfies this with $\alpha_n = 1/n$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Compare $\varepsilon$-Greedy and Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithms. Show the UCB action selection rule and explain why it is called "optimistic in the face of uncertainty."*

**Model Solution:**

**$\varepsilon$-Greedy:** Selects greedy action with prob $1-\varepsilon$ and a uniformly random action with prob $\varepsilon$. **Independent of uncertainty** — treats all non-greedy arms equally regardless of how little they have been sampled.

**UCB action selection rule:**

$$
A_t \doteq \arg\max_a \left[ Q_t(a) + c \cdot \sqrt{ \frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)} } \right], \quad c > 0
$$

where $N_t(a)$ is the number of times action $a$ was selected prior to time $t$, and $c$ controls the exploration strength.

**Why "Optimistic in the Face of Uncertainty":**

- The confidence bonus $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ is **large when $N_t(a)$ is small** (i.e., the action has been rarely tried).
- It **shrinks as $N_t(a)$ grows**, eventually letting the $Q_t(a)$ term dominate.
- The bonus acts as an **upper bound** on the true value with high probability; UCB assumes that under-sampled actions might still be optimal.
- This contrasts with $\varepsilon$-greedy which explores blindly without leveraging uncertainty.

**Trade-offs:**

| Criterion | $\varepsilon$-Greedy | UCB |
|---|---|---|
| Computational cost | Very low | Requires tracking $N_t(a)$ and $\ln t$ |
| Tuning | Only $\varepsilon$ | Both $\varepsilon$ analog $c$ and bonus form |
| Stationary performance | Good with tuned $\varepsilon$ | Generally superior |
| Non-stationary performance | Better (constant $\varepsilon$ keeps exploring) | Worse (confidence grows with $t$, over-explores stale info) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Gradient Bandits and Non-Stationary Environments `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Explain the Gradient Bandit Algorithm. Derive the softmax action probability and the gradient ascent update rule with baseline.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Numerical preferences.** Each action $a$ has a numerical **preference** $H_t(a) \in \mathbb{R}$. Larger preference means higher selection probability.

**Step 2 — Softmax action probability.**

$$
\Pr\{A_t = a\} \doteq \pi_t(a) = \frac{ e^{ H_t(a) } }{ \sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{ H_t(b) } }
$$

This gives a smooth, differentiable mapping from preferences to probabilities.

**Step 3 — Performance measure.** Under policy $\pi_t$, expected reward is:

$$
E[R_t] = \sum_{a} \pi_t(a) \, q_*(a)
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. preference.**

$$
\frac{\partial E[R_t]}{\partial H_t(a)} = \sum_{b} q_*(b) \frac{\partial \pi_t(b)}{\partial H_t(a)} = \sum_{b} q_*(b) \pi_t(b)\left[ \mathbb{1}_{a=b} - \pi_t(a) \right]
$$

$$
= \pi_t(a)\left[ q_*(a) - \sum_{b} \pi_t(b) q_*(b) \right] = \pi_t(a)\left[ q_*(a) - E[R_t] \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Stochastic ascent using the unbiased REINFORCE update with baseline $\bar{R}_t$:**

$$
H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha \left( R_t - \bar{R}_t \right) \left( \mathbb{1}_{ a = A_t } - \pi_t(a) \right), \quad \forall a
$$

where $\bar{R}_t = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{i=1}^{t} R_i$ is the baseline (average reward so far).

**Step 6 — Initialization.** $H_1(a) = 0$ for all $a$ → uniform initial selection.

**Interpretation:**
- The term $\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a)$ is positive when $a$ was chosen, negative otherwise.
- The reward comparison $(R_t - \bar{R}_t)$ provides a reinforcement signal: rewards above baseline increase the preference of $A_t$, while below-baseline rewards decrease it.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Discuss the impact of non-stationarity on action-value estimation. Show why the constant step-size $\alpha$ provides exponential recency weighting and is preferred over $\alpha_n = 1/n$ in such settings.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Non-stationarity assumption.** Reward distributions drift over time; $q_*(a)$ is no longer constant. Recent rewards are more representative of the current state than old rewards.

**Step 2 — Constant step-size update:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha \left( R_n - Q_n \right) = (1-\alpha) Q_n + \alpha R_n
$$

**Step 3 — Expand recursively to show exponential decay.**

$$
Q_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha (1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i
$$

**Step 4 — Weight interpretation.** The weight on reward $R_i$ is $\alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i}$, which is exponentially decaying with recency. The weight on the initial estimate $Q_1$ is $(1-\alpha)^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Step 5 — Comparison with $\alpha_n = 1/n$:**

$$
Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i
$$

The weight on every $R_i$ is exactly $\frac{1}{n}$ — equal weight regardless of age. This fails to track drift.

**Step 6 — Practical implication.** Use $\alpha \in [0.1, 0.3]$ for moderate non-stationarity (see Sutton & Barto empirical results).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in N-Armed Bandit questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the indicator function** $\mathbb{1}\{A_i = a\}$ in the sample-average formula. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha_n = 1/n$ with a generic constant step-size.** The former is for stationary environments; the latter is for non-stationary. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Writing the UCB formula with $\ln N_t(a)$ in the numerator instead of $\ln t$.** This is a common slip that destroys the theoretical guarantee. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the baseline $\bar{R}_t$** in the gradient bandit update — examiners specifically test for this term. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Failing to justify "why" an algorithm is optimistic** — the UCB bonus is an upper bound with high probability, not just an arbitrary fudge factor. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up the incremental update derivation** by skipping the $(n-1) Q_n$ factoring step. The full chain must be shown. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1, N-Armed Bandit:**

- **Definition:** Single-stage decision problem with $k$ actions, stationary reward distributions $R(a)$, unknown $q_*(a) = E[R_t \mid A_t = a]$.
- **Objective:** Maximize $E[\sum_{t=1}^{T} R_t]$ over horizon $T$.
- **True value vs. estimate:** $q_*(a)$ is unknown; $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ is learned from samples.
- **Sample-average estimator:** $\hat{Q}_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \mathbb{1}\{A_i=a\}}{N_t(a)}$.
- **Incremental update:** $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n (R_n - Q_n)$, with $\alpha_n = 1/n$ (stationary) or constant $\alpha$ (non-stationary).
- **Greedy action:** $A_t^* = \arg\max_a \hat{Q}_t(a)$.
- **Exploration–Exploitation trade-off:** central dilemma of RL; never resolved by greediness alone.
- **$\varepsilon$-Greedy:** random exploration with prob $\varepsilon$; simple but asymptotically suboptimal.
- **Optimistic Initialization:** sets $Q_1(a)$ high to encourage early exploration automatically.
- **UCB rule:** $A_t = \arg\max_a [Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}]$ — optimistic in the face of uncertainty.
- **Gradient Bandit:** uses softmax preferences $H_t(a)$; update $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$.
- **Baseline $\bar{R}_t$:** average of all rewards so far; reduces variance without introducing bias.
- **Regret metric:** $\text{Regret}(T) = \sum_a \Delta_a E[N_T(a)]$; goal is sublinear $o(T)$ regret.
- **Stationary vs. non-stationary:** choose $\alpha_n = 1/n$ or constant $\alpha$ accordingly.
- **Engineering applications:** A/B testing, clinical trials, adaptive routing, recommendation systems, hyperparameter tuning.
- **Why "single-stage":** no state transition; reward depends only on chosen action, not on prior history.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of N-Arm Bandit Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simulation of N-Arm Bandit Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **N-Armed Bandit Problem** (also called the **k-Armed Bandit Problem** or **Multi-Armed Bandit, MAB**) is the foundational sequential decision-making problem in Reinforcement Learning. It is formally defined as follows:

> A learning agent is faced repeatedly with a choice among **N** different actions (or "options"). After each choice, it receives a numerical reward drawn from a stationary probability distribution that depends on the action selected. The agent's objective is to **maximize the expected total reward over a sequence of T plays** by identifying, through trial and error, the action (or set of actions) with the highest expected payoff.

Mathematically, at each time step $t$, the agent selects an action $A_t \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ and receives a reward $R_t$. The **value** (expected reward) of an arbitrary action $a$ is denoted as:

$$q_*(a) \doteq \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$$

If the agent knew the true value $q_*(a)$ for every action, the optimal solution would be trivial — it would always pick the **greedy action**:

$$A_t^* \doteq \arg\max_{a} q_*(a)$$

However, the agent does not know $q_*(a)$ with certainty and must **estimate** it from experience. The estimated value of action $a$ at time $t$ is denoted $Q_t(a) \approx q_*(a)$. The greedy selection rule based on estimates becomes:

$$A_t \doteq \arg\max_{a} Q_t(a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The N-armed bandit problem is the *simplest* formal reinforcement-learning setting because it isolates the **exploration–exploitation dilemma** — the central tradeoff in all of RL — without the complication of state transitions across time.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine walking into a casino with **100 slot machines** (one-armed bandits). Each machine has a different but **fixed** average payout rate that you do not know. You have a limited budget of 1000 coin pulls.

- **Pure Exploitation**: After the first 10 pulls, you commit to whichever machine gave the best single payout so far and stick with it. You might get stuck at a "lucky but actually mediocre" machine.
- **Pure Exploration**: You try every machine equally, gathering perfect statistics — but you waste most of your coins on machines you already know are bad.
- **Balanced Strategy (Bandit Solution)**: You spend a small fraction of pulls *probing* machines that *look* promising based on early data, while mostly pulling the machine that currently seems best.

This tug-of-war between **exploiting current knowledge** and **exploring to gain better knowledge** is exactly what the N-armed bandit framework mathematically models.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Action** $A_t$ | The choice made by the agent at step $t$ |
| **Reward** $R_t$ | Numerical feedback signal received after action |
| **Action Value** $q_*(a)$ | True expected reward of action $a$ |
| **Estimated Value** $Q_t(a)$ | Agent's running estimate of $q_*(a)$ |
| **Greedy Action** | Action with highest current $Q_t(a)$ |
| **Non-greedy / Exploratory Action** | Suboptimal-looking action tried for information |
| **Stationary Distribution** | Reward distribution does not change over time |
| **Regret** | Opportunity loss vs. always playing the optimal arm |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **$\varepsilon$** (epsilon) and the learning-rate constant **$\alpha$** are the two most critical hyper-parameters in bandit simulation. KTU examiners frequently test their precise definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exploration vs. Exploitation Tradeoff

The bandit problem reduces to one fundamental question: *Given my current estimates $Q_t(a)$, should I play what looks best right now (exploit), or try something uncertain that might be even better (explore)?*

- **Exploitation** maximizes expected reward **on the current play**, using current knowledge.
- **Exploration** may produce a worse current reward, but improves estimates and **increases the chance of higher long-term reward**.
- A purely greedy agent may lock onto a suboptimal action because its early samples were misleadingly high (the law of small numbers).
- A purely exploratory agent never uses its knowledge and earns the average reward, missing the best arm.

> [!TIP]
> The **KTU board pattern** typically asks students to *name and explain* the dilemma in 3-mark questions, and to *design a strategy* that balances it in 14-mark derivations.

## 2.2 Action-Value Estimation Methods

### Method 1: Sample-Average Method

The simplest estimator averages all rewards actually observed for action $a$:

$$Q_t(a) \doteq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbf{1}_{A_i = a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbf{1}_{A_i = a}}$$

where $\mathbf{1}_{A_i = a}$ is the indicator function (equals 1 if action $a$ was taken at step $i$, else 0). As $t \to \infty$, by the Law of Large Numbers, $Q_t(a) \to q_*(a)$.

### Method 2: Incremental Update Rule (Most KTU-Relevant)

A more computationally efficient form is the **incremental update rule**:

$$Q_{n+1} \doteq Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]$$

This form is preferred because the agent does not need to store the entire history of rewards — only the running estimate $Q_n$ and the count $n$.

### Method 3: Exponential Recency-Weighted Average

For non-stationary problems, the step-size can be held constant $\alpha \in (0,1)$:

$$Q_{n+1} \doteq Q_n + \alpha\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr] = (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i$$

This weights recent rewards more heavily.

## 2.3 Exploration Strategies

### (a) $\varepsilon$-Greedy Strategy

At each step, with probability $\varepsilon$ the agent selects an action **uniformly at random** from all $N$ actions (exploration); with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ it selects the **greedy** action (exploitation):

$$A_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a} Q_t(a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \\ \text{random action from } \{1,\dots,N\} & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \end{cases}$$

- **Advantages**: Simple, parameter-free besides $\varepsilon$, asymptotically optimal in stationary problems.
- **Drawback**: Tries all actions equally regardless of promise; even clearly bad arms get explored with probability $\varepsilon / N$.

### (b) Optimistic Initial Values

Initial estimates $Q_1(a)$ are set to a high value (e.g., $+5$) rather than zero. This drives early exploration because the agent is "disappointed" by every reward and keeps trying new arms. Once true values emerge, the agent settles. **Note:** This is *not* effective for non-stationary problems.

### (c) Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) Action Selection

UCB addresses the wasteful exploration of $\varepsilon$-greedy by selecting actions according to:

$$A_t \doteq \arg\max_{a}\left[\,Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\,\right]$$

where $c > 0$ controls the confidence level and $N_t(a)$ is the number of times action $a$ has been selected up to step $t$. The square-root term is an **uncertainty bonus**: rarely-tried actions get a large bonus, while well-tried actions do not. If $N_t(a) = 0$, the action is treated as the maximum.

### (d) Softmax (Boltzmann) Exploration

Probabilities are assigned to actions using a Gibbs/Boltzmann distribution:

$$\Pr(A_t = a) \doteq \frac{e^{Q_t(a)/\tau}}{\sum_{b=1}^{N} e^{Q_t(b)/\tau}}$$

where $\tau > 0$ is the **temperature**. High $\tau \to$ uniform (more exploration); low $\tau \to$ greedy (more exploitation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ | True action value | Theoretical optimum |
| 2 | $Q_t(a) \approx q_*(a)$ | Estimated action value | Computed by agent |
| 3 | $Q_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Sample-average estimate | Estimator |
| 4 | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \tfrac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ | Incremental update (stationary) | Online update |
| 5 | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha[R_n - Q_n]$ | Incremental update (non-stationary) | Online update |
| 6 | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ | Greedy rule | Exploitation |
| 7 | $P(\text{random}) = \varepsilon,\; P(\text{greedy}) = 1-\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$-greedy selection | Exploration strategy |
| 8 | $A_t = \arg\max_a\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\tfrac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$ | UCB selection | Smart exploration |
| 9 | $\Pr(A_t = a) = \frac{e^{Q_t(a)/\tau}}{\sum_b e^{Q_t(b)/\tau}}$ | Softmax action probability | Probabilistic exploration |
| 10 | $\text{Regret}_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\!\bigl[q_* - Q(A_t)\bigr]$ | Total opportunity loss | Performance metric |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Trials**: Sequentially assign patients to treatments; explore to find best drug, exploit to treat well.
- **Adaptive Routing / A/B Testing**: Allocate web traffic between ad variants to maximize clicks.
- **Resource Allocation in Cloud Computing**: Schedule jobs on the fastest available server.
- **Cognitive Radio**: Choose idle frequency channels for opportunistic spectrum access.
- **Recommendation Systems**: Decide which item to recommend to maximize user engagement.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Incremental Update Rule

The incremental update rule is *the* cornerstone formula of the entire N-armed bandit framework. KTU examiners consider it high-yield. Below is its full derivation.

### Starting Point — Sample-Average Definition

The $n$-th estimate $Q_n$ of the expected reward is defined as the arithmetic mean of the first $n$ observed rewards:

$$Q_n \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_{n-1}}{n-1}$$

This is the "vanilla" estimator. The problem: storing all $n-1$ rewards is computationally expensive.

### Step 1 — Write the $(n+1)$-th estimate

$$Q_{n+1} \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{n-1} + R_n}{n}$$

### Step 2 — Separate the new term

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\!\left[\,\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\,\right] = \frac{1}{n}\!\left[(n-1)\cdot\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\right]$$

**Logic:** We factor out $(n-1)$ so the inner sum re-appears as $Q_n$.

### Step 3 — Substitute the definition of $Q_n$

Recognizing that $Q_n = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i$:

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\bigl[(n-1)\,Q_n + R_n\bigr]$$

### Step 4 — Expand the fraction

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{n-1}{n}\,Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\,R_n = Q_n - \frac{1}{n}Q_n + \frac{1}{n}R_n$$

### Step 5 — Group the $Q_n$ terms

$$Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]$$

This is the celebrated **incremental update rule**. It has the elegant general form:

$$\text{NewEstimate} \leftarrow \text{OldEstimate} + \text{StepSize}\bigl[\text{Target} - \text{OldEstimate}\bigr]$$

where $\text{StepSize} = 1/n$ is the step-size parameter and $\text{Target} - \text{OldEstimate} = R_n - Q_n$ is the **prediction error**.

### Step 6 — Verify with a numerical check

Suppose $Q_2 = 4$, $R_2 = 6$, $n = 2$:

$$Q_3 = Q_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}[R_2 - Q_2] = 4 + 0.5 \cdot [6 - 4] = 4 + 1 = 5$$

Check via direct average: $\frac{R_1 + R_2}{2} = \frac{2 + 6}{2} = 4 = Q_2$ ✓. Now with three samples $\{2, 6, 7\}$: average $= 15/3 = 5$ ✓.

---

## 3.2 General Convergence of the Sample-Average Estimator

We now prove that $Q_n \to q_*(a)$ as $n \to \infty$ under stationarity.

**Claim:** $\mathbb{E}[Q_n] = q_*(a)$.

*Proof sketch:* Assume each $R_i \sim$ distribution with mean $q_*$:

$$\mathbb{E}[Q_n] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i\right] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[R_i] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_* = q_*$$

By the **Law of Large Numbers**, $Q_n \xrightarrow{\text{a.s.}} q_*$ as $n \to \infty$, and $\operatorname{Var}(Q_n) = \sigma^2/n \to 0$. Hence the estimator is **consistent** and **unbiased**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 3-Armed Bandit Simulation

> **Problem Setup:** A 3-armed bandit has true (unknown) reward distributions:
>
> $$\mu_1 = 1.0, \quad \mu_2 = 1.5, \quad \mu_3 = 2.0$$
>
> (all rewards deterministic for simplicity; real problems use stochastic rewards). Use $\varepsilon$-greedy with $\varepsilon = 0.1$ and **optimistic initialization** $Q_1(a) = +5$ for all $a$. Run for **T = 10 steps** and tabulate the agent's decisions and updates.

### Deterministic Trace

Because rewards equal $\mu_a$ exactly, the first pull of any arm returns its mean.

**Step 1 (t=1):** All $Q_1 = 5$. Ties broken by arm 1 (greedy). Pull arm 1.

$$R_1 = 1.0, \quad n_1 = 1, \quad Q_2(1) = 5 + \tfrac{1}{1}(1.0 - 5) = 1.0$$

Now $Q_2 = (1.0, 5, 5)$. **Greedy: arm 2 or 3.**

**Step 2 (t=2):** Greedy → arm 2.

$$R_2 = 1.5, \quad n_2 = 1, \quad Q_3(2) = 5 + 1 \cdot (1.5 - 5) = 1.5$$

Now $Q_3 = (1.0, 1.5, 5)$. **Greedy: arm 3.**

**Step 3 (t=3):** Greedy → arm 3.

$$R_3 = 2.0, \quad n_3 = 1, \quad Q_4(3) = 5 + 1 \cdot (2.0 - 5) = 2.0$$

Now $Q_4 = (1.0, 1.5, 2.0)$. **Greedy: arm 3** (correct optimum!).

**Step 4 (t=4):** Greedy → arm 3 (probability 0.9) OR random (probability 0.1). Generate a uniform random number $u$. Say $u = 0.34 > 0.1$, so exploit:

$$R_4 = 2.0, \quad n_3 = 2, \quad Q_5(3) = 2.0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2.0 - 2.0) = 2.0$$

**Steps 5–10:** Continue; the agent now stays on arm 3 unless a $u < 0.1$ event triggers exploration, in which case it tries arm 1 or 2 and finds lower rewards, reinforcing arm 3 as optimal.

### Final Simulation Table

| $t$ | $A_t$ | $R_t$ | $Q_t(1)$ | $Q_t(2)$ | $Q_t(3)$ | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 5.0 | 5.0 | Greedy |
| 2 | 2 | 1.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 5.0 | Greedy |
| 3 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 4 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 5 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 6 | 1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Random ($\varepsilon$) |
| 7 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 8 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 9 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |
| 10 | 3 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | Greedy |

**Total Reward** $= 1.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 \times 7 + 1.0 = 16.5$. **Optimal (always arm 3)** $= 20.0$. **Regret** $= 3.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> In real KTU problems, rewards are **stochastic** (drawn from a Normal or Bernoulli distribution). Students should show how a single draw deviates from the mean and demonstrate the *expected* behavior across many independent simulations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Strategies

| Strategy | Exploration Type | Best For | Key Parameter | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Greedy | None | Pure exploitation | None | Gets stuck on first lucky arm |
| $\varepsilon$-greedy | Uniform random | Stationary bandits | $\varepsilon$ | Wastes pulls on clearly bad arms |
| Optimistic Initial Values | Driven by initial values | Stationary bandits | $Q_1(a)$ | Useless for non-stationary |
| UCB | Uncertainty-based | Stationary, low regret | $c$ | Requires $N_t(a)$ bookkeeping |
| Softmax (Boltzmann) | Probability-proportional | Rank-sensitive problems | $\tau$ | Sensitive to $\tau$ scaling |
| Gradient Bandit | Preference-based | Non-stationary | $\alpha, H_0$ | Needs baseline |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Bandit Decision Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["Initialize Qa = 0 for all N actions"]
    initB["Set epsilon, step size alpha, horizon T"]
    start["Begin Simulation Loop t = 1 to T"]
    randCheck{"Random number u less than epsilon?"}
    explore["SELECT Random Action uniformly from 1 to N"]
    exploit["SELECT Greedy Action = argmax Qa"]
    execute["EXECUTE Action in environment"]
    observe["OBSERVE Reward R_t"]
    update["UPDATE Qa using incremental formula"]
    increment["Increment t"]
    endLoop{"t greater than T?"}
    output["REPORT Final estimates Qa, total reward, regret"]
    stop["STOP Simulation"]

    initA --> initB --> start --> randCheck
    randCheck -- YES --> explore
    randCheck -- NO  --> exploit
    explore --> execute
    exploit --> execute
    execute --> observe --> update --> increment --> endLoop
    endLoop -- NO  --> randCheck
    endLoop -- YES --> output --> stop
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph env["ENVIRONMENT MODULE"]
        armBank["Bandit Arm Bank N distributions"]
        sampler["Reward Sampler"]
    end

    subgraph agent["AGENT MODULE"]
        estimator["Action-Value Estimator Q Table"]
        selector["Action Selector epsilon-greedy or UCB or softmax"]
        updater["Incremental Updater"]
    end

    subgraph metric["METRIC MODULE"]
        rewardLog["Per-step Reward Logger"]
        regretCalc["Cumulative Regret Calculator"]
        plotter["Convergence Plotter"]
    end

    selector -- "Action A_t" --> sampler
    armBank --> sampler
    sampler -- "Reward R_t" --> updater
    estimator <-- "Old estimates" --> updater
    updater -- "New estimates Q_new" --> estimator
    estimator --> selector
    sampler -- "Reward" --> rewardLog
    rewardLog --> regretCalc
    rewardLog --> plotter
```

## 4.3 Exploration Strategy Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Choose Exploration Strategy"]
    q1{"Is the problem stationary?"}
    q2{"Do you need regret guarantees?"}
    q3{"Is reward distribution nearly uniform?"}

    root --> q1
    q1 -- YES --> q2
    q1 -- NO  --> strat1["GRADIENT BANDIT or Constant step size alpha"]
    q2 -- YES --> strat2["UPPER CONFIDENCE BOUND UCB"]
    q2 -- NO  --> q3
    q3 -- YES --> strat3["EPSILON GREEDY simple"]
    q3 -- NO  --> strat4["SOFTMAX BOLTZMANN with temperature tau"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model QP, June 2024, Module 1]**

**State and explain the N-armed bandit problem. Why is it considered the simplest reinforcement learning setting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The N-armed bandit problem is a sequential decision-making task in which a learning agent repeatedly chooses one of **N** available actions and receives a numerical reward drawn from a stationary probability distribution associated with that action. Each action $a$ has an unknown expected value $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R \mid A = a]$. The agent's goal is to maximize total expected reward over $T$ plays by estimating $q_*(a)$ from experience and selecting high-value actions.

It is considered the *simplest* RL setting because **(i)** there is only one state — the agent does not need to model state transitions or temporal dependencies; **(ii)** rewards depend solely on the immediate action, not on past actions; and **(iii)** the entire learning challenge reduces to balancing **exploration** (gathering information about all arms) versus **exploitation** (acting on current best estimate).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**What is the exploration–exploitation tradeoff? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The exploration–exploitation tradeoff is the central dilemma in reinforcement learning where the agent must choose between:
- **Exploitation** — selecting the action that currently appears best based on estimated values $Q_t(a)$, to maximize immediate reward.
- **Exploration** — selecting seemingly suboptimal actions to refine their value estimates and possibly discover better long-term options.

**Example:** In a clinical trial with 4 drug treatments, the doctor may **exploit** by prescribing the drug showing best results so far, ensuring current patients get the best-known care. However, by **exploring** a less-tested drug, the doctor might discover a genuinely superior treatment that helps future patients. Pure exploitation may lock in a suboptimal drug; pure exploration wastes patients on inferior options.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Incremental Update Derivation + Strategy Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — Model QP, June 2024, Module 1, Q1 (a) & (b)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the sample-average definition, derive the incremental update rule for action-value estimation in the N-armed bandit problem. Show that the new estimate can be written as $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \tfrac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ and interpret each term.
