**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Sample-average definition (1 mark):**

$$Q_n \doteq \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i$$

2. **Write the next estimate by adding $R_n$ and dividing by $n$ (1 mark):**

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \frac{1}{n}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\right]$$

3. **Split the average (1 mark):**

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{n-1}{n}\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i\right] + \frac{1}{n}R_n$$

4. **Substitute $Q_n$ inside the bracket (1 mark):**

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{n-1}{n}Q_n + \frac{1}{n}R_n$$

5. **Algebraic rearrangement (1 mark):**

$$Q_{n+1} = Q_n - \frac{Q_n}{n} + \frac{R_n}{n} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}(R_n - Q_n)$$

6. **Interpretation of terms (2 marks):**
- $Q_n$ — the **OldEstimate** (current belief about the action's value).
- $\tfrac{1}{n}$ — the **StepSize**, which shrinks as more samples are seen, ensuring the influence of any single observation diminishes.
- $R_n - Q_n$ — the **prediction error** (surprise), the difference between what was observed and what was expected. The update moves the estimate *toward* the target reward, scaled by the error.

General form: $\text{NewEstimate} = \text{OldEstimate} + \text{StepSize} \times [\text{Target} - \text{OldEstimate}]$.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare $\varepsilon$-greedy, UCB, and softmax exploration strategies with respect to how they handle the exploration–exploitation tradeoff. Which one gives the lowest asymptotic regret and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $\varepsilon$-greedy | UCB | Softmax |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exploration trigger** | Random with probability $\varepsilon$ | Deterministic bonus for under-tried arms | Probabilistic, weighted by $Q_t$ |
| **Information used** | Only $Q_t(a)$ | $Q_t(a)$ and $N_t(a)$ (visit count) | $Q_t(a)$ and temperature $\tau$ |
| **Parameter** | $\varepsilon$ | Confidence $c$ | Temperature $\tau$ |
| **Behavior on bad arms** | Still pulled with prob $\varepsilon/N$ | Pulled less as $N_t(a)$ grows | Pulled with low probability |
| **Asymptotic regret** | Linear in $T$ | $O(\log T)$ | Depends on $\tau$ schedule |

**Asymptotic Regret Discussion (3 marks):**

UCB achieves the **lowest asymptotic regret**, growing only as $O(\log T)$, because its uncertainty bonus $\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ automatically *decays* as an arm is tried more often. Once an arm is clearly inferior, its high visit count $N_t(a)$ makes the bonus negligible, and UCB stops wasting pulls on it. By contrast, $\varepsilon$-greedy keeps pulling every arm with probability $\varepsilon/N$ forever, producing **linear regret** $O(T)$. Softmax can approach UCB-like behavior if $\tau$ is annealed, but in its basic form also produces linear regret.

Thus, UCB's *adaptive, count-driven* exploration is provably superior for stationary bandits when $N$ is small to moderate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Algorithm Simulation + Numerical Trace

**[KTU University Exam — Model QP, Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 (a) & (b)]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure of the $\varepsilon$-greedy algorithm for the N-armed bandit problem. Clearly specify initialization, selection rule, update rule, and the role of $\varepsilon$.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):**
For each arm $a \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$:
- Set $Q_1(a) \leftarrow 0$ (or an optimistic value).
- Set $N(a) \leftarrow 0$, the count of times arm $a$ has been pulled.
- Set the exploration parameter $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$ (e.g., 0.1).
- Set the horizon $T$ (number of total plays).

**Step 2 — Action Selection at time $t$ (2 marks):**
Draw a uniform random number $u \sim U(0,1)$:
- If $u < \varepsilon$ → **Explore**: pick $A_t$ uniformly at random from $\{1, \dots, N\}$.
- Else → **Exploit**: pick $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ (greedy action; ties broken arbitrarily).

**Step 3 — Reward Observation (1 mark):**
Execute $A_t$ on the bandit and observe reward $R_t$, drawn from the (stationary) distribution of arm $A_t$.

**Step 4 — Value Update (2 marks):**
Increment the count $N(A_t) \leftarrow N(A_t) + 1$, then update the estimate:

$$Q_{t+1}(A_t) = Q_t(A_t) + \frac{1}{N(A_t)}\bigl[R_t - Q_t(A_t)\bigr]$$

Other arms keep their old estimates: $Q_{t+1}(a) = Q_t(a)$ for $a \neq A_t$.

**Step 5 — Loop and Terminate (1 mark):**
Repeat Steps 2–4 for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$. Report final estimates, total reward, and regret.

**Role of $\varepsilon$:** Controls the long-run fraction of plays spent exploring. Small $\varepsilon$ exploits more but risks premature commitment to a suboptimal arm. Large $\varepsilon$ explores more but wastes pulls on clearly bad arms. KTU-typical choice: $\varepsilon = 0.1$.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3-armed bandit has true expected rewards $q_*(1) = 0.5$, $q_*(2) = 1.0$, $q_*(3) = 1.5$. Assume stochastic rewards drawn i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(q_*(a), 1)$ each time the arm is pulled. Simulate 8 steps with $\varepsilon$-greedy ($\varepsilon = 0.2$) and sample-average updates. Use the following deterministic observed rewards for traceability:

$t: 1 \to 0.4,\; 2 \to 1.1,\; 3 \to 0.6,\; 4 \to 1.4,\; 5 \to 1.6,\; 6 \to 0.5,\; 7 \to 1.5,\; 8 \to 1.7$

Initialize $Q_1(a) = 0$ for all arms. Use the rule: if $u < \varepsilon$ pick arm $= 1 + (t \bmod 3)$; else pick greedy. Show $Q_t$ after each step and the optimal action identified.

**Model Answer — Full Trace:**

| $t$ | $u$ | $u<\varepsilon?$ | Rule | $A_t$ | $R_t$ | $Q_t(1)$ | $Q_t(2)$ | $Q_t(3)$ | Best |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.05 | YES | random, $1+(1\bmod 3)=2$ | 2 | 1.1 | 0.00 | **1.10** | 0.00 | 2 |
| 2 | 0.32 | NO | greedy(2) | 2 | 0.6 | 0.00 | **0.85** | 0.00 | 2 |
| 3 | 0.15 | YES | random, $1+(3\bmod 3)=1$ | 1 | 0.4 | **0.40** | 0.85 | 0.00 | 2 |
| 4 | 0.40 | NO | greedy(2) | 2 | 1.4 | 0.40 | **1.05** | 0.00 | 2 |
| 5 | 0.18 | YES | random, $1+(5\bmod 3)=3$ | 3 | 1.6 | 0.40 | 1.05 | **1.60** | 3 |
| 6 | 0.55 | NO | greedy(3) | 3 | 0.5 | 0.40 | 1.05 | **1.05** | 2 |
| 7 | 0.10 | YES | random, $1+(7\bmod 3)=2$ | 2 | 1.5 | 0.40 | **1.13** | 1.05 | 2 |
| 8 | 0.61 | NO | greedy(2) | 2 | 1.7 | 0.40 | **1.25** | 1.05 | 2 |

**Update calculations shown:**

$t=1$: $Q_2(2) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{1}(1.1 - 0) = 1.1$.

$t=2$: $Q_3(2) = 1.1 + \tfrac{1}{2}(0.6 - 1.1) = 1.1 - 0.25 = 0.85$.

$t=3$: $Q_4(1) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{1}(0.4 - 0) = 0.4$.

$t=4$: $Q_5(2) = 0.85 + \tfrac{1}{2}(1.4 - 0.85) = 0.85 + 0.275 = 1.125 \approx 1.13$.

$t=5$: $Q_6(3) = 0 + \tfrac{1}{1}(1.6 - 0) = 1.6$.

$t=6$: $Q_7(3) = 1.6 + \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5 - 1.6) = 1.6 - 0.55 = 1.05$.

$t=7$: $Q_8(2) = 1.125 + \tfrac{1}{3}(1.5 - 1.125) = 1.125 + 0.125 = 1.25$.

**Total reward** $= 1.1 + 0.6 + 0.4 + 1.4 + 1.6 + 0.5 + 1.5 + 1.7 = 8.8$.

**Optimal (always arm 3, $\mu = 1.5$)** $= 12.0$. **Regret** $= 3.2$.

**Final identified optimal action:** Arm 2 by estimate, although the *true* optimum is arm 3. This demonstrates that with limited samples and high $\varepsilon$, the agent may mis-identify the best arm — a key practical insight in bandit design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Bandit Problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing step-size $\alpha$ with $1/n$:** Students confuse the *constant* step-size (used in non-stationary problems) with the *decreasing* $1/n$ (used in stationary problems). KTU examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if this is not stated explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the indicator function $\mathbf{1}_{A_i=a}$** in the sample-average formula. Always write it.
> 3. **Treating $\varepsilon$-greedy as "the optimal strategy"**: It is *simple*, not optimal. UCB has provably lower regret.
> 4. **Mis-defining greedy action:** It is $\arg\max_a Q_t(a)$, not the action with the largest *single observed* reward. The difference is crucial.
> 5. **Using $Q_1(a) = 0$ when optimistic initialization is required**, or vice-versa — the two methods give very different convergence curves.
> 6. **In numerical traces, students forget to update the count $N(A_t)$**, leading to wrong incremental step-size on subsequent pulls of the same arm.
> 7. **Confusing reward $R_t$ with action-value $Q_t(a)$**: $R_t$ is one sample; $Q_t(a)$ is the running average of all samples for arm $a$.
> 8. **Forgetting to handle the $N_t(a) = 0$ case in UCB**: UCB requires treating unvisited arms as having infinite value (forcing exploration).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: N-armed bandit = choose 1 of $N$ actions each step, receive reward, maximize total reward.
- **Objective**: Estimate $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R \mid A=a]$ for all $a$; pick greedy.
- **Core dilemma**: Exploration vs. Exploitation — the heartbeat of all RL.
- **Sample-average estimator**: $Q_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — unbiased and consistent under stationarity.
- **Incremental update (HIGH-YIELD)**: $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \tfrac{1}{n}(R_n - Q_n)$ — derived by splitting the average.
- **General incremental form**: New = Old + StepSize × (Target − Old).
- **$\varepsilon$-greedy rule**: Probability $\varepsilon$ random, $1-\varepsilon$ greedy. **Asymptotic regret: linear.**
- **UCB rule**: $A_t = \arg\max_a\!\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}\right]$. **Asymptotic regret: $O(\log T)$.**
- **Softmax rule**: Probability $\propto e^{Q_t(a)/\tau}$. Higher $\tau$ = more exploration.
- **Optimistic initialization**: Start $Q_1(a)$ high to force early exploration; useless for non-stationary problems.
- **Constant step-size $\alpha$**: Used for non-stationary bandits to weight recent rewards more heavily.
- **Unvisited arms in UCB**: Treat $N_t(a)=0$ as having infinite upper-confidence (forces exploration).
- **Regret formula**: $\sum_{t=1}^{T}\!\bigl[q_* - Q(A_t)\bigr]$.
- **KTU must-show derivations**: (i) incremental update from sample-average, (ii) trace simulation table for a given $\varepsilon$, (iii) comparison table of strategies.
- **Stationarity assumption**: All KTU bandit problems assume rewards are drawn from fixed distributions unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Pitfall**: Never confuse $R_t$ (one observation) with $Q_t(a)$ (running mean estimate).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Action-Value Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Action-Value Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework for Reinforcement Learning (PECDT755), **Action-Value Methods** are formally defined as a class of *model-free estimation techniques* in which an agent learns the *expected return* (or value) of taking each available action in a given state, and subsequently uses these estimated action-values to guide its *action-selection policy*. These methods form the foundation of the classical *k-armed bandit problem* and constitute the conceptual precursor to full *temporal-difference (TD) learning* and *Q-learning* architectures covered in later modules.

Mathematically, the *true value* of an action $a$ is the *expected reward* given that action $a$ is selected:

$$q_*(a) \doteq \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$$

The agent's task in action-value methods is to estimate $q_*(a)$ via samples, producing an estimate $Q_t(a) \approx q_*(a)$, and to use these estimates to choose actions that maximize long-term accumulated reward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Action-Value Methods are the *first building block* of RL introduced in Module 1. The two critical sub-topics are:
> 1. *Sample-average estimation of action values* (incremental formulation).
> 2. *Action-selection rules*: greedy, $\epsilon$-greedy, **Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB)**, and *gradient bandits*.
> Mastery of this topic is mandatory for the End Semester Examination (ESE) Part A and frequently appears in Part B derivations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a new city and want to find the best coffee shop. You have **10 unknown options (k = 10 actions)**. Each day you try one shop and rate the coffee (the *reward*). The "true value" of a shop is its *average* rating across all customers — you don't know it yet. You keep a running average of every shop you've tried.

- **Greedy strategy** → Always return to the best shop you've tried so far. *Risk:* you'll never discover a better shop.
- **$\epsilon$-greedy** → Most days, return to the best. But with small probability $\epsilon$, try a *random* shop to keep exploring.
- **UCB** → Be optimistic about shops you've visited rarely — they *might* be amazing, give them a chance.
- **Gradient bandits** → Maintain *preferences* for each shop rather than value estimates; the higher the preference, the more likely you pick it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Used in KTU Problems:** The metric used to compare action-value methods is **average reward over time**, evaluated across a fixed number of **steps (n)**. The standard *baseline* against which all exploratory methods are compared is the **greedy method**, whose suboptimality quantifies the *exploration cost*.

### 1.3 Standard Constants / Boundary Parameters

- **Number of arms (k)**: usually a small integer, $k \geq 2$.
- **Number of steps (n)**: total horizon for evaluation, often $n = 1000$ in KTU numerical problems.
- **$\epsilon$ (epsilon)**: exploration probability, typical values $0.01$, $0.1$, $0.2$.
- **$c$ (UCB confidence parameter)**: typically $c = 1$ to $c = 2$.
- **$\alpha$ (step-size)**: learning rate, $0 < \alpha \leq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Foundational Tasks of Action-Value Methods

Action-value methods solve two intertwined sub-problems:

1. **Estimation Task:** Maintain an estimate $Q_t(a)$ of $q_*(a)$ from observed rewards.
2. **Control Task (Action Selection):** Use $\{Q_t(a)\}_{a=1..k}$ to select the next action $A_t$.

The two tasks are coupled: better estimates → better actions → better reward samples → better estimates.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of an Action-Value Method

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Set $Q_1(a) = 0$ for all $a$ (or some optimistic prior; see §2.6).
- **Step 2 — Action Selection:** Pick $A_t$ via the chosen rule (greedy / $\epsilon$-greedy / UCB / gradient).
- **Step 3 — Environment Interaction:** Observe reward $R_t$ from the environment.
- **Step 4 — Value Update:** Update $Q_{t+1}(A_t)$ using the new sample $R_t$.
- **Step 5 — Iterate:** Repeat Steps 2–4 for $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

### 2.3 Sample-Average Estimation (Foundational Method)

The simplest estimator is the **sample average** of observed rewards for action $a$:

$$Q_t(a) \doteq \frac{\text{sum of rewards when } a \text{ taken prior to } t}{\text{number of times } a \text{ taken prior to } t} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \cdot \mathbb{1}_{A_i = a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_i = a}}$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{\text{condition}}$ is the indicator function ($\mathbb{1} = 1$ if condition holds, else $0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sample average?** It is an *unbiased estimator* of $q_*(a)$ and converges to it as the number of samples $\to \infty$ by the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)**. KTU questions frequently test this convergence property.

### 2.4 Greedy and $\epsilon$-Greedy Action Selection

**Greedy action selection:**

$$A_t \doteq \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$$

Greedy action selection exploits current knowledge but **never explores**, leading to suboptimal long-run performance.

**$\epsilon$-greedy action selection (with probability $\epsilon$ behave randomly):**

$$A_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q_t(a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \epsilon \\ \text{uniformly random action} & \text{with probability } \epsilon \end{cases}$$

The advantage: $\epsilon$-greedy guarantees that every action is sampled infinitely often in the limit $t \to \infty$, so $Q_t(a) \to q_*(a)$ for all $a$, and greedy action is therefore optimal in the limit.

### 2.5 Incremental Implementation (Memory-Efficient Update)

Storing all past rewards is infeasible. KTU requires the *incremental* form:

$$Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]$$

This is the standard "error-correction" form: $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha \, \text{Error}$ with step-size $\alpha = 1/n$.

### 2.6 Non-Stationary Problems and Fixed Step-Size

For *non-stationary* problems (where $q_*(a)$ drifts over time), constant step-size is used:

$$Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr], \quad 0 < \alpha \leq 1$$

This gives a *weighted recent average* where recent rewards carry more weight.

### 2.7 Optimistic Initial Values

Setting $Q_1(a)$ to a high value (e.g., $+5$ when rewards are 0/1) encourages early exploration. **Why?** All actions look promising initially → every action is tried → the system quickly discovers the true best action.

### 2.8 Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB) Action Selection

UCB addresses the inefficiency of $\epsilon$-greedy (which explores *blindly*) by exploring *smartly* — preferring actions whose value estimate is *uncertain*:

$$A_t \doteq \arg\max_a \left[ Q_t(a) + c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$

- $N_t(a)$ = number of times action $a$ has been selected up to step $t$.
- $c$ = confidence level controlling the degree of exploration.
- The square-root term is large for actions rarely tried → they are picked.

### 2.9 Gradient Bandit Algorithms

A fundamentally different approach: maintain a numerical *preference* $H_t(a)$ for each action. The probability of choosing action $A_t$ uses the **softmax (Boltzmann) distribution**:

$$\Pr\{A_t = a\} \doteq \frac{e^{H_t(a)}}{\sum_{b=1}^{k} e^{H_t(b)}} = \pi_t(a)$$

Update rule (using all actions, not just $A_t$):

$$H_{t+1}(a) \doteq H_t(a) + \alpha \bigl(R_t - \bar{R}_t\bigr)\bigl(\mathbb{1}_{a = A_t} - \pi_t(a)\bigr), \quad \forall a$$

where $\bar{R}_t$ is the average of all rewards seen so far (used as a *baseline*).

### 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / KTU Use |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | True action value | $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$ | Definition — required in Part A |
| 2 | Sample-average estimate | $Q_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \mathbb{1}_{A_i=a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_i=a}}$ | Foundation estimator |
| 3 | Greedy selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$ | Pure exploitation |
| 4 | $\epsilon$-greedy selection | $A_t = \text{greedy}$ w.p. $1-\epsilon$, random w.p. $\epsilon$ | KTU-favourite exploration rule |
| 5 | Incremental update | $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha[R_n - Q_n]$ | Memory-efficient |
| 6 | Stationary step-size | $\alpha_n = 1/n$ | Guarantees convergence by LLN |
| 7 | Fixed step-size | $\alpha = $ const | For non-stationary problems |
| 8 | UCB selection | $A_t = \arg\max_a \left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}\right]$ | Smart exploration |
| 9 | Softmax policy | $\pi_t(a) = e^{H_t(a)} / \sum_b e^{H_t(b)}$ | Gradient bandit |
| 10 | Gradient update | $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ | Baseline $\bar{R}_t$ required |
| 11 | Indicator function | $\mathbb{1}_{\text{cond}} \in \{0,1\}$ | Used in sample averages |
| 12 | Average reward baseline | $\bar{R}_t = \frac{1}{t-1}\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i$ | Used in gradient bandits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Action-value methods underpin **A/B testing in industrial recommendation systems**, **adaptive clinical trial design**, **network routing (multi-armed bandit formulations)**, and **online advertisement selection**. The $\epsilon$-greedy and UCB rules are routinely deployed in production at companies like Google, Netflix, and Amazon.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Incremental Update Rule from Sample Average

**Goal:** Show that the recursive update $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}[R_n - Q_n]$ is *algebraically identical* to the sample average formula.

Let $R_i$ denote the reward received on the $i$-th selection of a particular action. The sample average after $n$ selections is:

$$Q_n \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{n-1}}{n-1}$$

For the $(n+1)$-th step, the new sample average is:

$$Q_{n+1} \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{n-1} + R_n}{n}$$

Add and subtract the old numerator term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{n+1} &= \frac{1}{n}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\left[(n-1) \cdot \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\left[(n-1)Q_n + R_n\right] \\
&= \frac{n-1}{n}Q_n + \frac{1}{n}R_n \\
&= Q_n - \frac{1}{n}Q_n + \frac{1}{n}R_n \\
&= Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line-by-line logic:**
- *Line 1 → Line 2:* Recognize the old average $(n-1)Q_n$ inside the new sum.
- *Line 2 → Line 3:* Factor out $\frac{1}{n}$.
- *Line 3 → Line 4:* Split into two additive terms.
- *Line 4 → Line 5:* Rearrange: $\frac{n-1}{n} = 1 - \frac{1}{n}$.
- *Line 5 → Line 6:* Factor out $Q_n$ and $R_n$ to obtain the canonical incremental form.

$\blacksquare$ — The derivation confirms that the incremental update is **exactly** the sample average, just implemented without storing any history.

### 3.2 Derivation: Convergence of the Sample-Average Estimator

By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers**, for an i.i.d. sequence $R_1, R_2, \ldots$ with finite mean $q_*(a)$:

$$\Pr\left\{\lim_{n \to \infty} Q_n = q_*(a)\right\} = 1$$

The expected squared error decreases as $1/n$:

$$\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Q_n - q_*(a))^2\bigr] = \frac{\text{Var}(R)}{n-1}$$

showing the *rate of convergence* depends inversely on the number of samples — a key result KTU may test in numerical questions.

### 3.3 Derivation: General Update Rule (Fixed Step-Size $\alpha$)

If the rewards are weighted exponentially (non-stationary case), the *n*-th reward gets weight $(1-\alpha)^{n-i}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{n+1} &= Q_n + \alpha\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr] \\
&= \alpha R_n + (1-\alpha)Q_n \\
&= \alpha R_n + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha R_{n-1} + (1-\alpha)Q_{n-1}\bigr] \\
&= (1-\alpha)^n Q_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sum of weights satisfies:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{n-i} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^n \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} 1$$

So the weights form a valid probability distribution. As $\alpha \to 0$, recent rewards dominate; as $\alpha \to 1$, all rewards are weighted equally. **KTU insight:** this general form unifies stationary ($\alpha = 1/n$) and non-stationary ($\alpha = $ const) cases.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Method | Exploration Strategy | Regret Behavior | Pros | Cons |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|------|------|
| Greedy | None | Worst (linear in $n$) | Simple | Locked into first best |
| $\epsilon$-greedy | Uniform random | $O(\ln n)$ asymptotically | Easy, robust | Blind exploration |
| Optimistic greedy | Forced by init | Linear in $k$ early, then 0 | Simple & fast in stationary | Requires domain knowledge |
| UCB | Smart (uncertainty) | $O(\ln n)$ | Optimal logarithmic regret | Needs $t$-dependent counts |
| Gradient bandit | Softmax of prefs | $O(\ln n)$ asymptotically | Handles relative rewards | Baseline-sensitive |

### 3.5 Numerical Example (Worked: 3-Armed Bandit, $n = 5$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Frequently Tested Numerical:** Trace the first 5 steps of an $\epsilon$-greedy agent with $Q_1(a) = 0$, $\epsilon = 0$, on the 3-armed bandit with true values $q_*(a_1) = 1$, $q_*(a_2) = 2$, $q_*(a_3) = 3$. Assume deterministic rewards.

| Step $t$ | Action $A_t$ | Reward $R_t$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ | $Q_4$ |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------|--------|--------|
| 1 | (init) | — | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | argmax ties → $a_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | argmax → $a_1$ (1) ties with $a_2, a_3$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | tie → $a_1$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |

*Greedy stays locked on $a_1$ — demonstrating the need for $\epsilon > 0$.*

Now with $\epsilon = 0.5$:

- Step 2: random action (say $a_2$) → $R_2 = 2$ → $Q_3(a_2) = 2$.
- Step 3: argmax → $a_2$ → $R_3 = 2$ → $Q_4(a_2) = 2$.
- Step 4: argmax → $a_2$ → $R_4 = 2$ → $Q_5(a_2) = 2$.
- Step 5: random (say $a_3$) → $R_5 = 3$ → $Q_6(a_3) = 3$ → next greedy is $a_3$.

**Conclusion:** $\epsilon$-greedy escapes the suboptimality. This is a **classic KTU 14-marker structure.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Action-Value Method Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Q1 a to 0 for all actions] --> B[Observe state and current time t]
    B --> C{Choose action via selection rule}
    C -->|Greedy| D1[argmax of Qt a]
    C -->|Epsilon Greedy| D2[Random action with prob epsilon else argmax]
    C -->|UCB| D3[argmax Qt a plus c sqrt ln t over N t a]
    C -->|Gradient| D4[Softmax over preferences Ht a]
    D1 --> E[Execute action At in environment]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Receive reward Rt from environment]
    F --> G[Update Qt plus 1 using incremental rule]
    G --> H[Increment time counter t to t plus 1]
    H --> B
    G --> I[Update preference Ht plus 1 for gradient bandits]
    I --> B
```

### 4.2 Exploration vs Exploitation Tradeoff (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ESTIMATION_MODULE
        E1[Collected Rewards Buffer] --> E2[Compute Qt a via sample average]
        E2 --> E3[Update rule incremental form]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_MODULE
        C1[Qt a estimates] --> C2{Exploration Mode}
        C2 -->|epsilon 0| C3[Pure Exploitation argmax]
        C2 -->|epsilon greater than 0| C4[Exploration random or UCB]
        C3 --> C5[Selected action At]
        C4 --> C5
    end
    ESTIMATION_MODULE --> CONTROL_MODULE
    C5 --> ENV[Environment]
    ENV --> R[Reward Rt]
    R --> ESTIMATION_MODULE
```

### 4.3 Convergence Behavior of $\epsilon$-Greedy vs UCB vs Gradient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start at t equals 1] --> INIT[Initialize Qt a equals 0 and Nt a equals 0]
    INIT --> LOOP{For each step t}
    LOOP --> SEL[Action selection based on method]
    SEL --> ACT[Execute action and observe Rt]
    ACT --> UPD[Update Qt and possibly Nt]
    UPD --> CHEK{Is t equals n or converged}
    CHEK -->|No| LOOP
    CHEK -->|Yes| END[Stop and report Qt a and average reward]
```

### 4.4 Action-Value Method Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        I1[State or context st]
        I2[Available actions a1 to ak]
    end
    subgraph MEMORY_LAYER
        M1[Qt a table of value estimates]
        M2[Nt a table of visit counts]
        M3[Ht a table of preferences gradient]
    end
    subgraph POLICY_LAYER
        P1[Action selector epsilon greedy UCB or softmax]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        O1[Chosen action At]
        O2[Updated memory entries]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    M1 --> P1
    M2 --> P1
    M3 --> P1
    P1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> M1
    O2 --> M2
    O2 --> M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** For non-stationary problems, the Memory Layer's update weights $M_1$ are governed by a fixed step-size $\alpha$, and the Policy Layer prioritizes recency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]** *Define the action-value function $q_*(a)$ used in reinforcement learning. How is it estimated in a k-armed bandit setting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The action-value of an action $a$ is the *expected reward* when that action is selected: $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$. (1 mark)
- If the expected reward of each action is unknown, it must be estimated from samples collected at runtime. (1 mark)
- The standard estimator is the **sample average** of observed rewards for action $a$: $Q_t(a) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} R_i \mathbb{1}_{A_i=a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{t-1} \mathbb{1}_{A_i=a}}$, which converges to $q_*(a)$ by the Law of Large Numbers. (1 mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]** *Distinguish between greedy and $\epsilon$-greedy action selection in action-value methods. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Greedy selection** always picks the action with the *highest current estimate*: $A_t = \arg\max_a Q_t(a)$. Advantage: exploits fully and gives best short-term reward. (1.5 marks)
- **$\epsilon$-greedy selection** picks the greedy action with probability $1-\epsilon$ and a *random* action with probability $\epsilon$. Advantage: guarantees continued exploration → asymptotic optimality, while still mostly exploiting. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Format (KTU ESE Standard):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Past Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** *Explain the **incremental implementation** of the sample-average action-value estimator. Derive the incremental update rule from the basic sample average formula.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Write the sample-average definition (2 marks):*

$$Q_n \doteq \frac{R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_{n-1}}{n-1} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i$$

*Step 2 — Write the next-step sample average (2 marks):*

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} R_i + R_n\right)$$

*Step 3 — Algebraic manipulation (2 marks):*

$$Q_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n}\bigl((n-1)Q_n + R_n\bigr) = Q_n + \frac{1}{n}\bigl[R_n - Q_n\bigr]$$

*Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):* The recursive rule $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n[R_n - Q_n]$ with $\alpha_n = 1/n$ has three properties: it requires only the *previous estimate* $Q_n$ and the *latest sample* $R_n$; it is *computationally cheap* (constant memory and time per update); and it is *numerically equivalent* to the closed-form sample average.

**(b)** *A 4-armed bandit has true action values $q_* = (1, 2, 3, 4)$. An $\epsilon$-greedy agent starts with $Q_1(a) = 0$ for all $a$, $\epsilon = 0.1$, and uses sample-average updates. The first 6 rewards observed (in order) when each action is selected for the first time are: $a_1 \to 1$, $a_2 \to 3$, $a_3 \to 2$, $a_4 \to 5$, then $a_3 \to 1$, $a_4 \to 2$. What action does the agent select at step 7? Justify using the greedy vs $\epsilon$-greedy rule.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Update $Q$-values after the first 4 selections (2 marks):*
- $Q(a_1) = 1$, $Q(a_2) = 3$, $Q(a_3) = 2$, $Q(a_4) = 5$ (each selected once).

*Step 2 — Step 5: greedy argmax = $a_4$.* $Q$ remains: $Q(a_1) = 1$, $Q(a_2) = 3$, $Q(a_3) = 2$, $Q(a_4) = 5$. (0.5 mark)

*Step 3 — Step 5 update with $R_5 = 1$ on $a_3$:*
- New $Q(a_3) = Q(a_3) + (1/2)(R_5 - Q(a_3)) = 2 + 0.5(1-2) = 1.5$. (1 mark)

*Step 4 — Step 6: greedy argmax = $a_4$ (5 > 3 > 1.5 > 1).* Update with $R_6 = 2$: (1 mark)
- New $Q(a_4) = 5 + (1/2)(2-5) = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5$.

*Step 5 — Step 7 selection (1.5 marks):*
- After step 6, $Q$ values are: $Q(a_1) = 1$, $Q(a_2) = 3$, $Q(a_3) = 1.5$, $Q(a_4) = 3.5$.
- Greedy argmax $= a_4$.
- $\epsilon$-greedy: with prob $0.9$ pick $a_4$; with prob $0.1$ pick uniformly from $\{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$.
- **Most likely action at step 7: $a_4$.**

*Step 6 — Final remark (1 mark):* Despite $a_4$ having the *lowest* true value (4), the noisy samples are biasing estimates. This is a *classic exploration issue* — the agent will only discover $a_4$ is suboptimal if it occasionally explores other arms via the $\epsilon$ channel.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *Explain the **Upper-Confidence-Bound (UCB)** action selection rule. Discuss how it addresses the limitations of $\epsilon$-greedy action selection.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — UCB rule statement (2 marks):*

$$A_t = \arg\max_a\left[Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$$

where $N_t(a)$ is the visit count and $c$ controls the exploration level.

*Step 2 — Interpretation of components (2 marks):*
- $Q_t(a)$: the *exploitation term* (current best estimate of reward).
- $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$: the *exploration bonus*, large for rarely-tried actions, decaying as $N_t(a)$ grows.

*Step 3 — Why it improves on $\epsilon$-greedy (2 marks):*
- $\epsilon$-greedy explores *blindly* (uniform random).
- UCB explores *intelligently* — it specifically targets actions whose estimates have high uncertainty.
- Theoretical result: UCB achieves $O(\sqrt{k n \ln n})$ regret, asymptotically optimal for stationary bandits.

*Step 4 — Practical considerations (1 mark):* UCB requires storing and updating $N_t(a)$ and is undefined at $t = 1$ (one convention: initialize all $N_t(a) = 0$ and select actions in a round-robin manner for the first $k$ steps). UCB is not directly suitable for non-stationary problems because $\ln t$ grows unbounded.

**(b)** *Consider a 3-armed bandit with true values $q_*(a) = (0.5, 0.6, 0.4)$. A gradient bandit agent initializes preferences $H_1(a) = 0$ for all $a$, uses step-size $\alpha = 0.1$, and the baseline $\bar{R}_t$ is the mean of all rewards seen so far. After the first 3 steps, observed rewards were $1, 0, 1$ corresponding to actions $a_1, a_2, a_3$ respectively. Compute the action-selection probabilities for step 4.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Initial softmax probabilities (1 mark):* With $H_1 = 0$ for all, $\pi_1(a) = e^0 / (e^0 + e^0 + e^0) = 1/3$ each.

*Step 2 — Compute baseline $\bar{R}_4$ (1 mark):*

$$\bar{R}_4 = \frac{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}{3} = \frac{1 + 0 + 1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667$$

*Step 3 — Step 1 update (1 mark):* $R_1 = 1$, $\bar{R}_1 = 0$ (convention):
- $H_2(a_1) = 0 + 0.1 \cdot (1 - 0) \cdot (1 - 1/3) = 0.1 \cdot 1 \cdot (2/3) = 0.0667$
- $H_2(a_2) = 0 + 0.1 \cdot (1 - 0) \cdot (0 - 1/3) = -0.0333$
- $H_2(a_3) = 0 + 0.1 \cdot (1 - 0) \cdot (0 - 1/3) = -0.0333$

*Step 4 — Step 2 update with $R_2 = 0$, $\bar{R}_2 = 1$ (1.5 marks):*
- $H_3(a_1) = 0.0667 + 0.1(0 - 1)(0 - 1/3) = 0.0667 + 0.0333 = 0.1000$
- $H_3(a_2) = -0.0333 + 0.1(0 - 1)(1 - 1/3) = -0.0333 - 0.0667 = -0.1000$
- $H_3(a_3) = -0.0333 + 0.1(0 - 1)(0 - 1/3) = -0.0333 + 0.0333 = 0$

*Step 5 — Step 3 update with $R_3 = 1$, $\bar{R}_3 = 0.5$ (1.5 marks):*
- $H_4(a_1) = 0.1 + 0.1(1 - 0.5)(0 - 1/3) = 0.1 - 0.0167 = 0.0833$
- $H_4(a_2) = -0.1 + 0.1(1 - 0.5)(0 - 1/3) = -0.1 - 0.0167 = -0.1167$
- $H_4(a_3) = 0 + 0.1(1 - 0.5)(1 - 1/3) = 0.1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (2/3) = 0.0333$

*Step 6 — Step 4 action-selection probabilities (1 mark):*

$$\pi_4(a) = \frac{e^{H_4(a)}}{\sum_b e^{H_4(b)}} = \frac{e^{H_4(a)}}{e^{0.0833} + e^{-0.1167} + e^{0.0333}}$$

Numerically:
- $e^{0.0833} \approx 1.087$, $e^{-0.1167} \approx 0.890$, $e^{0.0333} \approx 1.034$.
- Sum $\approx 3.011$.

$$\pi_4(a_1) \approx 0.361, \quad \pi_4(a_2) \approx 0.296, \quad \pi_4(a_3) \approx 0.343$$

**Observation:** $a_1$ is the most probable choice at step 4, despite its true value being *lower* than $a_2$ — this reflects the slow convergence of gradient bandits with small $\alpha$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sample average formula sign error:** Many students mistakenly write $Q_t(a) = \frac{\sum R_i}{N+1}$ — the denominator is the *count up to* $t-1$, not $t$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Indicator function misuse:** In derivations, dropping $\mathbb{1}_{A_i = a}$ in the numerator yields an incorrect formula. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **UCB undefined at $t=1$:** $N_t(a) = 0$ causes division by zero. Examiners expect students to mention the *initial round-robin tie-breaking rule*. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> 4. **Gradient update missing baseline:** Writing $H_{t+1}(a) = H_t(a) + \alpha R_t(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ *without* the baseline term $(R_t - \bar{R}_t)$ is incomplete and loses 2 marks. Always include the baseline.
> 5. **$\epsilon$-greedy confused with $\epsilon$-soft:** Some texts use $\epsilon$-soft to mean *all* actions have min probability $\epsilon/k$. Be clear which definition you adopt.
> 6. **Law of Large Numbers citation:** KTU examiners reward students who explicitly state the LLN when justifying sample-average convergence. (Gain 0.5 mark.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Action-Value Methods**

- 🔹 **Action-value $q_*(a) = \mathbb{E}[R_t \mid A_t = a]$** is the *expected* reward; we estimate it via $Q_t(a)$ using observed samples.
- 🔹 **Sample-average estimator** is unbiased and converges to $q_*(a)$ by the **Law of Large Numbers**.
- 🔹 **Incremental form:** $Q_{n+1} = Q_n + \alpha_n [R_n - Q_n]$; for stationary problems, $\alpha_n = 1/n$; for non-stationary, $\alpha = $ const.
- 🔹 **Greedy action selection** exploits but never explores → can be permanently suboptimal.
- 🔹 **$\epsilon$-greedy action selection** adds a uniform random exploration channel — simple, robust, asymptotically optimal.
- 🔹 **Optimistic initial values** force early exploration by making untried actions look attractive.
- 🔹 **UCB action selection** adds an uncertainty bonus $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ — smarter exploration than $\epsilon$-greedy.
- 🔹 **Gradient bandit algorithm** uses softmax over preferences $H_t(a)$ and updates via $(R_t - \bar{R}_t)(\mathbb{1}_{a=A_t} - \pi_t(a))$ — the baseline $\bar{R}_t$ is essential.
- 🔹 The two main tasks in action-value methods are **estimation** (compute $Q_t(a)$) and **control** (pick $A_t$).
- 🔹 KTU numerical problems typically test: (i) incremental update tracing, (ii) $\epsilon$-greedy first-5-step walk-throughs, (iii) gradient bandit preference updates.
- 🔹 Convergence theorems and the LLN are *favourite theoretical short-answer* topics.
- 🔹 Distinguish **stationary vs non-stationary** problems — choice of step-size $\alpha$ depends on which applies.
- 🔹 **Regret** (not just average reward) is the modern theoretical performance metric — UCB and gradient achieve $O(\ln n)$ regret.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incremental Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incremental Implementation** in Reinforcement Learning is a *computational paradigm* in which statistical estimates (such as **expected rewards**, **state-value functions** $V(s)$, or **action-value functions** $Q(s,a)$) are updated **sequentially and online** as each new sample becomes available, without retaining the entire history of previously observed data.

Formally, given a sequence of observations $\{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ and an estimate of their expectation $R_n$, the incremental scheme computes $R_{n+1}$ using only the *previous estimate* $R_n$ and the *new sample* $X_{n+1}$, avoiding the re-summation of all $n+1$ samples. This principle is the mathematical backbone of **stochastic approximation** in RL, powering algorithms such as Monte Carlo prediction, **Temporal-Difference (TD) learning**, and multi-armed bandit strategies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Running Test Average:**
> Imagine you are a student taking a series of mock tests. Rather than remembering **every single score** (which becomes infeasible after 100 tests), you maintain a **running average** in your head. After each new test score, you update the average as:
> *"New Average = Old Average + (small correction based on the new score)."*
> After many tests, your running average converges to your *true* ability level. **Incremental Implementation does exactly this for value functions in RL** — it forgets individual samples and refines a *single running estimate* using a correction proportional to the prediction error.

### 1.3 Why It Matters (Engineering Relevance)

| Engineering Domain | Role of Incremental Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Robotics** | Enables real-time policy updates from streaming sensor data. |
| **Dynamic Pricing Systems** | Allows online adaptation of price-value functions without full retraining. |
| **Recommender Systems (A/B Testing)** | Drives contextual bandit algorithms for live user engagement optimization. |
| **Industrial Process Control** | Supports adaptive control loops that refine their Q-values during deployment. |
| **Game AI (e.g., AlphaZero)** | Forms the basis of self-play value network updates after every move. |

### 1.4 Core Operational Concept

The general mathematical skeleton of any incremental RL update has the form:

$$
\text{NewEstimate} \;\leftarrow\; \text{OldEstimate} \;+\; \alpha \cdot \big[\text{Target} - \text{OldEstimate}\big]
$$

The bracketed term $[\text{Target} - \text{OldEstimate}]$ is universally called the **prediction error** or **TD-error** (in temporal-difference contexts). The scalar $\alpha$ is the **step-size parameter**, controlling the *learning rate*. This single equation is the **alpha and omega** of all incremental RL algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Sample Collection (Streaming Data Model)**
  - The agent interacts with the environment sequentially. At time $n$, a single new sample $X_{n+1}$ (e.g., a reward, return, or TD-target) is observed.
  - No *offline dataset* is assumed; data is *generated* by the policy-environment interaction loop.

- **Step 2 — Incremental Update of the Mean (Why It Works)**
  - A naive computation of the mean requires $\mathcal{O}(n)$ memory and $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time per query.
  - The incremental formulation reduces this to **$\mathcal{O}(1)$ memory** and **$\mathcal{O}(1)$ time per update**, making it suitable for lifelong learning agents.

- **Step 3 — The "Why" Behind the General Form**
  - The general update $R \leftarrow R + \alpha[\text{Target} - R]$ is essentially a **stochastic gradient descent** step on the squared error $\frac{1}{2}[\text{Target} - R]^2$.
  - The derivative w.r.t. $R$ is $-(\text{Target} - R)$, so moving $R$ in the *positive* direction of the negative gradient gives the update.

- **Step 4 — Step-Size Schedules (The "How" of Convergence)**
  - For **guaranteed convergence** to the true mean, the sequence $\{\alpha_n\}$ must satisfy the **Robbins-Monro conditions** (detailed in Section 3).
  - In practice, RL often uses *constant* $\alpha$, sacrificing asymptotic convergence for **adaptivity to non-stationary environments**.

- **Step 5 — Generalization to State-Value and Action-Value Estimation**
  - The same update rule is reused for:
    - **Monte Carlo (MC) prediction:** Target = actual return $G_t$.
    - **TD(0) prediction:** Target = $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$.
    - **n-step TD:** Target = $G_{t:t+n}$.
    - **Bandit action-value:** Target = observed reward $R_t$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are board-exam-essential. Memorize the boundary conditions and convergence criteria.**

| # | Formula / Condition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use / Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Arithmetic mean of first $n$ samples | Batch (non-incremental) baseline |
| 2 | $R_{n+1} = R_n + \dfrac{1}{n+1}\big[X_{n+1} - R_n\big]$ | Incremental mean with $\alpha = 1/(n+1)$ | Guarantees exact running average |
| 3 | $R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha \big[X_{n+1} - R_n\big]$ | General incremental update | Foundation of all RL online methods |
| 4 | $R_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)^n R_1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha (1-\alpha)^{n-i} X_i$ | Weighted average expansion (constant $\alpha$) | Shows exponential recency weighting |
| 5 | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\big]$ | TD(0) incremental update | TD prediction in unknown MDPs |
| 6 | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1},a) - Q(S_t,A_t)\big]$ | Q-Learning incremental update | Model-free off-policy control |
| 7 | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty$ | Robbins-Monro Condition 1 (sufficient exploration) | Necessary for convergence in stochastic approx. |
| 8 | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^{2} < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro Condition 2 (noise decays) | Necessary for convergence in stochastic approx. |
| 9 | $\text{TD Error } \delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | One-step temporal-difference error | Backbone of eligibility-trace methods |
| 10 | $R_{n+1} - R_n = \dfrac{1}{n+1}\big[X_{n+1} - R_n\big]$ | Difference equation of running mean | Used to prove $R_n \to \mathbb{E}[X]$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Case Study — HVAC Control in Smart Buildings:**
> The heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning (HVAC) system of a smart building can be cast as an MDP. An incremental RL agent updates its estimate of "comfort-cost value" $V(s)$ every minute using the TD-error from the new room temperature reading. The incremental approach ensures the building learns occupant preferences **without storing years of historical temperature data**, which is critical for memory-constrained embedded controllers on the edge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Batch Mean to Incremental Mean

We start with the *batch* definition of the sample mean and algebraically transform it into the incremental form.

**Given:** $R_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ (the average of the first $n$ samples).

**Step (a):** Write the new mean $R_{n+1}$ explicitly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} X_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b):** Split the summation into the first $n$ terms and the $(n+1)$-th term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \;+\; X_{n+1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c):** Factor out $n$ from the partial sum to expose the *old mean* $R_n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[n \cdot \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \;+\; X_{n+1}\right] \\
        &= \frac{1}{n+1}\left[n \cdot R_n \;+\; X_{n+1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d):** Distribute $\frac{1}{n+1}$ and rearrange.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \frac{n}{n+1} R_n \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1} X_{n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (e):** Recognize that $\dfrac{n}{n+1} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{n+1}$ and rewrite.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{n+1}\right) R_n \;+\; \frac{1}{n+1} X_{n+1} \\
        &= R_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\big[X_{n+1} - R_n\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha_n [X_{n+1} - R_n] \quad \text{with} \quad \alpha_n = \dfrac{1}{n+1}}$.

This shows that the simple running mean is a *special case* of the general incremental update where the step-size $\alpha_n$ decreases as $1/(n+1)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Robbins-Monro Convergence Theorem (Statement & Conditions)

The Robbins-Monro theorem (1951) provides conditions under which the iterative scheme

$$
R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha_n \big[b - R_n\big]
$$

converges to the root $R^*$ of the function $g(r) = \mathbb{E}[X \mid r] - r$, where $X$ is a noisy observation of $g(r)$ plus noise.

**Robbins-Monro Conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(C1)} \quad & \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty \\
\text{(C2)} \quad & \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^{2} < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition of Each Condition:**

- **(C1):** "The step-sizes are large enough to reach any point." If $\sum \alpha_n$ were finite, the total movement would be bounded, and the algorithm could *never* correct a large initial bias. (e.g., $\alpha_n = 1/n$ gives $\sum 1/n = \infty$ — *acceptable*.)
- **(C2):** "The step-sizes shrink fast enough to suppress noise." If $\sum \alpha_n^2$ diverges, stochastic fluctuations never die out, and the iterates jitter forever. (e.g., $\alpha_n = 1/n$ gives $\sum 1/n^2 < \infty$ — *acceptable*.)

**Verdict for $\alpha_n = 1/n$:** Both conditions hold, so the running mean *converges with probability 1* to the true expectation. This is the rigorous mathematical justification for incremental averaging in RL.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exponential Weighted Average Form (Constant Step-Size)

For a *constant* step-size $\alpha$, expand the recursive update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= R_n + \alpha(X_{n+1} - R_n) \\
        &= (1-\alpha)R_n + \alpha X_{n+1} \\
        &= (1-\alpha)\big[(1-\alpha)R_{n-1} + \alpha X_n\big] + \alpha X_{n+1} \\
        &= (1-\alpha)^2 R_{n-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)X_n + \alpha X_{n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Unrolling recursively back to $R_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= (1-\alpha)^n R_1 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n}(1-\alpha)^{n-i} X_{i+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The weight on the oldest sample is $(1-\alpha)^n$, which decays *geometrically*. For example, with $\alpha = 0.1$, the weight on a sample 50 steps old is $0.9^{50} \approx 0.0052 \approx 0.5\%$. This shows that **constant step-size incremental updates effectively "forget" distant past samples** — ideal for non-stationary environments where the underlying distribution drifts over time.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Batch Computation | Incremental (Exact, $\alpha=1/n$) | Incremental (Constant $\alpha$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Time per step | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Convergence | Exact | Yes (w.p. 1) | To a neighborhood |
| Adapts to drift? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Satisfies Robbins-Monro? | N/A | **Yes** | No (Condition 1 violated if $\alpha$ constant; only C2 holds) |
| Use in practice | Offline analysis | Stationary online learning | Non-stationary online learning |
| Example in RL | Final evaluation of $\pi$ | MC prediction (stationary $\pi$) | Q-Learning with $\alpha = 0.1$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Incremental Update Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Initialize Estimate R = R1]) --> A1[Receive new sample Xn+1 from environment]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Prediction Error: e = Target - R]
    A2 --> A3[Determine Step-Size alpha based on schedule]
    A3 --> A4[Update Estimate: R_new = R_old + alpha times e]
    A4 --> A5[Store R_new as current estimate]
    A5 --> A6{Continue Learning?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A1
    A6 -- No --> A7([End: Return final estimate R])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Batch vs. Incremental Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BATCH[Batch Pipeline]
        B1[Buffer all N samples] --> B2[Recompute sum every step]
        B2 --> B3[Output: Exact mean of N samples]
    end

    subgraph INCR[Incremental Pipeline]
        C1[Keep only current estimate R_n] --> C2[On new sample, apply update rule]
        C2 --> C3[Output: Approximate running mean]
        C3 --> C2
    end

    INPUT[Streaming Data] --> BATCH
    INPUT --> INCR

    BATCH -.Memory: O(N).-> BNOTE[High memory footprint]
    INCR -.Memory: O(1).-> CNOTE[Constant memory footprint]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — The Three Roles of the Step-Size $\alpha$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Step-Size alpha] --> S1{Large alpha near 1?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Fast adaptation, high variance, noisy estimates]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Small alpha near 0?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Smooth estimates, slow learning, may miss drift]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Balanced regime typical of practical RL]
    S2 --> S6[Example: alpha = 0.5 in early exploration]
    S4 --> S7[Example: alpha = 0.01 in converged phase]
    S5 --> S8[Example: alpha = 0.1 in Q-Learning]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for Exams:** Always annotate your Mermaid/flowchart in the answer sheet with a **one-line caption** stating the *function* of each block. KTU examiners reward clarity of thought over artistic complexity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Incremental Implementation in Reinforcement Learning. Why is it preferred over batch computation in online settings?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Incremental Implementation is a method of updating statistical estimates (such as state values or action values) sequentially after each new observation, using only the current estimate and the latest sample — without storing the full history of past samples. **(1 mark)**

Mathematically, it follows the form:
$$R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha_n [X_{n+1} - R_n]$$
**(1 mark)**

It is preferred over batch computation because it requires **$\mathcal{O}(1)$ memory** and **$\mathcal{O}(1)$ time per update**, enabling real-time online learning in environments where data arrives as a stream. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*State the Robbins-Monro conditions and explain their significance in the convergence of incremental updates.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Robbins-Monro conditions are two requirements on the step-size sequence $\{\alpha_n\}$ for convergence of the stochastic approximation scheme $R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha_n [b - R_n]$: **(1 mark)**

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^{2} < \infty$$
**(1 mark)**

The first condition ensures the step-sizes are large enough to *overcome initial bias*; the second ensures they shrink fast enough to *dampen stochastic noise*. Together they guarantee almost-sure convergence to the true expected value. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B carries 14 marks and provides an internal choice. Each choice has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. The complete step-by-step solution is provided below for both alternatives.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the incremental formulation for computing the running average of rewards $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$, starting from the batch definition of the arithmetic mean. Show clearly that $R_{n+1} = R_n + \frac{1}{n+1}[R_{n+1} - R_n]$ follows from the batch formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Starting from the batch definition $R_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$, write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} R_i \\
        &= \frac{1}{n+1}\Big[\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i + R_{n+1}\Big] \\
        &= \frac{1}{n+1}\Big[n \cdot R_n + R_{n+1}\Big] \\
        &= \frac{n}{n+1} R_n + \frac{1}{n+1} R_{n+1} \\
        &= R_n - \frac{1}{n+1} R_n + \frac{1}{n+1} R_{n+1} \\
        &= R_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\big[R_{n+1} - R_n\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks distribution:** (1) Writing the batch definition; (1) Splitting the sum; (1) Factoring out $R_n$; (2) Algebraic rearrangement; (2) Final boxed form. **(7/7)**

**(b)** Generalize the above to a general step-size $\alpha$. Show that the update $R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha [R_{n+1} - R_n]$ is a weighted average of the form $R_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)R_n + \alpha R_{n+1}$. Hence interpret the meaning of choosing $\alpha = 0$ versus $\alpha = 1$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Starting from the general incremental update, expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= R_n + \alpha \big[R_{n+1} - R_n\big] \\
        &= R_n + \alpha R_{n+1} - \alpha R_n \\
        &= (1-\alpha) R_n + \alpha R_{n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $R_{n+1}$ is a **convex combination** of the old estimate $R_n$ and the new sample $R_{n+1}$, with weights $(1-\alpha)$ and $\alpha$. **(1 mark)**

- **If $\alpha = 0$:** $R_{n+1} = R_n$. The agent *learns nothing*; the new sample is completely ignored. **(1 mark)**
- **If $\alpha = 1$:** $R_{n+1} = R_{n+1}$. The agent *forgets* its history entirely and adopts only the latest sample — a highly noisy estimator. **(1 mark)**
- **In between (e.g., $\alpha = 0.1$):** The agent performs a smooth, exponential-weighted blend of past and present. **(1 mark)**

Additionally, the constant $\alpha$ form leads to the geometrically weighted history:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{n+1} &= (1-\alpha)^n R_1 + \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{n}(1-\alpha)^{n-i} R_{i+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

which justifies why constant-$\alpha$ updates are **adaptive to non-stationary environments** — older samples carry exponentially smaller weight. **(2 marks)** **(7/7)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a clear diagram, explain how the incremental update mechanism is embedded in the TD(0) prediction algorithm. Write the full update rule and identify the role of each term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The TD(0) incremental update rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(S_t) &\leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block-by-block role identification:**

- **$V(S_t)$** — Current value estimate of the state $S_t$ at time $t$. **(1 mark)**
- **$R_{t+1}$** — Immediate reward observed after taking action from $S_t$. **(1 mark)**
- **$\gamma V(S_{t+1})$** — Discounted value of the *next* state, bootstrapping from the current estimate. **(1 mark)**
- **$R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$** — The **TD target**, i.e., the improved estimate of the value of $S_t$. **(1 mark)**
- **$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$** — The **TD error**; it measures the *discrepancy* between the current estimate and the bootstrapped target. **(1 mark)**
- **$\alpha$** — The step-size, controlling how aggressively the value is updated in response to the TD error. **(1 mark)**

**Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
+----------+    a_t    +----------+    R_{t+1}    +----------+
|   S_t    |  -------> |   S_{t+1}|  -----------> |  Agent   |
| V(S_t)   |           | V(S_{t+1})|              |  Update  |
+----------+           +----------+               | V(S_t)+= |
                                                   | alpha*delta|
                                                   +-----------+
```

The update loop is: **(1 mark)**
(i) Observe $S_t$, take action $a_t$.
(ii) Receive $R_{t+1}$ and observe $S_{t+1}$.
(iii) Compute $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$.
(iv) Apply $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$. **(7/7)**

**(b)** State and explain the Robbins-Monro conditions. Show that the step-size $\alpha_n = 1/n$ satisfies both conditions, and explain why this choice is suitable for *stationary* but not *non-stationary* environments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Statement of Robbins-Monro Conditions:**

For the iterative scheme $R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha_n [b - R_n]$ to converge almost surely to the root $R^*$, the step-size sequence $\{\alpha_n\}$ must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(C1)} \quad & \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n = \infty \\
\text{(C2)} \quad & \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^{2} < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)** for statement; **(1 mark)** for conditions.

**Verification for $\alpha_n = 1/n$:**

- (C1): The harmonic series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1/n$ diverges, so C1 holds. **(1 mark)**
- (C2): The series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6$ converges, so C2 holds. **(1 mark)**

**Suitability discussion:**

- **Stationary environments:** The true mean $R^*$ is *fixed*. The decaying step-size $1/n$ eventually becomes so small that updates are negligible, and the algorithm *locks in* on the true mean. **(1 mark)**
- **Non-stationary environments:** The true mean *drifts over time*. With $\alpha_n = 1/n$, the algorithm becomes increasingly sluggish and can no longer track the drift — it permanently under-weights recent samples. A **constant step-size** $\alpha \in (0,1)$ is preferred here, because it ensures every recent sample has meaningful influence. **(2 marks)** **(7/7)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_n$ (the running mean) with $R_{t+1}$ (a single reward):** Students frequently mislabel the incremental update equation. The **target** in the bracket is the *new sample* (a single observation), not the running mean itself.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ symbol:** Many students write $R_{n+1} = R_n + (R_{n+1} - R_n)$, which collapses to $R_{n+1} = R_{n+1}$ (a tautology!) and earns **zero marks**. The step-size $\alpha$ is *non-negotiable* in the general form.
> 3. **Mixing up Robbins-Monro conditions:** Condition 1 is $\sum \alpha_n = \infty$ (NOT $\alpha_n \to 0$); Condition 2 is $\sum \alpha_n^2 < \infty$ (NOT $\alpha_n^2 \to 0$). These are *series* conditions, not *limit* conditions. Examiners specifically test for this distinction.
> 4. **In TD(0), misidentifying the TD target:** The TD target is $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$, **not** $R_{t+1}$ alone. Forgetting the bootstrapped value term costs the full 7 marks in Part B.
> 5. **No block diagram in long answers:** For 7-mark sub-parts that ask "with a clear diagram", examiners allocate at least **1 mark** for a labeled sketch. Omitting it forfeits that mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Glance through this section 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Core Definition:** Incremental Implementation = online, $\mathcal{O}(1)$-memory, $\mathcal{O}(1)$-time update of running statistical estimates in RL.
- **Master Formula:** $R_{n+1} = R_n + \alpha [X_{n+1} - R_n]$ — applicable to bandit averages, MC returns, and TD targets.
- **Exact-Average Special Case:** $\alpha = 1/(n+1)$ gives the *exact* running mean.
- **Constant-$\alpha$ Behavior:** $R_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)^n R_1 + \alpha \sum (1-\alpha)^{n-i} X_{i+1}$ — exponential recency weighting, *non-stationary friendly*.
- **TD(0) Update:** $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$.
- **Q-Learning Update:** $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a} Q(S_{t+1},a) - Q(S_t,A_t)]$.
- **TD Error:** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ — the "innovation" that drives all incremental corrections.
- **Robbins-Monro Conditions:** (1) $\sum \alpha_n = \infty$ and (2) $\sum \alpha_n^2 < \infty$ — *necessary and sufficient* (with mild regularity) for almost-sure convergence.
- **$\alpha_n = 1/n$ Satisfies Both:** Harmonic series diverges, Basel series converges.
- **$\alpha_n = 1/n^{0.6}$ Fails C1:** $\sum 1/n^{0.6} = \infty$ ✓ but $\sum 1/n^{1.2} < \infty$ — actually this *does* satisfy both. Memorize only the harmonic case as the canonical example.
- **Constant $\alpha$ Violates C1:** $\sum \alpha = \infty$ is *trivially true*, so the issue is different — constant $\alpha$ does *not* converge to a *point* but to a *neighborhood* of the target. This is *intentional* in non-stationary settings.
- **Memory Advantage:** Batch needs $\mathcal{O}(n)$; Incremental needs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — always state this when justifying "why incremental" in exams.
- **Engineering Domains:** Robotics, dynamic pricing, recommender systems, HVAC control, game AI — at least one example should appear in long answers for context.
- **Exam Pattern Cue:** 3-mark questions test *definition + significance*; 7-mark sub-parts test *derivation + interpretation*; 14-mark questions combine both with a diagram.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Incremental Implementation. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Epsilon-Greedy Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Epsilon-Greedy Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epsilon-Greedy Method (ε-Greedy):** A fundamental action-selection policy in Reinforcement Learning that resolves the **Exploration–Exploitation Dilemma** by selecting the greedy (best-known) action with probability **(1 − ε)** and selecting a uniformly random action with probability **ε**, where **0 ≤ ε ≤ 1** is a tunable exploration parameter.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the ε-greedy policy π(s) for a state **s** is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\pi(s) = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(s, a), & \text{with probability } 1 - \varepsilon \\ a \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{A}), & \text{with probability } \varepsilon \end{cases}
$$

where **Q(s, a)** is the **action-value function** (the estimated return of taking action **a** in state **s**), and **𝒜** is the action space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Choice Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are a student in Kerala trying to find the best biryani spot near your college in Trivandrum.
>
> - **Exploitation:** You keep going to the *same restaurant* you already know is good (greedy action).
> - **Exploration:** Occasionally (say, 1 day out of 10, i.e., ε = 0.1), you randomly try a *new* restaurant, which might turn out to be even better.
> - **ε-Greedy:** With 90% probability you exploit your current favourite; with 10% probability you explore randomly.
>
> Over hundreds of lunch breaks, this simple rule outperforms both pure exploitation (you get stuck at a mediocre spot) and pure exploration (you keep trying bad places randomly).

### 1.3 Why ε-Greedy Matters in Reinforcement Learning

The agent must balance two competing goals:

| Aspect | Exploitation (1 − ε) | Exploration (ε) |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Maximize immediate known reward | Discover potentially better long-term actions |
| **Risk** | Sub-optimal convergence (local maxima) | Short-term reward loss |
| **Analogy** | Eating at favourite restaurant | Trying a new restaurant |
| **Probability** | **(1 − ε)** | **ε** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ε-Greedy is the *baseline* policy-improvement operator in Module 1. Every advanced strategy (UCB, Thompson Sampling, Softmax) is essentially a *smarter* way to do what ε-greedy does — explore sometimes, exploit most of the time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The 5-Step Logic of ε-Greedy

1. **Maintain an Action-Value Table:** For each state-action pair, keep a running estimate $\hat{Q}(s, a)$ of the true value $Q^*(s, a)$.
2. **Generate a Random Number:** Draw $r \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$ at every decision step.
3. **Decision Threshold Check:** Compare **r** with the hyperparameter **ε**.
4. **Branch on Outcome:**
   - If $r < \varepsilon$ → **EXPLORE** (random action from 𝒜)
   - If $r \geq \varepsilon$ → **EXPLOIT** (greedy: $\arg\max_a \hat{Q}(s, a)$)
5. **Update & Repeat:** Observe reward **R**, update $\hat{Q}(s, a)$, and continue.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1–2 (Estimation + Randomness):** The agent's knowledge is imperfect; randomness injects a controlled "chance" to escape local optima.
- **Step 3 (Threshold):** The single hyperparameter **ε** acts as a *dial* — set ε = 0 → pure greedy (no exploration); set ε = 1 → pure random (no learning).
- **Step 4 (Greedy vs Random):** Greedy leverages the *best known* action; random ensures *every* action is sampled infinitely often in the limit (a key convergence property).
- **Step 5 (Update):** Without updating $\hat{Q}$, the agent cannot learn; this is typically done using the **sample-average** rule or **exponential recency-weighted average**.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU 2024 Exams** — Every formula below is a high-probability exam item.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ε-Greedy Policy** | $\pi(s) = \arg\max_a \hat{Q}(s,a)$ w.p. $1-\varepsilon$; else $a \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{A})$ | Action-selection rule |
| 2 | **Sample-Average Estimate** | $\hat{Q}_n(a) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Average of first *n* rewards for action *a* |
| 3 | **Incremental Update Rule** | $\hat{Q}_{n+1}(a) = \hat{Q}_n(a) + \alpha_n \left[ R_n - \hat{Q}_n(a) \right]$ | $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ for sample average |
| 4 | **Constant Step-Size Update** | $\hat{Q}_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)\hat{Q}_n + \alpha R_n$ | $\alpha \in (0,1)$ fixed |
| 5 | **Decaying ε-Greedy Schedule** | $\varepsilon_t = \min\!\left(1, \frac{c \cdot \mathcal{A}}{t}\right)$ | $c > 0$, *t* = episode/time step |
| 6 | **Greedy Probability Mass** | $P(a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ | Probability of selecting optimal action $a^*$ |
| 7 | **Sub-Optimal Action Probability** | $P(a \neq a^*) = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$ | Uniform over non-greedy actions |
| 8 | **GLIE Condition (Greedy in Limit)** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \varepsilon_t = 0$ **and** $\sum_t \varepsilon_t = \infty$ | Guarantees convergence to optimal |
| 9 | **Expected Regret Bound** | $\mathbb{E}[\text{Regret}_T] = \mathcal{O}\!\left( \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert \ln T}{\Delta} \right)$ | Sub-linear in *T*, scaled by gap Δ |
| 10 | **Greedy Action Definition** | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \hat{Q}(s,a)$ | Ties broken arbitrarily / uniformly |

> **Engineering Utility:** ε-greedy is the workhorse of **multi-armed bandits**, **A/B testing systems at Google/Amazon**, **clinical trial design**, and **adaptive routing in computer networks**. Its simplicity makes it the *first policy taught* in any industrial RL pipeline.

### 2.4 The Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off — Formal Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (EE Dilemma):** An agent must choose between actions that **maximize immediate reward based on current knowledge** (exploitation) and actions that **may yield higher future reward but are uncertain** (exploration). ε-greedy is the *simplest* stochastic policy that addresses this trade-off in a tunable, parameter-controlled manner.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Selecting the Optimal Action

We derive the closed-form expression for $P(a = a^*)$ under ε-greedy.

> **Given:**
> - Total number of actions: $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = k$
> - Greedy action selected w.p. $1 - \varepsilon$
> - Random action (uniform over $k$ actions) selected w.p. $\varepsilon$

> **Step 1 — Partition the total probability into two disjoint cases.**

$$
P(a = a^*) = P(\text{exploit AND } a=a^*) + P(\text{explore AND } a=a^*)
$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute the probabilities of each event.**

$$
P(a = a^*) = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \underbrace{P(a = a^* \mid \text{exploit})}_{= \, 1} + \varepsilon \cdot \underbrace{P(a = a^* \mid \text{explore})}_{= \, 1/k}
$$

> **Step 3 — Simplify using the law of total probability.**

$$
P(a = a^*) = (1 - \varepsilon) + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{k}
$$

> **Step 4 — Final compact form (this is the KTU "must-know" identity).**

$$
\boxed{\, P(a = a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Incremental Update Rule from Sample Average

> **Goal:** Show that the incremental formula is *mathematically identical* to the sample-average estimator.

> **Step 1 — Write the sample-average estimate after $(n+1)$ observations.**

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} R_i
$$

> **Step 2 — Split the sum to isolate the new reward $R_{n+1}$.**

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i + R_{n+1} \right)
$$

> **Step 3 — Express the inner sum using the previous estimate $\hat{Q}_n$.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = n \cdot \hat{Q}_n
$$

> **Step 4 — Substitute and factor.**

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \frac{1}{n+1}\left( n\hat{Q}_n + R_{n+1} \right) = \frac{n}{n+1}\hat{Q}_n + \frac{1}{n+1}R_{n+1}
$$

> **Step 5 — Rewrite using $\hat{Q}_n$ as the anchor.**

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \hat{Q}_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\left( R_{n+1} - \hat{Q}_n \right)
$$

> **Step 6 — Identify the step-size $\alpha_n = \dfrac{1}{n+1}$.**

$$
\boxed{\, \hat{Q}_{n+1} = \hat{Q}_n + \alpha_n \left( R_{n+1} - \hat{Q}_n \right) \,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the foundation of **Temporal-Difference (TD) learning** in later KTU modules — note the structure "New Estimate ← Old Estimate + Step-Size × (Target − Old Estimate)".

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Regret Bound for Stationary ε-Greedy

> **Setup:** Consider a *k*-armed bandit. For each arm *a*, let $\Delta_a = Q^* - Q(a)$ be the *sub-optimality gap*. Stationary ε-greedy plays a random arm w.p. ε.

> **Step 1 — Expected per-step regret.**

$$
\mathbb{E}[r_t] = \sum_{a \neq a^*} \pi_t(a) \cdot \Delta_a \leq \varepsilon \cdot \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a
$$

> **Step 2 — Cumulative regret over $T$ steps.**

$$
\text{Regret}_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} r_t
$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the law of large numbers (bounded gaps).**

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{Regret}_T] \leq \varepsilon \cdot T \cdot \max_a \Delta_a
$$

> **Step 4 — Choose optimal ε analytically.**

Minimising $f(\varepsilon) = \varepsilon T$ subject to sampling each arm $\propto \varepsilon/k$ gives an $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT \ln T})$ regret bound.

> **Step 5 — Final form (logarithmic regret).**

$$
\boxed{\, \mathbb{E}[\text{Regret}_T] = \mathcal{O}\!\left( \sqrt{kT \ln T} \right) \,}
$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-armed bandit has true values $Q^* = [0.4, \; 0.5, \; 0.3, \; 0.6]$. An agent uses ε-greedy with **ε = 0.2**. Compute the probability of selecting the optimal action on any given step.

> **Solution:**

- Optimal action: $a^* = 4$ (value 0.6); so $k = 4$.

$$
P(a = a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{k} = 1 - 0.2 + \dfrac{0.2}{4} = 0.8 + 0.05 = 0.85
$$

$$
P(a \neq a^*) = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \quad \text{(split uniformly as } 0.0375 \text{ per non-greedy arm)}
$$

> **Verification:** $4 \times 0.0375 + 0.85 = 0.15 + 0.85 = 1.00$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — ε-Greedy Action Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State s observed by Agent] --> B[Lookup Q-table for state s]
    B --> C[Generate r uniform 0 to 1]
    C --> D{Is r less than epsilon}
    D -- YES --> E[EXPLORATION BRANCH]
    D -- NO --> F[EXPLOITATION BRANCH]
    E --> G[Sample a uniformly from Action Set A]
    F --> H[Compute a star equals argmax of Q s comma a]
    G --> I[Execute action a in Environment]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Receive reward R and next state s prime]
    J --> K[Update Q s comma a via incremental rule]
    K --> L[Increment time step t by 1]
    L --> M{More steps remaining}
    M -- YES --> A
    M -- NO --> N[End of Episode]
```

### 4.2 State-Transition Flow — ε-Greedy Convergence Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Initial High Exploration: epsilon equals 1.0] --> A1[Random Actions Dominate]
    A1 --> A2[Q-table populated with noisy estimates]
    A2 --> A3[Decay schedule reduces epsilon over time]
    A3 --> A4[Exploration Decreases: epsilon equals 0.5]
    A4 --> A5[Exploitation Increases]
    A5 --> A6[GLIE Condition satisfied: epsilon approaches 0]
    A6 --> A7[Convergence to Optimal Policy pi star]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Matrix — ε-Greedy vs Other Strategies

| Criterion | **ε-Greedy** | **ε-Decreasing** | **UCB** | **Softmax / Boltzmann** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exploration type | Uniform random | Uniform random (decaying) | Optimism-based | Probability-weighted |
| Hyperparameters | ε (one) | ε₀, decay rate *c* | Confidence scalar *c* | Temperature τ |
| Best-arm guarantee (asymptotic) | No (if ε constant) | Yes (GLIE) | Yes | Yes |
| Implementation complexity | **Lowest** | Low | Medium | Low |
| Regret bound | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT\ln T})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT\ln T})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT\ln T})$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT})$ |
| KTU Module 1 focus | **Primary topic** | Extension | Referenced | Comparison only |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define the ε-greedy action-selection method. State its policy equation and explain the role of the parameter ε.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **(1 Mark)** ε-greedy is a stochastic policy for action selection in reinforcement learning that balances exploration and exploitation.
> - **(1 Mark)** The policy is: with probability $1-\varepsilon$, choose the greedy action $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$; with probability $\varepsilon$, choose an action uniformly at random from the action set 𝒜.
> - **(1 Mark)** The parameter ε (where $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$) controls the exploration rate — larger ε means more exploration, smaller ε means more exploitation. ε = 0 gives pure greedy, ε = 1 gives pure random.

**Q2. What is the GLIE condition for an ε-greedy policy? Why is it necessary?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **(1 Mark)** GLIE = **Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration**.
> - **(1 Mark)** Two conditions: (i) all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often in the limit, and (ii) the policy converges to a greedy policy as $t \to \infty$, i.e., $\lim_{t\to\infty} \varepsilon_t = 0$.
> - **(1 Mark)** Necessity: GLIE ensures the value estimates $Q(s,a)$ converge to their true values $Q^*(s,a)$ and the policy converges to the optimal policy $\pi^*$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 2024 Scheme, Full 14-Mark Pattern with Internal Choice]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for the probability of selecting the optimal action under the ε-greedy policy for a *k*-armed bandit. Show every algebraic step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 5-armed bandit with true action values $Q^* = [0.2,\; 0.8,\; 0.5,\; 0.3,\; 0.1]$. An ε-greedy agent uses **ε = 0.1** for the first 100 steps and then **ε = 0.01** for the next 900 steps. Calculate:
  (i) The probability of selecting the optimal action in each phase.
  (ii) The expected number of times the optimal action is selected across 1000 steps.
  (iii) The expected number of *sub-optimal* selections in Phase 1. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the policy using the law of total probability.
> **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Split into two cases (exploit / explore) and assign probabilities.
> **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute the probability that the explore branch hits the greedy action.
> **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Simplify to the final compact form.

The full derivation is identical to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected final boxed answer is:

$$
P(a = a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{k}
$$

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**(i) Probability of selecting optimal action (2 Marks):**

- $k = 5$, optimal arm = arm 2 (value 0.8).

**Phase 1 (ε = 0.1):**

$$
P_1(a = a^*) = 1 - 0.1 + \dfrac{0.1}{5} = 0.9 + 0.02 = 0.92
$$

**Phase 2 (ε = 0.01):**

$$
P_2(a = a^*) = 1 - 0.01 + \dfrac{0.01}{5} = 0.99 + 0.002 = 0.992
$$

**(ii) Expected number of optimal selections (3 Marks):**

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_1^*] = 100 \times 0.92 = 92 \text{ selections}
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_2^*] = 900 \times 0.992 = 892.8 \approx 893 \text{ selections}
$$

$$
