\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{total}}^*] = 92 + 893 = 985 \text{ optimal selections out of 1000}
$$

**(iii) Expected sub-optimal selections in Phase 1 (2 Marks):**

$$
P_1(a \neq a^*) = 1 - 0.92 = 0.08
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[\text{sub-optimal in Phase 1}] = 100 \times 0.08 = 8 \text{ selections}
$$

(Equivalently, $\frac{0.1}{5} = 0.02$ per sub-optimal arm × 4 arms = 0.08 ✓)

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the exploration–exploitation dilemma in reinforcement learning. With a clear diagram, show how the ε-greedy method resolves this dilemma. Discuss what happens when **ε = 0** and when **ε = 1**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reinforcement learning agent uses the incremental update rule:
$$\hat{Q}_{n+1}(a) = \hat{Q}_n(a) + \alpha_n [R_n - \hat{Q}_n(a)]$$
Starting from $\hat{Q}_0 = 0$ and receiving the first four rewards $R_1 = 2, R_2 = 4, R_3 = 6, R_4 = 8$, compute the running estimate $\hat{Q}_n$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$ using the sample-average step-size $\alpha_n = 1/n$. Show every intermediate step. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks)**

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - **2 Marks:** Definition of EE dilemma (exploitation = use known info; exploration = gather new info).
> - **2 Marks:** ε-greedy resolution with policy equation.
> - **2 Marks:** Boundary cases (ε = 0 → no exploration; ε = 1 → no exploitation).
> - **1 Mark:** Diagram (use the Mermaid flow from Section 4.1).

- **ε = 0:** Pure exploitation; agent always picks $\arg\max_a \hat{Q}(s,a)$ → can get stuck on a sub-optimal action forever.
- **ε = 1:** Pure exploration; agent picks uniformly at random → never exploits learnt knowledge; reward is just the average over arms.

##### **Part (b) — Incremental Update Calculation (7 Marks)**

Using $\alpha_n = 1/n$:

**n = 1 (1 Mark):**

$$
\hat{Q}_1 = 0 + \frac{1}{1}(2 - 0) = 2.0
$$

**n = 2 (2 Marks):**

$$
\hat{Q}_2 = 2.0 + \frac{1}{2}(4 - 2.0) = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0
$$

**n = 3 (2 Marks):**

$$
\hat{Q}_3 = 3.0 + \frac{1}{3}(6 - 3.0) = 3.0 + 1.0 = 4.0
$$

**n = 4 (2 Marks):**

$$
\hat{Q}_4 = 4.0 + \frac{1}{4}(8 - 4.0) = 4.0 + 1.0 = 5.0
$$

> **Verification (Bonus Point Opportunity):** Sample average of $\{2, 4, 6, 8\}$ = $(2+4+6+8)/4 = 20/4 = 5.0$ ✓

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{\varepsilon}{k}$ term in $P(a=a^*)$** — many students write only $1-\varepsilon$ and miss the explore-branch contribution. This costs **2 marks** in derivation questions.
> 2. **Using fixed ε and claiming convergence to optimal policy** — examiners will deduct **1 mark** if you do not explicitly mention the GLIE / ε-decaying condition.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate steps in incremental update calculations** — every single application of $\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \hat{Q}_n + \alpha_n(R_n - \hat{Q}_n)$ must be written out; do not skip from $n=1$ to $n=4$ directly.
> 4. **Confusing $\alpha_n = 1/n$ (sample average) with constant $\alpha$ (exponential recency-weighted average)** — they are *different* estimators; label clearly.
> 5. **Treating ties in $\arg\max$** — the question "what happens when two actions have equal Q-values?" is a favourite **2-mark sub-question**; answer: *uniform random tie-breaking*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — ε-Greedy Method**

- **Core Idea:** Greedy w.p. $1-\varepsilon$, random w.p. $\varepsilon$.
- **Key Identity:** $P(a=a^*) = 1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{k}$ *(must know by heart)*.
- **Hyperparameter:** ε ∈ [0, 1] — *tune it, don't fix it*.
- **Sample-Average Update:** $\hat{Q}_{n+1} = \hat{Q}_n + \dfrac{1}{n+1}(R_{n+1} - \hat{Q}_n)$.
- **GLIE Condition:** All $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often + $\varepsilon_t \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.
- **Boundary Cases:** ε = 0 (pure greedy, no exploration) | ε = 1 (pure random, no exploitation).
- **Decaying Schedule:** $\varepsilon_t = \min\!\left(1, \dfrac{c \cdot k}{t}\right)$ guarantees GLIE.
- **Regret:** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{kT\ln T})$ — sub-linear, so the agent *does* learn.
- **Limitation:** Constant ε never converges; uniform random exploration is *inefficient* (no preference for promising-but-uncertain actions — this motivates **UCB** and **Thompson Sampling** in advanced modules).
- **Real-World Use:** A/B testing, recommendation systems, adaptive clinical trials, network routing.
- **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 for the complete action-selection flowchart.
- **Numericals to Practice:** Probability calculation, incremental update trace, expected selection count.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploration vs Exploitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exploration vs Exploitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, the **Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off** is the foundational decision-making dilemma faced by a learning **agent** at every time step. It is formally defined as:

> **Exploration** is the act of choosing an action that the agent has insufficient knowledge about, in order to gather new information about the environment's transition dynamics and reward structure. **Exploitation** is the act of choosing the action that is currently believed to deliver the **maximum expected reward** based on the agent's existing knowledge (value estimates).

Mathematically, at state $S_t$, the agent must choose between:
- **Exploitation:** $A_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} Q(S_t, a)$ — selecting the action with the highest estimated value.
- **Exploration:** $A_t \sim \pi_{\text{explore}}$ — sampling from a policy biased toward uncertainty reduction.

The trade-off is governed by a single design parameter: the **exploration rate** $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Exploration–Exploitation is the **first concept** introduced in any RL course because *every* RL algorithm (Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, PPO) must address it. Questions appear in **Part A (3 marks)** almost every semester, and frequently in **Part B (14 marks)** through ε-greedy policy derivation or regret analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Dilemma"

Imagine you are in **Kerala** and have just moved to a new city:

- **Exploitation:** Every evening, you go to the same biryani restaurant near your hostel because you *know* it's good (high expected reward). You maximize your short-term satisfaction, but you may be missing out on a better **meals** restaurant three streets away.
- **Exploration:** One evening, you try a new restaurant. You might discover it is **fantastic** (reward = 9/10) or terrible (reward = 2/10). Either way, you have *updated* your mental model.

The smart strategy is **not to do all of one or the other**. A balanced approach — *occasionally exploring* — leads to the best **long-term** cumulative reward. This is the essence of the Exploration–Exploitation trade-off.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Agent | — | The learner / decision-maker |
| Action | $A_t$ | Choice made at time $t$ |
| State | $S_t$ | Current environment configuration |
| Reward | $R_{t+1}$ | Scalar feedback signal |
| Q-value | $Q(s, a)$ | Estimated expected return |
| Exploration rate | $\varepsilon$ | Probability of taking a random action |
| Regret | $\rho_T$ | Cumulative loss from not choosing optimal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** In theoretical RL, the *quality* of an exploration strategy is measured by **cumulative regret** $R_T$, with the goal of achieving **sublinear regret**, i.e., $R_T = o(T)$ as the time horizon $T \to \infty$. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 1.4 Why This Trade-off Exists — The Core Tension

- **Pure Exploitation** → Agent gets *stuck* in a local optimum (a **suboptimal policy**).
- **Pure Exploration** → Agent never uses its knowledge, so cumulative reward is low.
- **Balanced Strategy** → Agent asymptotically converges to the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$.

The **No-Free-Lunch Theorem** of exploration states: *no single exploration strategy is optimal in all environments*. Hence, the choice of strategy is problem-dependent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decision Pipeline at Each Time Step

The agent's interaction with the environment follows this logical chain at every step $t$:

1. **Observe** current state $S_t$.
2. **Estimate** $Q(S_t, a)$ for all $a \in \mathcal{A}$ using existing data.
3. **Compute uncertainty** $\sigma(S_t, a)$ for each action.
4. **Decide**: 
    - With probability $\varepsilon$ → **Explore** (random or uncertainty-driven action).
    - With probability $1 - \varepsilon$ → **Exploit** (greedy on $Q$).
5. **Execute** action $A_t$, observe reward $R_{t+1}$ and next state $S_{t+1}$.
6. **Update** the value estimate $Q(S_t, A_t)$ using a learning rule.
7. **Repeat** for $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, T$.

The **'Why'** behind step 4: the exploration rate $\varepsilon$ controls the agent's *information gathering* vs *reward maximization* balance. The **'How'** is encoded in the chosen **exploration strategy** (ε-greedy, softmax, UCB, Thompson, etc.).

---

## 2.2 Exploration Strategies — Engineering Taxonomy

### Strategy A: **ε-Greedy (Most Common in KTU Exams)**

- With probability $\varepsilon$, choose a **uniformly random** action.
- With probability $1 - \varepsilon$, choose $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$.
- **Decay variant:** $\varepsilon_t = \max(\varepsilon_{\min}, \varepsilon_0 \cdot \text{decay}^t)$.

### Strategy B: **Softmax / Boltzmann Exploration**

- Action probabilities are weighted by exponentials of Q-values:

$$P(a \mid s) = \frac{\exp(Q(s, a) / \tau)}{\sum_{a' \in \mathcal{A}} \exp(Q(s, a') / \tau)}$$

- The **temperature** $\tau > 0$ controls exploration: high $\tau$ → uniform (more exploration); low $\tau$ → greedy (more exploitation).

### Strategy C: **Upper Confidence Bound (UCB-1)**

- Selects action by optimism under uncertainty:

$$A_t = \arg\max_{a} \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$

- The second term is a **confidence bonus** that decays as action $a$ is taken more often.

### Strategy D: **Thompson Sampling**

- Maintains a posterior distribution over Q-values (e.g., Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$).
- Samples a value from the posterior and chooses the action with the **maximum sample**.

### Strategy E: **Optimistic Initialization**

- Initialize $Q(s, a)$ to a high value $Q_{\max}$.
- Encourages exploration because untried actions look attractive.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar in absolute-value and probability notation is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown tables.

| **Strategy** | **Action Selection Rule** | **Key Parameter** | **Regret Bound** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ε-Greedy | $A_t = \text{random}$ w.p. $\varepsilon$, else $\arg\max Q$ | $\varepsilon$ | Linear (no decay); $O(\log T)$ with decay | Simple discrete actions |
| ε-Decay | Same, with $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \delta^t$ | $\varepsilon_0, \delta$ | $O(\log T)$ | Long-horizon tasks |
| Softmax (Boltzmann) | $P(a) = \frac{\exp(Q(s,a)/\tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)}$ | Temperature $\tau$ | Task-dependent | Continuous-valued Q |
| UCB-1 | $A_t = \arg\max_a \left[ Q_t(a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$ | Confidence $c$ | $O(\sqrt{K T \ln T})$ | Bandits, finite MDPs |
| Thompson Sampling | $A_t = \arg\max_a \tilde{Q}_t(a), \ \tilde{Q}_t \sim p(Q \mid \mathcal{D})$ | Prior | $O(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ | Bayesian RL |
| Optimistic Init. | $A_t = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ with $Q_0 = Q_{\max}$ | $Q_{\max}$ | $O(\log T)$ | Finite-state MDPs |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Why Exploration–Exploitation Matters** |
|---|---|
| **Robotics (KTU Robotics Specialization)** | A manipulator must try novel trajectories to learn manipulation; exploiting bad priors may damage hardware. |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Vehicle must occasionally test edge cases (rainy curves, construction zones) rather than always repeating known safe routes. |
| **Recommender Systems** | Streaming platforms balance showing proven content (exploit) vs surfacing new items (explore). |
| **A/B Testing in Industry** | Conversion-rate optimization is fundamentally a **multi-armed bandit** problem. |
| **Healthcare AI** | Treatment policies must explore alternative therapies while exploiting known effective ones (off-policy constraints apply). |
| **Smart Grid Energy Dispatch** | Operators explore demand-response schemes but must reliably meet current load. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **decay schedule** of $\varepsilon$ is often more important than the choice of strategy. A poorly tuned $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon = 0.1$ fixed forever) wastes 10% of all future rewards even after convergence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Reward Under ε-Greedy

We derive the **expected per-step reward** when an agent follows ε-greedy over a fixed set of actions with true values $q_*(a)$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the expectation.** Let the optimal action be $a^* = \arg\max_a q_*(a)$. The probability of selecting $a^*$ under ε-greedy is:

$$P(A_t = a^*) = (1 - \varepsilon) + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert}$$

> **Logic:** We choose greedily with probability $1 - \varepsilon$, and if we explore randomly (prob $\varepsilon$), the chance of landing on $a^*$ by chance is $1 / \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ where $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ is the cardinality of the action space.

**Step 2 — Expected reward of greedy action.** The greedy reward contribution is:

$$E[R_{\text{greedy}}] = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot q_*(a^*)$$

**Step 3 — Expected reward of random action.** Assuming uniform random selection across non-greedy actions:

$$E[R_{\text{random}}] = \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} q_*(a^*) + \frac{\varepsilon (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \cdot \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$$

where $\bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$ is the average true value of non-optimal actions.

**Step 4 — Combine both terms.** Adding Steps 2 and 3:

$$E[R_t] = (1 - \varepsilon) q_*(a^*) + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} q_*(a^*) + \frac{\varepsilon (\vert \mathcal{A} \vert - 1)}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.** Group the $q_*(a^*)$ terms:

$$E[R_t] = \left( 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) q_*(a^*) + \varepsilon \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the regret per step.** The optimal reward is $q_*(a^*)$. The single-step regret is:

$$\ell_t = q_*(a^*) - E[R_t] = \varepsilon \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) \left[ q_*(a^*) - \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}} \right]$$

> **Key Insight:** This is **constant per step** when $\varepsilon$ is fixed, which means **total regret $R_T$ grows linearly in $T$** — a critical KTU result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Regret Bound for ε-Greedy with Decay

We now let $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 / t$ and derive the **asymptotic regret**.

**Step 1 — Total regret definition.**

$$R_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \varepsilon_t \cdot \Delta_{\max}$$

where $\Delta_{\max} = q_*(a^*) - \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$ is the maximum per-step loss.

**Step 2 — Substitute the decay schedule.**

$$R_T = \Delta_{\max} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the harmonic series.** By definition, the harmonic number is:

$$H_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t} = \ln T + \gamma + O(1/T)$$

where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the **Euler–Mascheroni constant**.

**Step 4 — Final asymptotic expression.**

$$R_T = \Delta_{\max} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \left[ \ln T + \gamma + O(1/T) \right] \quad \xrightarrow{T \to \infty} \quad O(\ln T)$$

> **KTU Gold Result:** *ε-Greedy with $\varepsilon_t = 1/t$ achieves **logarithmic regret**, hence the policy is **no-regret**.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — UCB-1 Regret Analysis

The UCB-1 action selection rule is repeated here for derivation:

$$A_t = \arg\max_{a} \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$

**Step 1 — Hoeffding's Inequality Setup.** For any action $a$ with true mean $q_*(a)$, after $N_t(a)$ samples, the deviation satisfies:

$$P\!\left( \vert Q_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert \geq c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right) \leq 2 t^{-2c^2}$$

**Step 2 — Union bound over all actions.** With $K = \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ actions, the probability that *any* action deviates is:

$$P\!\left( \exists a : \vert Q_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert \geq c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right) \leq 2K t^{-2c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Choose $c$ for proper confidence.** Setting $2c^2 = 1$ (i.e., $c = 1/\sqrt{2}$) ensures the bound is summable:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} 2K t^{-1} = \infty \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Borel–Cantelli applies}$$

**Step 4 — Bound the number of suboptimal pulls.** Any suboptimal action $a$ is pulled only while its UCB exceeds that of the optimal $a^*$. After enough samples, this stops. Standard analysis yields:

$$N_T(a) \leq \frac{4 c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a^2} + O(1)$$

**Step 5 — Total regret bound.**

$$R_T = \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a \cdot E[N_T(a)] \leq 8 c^2 \sum_{a \neq a^*} \frac{\ln T}{\Delta_a} + O(K)$$

$$R_T = O\!\left( \sqrt{K T \ln T} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** The comparison *"ε-Greedy achieves $O(\ln T)$ only with $1/t$ decay; UCB-1 achieves $O(\sqrt{K T \ln T})$ which is **distribution-dependent** and often tighter in practice"* is a very high-yield point.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Comparison Table

| **Metric** | **ε-Greedy (fixed)** | **ε-Greedy (decay $1/t$)** | **Softmax (Boltzmann)** | **UCB-1** | **Thompson Sampling** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regret $R_T$ | $\Theta(T)$ | $O(\ln T)$ | Task-dep. | $O(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ | $O(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ |
| Implementation | Trivial | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Hard (needs posterior) |
| Hyperparams | 1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | Prior + likelihood |
| Stochasticity | Yes | Yes (decreasing) | Yes | Deterministic | Yes |
| Information use | Ignores uncertainty | Ignores uncertainty | Soft on values | Uses counts | Uses full posterior |
| KTU frequency | **Very High** | High | Medium | **Very High (theory)** | Medium |

---

## 3.5 Decay Schedule Variants — Engineering Trade-off

Let $\varepsilon_t$ be the exploration rate at step $t$. The four most common schedules are:

1. **Constant:** $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0$ — *Simplest, but linearly suboptimal.*
2. **Linear decay:** $\varepsilon_t = \max(\varepsilon_{\min}, \varepsilon_0 - kt)$ — *Common in practice.*
3. **Exponential decay:** $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \delta^t$ — *Used in DQN papers.*
4. **Harmonic decay:** $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 / t$ — *Theoretically optimal for regret.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Exploration–Exploitation Decision Loop

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete decision-making cycle** of an ε-greedy agent interacting with an MDP environment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Episode t] --> B[Observe State S_t]
    B --> C[Look up Q-values Q S_t a]
    C --> D{Generate random number r in 0 1}
    D -->|r less than epsilon| E[EXPLORE: pick random action A_t]
    D -->|r greater than epsilon| F[EXPLOIT: pick argmax Q S_t A_t]
    E --> G[Execute A_t in environment]
    F --> G[Execute A_t in environment]
    G --> H[Receive reward R_t+1 and next state S_t+1]
    H --> I[Update Q-value estimate using Bellman rule]
    I --> J[Decay epsilon if schedule active]
    J --> K{t equals T end}
    K -->|No| A
    K -->|Yes| L[Converge to optimal policy pi star]
```

---

## 4.2 Strategy Selection Decision Tree

This Mermaid block diagram helps a KTU student **choose** the right exploration strategy for a given problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Problem Given: need exploration strategy] --> B0{Is action space small and discrete?}
    B0 -->|Yes| C0{Need theoretical regret guarantee?}
    B0 -->|No| D0[Use function approximation with noise]
    C0 -->|Yes| E0[Use UCB-1 or Thompson Sampling]
    C0 -->|No| F0[Use epsilon-Greedy with decay]
    E0 --> G0{Have Bayesian prior available?}
    G0 -->|Yes| H0[Choose Thompson Sampling]
    G0 -->|No| I0[Choose UCB-1 with c equals sqrt 2]
    F0 --> J0[Choose epsilon schedule: 1 over t or exponential]
    D0 --> K0[Use stochastic policy gradient or entropy regularization]
```

---

## 4.3 Regret Growth Comparison Chart (Conceptual)

This Mermaid-style block architecture maps the **relative regret growth** of three strategies over time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StrategyRegrets [Cumulative Regret R T vs Time T]
        direction LR
        R1[Constant Epsilon Greedy: Linear R T equals O T] --- R2[Decaying Epsilon Greedy: Logarithmic O log T] --- R3[UCB-1 or Thompson: O sqrt K T log T]
    end
    N1[Fastest Regret Growth] --> R1
    N2[Moderate] --> R2
    N3[Slowest] --> R3
```

---

## 4.4 Softmax Boltzmann Temperature Effect Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Q-values Q s a for all actions] --> B1[Compute exponentials exp Q over tau]
    B1 --> C1[Sum across all actions to get denominator Z]
    C1 --> D1[Compute normalized probabilities P a]
    D1 --> E1[Sample action A from categorical P]
    F1[Tau High: nearly uniform] --> G1[More exploration]
    F2[Tau Low: nearly greedy] --> G2[More exploitation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1.** *Define the Exploration–Exploitation trade-off in Reinforcement Learning. Why is it important?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Exploration is the act of trying new actions to discover their rewards, while exploitation is the act of choosing the best-known action based on current estimates.
- **(1 mark) Trade-off:** An agent must balance both — too much exploration wastes reward potential, too much exploitation risks getting stuck in a suboptimal policy.
- **(1 mark) Importance:** Without a proper balance, the agent cannot guarantee convergence to the optimal policy $\pi^*$ and may suffer linear cumulative regret.

---

### **Q2.** *Compare ε-greedy and softmax (Boltzmann) exploration strategies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) ε-Greedy:** Selects a random action with fixed probability $\varepsilon$ regardless of Q-value magnitudes. Simple but does not differentiate between nearly-optimal and terrible actions.
- **(1 mark) Softmax:** Uses $P(a) = \exp(Q(s,a)/\tau) / \sum \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)$, so actions with higher Q-values are sampled more often. The temperature $\tau$ controls the exploration breadth.
- **(1 mark) Verdict:** Softmax is smoother and uses Q-value information; ε-greedy is simpler and computationally cheaper.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECDT755 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the ε-greedy exploration strategy with a suitable algorithm. Derive the per-step expected reward and total regret when ε is held constant. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Show that with the harmonic decay schedule $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 / t$, the cumulative regret becomes $O(\ln T)$. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Algorithm — ε-Greedy (1 mark):**

For each episode $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:
1. With probability $\varepsilon$, select $A_t \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{A})$.
2. Else, select $A_t = \arg\max_a Q(S_t, a)$.
3. Execute $A_t$, observe $R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}$.
4. Update $Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)]$.

**Per-step expected reward derivation (3 marks):** Following Section 3.1:

$$E[R_t] = \left( 1 - \varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) q_*(a^*) + \varepsilon \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$$

**Single-step regret (1 mark):**

$$\ell_t = q_*(a^*) - E[R_t] = \varepsilon \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) \left[ q_*(a^*) - \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}} \right]$$

**Total regret (2 marks):**

$$R_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t = \varepsilon T \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right) \Delta_{\max} = \Theta(T)$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** *Constant ε-greedy suffers **linear regret** and is therefore **not no-regret**.*

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** With $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 / t$:

$$R_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\varepsilon_0}{t} \cdot \Delta_{\max} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Factor out constants:

$$R_T = C \cdot \varepsilon_0 \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t}, \quad C = \Delta_{\max} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A} \vert} \right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the harmonic series identity:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{t} = H_T = \ln T + \gamma + O(1/T)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Final asymptotic form:

$$R_T = O(\ln T) \quad \text{as} \quad T \to \infty$$

> **Conclusion:** *Harmonically decayed ε-greedy is **no-regret** because $\ln T / T \to 0$.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Describe the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB-1) action selection rule. Explain the role of the confidence bonus term. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Derive the cumulative regret bound for UCB-1 in a $K$-armed bandit setting using Hoeffding's inequality. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Action selection rule (2 marks):**

$$A_t = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[ Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$

**Meaning of each term (3 marks):**
- $Q_t(a)$: The empirical mean reward of action $a$ — represents **exploitation**.
- $c \sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$: The **confidence bonus** — represents **exploration via optimism**.
- $N_t(a)$: Number of times $a$ has been selected up to time $t$.
- $c$: A confidence parameter (typically $c = \sqrt{2}$).
- $\ln t$: Slow growth — bonus decays gracefully.

**Role of the confidence bonus (2 marks):** It quantifies the agent's **uncertainty** about $Q_t(a)$. Actions tried few times have high bonus, making them attractive despite low mean — implementing the **principle of optimism under uncertainty**.

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** By Hoeffding's inequality, for action $a$ with $N_t(a)$ samples:

$$P\!\left( \vert Q_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert \geq c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right) \leq 2 \exp(-2 c^2 \ln t) = 2 t^{-2c^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Set $2c^2 = 1$, so $c = 1/\sqrt{2}$, giving a tail bound $\leq 2/t$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the union bound over $K$ actions:

$$P\!\left( \exists a : \vert Q_t(a) - q_*(a) \vert \geq c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right) \leq \frac{2K}{t}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the Borel–Cantelli lemma — since $\sum 2K/t = \infty$ but in the modified sense, we conclude that after $T$ steps, each suboptimal action $a$ is pulled no more than:

$$N_T(a) \leq \frac{4 c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a^2} + O(1)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Sum the per-action regret over all suboptimal actions:

$$R_T = \sum_{a \neq a^*} \Delta_a \cdot E[N_T(a)] \leq \sum_{a \neq a^*} \left( \frac{4 c^2 \ln T}{\Delta_a} + O(1) \right) \Delta_a$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final bound:

$$\boxed{R_T = O(\sqrt{K T \ln T})}$$

> **Conclusion:** *UCB-1 is **distribution-dependent** and achieves sublinear regret, hence is no-regret.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant term** in regret derivations — you must include $\Delta_{\max} (1 - 1/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$, not just $\varepsilon$. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing greedy and ε-greedy notation** — writing $A_t = \arg\max Q$ for ε-greedy without mentioning the random branch. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Using $\ln$ and $\log_2$ interchangeably** — KTU expects natural log $\ln$ in regret bounds.
> 4. **Not defining $\Delta_{\max}$ or $\Delta_a$** — the *gap* between optimal and suboptimal action values. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping the harmonic series derivation** — examiners check the step $H_T = \ln T + \gamma$. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 6. **Writing the UCB bonus as $c \sqrt{N_t(a)/\ln t}$ (inverted)** — common sign error. **(Lose 2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Exploration vs Exploitation**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- **Exploration:** Trying new actions to gather information.
- **Exploitation:** Selecting the best-known action by current estimates.
- **Trade-off:** Balancing short-term reward maximization with long-term information gain.

### ✅ Strategy List (High-Yield)
- **ε-Greedy** — simple, $\varepsilon$ probability random.
- **ε-Decay (Harmonic $1/t$):** gives $O(\ln T)$ regret.
- **Softmax / Boltzmann:** temperature-controlled probabilistic action selection.
- **UCB-1:** optimism under uncertainty, $O(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ regret.
- **Thompson Sampling:** Bayesian posterior sampling.
- **Optimistic Initialization:** start with high Q-values.

### ✅ Critical Formulas to Memorize

| **Formula** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|
| $E[R_t] = (1 - \varepsilon + \varepsilon/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert) q_*(a^*) + \varepsilon (1 - 1/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert) \bar{q}_{\text{non-opt}}$ | ε-greedy expected reward |
| $\ell_t = \varepsilon (1 - 1/\vert \mathcal{A} \vert) \Delta_{\max}$ | Per-step regret (constant ε) |
| $R_T = O(\varepsilon_0 \ln T)$ | Total regret with $1/t$ decay |
| $P(a \mid s) = \exp(Q(s,a)/\tau) / \sum \exp(Q(s,a')/\tau)$ | Boltzmann selection |
| $A_t = \arg\max_a [Q_t(a) + c \sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}]$ | UCB-1 selection |
| $R_T = O(\sqrt{KT \ln T})$ | UCB-1 regret bound |

### ✅ Common KTU Pitfalls
- Always define $\Delta_a$ (the gap) explicitly.
- Use $\ln$ (natural log), not $\log_{10}$ or $\log_2$.
- Note that ε-greedy with **fixed** $\varepsilon$ has **linear** regret.
- UCB-1 needs $N_t(a) > 0$ for all $a$ (initialize by trying each once).

### ✅ Connection to Future Modules
- **Module 2 (MDPs):** ε-greedy becomes a stochastic policy $\pi(a \mid s)$.
- **Module 3 (Q-Learning):** Q-update rule assumes actions chosen by ε-greedy.
- **Module 5 (Deep RL):** In DQN, ε-greedy is annealed from 1.0 to 0.01 over training.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pursuit Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pursuit Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 | Pursuit Algorithm — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Pursuit Algorithm** is a classical *non-stationary, associative reward-penalty* reinforcement learning technique primarily used to solve the **multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem** and discrete Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Formally, it is a member of the family of **Learning Automata (LA)** introduced by Thathachar and Sastry (1985–1986).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT755 — Module 1):**
> Pursuit Algorithms belong to the class of *estimator-based* reinforcement learning algorithms where the agent continuously maintains (a) a **reward probability estimate vector** $\hat{Q}(t)$ and (b) a **mixed action probability vector** $P(t)$. The agent then *pursues* (i.e., incrementally increases the selection probability of) the currently estimated best action.

Mathematically, the algorithm operates on a finite action set $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}$ and maintains two parallel structures updated at every discrete time-step $t \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$:

- A **reward probability estimate vector** $\hat{Q}(t) = \left[\hat{Q}_1(t), \hat{Q}_2(t), \ldots, \hat{Q}_k(t)\right]$, where each $\hat{Q}_i(t) \in [0, 1]$ represents the running estimate of the expected reward from action $a_i$.
- An **action probability vector** $P(t) = \left[P_1(t), P_2(t), \ldots, P_k(t)\right]$, where each $P_i(t) \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t) = 1$, representing the agent's current stochastic policy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a tourist in a city with **10 unfamiliar restaurants** and a limited number of dinner nights. On the **first night**, you have *no information*, so you might pick one randomly (each restaurant gets a 10% chance). After dinner, you rate the experience. On **subsequent nights**, you don't completely abandon the bad restaurants, but you *shift your probability mass* — a little more toward the highest-rated one, slightly away from the worst. This dynamic re-weighting based on accumulating experience **is exactly what the Pursuit Algorithm does**: it *pursues* the action that *currently looks best* based on its running reward estimates.

The two key engineering insights of this analogy are:
1. The agent **never permanently locks in** to one option (because the environment may be non-stationary — restaurants can change chefs).
2. The agent **chases a moving target** — if a different action overtakes the current leader in estimated reward, the pursuit switches.

## 1.3 Why the Pursuit Algorithm Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:**
> - **Recommendation Systems** — dynamically shifting user exposure to better-performing items.
> - **Adaptive Network Routing** — pursuing lower-latency links in fluctuating traffic.
> - **A/B Testing in Online Advertising** — converging on the best-performing creative without permanent lock-in.
> - **Cognitive Radio Spectrum Sensing** — selecting the least-interfered channel in a non-stationary RF environment.

The algorithm is **$\epsilon$-optimal**, meaning that for sufficiently small step-size parameters ($\alpha$ and $\beta$), it converges to selecting the optimal action with probability arbitrarily close to **1.0** even in stationary bandit environments.

## 1.4 Algorithmic Personality at a Glance

| Property | Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Environment Type | Stationary & Slowly Non-Stationary Bandits | Covered in Module 1 |
| Memory | Finite — two $k$-dimensional vectors | Tractable for board problems |
| Convergence Guarantee | $\epsilon$-optimal under mild conditions | Frequently asked in Part A |
| Information Used | Estimated reward + current best action | Core of "pursuit" semantics |
| Reward Model | Stochastic (Bernoulli-like) | Standard KTU assumption |

# Module 1 | Pursuit Algorithm — Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Pursuit Algorithm runs in an **iterative loop** for $t = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, T$ (where $T$ is the horizon). The loop executes the following **five canonical steps** at every time-step:

> [!NOTE]
> The pursuit is performed **on the action probability vector**, while the *learning* of the reward landscape is performed **on the estimate vector**. These two updates happen in *parallel* at each time-step — this duality is the signature of the algorithm.

1. **Action Selection (Sampling Step):**
   The agent draws an action $a(t)$ from the discrete probability distribution $P(t)$. Formally:
   $$a(t) \sim P(t) = \left[P_1(t), P_2(t), \ldots, P_k(t)\right]$$

2. **Environment Interaction (Reward Observation):**
   The environment returns a stochastic reward $r(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ (binary reward model — the KTU-default assumption) drawn from a Bernoulli distribution with mean $\mu_{a(t)}$ corresponding to the chosen action:
   $$r(t) \sim \text{Bernoulli}\left(\mu_{a(t)}\right)$$

3. **Reward Estimate Update (Exponentially-Weighted Average):**
   The running reward estimate for the selected action is updated using a *learning rate* $\alpha \in (0, 1]$. All other entries of $\hat{Q}$ remain unchanged:
   $$\hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t+1) = \hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t) + \alpha \cdot \left[ r(t) - \hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t) \right]$$
   $$\hat{Q}_i(t+1) = \hat{Q}_i(t), \quad \forall i \neq a(t)$$

4. **Best-Action Identification (Greedy W.r.t. Estimates):**
   The action with the highest current reward estimate is flagged as the "pursuit target":
   $$a^*(t+1) = \arg\max_{i \in \mathcal{A}} \, \hat{Q}_i(t+1)$$
   Tie-breaking rule: choose the *lowest-index* action among ties (standard KTU convention).

5. **Probability Vector Pursuit (The Core Step):**
   The action probability vector is *incrementally pulled* toward putting more mass on the pursuit target. A second step-size $\beta \in (0, 1]$ controls the *aggressiveness* of pursuit:
   $$P_{a^*}(t+1) = P_{a^*}(t) + \beta \cdot \left[ 1 - P_{a^*}(t) \right]$$
   $$P_i(t+1) = P_i(t) - \beta \cdot P_i(t), \quad \forall i \neq a^*(t)$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Sampling):** Maintains exploration as long as all $P_i > 0$ — prevents permanent commitment.
- **Step 3 (Estimate Update):** Uses an exponential moving average so that recent rewards count more than stale ones — crucial for *non-stationarity*.
- **Step 4 (Greedy Pursuit Target):** The agent bets on its current best guess, but since $\hat{Q}$ is itself evolving, the target can shift — this is the "moving target" semantics.
- **Step 5 (Probability Shift):** The $+\beta(1 - P_{a^*})$ term ensures the target's probability *asymptotically approaches 1* without ever fully reaching it (in finite time) when $\beta < 1$. The $-\beta P_i$ term smoothly decays competing actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The two-parameter structure $(\alpha, \beta)$ decouples the *learning rate* (how fast you believe the data) from the *pursuit rate* (how fast you commit to the best). Setting $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta = 1$ yields a greedy variant; smaller values yield smoother, more exploratory variants. This decoupling is a *favourite* 7-mark conceptual question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Domain / Range | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Number of actions (arms) | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ | Bandit cardinality |
| $P_i(t)$ | Probability of selecting arm $i$ at time $t$ | $[0, 1]$, $\sum_i P_i = 1$ | Policy output |
| $\hat{Q}_i(t)$ | Estimated mean reward of arm $i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Knowledge state |
| $\alpha$ | Reward estimate learning rate | $(0, 1]$ | Belief update speed |
| $\beta$ | Pursuit (probability shift) rate | $(0, 1]$ | Commitment speed |
| $a^*(t)$ | Estimated best action (pursuit target) | $\mathcal{A}$ | Greedy choice |
| $r(t)$ | Observed reward at time $t$ | $\{0, 1\}$ | Environment feedback |
| $\mu_i$ | True mean reward of arm $i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Hidden parameter |
| $T$ | Total time horizon | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ | Run length |

**Two-Parameter Update Pair (boxed for memory):**

$$P_{a^*}(t+1) = P_{a^*}(t) + \beta \left[1 - P_{a^*}(t)\right] = (1-\beta)\, P_{a^*}(t) + \beta$$

$$P_i(t+1) = (1 - \beta)\, P_i(t), \quad \forall i \neq a^*(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compact form on the right shows that the pursuit target's probability is a *convex combination* of its old value and **1** — which is precisely the convex-update structure expected in board problems.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The Pursuit Algorithm is the workhorse for scenarios where the **reward function is stationary or slowly varying** and the **action set is small to moderate** ($k \leq 20$ in practice). In modern deep-RL, the same two-vector (estimator + policy) philosophy reappears in **policy gradient methods (REINFORCE)**, **actor-critic architectures**, and **gradient bandits** — making the Pursuit Algorithm a *conceptual ancestor* of today's deep RL toolkit. Mastering it provides the foundation needed for Module 2 (MDPs) and Module 3 (Q-Learning).

# Module 1 | Pursuit Algorithm — Exhaustive Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability Vector Mass Conservation

**Statement to Prove:** After every pursuit update, the action probability vector remains a valid probability distribution, i.e., $\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t+1) = 1$, given that $\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t) = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *standard 7-mark proof* in KTU end-semester papers. Memorize the algebraic sequence.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t+1) &= P_{a^*}(t+1) + \sum_{i \neq a^*} P_i(t+1) \\[4pt]
&= \Big[ P_{a^*}(t) + \beta \left( 1 - P_{a^*}(t) \right) \Big] + \sum_{i \neq a^*} \Big[ P_i(t) - \beta \cdot P_i(t) \Big] \\[4pt]
&= P_{a^*}(t) + \beta - \beta \, P_{a^*}(t) + \sum_{i \neq a^*} P_i(t) - \beta \sum_{i \neq a^*} P_i(t) \\[4pt]
&= P_{a^*}(t) + \beta - \beta \, P_{a^*}(t) + \big( 1 - P_{a^*}(t) \big) - \beta \big( 1 - P_{a^*}(t) \big) \\[4pt]
&= \big[ P_{a^*}(t) - \beta P_{a^*}(t) - P_{a^*}(t) + \beta P_{a^*}(t) \big] + \big[ \beta - \beta \big] + 1 \\[4pt]
&= 0 + 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of Each Algebraic Step:**

- **Line 1 → 2:** Substitute the pursuit update rule for the target arm and the decay rule for all other arms.
- **Line 2 → 3:** Expand the bracketed terms (distributive law).
- **Line 3 → 4:** Use the conservation identity $\sum_{i \neq a^*} P_i(t) = 1 - P_{a^*}(t)$.
- **Line 4 → 5:** Group $P_{a^*}$ terms together and constant terms together; all $\beta$-laden terms cancel.
- **Line 5 → Result:** Mass is conserved at every time-step — the vector remains a valid probability distribution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Asymptotic Limit of the Pursuit Target's Probability

**Statement:** As $t \to \infty$ (and assuming $a^*$ remains the pursuit target consistently after some time $T_0$), we have $P_{a^*}(t) \to 1$.

**Derivation (Recurrence Expansion):**

Let $p_t \equiv P_{a^*}(t)$. The update is:

$$p_{t+1} = p_t + \beta(1 - p_t) = (1 - \beta) p_t + \beta$$

Unrolling the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{t+1} &= (1-\beta) p_t + \beta \\
p_{t+1} &= (1-\beta) \left[ (1-\beta) p_{t-1} + \beta \right] + \beta \\
&= (1-\beta)^2 p_{t-1} + \beta (1-\beta) + \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the unrolling for $n$ steps backward (from initial time $0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{t+1} &= (1-\beta)^{t+1} \, p_0 + \beta \sum_{j=0}^{t} (1-\beta)^j
\end{aligned}
$$

The geometric sum evaluates to $\frac{1 - (1-\beta)^{t+1}}{1 - (1-\beta)} = \frac{1 - (1-\beta)^{t+1}}{\beta}$, hence:

$$
p_{t+1} = (1-\beta)^{t+1} \, p_0 + \beta \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\beta)^{t+1}}{\beta} = (1-\beta)^{t+1} \, p_0 + 1 - (1-\beta)^{t+1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - (1-\beta)^t \left( 1 - P_{a^*}(0) \right)\,}
$$

**Taking the Limit as $t \to \infty$:**

Since $\vert 1 - \beta \vert < 1$ for $\beta \in (0, 1]$, the term $(1-\beta)^t \to 0$. Therefore:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves the algorithm *converges* to selecting the best action **with probability 1** under consistent pursuit, but the convergence rate is governed by the *half-life* $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta}$. A larger $\beta$ means faster commitment — a tradeoff the examiner often tests.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reward Estimate Convergence (Exponential Smoothing Limit)

**Statement:** If arm $i$ is selected infinitely often and the environment is stationary with true mean $\mu_i$, then $\hat{Q}_i(t) \to \mu_i$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Derivation:** Define the tracking error $e_t = \hat{Q}_i(t) - \mu_i$. From the update rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{t+1} &= \hat{Q}_i(t+1) - \mu_i \\
&= \hat{Q}_i(t) + \alpha \left[ r(t) - \hat{Q}_i(t) \right] - \mu_i \\
&= (1 - \alpha) \hat{Q}_i(t) + \alpha \, r(t) - \mu_i \\
&= (1-\alpha) \left[ e_t + \mu_i \right] + \alpha \, r(t) - \mu_i \\
&= (1-\alpha) e_t + (1-\alpha) \mu_i + \alpha \, r(t) - \mu_i \\
&= (1-\alpha) e_t + \alpha \left[ r(t) - \mu_i \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations (using $E[r(t)] = \mu_i$):

$$E[e_{t+1}] = (1-\alpha) E[e_t] + \alpha \cdot 0 = (1-\alpha) E[e_t]$$

Unrolling: $E[e_t] = (1-\alpha)^t \, e_0 \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ since $\vert 1 - \alpha \vert < 1$ for $\alpha \in (0, 1]$.

$$\boxed{\,\lim_{t \to \infty} \hat{Q}_i(t) = \mu_i \quad \text{(in mean)}\,}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-armed bandit has true reward means $\mu_1 = 0.2$, $\mu_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_3 = 0.8$. Run the Pursuit Algorithm for $t = 1, 2, 3$ with $\alpha = \beta = 0.5$, initial $P(0) = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$, and initial $\hat{Q}(0) = (0.5, 0.5, 0.5)$. Assume the sampled rewards are $r(1)=0, r(2)=1, r(3)=1$.

**Solution Trace:**

| Step $t$ | Sampled $a(t)$ | $r(t)$ | Updated $\hat{Q}$ | $a^*$ | Updated $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0 | $(0.25, 0.5, 0.5)$ | $a_3$ | $(1/6, 1/6, 2/3)$ |
| 2 | $a_3$ (prob 2/3) | 1 | $(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)$ | $a_3$ | $(1/12, 1/12, 5/6)$ |
| 3 | $a_3$ (prob 5/6) | 1 | $(0.25, 0.5, 0.875)$ | $a_3$ | $(1/24, 1/24, 11/12)$ |

**Detailed Computation for $t=1$:**

- Action $a_1$ sampled (probability 1/3 from initial uniform $P$).
- $r(1) = 0$, so $\hat{Q}_1(1) = 0.5 + 0.5(0 - 0.5) = 0.25$. Others unchanged.
- $a^*(1) = \arg\max(0.25, 0.5, 0.5) = a_3$ (lowest-index tie-break).
- Pursuit update with $\beta = 0.5$:
  - $P_3(1) = 1/3 + 0.5(1 - 1/3) = 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3$.
  - $P_1(1) = 1/3 - 0.5 \cdot 1/3 = 1/6$. Similarly $P_2(1) = 1/6$.

**Detailed Computation for $t=2$:**

- $a(2) = a_3$ sampled with probability 2/3. $r(2)=1$.
- $\hat{Q}_3(2) = 0.5 + 0.5(1 - 0.5) = 0.75$.
- $a^*(2) = a_3$. Pursuit with $\beta = 0.5$:
  - $P_3(2) = 2/3 + 0.5(1 - 2/3) = 2/3 + 1/6 = 5/6$.
  - $P_1(2) = 1/6 - 0.5 \cdot 1/6 = 1/12$. Same for $P_2(2)$.

> [!TIP]
> The probability of the optimal arm (true mean 0.8) climbs from **1/3 → 2/3 → 5/6 → 11/12** — a clean geometric sequence governed by $(1-\beta)^t$ as derived in 3.2. This numerical trace is the **single most common 7-mark question** in KTU exams for this topic.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Convergence Properties Summary

| Aspect | Behaviour | Governing Parameter | KTU Takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probability Convergence | $P_{a^*}(t) \to 1$ exponentially | $\beta$ | Faster if $\beta \uparrow$ |
| Estimate Convergence | $\hat{Q}_i(t) \to \mu_i$ in mean | $\alpha$ | Faster if $\alpha \uparrow$ |
| Regret Bound | $O(\sqrt{T \log k})$ in stationary case | Both | Comparable to UCB |
| Mass Conservation | Always $\sum P_i = 1$ | $\beta$ (any valid) | Invariant property |
| Stationary Optimality | $\epsilon$-optimal | Small $\alpha, \beta$ | Asymptotic guarantee |
| Non-Stationary Tracking | Possible with $\alpha$ decoupled from prior | $\alpha \approx 1/N_i$ | Recommended fix |

# Module 1 | Pursuit Algorithm — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end execution loop** of the Pursuit Algorithm. Every node uses a pure alphanumeric identifier prefixed with a letter to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard (no reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone labels; no markdown bolding inside quoted labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Initialise P0 and Q0] --> nodeB[Set time t equals 1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sample action a from probability vector P of t]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Execute action a in environment]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Observe stochastic reward r]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Update reward estimate for arm a using alpha]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Identify best arm a star as argmax of Q]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Update probability of a star using beta]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Decay probabilities of all other arms using beta]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Increment time t by 1]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Is t less than T?}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeK -->|No| nodeL[Terminate: Return final policy P of T]
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**

- The **inner loop** (nodes `C → D → E → F → G → H → I`) executes once per time-step and is the "heart" of the algorithm.
- The **decision diamond** at node `K` is the loop controller — a standard KTU test point ("trace the algorithm for 5 iterations").
- The **two boxed updates** (`F` and `H`/`I`) are the two learning signals that distinguish the Pursuit Algorithm from a pure $\epsilon$-greedy agent.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Conceptual View)

This diagram captures the **dual-vector architecture** of the Pursuit Algorithm — the two parallel data structures and their update pathways. It is intended as a *conceptual schematic* suitable for KTU design-type questions ("Draw the block diagram of the Pursuit Algorithm").

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Environment E] -->|reward r| nodeB[Reward Estimator Module]
    nodeB -->|update with alpha| nodeC[Q Vector Storage]
    nodeC -->|read Q values| nodeD[Best Arm Selector]
    nodeD -->|identify a star| nodeE[Policy Update Module]
    nodeC -->|read Q values| nodeF[Sampler Module]
    nodeF -->|sample action a| nodeG[Action Probability Vector P]
    nodeG -->|read P values| nodeE
    nodeE -->|update with beta| nodeG
    nodeG -->|selected action a| nodeA
    nodeH[Hyperparameters alpha and beta] --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeE
```

**Architectural Insight:**

- The **Q Vector Storage** (node `C`) is the *knowledge* component — the agent's belief about the world.
- The **P Vector Storage** (node `G`) is the *behaviour* component — the agent's current policy.
- The **Reward Estimator** (node `B`) writes to Q; the **Policy Update Module** (node `E`) writes to P. The two writers operate in parallel but *couple* through the shared best-arm signal (node `D`).
- The **Hyperparameters block** (node `H`) injects the learning rates externally — this is what is "tuned" in practice.

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, *design-type* questions often ask for such block diagrams. Memorize this dual-vector architecture; it is the canonical answer to "Explain the architecture of estimator-based RL algorithms."

## 4.3 Algorithmic State-Transition Diagram (Compact View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateInit: Initialise P0 and Q0
    stateInit --> stateSelect: Begin loop at t equals 1
    stateSelect --> stateAct: Sample a from P of t
    stateAct --> stateReward: Execute a, receive r
    stateReward --> stateUpdateQ: Update Q of a with alpha
    stateUpdateQ --> stateFindBest: Compute a star equals argmax of Q
    stateFindBest --> stateUpdateP: Apply pursuit rule with beta
    stateUpdateP --> stateCheck: Increment t and check stopping
    stateCheck --> stateSelect: If t less than T
    stateCheck --> stateFinal: If t equals T
    stateFinal --> [*]: Return final P and Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state diagram is the **most exam-friendly representation** of the algorithm — use it whenever a 7-mark question asks to "describe the working of the Pursuit Algorithm with a neat diagram."
