# Module 1 | Pursuit Algorithm — KTU 2024 Scheme Question Bank & Rapid Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Pursuit Algorithm in the context of multi-armed bandit problems. Highlight the role of the two vectors it maintains.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Pursuit Algorithm is a reinforcement learning technique used to solve the **multi-armed bandit problem** in which an agent must repeatedly choose among $k$ actions to maximize cumulative reward. It maintains two vectors:

1. **Reward probability estimate vector** $\hat{Q}(t) = \left[\hat{Q}_1(t), \ldots, \hat{Q}_k(t)\right]$ — the agent's *belief* about the mean reward of each arm.
2. **Action probability vector** $P(t) = \left[P_1(t), \ldots, P_k(t)\right]$ — the agent's *current stochastic policy* with $\sum_i P_i(t) = 1$.

At every step, the agent *pursues* the action with the highest estimated reward by incrementing its probability and decaying the rest, controlled by step-size $\beta$. The estimate itself is updated using learning rate $\alpha$ based on observed reward. **(3 marks — full credit)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the Pursuit Algorithm? What happens when $\beta = 1$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **$\alpha$ (learning rate for reward estimate):** Controls how fast $\hat{Q}_i(t)$ adapts to new reward observations. Higher $\alpha$ means faster adaptation (and more sensitivity to noise).
- **$\beta$ (pursuit rate for probability vector):** Controls how aggressively $P(t)$ is shifted toward the estimated best action. Higher $\beta$ means faster commitment.
- **When $\beta = 1$:** The pursuit update becomes $P_{a^*}(t+1) = 1$ and $P_i(t+1) = 0$ for all $i \neq a^*$. The agent *commits fully* to the current best arm in a single step, eliminating future exploration. This is **degenerate and undesirable** because the agent can no longer correct a wrong initial estimate. **(3 marks — full credit)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students most commonly lose marks on this topic by: (1) confusing the **two updates** (estimate vs. probability) and mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$; (2) forgetting to verify that the probability vector is conserved; (3) using $\alpha = \beta = 1$ without commenting on its consequences; (4) failing to show all algebraic steps in the probability-update derivation. Always *label* which vector is being updated in each equation.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the architecture of the Pursuit Algorithm. Discuss the role of the reward estimate vector $\hat{Q}(t)$ and the action probability vector $P(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the update rule $P_{a^*}(t+1) = (1-\beta) P_{a^*}(t) + \beta$, derive the closed-form expression for $P_{a^*}(t)$ and show that it converges to 1 as $t \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architectural Explanation (7 marks):**

The Pursuit Algorithm operates on a **dual-vector architecture** comprising:

- **Reward Estimate Vector $\hat{Q}(t)$:** Maintains the running mean reward of each arm, initialized to equal values (e.g., $0.5$ or the optimistic value $1$ for binary rewards). Updated as:
  $$\hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t+1) = \hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t) + \alpha \left[ r(t) - \hat{Q}_{a(t)}(t) \right]$$
  This is a **stochastic approximation** update belonging to the family of **exponentially weighted moving averages** (akin to an online mean estimator).

- **Action Probability Vector $P(t)$:** Represents the agent's stochastic policy. The vector is initialized uniformly: $P_i(0) = 1/k$ for all $i$. After the best action is identified, the vector is *pursued* via:
  $$P_{a^*}(t+1) = (1-\beta) P_{a^*}(t) + \beta, \quad P_i(t+1) = (1-\beta) P_i(t) \text{ for } i \neq a^*$$

- **Flow of Information:** The environment provides a reward; the estimator updates $\hat{Q}$; the best-arm selector computes $a^*$; the pursuit module updates $P$. The hyperparameters $(\alpha, \beta)$ are exogenous and tunable.

**Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 above. **(Full 7 marks require naming both vectors, writing both update rules, and presenting a labelled block diagram.)**

**(b) Closed-Form Derivation (7 marks):**

Given the recurrence $p_{t+1} = (1-\beta) p_t + \beta$, with $p_t \equiv P_{a^*}(t)$ and initial condition $p_0 = P_{a^*}(0)$:

**Step 1 — Unroll the recurrence once:**

$$p_1 = (1-\beta) p_0 + \beta$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and unroll again:**

$$p_2 = (1-\beta) p_1 + \beta = (1-\beta)^2 p_0 + \beta(1-\beta) + \beta$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $t$ steps** (sum the geometric series):

$$
p_t = (1-\beta)^t p_0 + \beta \sum_{j=0}^{t-1} (1-\beta)^j
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric sum** (using $S_n = \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$ for $r = 1-\beta$):

$$
p_t = (1-\beta)^t p_0 + \beta \cdot \frac{1 - (1-\beta)^t}{\beta} = (1-\beta)^t p_0 + 1 - (1-\beta)^t
$$

**Step 5 — Combine terms:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - (1-\beta)^t \left( 1 - P_{a^*}(0) \right)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit** as $t \to \infty$:

Since $0 < \beta \leq 1$, we have $\vert 1 - \beta \vert < 1$, so $(1-\beta)^t \to 0$. Therefore:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - 0 = 1$$

This proves that the algorithm **converges to the optimal action with probability 1** under consistent pursuit. The half-life of convergence is $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \beta$. **(Full 7 marks for complete derivation with all six steps.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** A 3-armed bandit has true reward means $\mu_1 = 0.3$, $\mu_2 = 0.6$, $\mu_3 = 0.4$. The Pursuit Algorithm is run with $\alpha = \beta = 0.5$, $P(0) = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$, $\hat{Q}(0) = (0.5, 0.5, 0.5)$, and the first three sampled rewards are $r(1)=1, r(2)=0, r(3)=1$ (corresponding to the sampled actions in the trace below). Trace the algorithm for $t = 1, 2, 3$ and tabulate the values of $P(t)$ and $\hat{Q}(t)$. **(7 marks)**

*Sample assumption:* For illustration, assume the algorithm samples $a(1) = a_1$, $a(2) = a_2$, $a(3) = a_3$.

**(b)** State and prove the **mass-conservation property** of the Pursuit Algorithm. Why is this property essential for the algorithm to be a valid stochastic policy? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Algorithmic Trace (7 marks):**

**Step $t=1$:**

- Sample $a(1) = a_1$ from uniform $P(0) = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$.
- Observe $r(1) = 1$.
- Update estimate: $\hat{Q}_1(1) = 0.5 + 0.5(1 - 0.5) = 0.75$. Others remain $0.5$.
- $a^*(1) = \arg\max(0.75, 0.5, 0.5) = a_1$.
- Pursuit update with $\beta = 0.5$:
  - $P_1(1) = 1/3 + 0.5(1 - 1/3) = 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3$.
  - $P_2(1) = 1/3 - 0.5(1/3) = 1/6$. Same for $P_3(1)$.

**State after $t=1$:** $P(1) = (2/3, 1/6, 1/6)$, $\hat{Q}(1) = (0.75, 0.5, 0.5)$.

**Step $t=2$:**

- Sample $a(2) = a_2$ from $P(1) = (2/3, 1/6, 1/6)$ (probability 1/6).
- Observe $r(2) = 0$.
- Update estimate: $\hat{Q}_2(2) = 0.5 + 0.5(0 - 0.5) = 0.25$. Others remain.
- $\hat{Q}(2) = (0.75, 0.25, 0.5)$. $a^*(2) = a_1$ (still the leader).
- Pursuit update:
  - $P_1(2) = 2/3 + 0.5(1 - 2/3) = 2/3 + 1/6 = 5/6$.
  - $P_2(2) = 1/6 - 0.5(1/6) = 1/12$. $P_3(2) = 1/6 - 0.5(1/6) = 1/12$.

**State after $t=2$:** $P(2) = (5/6, 1/12, 1/12)$, $\hat{Q}(2) = (0.75, 0.25, 0.5)$.

**Step $t=3$:**

- Sample $a(3) = a_3$ (probability 1/12) from $P(2)$.
- Observe $r(3) = 1$.
- Update estimate: $\hat{Q}_3(3) = 0.5 + 0.5(1 - 0.5) = 0.75$. Others remain.
- $\hat{Q}(3) = (0.75, 0.25, 0.75)$. $a^*(3) = a_1$ (lowest-index tie-break).
- Pursuit update:
  - $P_1(3) = 5/6 + 0.5(1 - 5/6) = 5/6 + 1/12 = 11/12$.
  - $P_2(3) = 1/12 - 0.5(1/12) = 1/24$. $P_3(3) = 1/24$.

**Final Tabulation (7 marks):**

| $t$ | $P(t)$ | $\hat{Q}(t)$ | $a^*(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$ | $(0.5, 0.5, 0.5)$ | — |
| 1 | $(2/3, 1/6, 1/6)$ | $(0.75, 0.5, 0.5)$ | $a_1$ |
| 2 | $(5/6, 1/12, 1/12)$ | $(0.75, 0.25, 0.5)$ | $a_1$ |
| 3 | $(11/12, 1/24, 1/24)$ | $(0.75, 0.25, 0.75)$ | $a_1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The probability of the estimated best arm ($a_1$) climbs as the geometric sequence $1/3, 2/3, 5/6, 11/12$ — confirming the closed-form expression $P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - (1-\beta)^t (1 - P_{a^*}(0))$ with $\beta = 0.5$.

**(b) Mass-Conservation Proof (7 marks):**

**Statement:** If $\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t) = 1$ at time $t$, then $\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t+1) = 1$ after the pursuit update.

**Proof:** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i(t+1) &= P_{a^*}(t+1) + \sum_{i \neq a^*} P_i(t+1) \\
&= \big[ P_{a^*}(t) + \beta(1 - P_{a^*}(t)) \big] + \sum_{i \neq a^*} (1-\beta) P_i(t) \\
&= P_{a^*}(t) + \beta - \beta P_{a^*}(t) + (1-\beta)(1 - P_{a^*}(t)) \\
&= P_{a^*}(t) + \beta - \beta P_{a^*}(t) + 1 - \beta - P_{a^*}(t) + \beta P_{a^*}(t) \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why This Property is Essential (3 marks for discussion):**

The action probability vector $P(t)$ is used as a *sampling distribution* — the agent draws $a(t) \sim P(t)$. For this to be a valid discrete probability distribution, three axioms must hold:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P_i(t) \geq 0$ — ensured by the fact that the update rule multiplies by $(1-\beta) \in [0, 1]$, never making probabilities negative.
2. **Normalization:** $\sum_i P_i(t) = 1$ — proven above.
3. **Boundedness:** $P_i(t) \leq 1$ — guaranteed because the target's update is a convex combination of $P_{a^*}$ and 1.

If mass conservation were violated, the algorithm would not be a valid stochastic policy and standard sampling techniques (e.g., inverse CDF, categorical sampling) would break down. **(Full 7 marks.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Pursuit Algorithm (Module 1):**

- **Definition:** Estimator-based bandit algorithm that maintains a *reward estimate vector* $\hat{Q}(t)$ and an *action probability vector* $P(t)$; incrementally *pursues* the action with the highest $\hat{Q}_i$.

- **Two Parameters:** $\alpha$ (estimate learning rate) and $\beta$ (pursuit rate). Both lie in $(0, 1]$.

- **Two Updates Per Time-Step:** (1) Stochastic approximation of $\hat{Q}$ for the selected arm; (2) Pursuit update of $P$ for the *estimated* best arm $a^* = \arg\max_i \hat{Q}_i$.

- **Pursuit Update Rule (memorize verbatim):**
  $$P_{a^*}(t+1) = P_{a^*}(t) + \beta[1 - P_{a^*}(t)], \quad P_i(t+1) = (1-\beta) P_i(t) \text{ for } i \neq a^*$$

- **Closed-Form Probability:** $P_{a^*}(t) = 1 - (1-\beta)^t [1 - P_{a^*}(0)]$.

- **Convergence:** $P_{a^*}(t) \to 1$ exponentially; $\hat{Q}_i(t) \to \mu_i$ in mean (both with rate $\sim (1-\beta)^t$ or $(1-\alpha)^t$).

- **Mass Conservation Invariant:** $\sum_i P_i(t) = 1$ for all $t \geq 0$.

- **Optimality:** $\epsilon$-optimal in stationary bandits; degrades under fast non-stationarity unless $\alpha$ is re-tuned.

- **KTU Favourite Questions:** (1) Numerical trace of 3-5 steps (7 marks). (2) Closed-form derivation of $P_{a^*}(t)$ (7 marks). (3) Mass-conservation proof (7 marks). (4) Define the algorithm and explain $\alpha, \beta$ (3 marks). (5) Block diagram of dual-vector architecture (3-7 marks).

- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  - Do not confuse $\alpha$ (estimate update) with $\beta$ (probability update).
  - Do not assume the algorithm locks in to the best arm in one step (unless $\beta = 1$).
  - Do not forget to verify $\sum_i P_i = 1$ after every update.
  - Do not apply the pursuit update to the *true* best arm — always to the *estimated* best arm $a^*$.

- **Modern Connection:** The same dual-vector (estimator + policy) philosophy underpins **policy gradient methods**, **actor-critic architectures**, and **Thompson sampling** in contemporary deep RL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Markov Decision Process (MDP)

A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a formal mathematical framework used to describe an environment in Reinforcement Learning (RL) where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of an agent. The "Definition and Properties" of an MDP constitute the foundational building blocks of every RL algorithm.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), an MDP is defined as a **5-tuple** $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$, where each element has a precise mathematical meaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ where:
> - $\mathcal{S}$ is a finite set of **states** of the environment.
> - $\mathcal{A}$ is a finite set of **actions** available to the agent.
> - $P : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{S} \to [0, 1]$ is the **state transition probability function**.
> - $R : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{R}$ is the **reward function**.
> - $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the **discount factor** (a dimensionless scalar).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The "Definition and Properties" of an MDP includes understanding the **Markov Property**, the **Memoryless Nature** of state transitions, the **Stationarity Assumption**, and the **Sequential Decision-Making** property. These four properties are repeatedly tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Walking Robot in a Grid World**
>
> Imagine a small robot placed on a $4 \times 4$ chessboard. The robot can move **North, South, East, or West**. Each square is a *state*. The robot's possible movements are *actions*. Sometimes when the robot tries to move East, a slippery floor pushes it South instead — that randomness is the *transition probability*. After each move, the robot either gains a point (reaches a charging dock) or loses a point (falls into a hole). The robot doesn't remember how it got to the current square — **only the current square matters for deciding the next move**. This "memoryless" quality is the *Markov Property*.

**Plain English Summary:**
Think of an MDP as a **board game rulebook**:
- $\mathcal{S}$ → the **squares** on the board.
- $\mathcal{A}$ → the **dice rolls** the player can choose.
- $P$ → the **probability** of landing on a new square after a dice roll.
- $R$ → the **prize money** given when landing on a square.
- $\gamma$ → a number between 0 and 1 (typically **0.9** or **0.99**) representing how much we value future rewards compared to immediate ones.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse **MDP** with **Markov Reward Process (MRP)** and **Markov Chain (MC)**. Remember: **MC has no actions or rewards, MRP has rewards but no actions, MDP has BOTH actions and rewards.** This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark KTU question.

### 1.3 The Markov Property — The Heart of "Definition and Properties"

The **Markov Property** (also called the *memoryless property*) is the single most important property in this module. It states:

> **Markov Property:** The future is independent of the past, given the present. Formally, the conditional probability of the next state and reward depends **only on the current state and action**, not on the entire history of prior states and actions.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots, S_0, A_0) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** If the Markov Property holds, the agent can **throw away its history** and make decisions based solely on the current state. This is what makes RL computationally tractable. Without it, the state space would explode to include every possible history.

### 1.4 The Four Key Properties of an MDP

| # | Property Name | Mathematical Statement | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Markov Property** | $P(s'\vert s, a) = P(s'\vert s, a, h)$ | History is irrelevant. |
| 2 | **Stationarity** | $P, R$ are time-invariant | The rules of the game don't change over time. |
| 3 | **Fully Observable** | Agent observes $S_t$ completely | The agent sees the true state, not a noisy version. |
| 4 | **Sequential Decision Making** | $a_t$ affects $s_{t+1}$ and $r_{t+1}$ | Today's action changes tomorrow's situation. |

### 1.5 Standard Constants and Parameters (KTU Board Standards)

The following numerical constants are standard in KTU exam problems and should be memorized:

- **Discount factor $\gamma$**: typically **0.9**, **0.95**, or **0.99** (dimensionless).
- **Step-size / Learning rate $\alpha$**: typically **0.1** to **0.5** (dimensionless).
- **Exploration parameter $\epsilon$**: typically **0.1** for $\epsilon$-greedy (dimensionless).
- **State space cardinality $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$**: integer count of states.
- **Action space cardinality $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$**: integer count of actions.
- **Horizon $T$**: integer, the total number of time steps in an episode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Components of an MDP — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 State Space $\mathcal{S}$

The **state** $s \in \mathcal{S}$ is a complete description of the environment at time $t$. A state is said to be **Markov** if it retains all relevant information from history needed for future decision-making.

- **Discrete state space**: $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ with $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = n$.
- **Continuous state space**: $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., joint angles of a robot arm).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "What is a state?", answer: *"A state $s_t$ is a signal that compactly summarizes all past information needed for the agent to make an optimal decision at time $t$."* This is the exact phrasing expected by KTU examiners.

#### 2.1.2 Action Space $\mathcal{A}$

The **action** $a \in \mathcal{A}$ is a move the agent can take from a given state.

- **Deterministic action space**: Agent picks a single action $a$.
- **Stochastic action space**: Agent samples $a \sim \pi(\cdot \vert s)$.
- **Discrete vs. Continuous**: $\mathcal{A} = \{0, 1\}$ for discrete; $\mathcal{A} = [-1, 1]$ for continuous.

#### 2.1.3 Transition Probability Function $P$

The **state transition probability** $P(s' \vert s, a)$ is the probability of landing in state $s'$ after taking action $a$ in state $s$:

$$P(s' \vert s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

**Properties of $P$:**

$$0 \leq P(s' \vert s, a) \leq 1 \quad \text{for all } s, s' \in \mathcal{S}, \; a \in \mathcal{A}$$

$$\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \vert s, a) = 1 \quad \text{for all } s \in \mathcal{S}, \; a \in \mathcal{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row-stochastic property:** Each row of the transition matrix sums to 1. This is a favorite KTU trick question. If a student writes $\sum_{s} P(s \vert s', a) = 1$, they get zero. It must be summed over $s'$, the **next state**, not $s$.

#### 2.1.4 Reward Function $R$

The **reward function** $R(s, a)$ gives the expected immediate reward received after taking action $a$ in state $s$:

$$R(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

**Alternative forms** (commonly tested in KTU):
- $R(s)$: reward depends only on state.
- $R(s, a, s')$: reward depends on transition (next state included).

#### 2.1.5 Discount Factor $\gamma$

The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ determines the present value of future rewards:

- $\gamma = 0$: Agent is **myopic** (cares only about immediate reward).
- $\gamma \to 1$: Agent is **far-sighted** (cares equally about future rewards).
- $\gamma = 1$ (special): Used only in **episodic** tasks with finite horizon.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In control systems, $\gamma$ is analogous to the **forgetting factor** in recursive least squares. In economics, it is the **time preference rate** of a rational agent.

### 2.2 The Return $G_t$ and Its Properties

The **return** $G_t$ is the cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ onwards:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Key properties of $G_t$:**

- **Recursive (bootstrap) form:** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
- **Bounded:** If rewards are bounded $\vert R \vert \leq R_{\max}$, then $\vert G_t \vert \leq R_{\max} / (1 - \gamma)$.
- **Linearity:** $G_t$ is a linear functional of the reward sequence.

### 2.3 The Markov Reward Process (MRP) — Special Case of MDP

An **MRP** is an MDP without actions: $\langle \mathcal{S}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$.

$$P(s' \vert s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot \pi(a \vert s)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield summary** specifically compiled for the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MDP Tuple | $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ | Sets + scalars |
| 2 | Transition Prob. | $P(s' \mid s, a) = \Pr(S_{t+1}=s' \mid S_t=s, A_t=a)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Row-Stochastic | $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ | Sum $= 1$ |
| 4 | Reward Function | $R(s, a) = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 7 | Return Recursion | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 8 | Markov Property | $P(s' \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s' \mid s_t, a_t, h_t)$ | Conditional equality |
| 9 | Bounded Return | $\vert G_t \vert \leq R_{\max} / (1 - \gamma)$ | Bound |
| 10 | MRP Reduction | $P^{\pi}(s' \mid s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) P(s' \mid s, a)$ | $[0, 1]$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Definition and Properties of MDP" framework is used in:

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: States are positions/velocities, actions are steering angles, rewards are safety metrics.
- **Robotics**: Industrial arm manipulation — states are joint angles, actions are torque commands.
- **Healthcare**: Dynamic treatment regimes — states are patient vitals, actions are drug dosages.
- **Finance**: Portfolio management — states are market conditions, actions are buy/sell/hold.
- **Networking**: Packet routing — states are queue lengths, actions are path selections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application Note:** The MDP framework is essentially a **discrete-time stochastic control process** and forms the bedrock of modern control theory. KTU examiners expect students to mention that without the Markov property, every RL problem would be **NP-hard** in the worst case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bounding the Return $G_t$ (Standard KTU 14-Mark Sub-Question)

**Statement:** Given a reward sequence with $\vert R_t \vert \leq R_{\max}$ for all $t$, and discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, prove that $\vert G_t \vert \leq \dfrac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of $G_t$.**

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the absolute value and the triangle inequality.**

By the triangle inequality $\vert \sum x_k \vert \leq \sum \vert x_k \vert$, we have:

$$\vert G_t \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \right\vert \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the powers of $\gamma$.**

Since $\gamma \geq 0$, we have $\vert \gamma^k \vert = \gamma^k$:

$$\vert G_t \vert \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \cdot \vert R_{t+k+1} \vert$$

**Step 4 — Apply the bound $\vert R_{t+k+1} \vert \leq R_{\max}$.**

$$\vert G_t \vert \leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k \cdot R_{\max} = R_{\max} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the geometric series.**

For $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, the infinite geometric series converges to:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{1}{1 - \gamma}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the derivation.**

$$\vert G_t \vert \leq R_{\max} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \gamma} = \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}$$

$$\boxed{\vert G_t \vert \leq \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This bound is essential for proving the **convergence of value iteration** and **policy iteration** algorithms (which are covered in later modules). KTU examiners love asking this as a 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Recursive Decomposition of $G_t$

**Statement:** Prove that $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition.**

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} = R_{t+1} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $j = k - 1$ in the second sum.**

Let $j = k - 1$, so $k = j + 1$. When $k = 1$, $j = 0$; when $k = \infty$, $j = \infty$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1} = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{j+1} R_{t+j+2}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\gamma$ from the sum.**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{j+1} R_{t+j+2} = \gamma \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \gamma^j R_{(t+1)+j+1} = \gamma \cdot G_{t+1}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two parts.**

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot G_{t+1}$$

$$\boxed{G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This recursive form is the **fundamental identity** that makes Temporal Difference (TD) learning possible. Every TD(0), TD($\lambda$), and Bellman equation relies on this decomposition.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reduction from MDP to MRP under Policy $\pi$

**Statement:** Show that given a policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ and an MDP, the resulting trajectory is an MRP with transition $P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) P(s' \vert s, a)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of next state.**

$$P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s)$$

**Step 2 — Marginalize over the action $a$.**

By the law of total probability:

$$P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \Pr(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a) \cdot \Pr(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the policy and transition definitions.**

$$P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot \pi(a \vert s)$$

**Step 4 — Verify row-stochasticity.**

$$\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \sum_{s'} \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) P(s' \vert s, a) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \underbrace{\sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a)}_{=1} = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) = 1$$

$$\boxed{P^{\pi}(s' \vert s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \vert s) P(s' \vert s, a)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 3-state MDP with $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ and $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2\}$. The reward sequence starting from $s_1$ is $R_1 = 1, R_2 = 2, R_3 = 3$. Compute the return $G_0$ for $\gamma = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Apply the return formula.**

$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3 + \gamma^3 R_4 + \ldots$$

Assuming $R_t = 0$ for $t \geq 4$ (finite horizon of 3):

$$G_0 = 1 + (0.5)(2) + (0.5)^2(3) = 1 + 1 + 0.75 = 2.75$$

**Step 2 — Verify using the recursive formula.**

$$G_2 = R_3 = 3$$
$$G_1 = R_2 + \gamma G_2 = 2 + (0.5)(3) = 2 + 1.5 = 3.5$$
$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma G_1 = 1 + (0.5)(3.5) = 1 + 1.75 = 2.75$$

$$\boxed{G_0 = 2.75}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Method:** KTU examiners often ask students to compute $G_t$ using both the **direct summation** and the **recursive** methods. Getting the same answer via two methods earns full marks.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Markov Property Verification

| Scenario | History Available | Is it Markov? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chess position | All pieces' current squares | **Yes** | Position captures all relevant history. |
| Driving car | Current velocity only | **No** | Road curvature ahead is missing. |
| Driving car | Position + velocity + road map | **Yes** | All needed info is encoded. |
| Thermometer | Current temperature only | **Yes** | Markov chain (no actions). |
| Stock price | Only current price | **No** | Volatility/trend is hidden. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Agent–Environment Interaction Loop in an MDP

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **agent–environment interaction loop** that defines every MDP. This is the **most-tested diagram** in KTU Module 2 and has appeared in nearly every past paper.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agentNode[AGENT]
    envNode[ENVIRONMENT]
    actionFlow[ACTION a sub t]
    stateFlow[STATE s sub t plus 1]
    rewardFlow[REWARD r sub t plus 1]

    agentNode -- actionFlow --> envNode
    envNode -- stateFlow --> agentNode
    envNode -- rewardFlow --> agentNode

    policyBox[POLICY PI a given s]
    valueBox[VALUE FUNCTION V sub pi of s]

    agentNode -. uses .-> policyBox
    agentNode -. evaluates .-> valueBox
    valueBox -. guides .-> policyBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At each time step $t$, the **agent** reads the state $S_t$ and uses its policy $\pi$ to select an action $A_t$. The **environment** then transitions to $S_{t+1}$ and emits reward $R_{t+1}$. The loop repeats until termination.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MDP

The following block diagram shows how the five components of the MDP tuple interact inside a software implementation. This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** version of the conceptual model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sSpace[STATE SPACE S]
    aSpace[ACTION SPACE A]
    transBlock[TRANSITION FUNCTION P]
    rewardBlock[REWARD FUNCTION R]
    gammaBlock[DISCOUNT FACTOR GAMMA]
    mdpCore[MDP TUPLE M]
    policyBlock[POLICY PI]
    trajBlock[TRAJECTORY GENERATOR]

    sSpace --> mdpCore
    aSpace --> mdpCore
    transBlock --> mdpCore
    rewardBlock --> mdpCore
    gammaBlock --> mdpCore

    mdpCore --> trajBlock
    policyBlock --> trajBlock

    trajBlock --> outBlock[OUTPUT SAMPLE TRAJECTORY]
    trajBlock --> outBlock2[REWARD SEQUENCE]
    trajBlock --> outBlock3[DISCOUNTED RETURN G sub t]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Markov Models (MC → MRP → MDP)

The following diagram clarifies the **containment relationship** between Markov Chains, MRPs, and MDPs. This is a high-yield visual that the KTU board examiner has flagged as a frequent 3-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mcBox[MARKOV CHAIN MC]
    mrpBox[MARKOV REWARD PROCESS MRP]
    mdpBox[MARKOV DECISION PROCESS MDP]

    mcBox -- add rewards --> mrpBox
    mrpBox -- add actions --> mdpBox
    mdpBox -- fix policy --> mrpBox
    mrpBox -- drop rewards --> mcBox
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the hierarchy:**
> - A **Markov Chain** has only states and transition probabilities $P(s' \vert s)$.
> - A **Markov Reward Process** adds rewards $R(s)$.
> - A **Markov Decision Process** adds actions $A$ and policy $\pi(a \vert s)$.
> - An **MDP with a fixed policy** $\pi$ collapses back into an MRP (this is the result of Derivation 3 in Section 3).

### 4.4 State Transition Diagram (Worked Schematic)

Consider a 3-state MDP $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ with a single deterministic action $a$. Transitions: $s_1 \to s_2$ (prob 0.7), $s_1 \to s_1$ (prob 0.3), $s_2 \to s_3$ (prob 1.0), $s_3 \to s_1$ (prob 1.0).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateS1[S1]
    stateS2[S2]
    stateS3[S3]
    stateS1 --> stateS2 : p 0.7
    stateS1 --> stateS1 : p 0.3
    stateS2 --> stateS3 : p 1.0
    stateS3 --> stateS1 : p 1.0
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** Always check that the outgoing probabilities from each state **sum to exactly 1**. If they don't, the matrix is **not a valid transition function**, and full marks are denied even if the rest is correct.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define a Markov Decision Process (MDP). List its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a mathematical framework for modeling sequential decision-making problems where an agent interacts with a stochastic environment.

It is formally defined as a 5-tuple $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ where:
1. $\mathcal{S}$ — finite set of states of the environment.
2. $\mathcal{A}$ — finite set of actions available to the agent.
3. $P : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{S} \to [0, 1]$ — state transition probability function.
4. $R : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{R}$ — reward function.
5. $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ — discount factor for future rewards.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **State and explain the Markov Property with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Markov Property** states that the future evolution of the process depends only on the **current state and action**, and is **conditionally independent of the entire history** of past states and actions.

Mathematically:

$$P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$

**Example:** In a chess game, the current board position is a Markov state because it contains all information necessary to determine the legality and consequence of every possible next move. The history of how that position was reached is irrelevant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write "the Markov Property means the system has no memory." This is **incomplete and loses 1 mark**. The correct phrase is: *"The system has no memory **beyond the current state**."* Always include the qualifier **"given the current state"**.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

> **(a)** Define a Markov Decision Process (MDP). Explain the **Markov Property** and **Stationarity** properties of an MDP in detail, with one engineering example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a 3-state MDP with $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $\mathcal{A} = \{a\}$, and reward sequence from $s_1$ given by $R_1 = 2, R_2 = 4, R_3 = 1$. Compute the discounted return $G_0$ for (i) $\gamma = 0.5$ and (ii) $\gamma = 0.9$. Verify using the recursive formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** An MDP is a 5-tuple $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ that models sequential decision-making under uncertainty. The agent observes state $S_t$, selects action $A_t$ via policy $\pi$, and the environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$ and emits reward $R_{t+1}$ according to transition probability $P(s' \vert s, a)$ and reward function $R(s, a)$.

**Markov Property (2.5 marks):** The Markov Property (memoryless property) states that the conditional distribution of the next state and reward depends only on the **current state and action**, not on the entire history:

$$P(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1}, r_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots, s_0, a_0)$$

If a state satisfies this, the agent can **discard all history** and act on the current state alone. *Engineering example:* In GPS navigation, the current location and traffic state form a Markov state — the agent does not need the entire route history to decide the next turn.

**Stationarity Property (2.5 marks):** Stationarity means that the transition probabilities $P(s' \vert s, a)$ and reward function $R(s, a)$ are **time-invariant**, i.e., they do not change as $t$ progresses:

$$P(s_{t+1} = s' \mid s_t = s, a_t = a) = P(s_{t+k+1} = s' \mid s_{t+k} = s, a_{t+k} = a) \quad \forall k \geq 0$$

*Engineering example:* The transmission dynamics of a stationary industrial robot (excluding wear-and-tear effects) are time-invariant — the probability of moving from joint angle 30° to 32° is the same on day 1 and day 1000.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Case (i): $\gamma = 0.5$**

$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3 = 2 + (0.5)(4) + (0.5)^2(1) = 2 + 2 + 0.25 = 4.25$$

**Recursive verification:**

$$G_2 = R_3 = 1$$
$$G_1 = R_2 + \gamma G_2 = 4 + (0.5)(1) = 4.5$$
$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma G_1 = 2 + (0.5)(4.5) = 2 + 2.25 = 4.25 \quad \checkmark$$

**Case (ii): $\gamma = 0.9$**

$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2 + \gamma^2 R_3 = 2 + (0.9)(4) + (0.9)^2(1) = 2 + 3.6 + 0.81 = 6.41$$

**Recursive verification:**

$$G_2 = R_3 = 1$$
$$G_1 = R_2 + \gamma G_2 = 4 + (0.9)(1) = 4.9$$
$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma G_1 = 2 + (0.9)(4.9) = 2 + 4.41 = 6.41 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{G_0^{\gamma=0.5} = 4.25 \quad \text{and} \quad G_0^{\gamma=0.9} = 6.41}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 14-Mark Numericals:** Students frequently **swap the order of summation** in the recursive verification (computing $G_0$ first instead of $G_2$ first). The correct order is **backward in time**: compute $G_2 \to G_1 \to G_0$. Losing this order costs **1 mark** even if the final answer is right.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

> **(a)** Define and distinguish between a **Markov Chain (MC)**, a **Markov Reward Process (MRP)**, and a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. Draw a clear comparison table. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 2-state MDP has $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2\}$ with the following transition probabilities for action $a_1$:
> $P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) = 0.4$, $P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_1) = 0.6$, $P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_1) = 0.7$, $P(s_2 \mid s_2, a_1) = 0.3$.
> Verify that this satisfies the row-stochastic property. Then, given a policy $\pi(a_1 \vert s) = 0.8$ and $\pi(a_2 \vert s) = 0.2$ (assume $P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_2) = 0.1$, $P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_2) = 0.9$, and similarly for $s_2$), compute the MRP transition probabilities $P^{\pi}(s' \vert s)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A **Markov Chain (MC)** is a stochastic process $\langle \mathcal{S}, P \rangle$ where the next state depends only on the current state via transition probabilities $P(s' \vert s)$. There are **no actions** and **no rewards**.

A **Markov Reward Process (MRP)** extends an MC by adding a reward function: $\langle \mathcal{S}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$. There are still no actions, but each state has an associated expected reward.

A **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is the most general framework: $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$. It includes both actions and rewards, with transitions $P(s' \vert s, a)$ and reward $R(s, a)$ that depend on the action taken.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | MC | MRP | MDP |
|---|---|---|---|
| States $\mathcal{S}$ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Actions $\mathcal{A}$ | No | No | Yes |
| Rewards $R$ | No | Yes | Yes |
| Transition | $P(s'\vert s)$ | $P(s'\vert s)$ | $P(s'\vert s, a)$ |
| Discount $\gamma$ | No | Yes | Yes |
| Policy $\pi$ | No | No | Yes |
| Decisions | No | No | Yes |

**Key insight:** An MDP with a fixed policy $\pi$ collapses into an MRP. An MRP with the reward function removed becomes an MC. This hierarchical structure is fundamental.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Row-stochastic verification (2 marks):** For action $a_1$:

$$\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_1, a_1) = P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) + P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_1) = 0.4 + 0.6 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s_2, a_1) = P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_1) + P(s_2 \mid s_2, a_1) = 0.7 + 0.3 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Computing MRP transitions (5 marks):**

Using $P^{\pi}(s' \mid s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) P(s' \mid s, a)$:

**From $s_1$ to $s_1$:**

$$P^{\pi}(s_1 \mid s_1) = \pi(a_1 \mid s_1) P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_1) + \pi(a_2 \mid s_1) P(s_1 \mid s_1, a_2)$$

$$P^{\pi}(s_1 \mid s_1) = (0.8)(0.4) + (0.2)(0.1) = 0.32 + 0.02 = 0.34$$

**From $s_1$ to $s_2$:**

$$P^{\pi}(s_2 \mid s_1) = (0.8)(0.6) + (0.2)(0.9) = 0.48 + 0.18 = 0.66$$

**Verification (row sum):** $0.34 + 0.66 = 1.00$ ✓

**From $s_2$ to $s_1$** (using analogous values $P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_1) = 0.7$, $P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_2) = 0.3$):

$$P^{\pi}(s_1 \mid s_2) = (0.8)(0.7) + (0.2)(0.3) = 0.56 + 0.06 = 0.62$$

**From $s_2$ to $s_2$:**

$$P^{\pi}(s_2 \mid s_2) = (0.8)(0.3) + (0.2)(0.7) = 0.24 + 0.14 = 0.38$$

**Verification (row sum):** $0.62 + 0.38 = 1.00$ ✓

**Final MRP Transition Matrix:**

$$P^{\pi} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.34 & 0.66 \\ 0.62 & 0.38 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — MRP Reduction:** Students commonly forget to **multiply by the policy probability** $\pi(a \vert s)$ for each action, treating the MDP and MRP as if they are the same. Always show the **two-term sum explicitly** in your answer. Skipping the policy term loses **2 full marks**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Definition and Properties**

- **MDP Tuple**: $M = \langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$ — memorize the order.
- **Markov Property**: $P(s' \mid s, a) = P(s' \mid s, a, h)$ — future depends only on present.
- **Stationarity**: $P$ and $R$ are time-invariant.
- **Row-stochasticity**: $\sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) = 1$ for every $(s, a)$ — never sum over $s$.
- **Return**: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Recursive Return**: $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ — always compute backward in time.
- **Bounded Return**: $\vert G_t \vert \leq R_{\max} / (1 - \gamma)$.
- **MRP Reduction**: $P^{\pi}(s' \mid s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) P(s' \vert s, a)$.
- **Hierarchy**: MC ⊂ MRP ⊂ MDP (with policy collapse: MDP → MRP).
- **Discount factor**: $\gamma \in [0, 1)$; $\gamma = 0$ is myopic, $\gamma \to 1$ is far-sighted.
- **Typical exam values**: $\gamma = 0.5, 0.9, 0.99$.
- **Engineering applications**: autonomous driving, robotics, healthcare, finance, networking.
- **Common error**: confusing MDP with MRP (MDP has actions, MRP does not).
- **Common error**: computing the recursive return in the wrong time order.
- **Common error**: omitting the qualifier "given the current state" when defining Markov Property.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Value Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In Reinforcement Learning (RL), a **Value Function** is a fundamental mathematical construct that estimates **how good** a particular state (or state-action pair) is for an agent, defined as the expected cumulative future reward an agent can obtain starting from that state (or state-action pair) and subsequently following a specific policy $\pi$.

Mathematically, the RL literature defines two principal value functions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Value Function $V^{\pi}(s)$:** The expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter.
> **Action Value Function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$:** The expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and thereafter following policy $\pi$.

The Greek letter $\pi$ (policy) represents the agent's strategy — a mapping from states to a probability distribution over actions. Value functions are the **central objects** upon which nearly all RL algorithms (DP, MC, TD, Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN) are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chess player evaluating a particular board position. You don't just look at the immediate piece capture; you mentally project forward — *"If I play from here, what is my expected long-term advantage?"* That mental projection is precisely the **Value Function**.

- The **State Value Function** is like asking: *"How favorable is my current chess position on average, assuming I play a sensible strategy from here on?"*
- The **Action Value Function** is like asking: *"How favorable would it be if I made a specific move (say, move my queen to e5) and then played sensibly afterward?"*

Another analogy: think of a taxi driver in a new city.
- $V^{\pi}(\text{location})$: *"If I am at MG Road and follow my usual driving policy, how much total fare can I expect to earn over the rest of the day?"*
- $Q^{\pi}(\text{location}, \text{turn left})$: *"If I am at MG Road and choose to turn left, and then follow my usual policy, how much fare should I expect?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Value Functions Matter in KTU 2024:** Without value functions, the RL agent has no mechanism to compare the long-term desirability of states or actions. They convert a delayed, sequential reward problem into an immediate scalar evaluation problem at every state.

## 1.3 Key Notation Reference

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $s, s'$ | Current state, next state |
| $a$ | Action taken in state $s$ |
| $r$ | Immediate reward received |
| $\gamma$ | **Discount factor** ($0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$) — controls present value of future rewards |
| $G_t$ | Return (cumulative discounted reward from time $t$) |
| $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ under policy $\pi$ |
| $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | Transition probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via $a$ |
| $R(s, a)$ | Expected immediate reward for taking $a$ in $s$ |

The **discount factor $\gamma$** is a dimensionless scalar, typically **0.9 or 0.99** in practice, representing how much the agent values immediate reward versus future reward.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition

Value functions decompose the RL objective (maximize expected cumulative reward) into a per-state scalar estimate. The logic unfolds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Define the Return:** First, define the random variable representing the agent's total future payoff:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

- **Step 2 — Take Expectation Over Trajectories:** Since rewards and transitions are stochastic, we take an expectation over the distribution of trajectories induced by following $\pi$. This expectation yields the value function.

- **Step 3 — Conditional Expectation on Current State:** Conditioning $G_t$ on starting in state $S_t = s$ yields the **state value function** $V^{\pi}(s)$.

- **Step 4 — Condition Further on the Action:** Conditioning also on the first action $A_t = a$ yields the **action value function** $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$.

- **Step 5 — Establish Recursive (Bellman) Form:** The value function satisfies a recursive consistency: the value of a state equals the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state. This is the famous **Bellman Equation**.

- **Step 6 — Search for the Optimal Policy:** Among all policies, the one achieving the highest value at every state defines the **optimal value function** $V^*(s)$ and **optimal action value** $Q^*(s,a)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Discounted Return | Cumulative future reward |
| 2 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | State Value Function | Value of being in state $s$ under $\pi$ |
| 3 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | Action Value Function | Value of taking $a$ in $s$ under $\pi$ |
| 4 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | State-Action Relationship | Total value = weighted sum of action values |
| 5 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \, V^{\pi}(s')$ | Action-State Relationship | $Q$ = reward + discounted value of next state |
| 6 | $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{\pi}(s') \right]$ | Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}$ | Recursive form of $V^{\pi}$ |
| 7 | $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^{\pi}(s',a')$ | Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^{\pi}$ | Recursive form of $Q^{\pi}$ |
| 8 | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} Q^*(s,a)$ | Optimal State Value | Best possible value of state $s$ |
| 9 | $Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^*(s')$ | Optimal Action Value | Best possible value of $(s,a)$ |
| 10 | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^*(s') \right]$ | Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$ | Recursive form of optimal $V$ |
| 11 | $Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')$ | Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$ | Recursive form of optimal $Q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize equations (6), (7), (10), and (11). KTU examiners frequently ask students to *"write the Bellman equation for $V^{\pi}$ and $Q^{\pi}$"* or *"derive the Bellman optimality equation"* for 7 to 14 marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Value functions power a vast array of engineering applications:

- **Robotics:** Value iteration computes optimal grasping trajectories for robotic arms.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Self-driving cars use value function approximations to evaluate lane-change decisions.
- **Game AI:** AlphaGo's core relies on approximating $V^*(s)$ and $Q^*(s,a)$ over the game tree.
- **Operations Research:** Inventory management, dynamic pricing, and supply chain optimization.
- **Healthcare RL:** Optimizing treatment policies for chronic diseases by valuing patient states.
- **Finance:** Portfolio allocation problems modeled as MDPs with value function evaluation.

The recursive nature of Bellman equations enables **Dynamic Programming (DP)**, which serves as the theoretical foundation for almost all modern RL methods covered in this PECDT755 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}(s)$

**Starting Point:** Definition of state value function
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 1 — Expand the return $G_t$ using its definition.**
The return can be split into the immediate reward and the discounted future return:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute this decomposition into the expectation.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 3 — Apply linearity of expectation.**
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the expectations by conditioning on the action $a$ and next state $s'$.**
The agent first picks an action $a$ according to $\pi$, then the environment transitions to $s'$. We condition the expectation on both:
$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \Big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

> In the deterministic-reward (or expected-reward) formulation used in most KTU problems, we write $R(s,a)$ directly as the expected immediate reward for the transition.

**Step 5 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V^{\pi}(s')$.**
By definition, $\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V^{\pi}(s')$.

**Step 6 — Assemble the final recursive form.**
$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \Big[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^{\pi}$**. It expresses the value of state $s$ as the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state, weighted by the policy and transition probabilities.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$

**Starting Point:** Definition of action value function
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$$

**Step 1 — Expand $G_t$.**
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and condition on the next state $s'$.**
$$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \Big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \Big]$$

**Step 3 — From state $s'$, the agent picks $a'$ according to $\pi$. Expand the inner expectation by conditioning on $a'$.**
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^{\pi}(s', a')$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain the recursive form.**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{\pi}(s, a) &= \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \Big[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, Q^{\pi}(s', a') \Big] \\
&= R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') \, Q^{\pi}(s', a')
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation for $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$

**Starting Point:** The optimal state value is the maximum value achievable over all policies:
$$V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$$

**Step 1 — At the optimum, the agent always picks the action with the highest $Q^*$.**
For the optimal policy $\pi^*$, the agent deterministically selects the action maximizing $Q^*(s,a)$:
$$\pi^*(a \mid s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s, a) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Replace the expectation over $\pi$ with a $\max$ over actions.**
Starting from the Bellman expectation equation for $V^{\pi}$ and substituting $\pi^*$:
$$\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \Big[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^*(s') \Big] \\
&= \max_{a} \Big[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^*(s') \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$**. The $\max$ replaces the probabilistic average, reflecting that the optimal agent always chooses the best action.

## 3.4 Derivation: Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$

**Starting Point:** The optimal action value satisfies:
$$Q^*(s, a) = R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^*(s')$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $V^*(s') = \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s, a) &= R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$**. It is the cornerstone of model-free methods like **Q-Learning**, where the update rule approximates this equation using sampled transitions.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Recursion Depth | Operator Used | Equation Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V^{\pi}(s)$ | One-step look-ahead under $\pi$ | Sum (expectation) | Bellman Expectation |
| $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ | One-step look-ahead under $\pi$ | Sum (expectation) | Bellman Expectation |
| $V^*(s)$ | One-step look-ahead under $\pi^*$ | $\max$ over actions | Bellman Optimality |
| $Q^*(s,a)$ | One-step look-ahead under $\pi^*$ | $\max$ over next actions | Bellman Optimality |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the state value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ and the action value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ in a Markov Decision Process. What is the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in these definitions?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) The state value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ is defined as the expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter: $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$.
- (1 Mark) The action value function $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ is defined as the expected return when starting in state $s$, taking action $a$, and then following policy $\pi$: $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$.
- (1 Mark) The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ weighs the relative importance of immediate versus future rewards. It ensures mathematical convergence of the infinite-horizon sum $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ and reflects a preference for sooner rewards.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Distinguish between the Bellman Expectation Equation and the Bellman Optimality Equation. Why is the latter non-linear?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) The Bellman Expectation Equation computes the value of a state under a *fixed* policy $\pi$. It uses an expectation (sum) operator over actions chosen by $\pi$. Example: $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)[R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- (1 Mark) The Bellman Optimality Equation computes the value of a state under the *optimal* policy $\pi^*$. It uses a $\max$ operator over actions to reflect the optimal choice.
- (1 Mark) The optimality equation is non-linear because of the $\max$ operation. The $\max$ of a set of linear functions is piecewise linear but globally non-linear, in contrast to the linear system of equations produced by the expectation equation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ from first principles. Clearly state each step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-state MDP shown in the table below, compute $V^{\pi}(s_1)$, $V^{\pi}(s_2)$, and $V^{\pi}(s_3)$ under the given policy. Assume $\gamma = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

| From $s$ | Action $a$ | To $s'$ | $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | $R(s, a, s')$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 1.0 | +1 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 0.4 | +2 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | 0.6 | +0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_3$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start with the definition of the state value function:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Expand the return $G_t$ as the sum of immediate reward and discounted future return:
$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \ldots = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substitute and apply linearity of expectation:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Condition on the action $a$ and next state $s'$, and apply the law of total expectation:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] \big]$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Recognize the inner conditional expectation as the state value of $s'$:
$$\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s'] = V^{\pi}(s')$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Final assembled form:
$$V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V^{\pi}(s') \big]$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Note that $s_3$ is a self-loop terminal-like state with zero reward and $P(s_3 \mid s_3, a_3) = 1$. Since the only reward on entering/leaving $s_3$ is zero, $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Compute $V^{\pi}(s_2)$ using the Bellman equation:
$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s_2) &= R(s_2, a_2, s_1) P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_2) + R(s_2, a_2, s_3) P(s_3 \mid s_2, a_2) \\
&\quad + \gamma \big[ P(s_1 \mid s_2, a_2) V^{\pi}(s_1) + P(s_3 \mid s_2, a_2) V^{\pi}(s_3) \big] \\
&= (2)(0.4) + (0)(0.6) + 0.9 \big[ 0.4 \cdot V^{\pi}(s_1) + 0.6 \cdot 0 \big] \\
&= 0.8 + 0.36 \, V^{\pi}(s_1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Compute $V^{\pi}(s_1)$:
$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = R(s_1, a_1, s_2) P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_1) + \gamma P(s_2 \mid s_1, a_1) V^{\pi}(s_2) = (1)(1.0) + 0.9(1.0) V^{\pi}(s_2)$$
$$V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 0.9 \, V^{\pi}(s_2)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Solve the system of two linear equations:
Substitute $V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 0.9 V^{\pi}(s_2)$ into $V^{\pi}(s_2) = 0.8 + 0.36 V^{\pi}(s_1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
V^{\pi}(s_2) &= 0.8 + 0.36 \big[ 1 + 0.9 V^{\pi}(s_2) \big] \\
V^{\pi}(s_2) &= 0.8 + 0.36 + 0.324 \, V^{\pi}(s_2) \\
V^{\pi}(s_2) (1 - 0.324) &= 1.16 \\
V^{\pi}(s_2) &= \frac{1.16}{0.676} \approx 1.716
\end{aligned}$$

Then $V^{\pi}(s_1) = 1 + 0.9 (1.716) = 1 + 1.5444 = 2.544$.

**Final Answer (1 Mark):** $V^{\pi}(s_1) \approx 2.544$, $V^{\pi}(s_2) \approx 1.716$, $V^{\pi}(s_3) = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Define the optimal state value function $V^*(s)$ and optimal action value function $Q^*(s,a)$. State and explain the Bellman Optimality Equations for both. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-action, 3-state MDP with the following transition and reward structure. Determine the optimal policy $\pi^*$ and compute $V^*(s_1)$. Take $\gamma = 0.9$. **(7 Marks)**

| From $s$ | Action | Next $s'$ | Probability | Reward |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s_1$ | $a_1$ | $s_2$ | 1.0 | 5 |
| $s_1$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | 1 |
| $s_2$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | $a_2$ | $s_1$ | 1.0 | -1 |
| $s_3$ | $a_1$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | $a_2$ | $s_3$ | 1.0 | 0 |

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** The optimal state value function $V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} V^{\pi}(s)$ — it is the maximum expected return achievable from state $s$ under any policy. The optimal action value function $Q^*(s,a) = \max_{\pi} Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ — it is the maximum expected return from $(s, a)$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** The Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$:
$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^*(s') \right]$$
The $\max$ operator arises because the optimal agent always picks the best action.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** The Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*$:
$$Q^*(s, a) = R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$
The agent first commits to $a$, then optimally picks the next action $a'$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Linkage: $V^*(s) = \max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$. The optimal policy is recovered by $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Note that $s_3$ is a terminal-like absorbing state with zero reward regardless of action, so $V^*(s_3) = 0$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** For state $s_2$:
- $Q^*(s_2, a_1) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_3) = 0$
- $Q^*(s_2, a_2) = -1 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_1)$

So $V^*(s_2) = \max(0, \, -1 + 0.9 V^*(s_1))$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** For state $s_1$:
- $Q^*(s_1, a_1) = 5 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_2)$
- $Q^*(s_1, a_2) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot V^*(s_1)$

So $V^*(s_1) = \max(5 + 0.9 V^*(s_2), \, 1 + 0.9 V^*(s_1))$.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Assume $a_1$ is optimal in $s_1$ (will verify). Then $V^*(s_1) = 5 + 0.9 V^*(s_2)$. Now consider $s_2$: if $V^*(s_1) > 1.11$, then $a_2$ is better in $s_2$. Try $V^*(s_1) = 5 + 0.9(0) = 5$ if $a_1$ is chosen in $s_2$. Then check if $V^*(s_1) = 1 + 0.9(5) = 5.5 > 5$, so $a_2$ in $s_1$ gives $5.5$ which is better. Recompute: $V^*(s_2) = \max(0, -1 + 0.9 \cdot 5.5) = \max(0, 3.95) = 3.95$. Then $V^*(s_1) = \max(5 + 0.9(3.95), 1 + 0.9(5.5)) = \max(8.555, 5.95) = 8.555$.

**Final Answer:** $\pi^*(s_1) = a_1$, $\pi^*(s_2) = a_2$, $\pi^*(s_3)$ = arbitrary, and $V^*(s_1) \approx 8.555$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **(2 Marks Lost Frequently):** Students forget to include the discount factor $\gamma$ in the Bellman equation. Always check for $\gamma$ before the value of the next state.
> - **(2 Marks Lost Frequently):** Confusing $V^*(s)$ with $V^{\pi}(s)$. The star denotes the *optimal* value; the policy $\pi$ is unspecified.
> - **(1-2 Marks Lost):** In optimality problems, using $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \cdot (\ldots)$ instead of $\max_a (\ldots)$. The optimal equation must use $\max$, not expectation.
> - **(1 Mark Lost):** Failing to verify which action is truly optimal. Always compute both $Q(s, a_1)$ and $Q(s, a_2)$ and pick the $\max$.
> - **(1 Mark Lost):** Not stating units / interpretation. Value functions are unitless dimensionless scalars (or share units of reward, e.g., currency or utility points).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Value Function Core:** A scalar estimate of expected cumulative future reward from a state (or state-action pair) under a policy $\pi$.
- **Two Variants:** $V^{\pi}(s)$ for states, $Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ for state-action pairs. The relation $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q^{\pi}(s,a)$ is foundational.
- **Discount Factor $\gamma$:** Always in $[0, 1]$. When $\gamma = 0$, only immediate reward matters; when $\gamma \to 1$, future rewards are valued almost equally.
- **Bellman Expectation Equation:** Linear equation for a *given* policy $\pi$. Uses **sum/expectation** operators.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation:** Non-linear equation for the *optimal* policy. Uses the **$\max$** operator in place of expectation.
- **Optimal Recovery:** The optimal policy is given by $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)$.
- **Convergence:** Value Iteration converges to $V^*$ for $\gamma < 1$. The rate is geometric with factor $\gamma$.
- **KTU High-Frequency Questions:** Writing Bellman equations from first principles (7 marks), distinguishing expectation vs. optimality (3 marks), solving a 3-state MDP (7 marks).
- **Backup Diagrams:** When asked to "explain the backup diagram for $V^{\pi}$," draw a tree with $s$ at the root, branching into actions, then into next states with their rewards and values.
- **Common Symbol Trap:** $\pi(a \mid s)$ is a probability, not a value. Do not confuse it with $Q(s, a)$.
- **Practical Insight:** Almost every RL algorithm either estimates $V^{\pi}$, $Q^{\pi}$, $V^*$, or $Q^*$. The choice of which to estimate defines the algorithm family.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bellman Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bellman Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bellman Equation**, named after mathematician **Richard Ernest Bellman**, is a fundamental recursive identity in dynamic programming and reinforcement learning. It expresses the value of a decision problem at a certain point in time in terms of the payoff from some initial choices and the value of the remaining decision problem that results from those initial choices.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, the Bellman equation is defined as a **recursive consistency condition** that the value functions of a Markov Decision Process (MDP) or a Markov Reward Process (MRP) must satisfy. It formally links the expected long-term return of a state (or state-action pair) to the immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of successor states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECDT755 Module 2):**
> The Bellman equations form the analytical backbone of RL. They come in **four canonical variants**:
> 1. Bellman Equation for MRP ($V$)
> 2. Bellman Expectation Equation for MDP ($V_\pi$)
> 3. Bellman Expectation Equation for MDP ($Q_\pi$)
> 4. Bellman Optimality Equation ($V_*$ and $Q_*$)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **6-day road trip from Kerala to Delhi**. On Day 1 you must decide the route. A friend asks, *"How good is it to start from Kochi?"* A perfectly rational answer is:

> *"The goodness of starting from Kochi equals the enjoyment of Day 1 plus the discounted enjoyment of all remaining days (Day 2 to Day 6)."*

But how do you know the enjoyment of all remaining days? **Exactly the same way!** It equals the enjoyment of Day 2 plus the discounted enjoyment of Days 3 to 6. This recursive unwinding is the **Bellman principle of optimality**.

**Plain English translation:**
- **Value of today = Reward today + Discounted value of tomorrow**
- This recursion, once written in math, is the Bellman equation.

### 1.3 The Components Every Bellman Equation Uses

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constants and Variables (Memorize These):**
> - **$S$** — Finite set of states (the world)
> - **$A$** — Finite set of actions (the choices)
> - **$P$** — State transition probability tensor: $P_{ss'}^a = \Pr(S_{t+1}=s' \mid S_t=s, A_t=a)$
> - **$R$** — Reward function: $R_{ss'}^a = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t=s, A_t=a, S_{t+1}=s']$
> - **$\gamma \in [0,1]$** — **Discount factor** (default for episodic or infinite-horizon problems). $\gamma=0$ means myopic; $\gamma \to 1$ means far-sighted.
> - **$\pi(a \mid s)$** — Policy: probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$
> - **$G_t$** — Return: $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$
> - **$V_\pi(s)$** — State-value function under policy $\pi$
> - **$Q_\pi(s,a)$** — Action-value function under policy $\pi$

The Bellman equations **decompose** $G_t$ into immediate reward + discounted future return, yielding a system of linear (or nonlinear) equations solvable for the value function.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Start from the return definition.**

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \dots \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}
$$

This identity is the seed. Every Bellman equation grows from it.

**Step 2 — Condition on the current state and take expectation.**

The state-value function is the expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\, G_t \,\middle|\, S_t = s \,\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the recursive return and split the expectation.**

The first term is the immediate expected reward; the second term uses the **tower property** $\mathbb{E}[X]=\mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbb{E}[X\mid Y]\bigr]$ to yield the expected value of the next state.

**Step 4 — Distinguish MRP (no action) vs MDP (with action).**

- In an **MRP**, the dynamics are fixed. The Bellman equation becomes **linear** in $V$.
- In an **MDP** with policy $\pi$, the agent samples actions from $\pi(\cdot\mid s)$. The equation becomes **linear in $\pi$ but still linear in $V_\pi$**.
- For **optimality**, we replace the expectation under $\pi$ with a **max** (or $\max$ over $Q$ for the optimal V). This produces the **Bellman Optimality Equation**, which is **nonlinear** and **max-affine**.

### 2.2 Why the Bellman Equation Matters (The "Why")

- It transforms an **infinite-horizon** sequence-of-rewards problem into a **system of equations** solvable by linear algebra (for MRP) or iterative methods (for MDP).
- It is the **defining equation** of a value function; a function $V$ that satisfies the Bellman equation **is** the value function.
- It enables the **bootstrapping** principle — every RL algorithm (TD(0), Q-learning, Value Iteration, Policy Iteration) is an **approximate solver** of a Bellman equation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Bellman Equations |
|---|---|
| **Robotics & Autonomous Navigation** | Path planning, locomotion gait optimization |
| **Power Systems** | Optimal energy dispatch, battery-charge scheduling |
| **Telecommunications** | Dynamic spectrum allocation, queue scheduling |
| **Finance** | Portfolio optimization under stochastic returns |
| **Supply Chain** | Multi-echelon inventory control |
| **Industrial Process Control** | Optimal batch control, model predictive control (MPC) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams** — memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Equation | Name | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'} V(s')$ | **Bellman Equation for MRP** | Linear |
| 2 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) Q_\pi(s,a)$ | **V from Q (consistency)** | Identity |
| 3 | $Q_\pi(s,a) = R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a \sum_{a'} \pi(a'\mid s') Q_\pi(s',a')$ | **Bellman Expectation for $Q_\pi$** | Linear |
| 4 | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \bigl[ R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a V_\pi(s') \bigr]$ | **Bellman Expectation for $V_\pi$** | Linear |
| 5 | $V_*(s) = \max_a \bigl[ R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a V_*(s') \bigr]$ | **Bellman Optimality for $V_*$** | Nonlinear (max-affine) |
| 6 | $Q_*(s,a) = R_s^a + \gamma \sum_{s'} P_{ss'}^a \max_{a'} Q_*(s',a')$ | **Bellman Optimality for $Q_*$** | Nonlinear (max-affine) |
| 7 | $V_*(s) = \max_a Q_*(s,a)$ | **Optimal V from Optimal Q** | Identity |
| 8 | $\pi_*(a\mid s) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_*(s,a')\right]$ | **Optimal policy from $Q_*$** | Identity |
| 9 | $\| T V - T V' \|_\infty \le \gamma \| V - V' \|_\infty$ | **Contraction property** | Operator theory |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Notation Rule:** In your answer sheet, **never** write $\sum_s' P_{ss'} V(s')$ with an unprimed summation index when the summand is primed. Always be explicit that $s'$ is the *next* state. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguous index notation.

### 2.5 Boundary Conditions & Special Cases

- **Finite horizon $T$:** the recursion terminates at $t = T$ with $V_T(s) = 0$ for all $s$.
- **$\gamma = 0$:** $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) R_s^a$ (pure immediate reward, myopic agent).
- **$\gamma = 1$ (only valid for episodic finite MDPs):** undiscounted total reward.
- **$T V_\pi = V_\pi$ (fixed point):** every Bellman expectation equation is the statement that $V_\pi$ is the **fixed point of a contraction operator** $T$.
