# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State the contraction mapping property of the Bellman optimality operator $T^*$ and explain its significance for the efficiency of Value Iteration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bellman optimality operator $T^* : \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert} \to \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$, defined by

$$
(T^* V)(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ r + \gamma V(s') \big]
$$

is a **$\gamma$-contraction** in the $\ell_\infty$ norm: for any two value functions $V, U$,

$$
\| T^* V - T^* U \|_\infty \leq \gamma \, \| V - U \|_\infty
$$

**Significance for efficiency:**
- **(1 mark)** The contraction property guarantees a **unique fixed point** $V^*$ (Banach fixed-point theorem).
- **(1 mark)** Repeated application reduces error geometrically: $\Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma^k \Vert V_0 - V^* \Vert_\infty$.
- **(1 mark)** Therefore Value Iteration needs only $O\!\left( \dfrac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{1-\gamma} \right)$ sweeps to reach $\epsilon$-optimality, **independent of $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$** in the sweep count.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between synchronous and asynchronous Dynamic Programming in terms of convergence and practical efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous DP | Asynchronous DP |
|---|---|---|
| Update order | All states updated in parallel using the *old* values | States updated in any order using the *newest* available values |
| Per-sweep work | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ | Same order, but reuses updated values immediately |
| Convergence guarantee | Yes (via contraction) | Yes, **provided** every state is updated infinitely often |
| Practical speed | Slower (must wait for full sweep) | Faster (no synchronization barrier; often faster by a constant factor) |

**(1 mark each row, plus 0–1 mark for conclusion).** Asynchronous DP is generally preferred in practice because it eliminates the overhead of storing two copies of $V$ and propagates new information immediately.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the convergence rate of Iterative Policy Evaluation. Show that the maximum norm of the error after $k$ sweeps is bounded by $\gamma^k \cdot \Vert V_0 - V^\pi \Vert_\infty$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For an MDP with $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 50$ states, $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 4$ actions, $\gamma = 0.95$, and a desired accuracy of $\epsilon = 0.01$, compute (i) the number of sweeps required for Iterative Policy Evaluation, and (ii) the total number of arithmetic operations assuming $\Vert V_0 - V^\pi \Vert_\infty \leq 10$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks**

We follow the derivation in Section 3.1.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the update rule $V_{k+1}(s) = (T^\pi V_k)(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ r + \gamma V_k(s') \big]$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Subtract the fixed-point equation $V^\pi(s) = (T^\pi V^\pi)(s)$ from the update:

$$
V_{k+1}(s) - V^\pi(s) = \gamma \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \big[ V_k(s') - V^\pi(s') \big]
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Take absolute value and use the boundedness of $\pi$ and $p$ (sum to 1) to get

$$
| V_{k+1}(s) - V^\pi(s) | \leq \gamma \cdot \max_{s'} | V_k(s') - V^\pi(s') |
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take $\max_s$ on both sides to obtain the recursive inequality

$$
\| V_{k+1} - V^\pi \|_\infty \leq \gamma \cdot \| V_k - V^\pi \|_\infty
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Unroll from $k = 0$ to $k$:

$$
\| V_k - V^\pi \|_\infty \leq \gamma^k \, \| V_0 - V^\pi \|_\infty
$$

This is the required geometric contraction with rate $\gamma$. $\blacksquare$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Hence Iterative Policy Evaluation converges at a **linear (geometric) rate** with contraction factor $\gamma$.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Number of sweeps (3 marks):**

Use the formula $k \geq \dfrac{\log(\Vert V_0 - V^\pi \Vert_\infty / \epsilon)}{1 - \gamma}$.

Substitute the values $\Vert V_0 - V^\pi \Vert_\infty = 10$, $\epsilon = 0.01$, $\gamma = 0.95$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\| V_0 - V^\pi \|_\infty}{\epsilon} &= \frac{10}{0.01} = 1000 \\
\log(1000) &= 3 \log(10) = 3 \times 2.3026 \approx 6.9078 \\
1 - \gamma &= 0.05 \\
k &\geq \frac{6.9078}{0.05} \approx 138.16
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the minimum integer $k$ is **139 sweeps**.

**(ii) Total operations (4 marks):**

Per-sweep cost is $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert) = 50^2 \times 4 = 10{,}000$ operations.

Total operations:

$$
\text{Total} = k \times \vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 139 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}390{,}000
$$

Approximately **$1.39 \times 10^6$ arithmetic operations**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)** framework. How does GPI guarantee convergence even when policy evaluation is performed for only a single sweep? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Prove that Value Iteration converges to the optimal value function $V^*$ in $O\!\left( \dfrac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{1-\gamma} \right)$ sweeps, and state the total computational complexity. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Generalized Policy Iteration is the general scheme in which the processes of **policy evaluation** and **policy improvement** interact, regardless of the granularity of each process. Formally, the value function $V$ and policy $\pi$ evolve as:

$$
\pi_{k+1} = \mathcal{G}(V_k), \quad V_{k+1} = (T^{\pi_{k+1}})^m V_k
$$

where $\mathcal{G}$ is the greedy operator and $m \geq 1$ is the number of evaluation sweeps per outer iteration.

**Why convergence holds even with $m = 1$ (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Even a single sweep of policy evaluation makes $V_{k+1}$ "closer" to $V^{\pi_{k+1}}$ because $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction toward $V^\pi$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** The greedy improvement step produces a strictly better policy: $V^{\pi_{k+1}}(s) \geq V^{\pi_k}(s)$ for all $s$, with strict inequality for at least one state unless $\pi_k = \pi^*$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Because there are only finitely many deterministic policies ($O(\vert \mathcal{A} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert})$), the process cannot cycle forever, and the values are monotonically non-decreasing and bounded above by $V^*$.
- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Hence the sequence $(V_k, \pi_k)$ converges to $(V^*, \pi^*)$.

**Special cases (1 mark):**
- $m \to \infty$ at every outer step → classical **Policy Iteration**.
- $m = 1$ → **Value Iteration**.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Value Iteration computes the sequence $V_{k+1} = T^* V_k$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Since $T^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction with unique fixed point $V^*$:

$$
\| V_{k+1} - V^* \|_\infty = \| T^* V_k - T^* V^* \|_\infty \leq \gamma \, \| V_k - V^* \|_\infty
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** By induction,

$$
\| V_k - V^* \|_\infty \leq \gamma^k \, \| V_0 - V^* \|_\infty
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Require $\gamma^k \Vert V_0 - V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \epsilon$:

$$
k \geq \frac{\log(\| V_0 - V^* \|_\infty / \epsilon)}{-\log \gamma} \leq \frac{\log(\| V_0 - V^* \|_\infty / \epsilon)}{1 - \gamma}
$$

So Value Iteration requires $O\!\left( \dfrac{\log(1/\epsilon)}{1 - \gamma} \right)$ sweeps.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Total time complexity. Per-sweep work is $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$. Multiplying:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = O\!\left( \frac{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert \, \log(1/\epsilon)}{1 - \gamma} \right)
$$

This is **polynomial** in $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$, $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$, and $1/\epsilon$, confirming that Value Iteration is an efficient algorithm for finite MDPs. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\log$ bases:** KTU expects **natural log ($\log$ = $\ln$)** unless otherwise stated. Mixing $\log_2$ and $\log_{10}$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator $1 - \gamma$:** Students often write only $\log(1/\epsilon)$ as the number of sweeps and miss the $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ factor. This is a **2-mark** loss.
> 3. **Mixing operator names:** Writing $T^\pi$ where $T^*$ is needed (or vice-versa) in convergence proofs is a **1-mark** deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring the policy iteration outer-loop cost:** A 14-mark question on policy iteration that does not mention the per-outer-iteration sweep cost will lose up to **3 marks**.
> 5. **Numerical substitution errors:** In Q-A(b), miscomputing $\log(1000)$ as $3$ instead of $6.9078$ is a **1-mark** deduction; use $\log_{10}$ if the question says so explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Efficiency of DP Algorithms**

- **Three canonical DP algorithms:** Iterative Policy Evaluation ($T^\pi$), Policy Iteration ($T^\pi$ + greedy improve), Value Iteration ($T^*$).
- **Contraction property:** Both $T^\pi$ and $T^*$ are $\gamma$-contractions in $\ell_\infty$ norm. This is the **single most important fact** in the entire module.
- **Geometric convergence:** $\Vert V_k - V^* \Vert_\infty \leq \gamma^k \Vert V_0 - V^* \Vert_\infty$.
- **Sweep count bound:** $k = O\!\left( \dfrac{\log(\Vert V_0 - V^* \Vert_\infty / \epsilon)}{1 - \gamma} \right)$.
- **Per-sweep cost:** $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert)$ for value iteration and policy evaluation.
- **Total time complexity:** $O\!\left( \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert^2 \vert \mathcal{A} \vert \log(1/\epsilon)}{1 - \gamma} \right)$ — **polynomial**, hence efficient.
- **Space complexity:** $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ for the value function.
- **Synchronous vs Asynchronous DP:** Both converge; asynchronous DP is faster in practice but requires every state to be updated infinitely often.
- **GPI (Generalized Policy Iteration):** The unified framework; value iteration and policy iteration are two extremes.
- **Banach fixed-point theorem:** Guarantees unique fixed point of any $\gamma$-contraction.
- **Why DP is "efficient" in the RL sense:** It is polynomial in problem size despite exponential state-action product $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ — a foundational result of the field.
- **Limitation:** DP still suffers from the **curse of dimensionality** in continuous or very large state spaces, motivating function approximation and sample-based methods.
- **Numerical exam tips:** Always carry the $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$ factor; use natural log; state the operator name explicitly; remember the per-sweep cost.

**Mnemonic for the KTU exam:** *"**C**ontraction **C**onverges **G**eometrically in **S**weeps"* — **C, C, G, S**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Monte Carlo (MC) Prediction** is a *model-free* Reinforcement Learning (RL) method used to estimate the *state-value function* $V_\pi(s)$ (or *action-value function* $Q_\pi(s,a)$) for a given policy $\pi$, by **averaging the sampled returns** observed from a collection of complete episodes of agent-environment interaction, without requiring prior knowledge of the environment's transition dynamics or reward function.

Formally, for a state $s$ visited in an episode, the **empirical return** $G_t$ starting from time $t$ is defined as:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

The MC prediction target is:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Monte Carlo Prediction estimates $V_\pi(s)$ by computing the **sample mean** of observed returns following visits to state $s$. It only learns from *completed episodes* (terminal states must eventually be reached), distinguishing it from Temporal-Difference (TD) methods that bootstrap from intermediate estimates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT755 Module 3):** MC methods are *guaranteed to converge* to the true value function for any fixed policy $\pi$ as the number of episodes $\to \infty$, provided all states are visited infinitely often.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **cricket coach** evaluating how good your team's batting strategy is in the *powerplay overs* (first 6 overs). You don't have a perfect mathematical model of how pitch conditions, bowling styles, and field placements interact. So what do you do?

You **play 100 matches** using that same strategy, and after every match, you note down the **total runs scored in the powerplay**. Then you average those 100 scores. That average is your *empirical estimate* of how good the strategy is — **this is exactly Monte Carlo Prediction!**

- The **strategy** = policy $\pi$
- The **matches** = episodes
- The **powerplay score** = return $G_t$
- The **running average** = MC estimate $V_\pi(s)$

You never "guess" intermediate scores; you wait for the **complete match** (episode) to finish, then use the actual outcome to refine your belief. The more matches you play, the more accurate the estimate becomes.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** MC prediction underpins AI systems in **autonomous driving simulators** (Waymo, Tesla), **AlphaGo's initial value networks**, **inventory management**, and **financial portfolio risk estimation** — wherever the underlying environment is too complex to model analytically.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchor

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Episode** | A complete sequence from start state $S_0$ to terminal state $S_T$ | $\tau = (S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, \dots, S_T)$ |
| **Return** | Cumulative discounted reward from time $t$ | $G_t$ |
| **Discount Factor** | Weight applied to future rewards | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ |
| **Visit** | An occurrence of state $s$ during an episode | $S_t = s$ |
| **Sample Mean** | Arithmetic average of sampled returns | $\bar{x}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why MC Prediction Works

The MC prediction algorithm rests on the **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** from probability theory:

> **Weak LLN:** As the number of independent samples $n \to \infty$, the sample mean converges in probability to the true expected value.

So if we treat each observed return $G_t^{(i)}$ from episode $i$ as an *i.i.d. sample* (approximately, under stochastic policies), then:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] \approx \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_t^{(i)}$$

where $N(s)$ = number of episodes in which state $s$ was visited.

## 2.2 The Two Canonical Variants of MC Prediction

### (a) First-Visit Monte Carlo (FVMC)
- For each episode, identify the **first** time step $t$ at which state $s$ is visited.
- Increment counter: $N(s) \leftarrow N(s) + 1$
- Update running total: $S(s) \leftarrow S(s) + G_t$
- Estimate: $V(s) \leftarrow S(s) / N(s)$

### (b) Every-Visit Monte Carlo (EVMC)
- For each episode, identify **every** time step $t$ at which state $s$ is visited.
- For each such visit, apply the same incremental update as above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favorite:** Both FVMC and EVMC converge to $V_\pi(s)$ as $N(s) \to \infty$, but **First-Visit MC** is more rigorously analyzed and is the standard textbook reference (Sutton & Barto, 2018, 2nd Edition). Every-Visit MC is a slight extension with looser convergence proofs but is often empirically similar.

## 2.3 Incremental Implementation (The "Online" Form)

For implementation efficiency, KTU examiners expect the **incremental update rule**:

$$N(s) \leftarrow N(s) + 1$$
$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \frac{1}{N(s)} \left[ G_t - V(s) \right]$$

The bracketed term $[G_t - V(s)]$ is the **prediction error** (Monte Carlo error), analogous to the TD error $\delta_t$ in TD learning. This is the seed of the equivalence between MC and TD methods that you will study in Module 4.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Total discounted reward from time $t$ until episode end |
| 2 | MC Value Estimate | $V(s) \approx \frac{1}{N(s)}\sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_t^{(i)}$ | Mean of observed returns from $s$ |
| 3 | Incremental Update | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \left[G_t - V(s)\right]$ | Online learning step; $\alpha$ = step-size |
| 4 | Constant Step-Size | $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ | Guarantees convergence when conditions hold |
| 5 | Boundary Condition | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | $\gamma = 0$: myopic; $\gamma \to 1$: far-sighted |
| 6 | Convergence Theorem | $V(s) \to V_\pi(s)$ as $N(s) \to \infty$ | LLN guarantee |
| 7 | Required Assumption | All states visited $\infty$ often | **Exploration problem** (addressed in GPI, later modules) |
| 8 | Episode Termination | $S_T$ must be reached | MC needs *episodic* tasks |
| 9 | Biasedness (constant $\alpha$) | $V(s) \to \mathbb{E}[\text{weighted history}]$ | Never fully forgets old data |
| 10 | Unbiasedness (averaging) | $\mathbb{E}[V(s)] = V_\pi(s)$ | First-visit MC with $\alpha = 1/n$ is unbiased |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** For Sutton & Barto's notation in KTU board exams, the return is **always** $G_t$, and the **episode** is required to terminate. Do not write MC updates for continuing tasks — they are undefined without further modification (e.g., average-reward formulation, which is *out of syllabus* for PECDT755).

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | MC Prediction Application |
|---|---|
| **Game AI (AlphaGo Zero)** | Initial evaluation of board positions via self-play rollouts |
| **Robotics** | Estimating expected cost-to-go in unknown terrains |
| **Finance** | Monte Carlo pricing of path-dependent options (Asian, Barrier) |
| **Supply Chain** | Estimating expected demand when MDP transitions are stochastic but tractable only via simulation |
| **Healthcare RL** | Estimating long-term patient outcome under a treatment policy |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Simulating millions of driving episodes to value states (lane position, velocity) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Definition to Sample Mean Estimate

We begin from the formal Bellman expectation equation for the state-value function:

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$

By the **definition of expected value of a discrete random variable**:

$$\mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right] = \sum_{g} g \cdot \mathbb{P}(G_t = g \mid S_t = s)$$

Since we **cannot compute** this expectation analytically (no model!), we replace it with a **sample average estimator** (unbiased, by LLN):

$$\hat{V}(s) = \frac{1}{N(s)} \sum_{i=1}^{N(s)} G_t^{(i)}$$

### Step-by-step conversion to the incremental form:

Starting from the sample mean definition:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \sum_{i=1}^{N+1} G_t^{(i)}$$

Splitting the sum:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)} \right]$$

Using $\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)} = N \cdot \hat{V}_N(s)$:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} + N \cdot \hat{V}_N(s) \right]$$

Separating the terms:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{N}{N+1} \hat{V}_N(s) + \frac{1}{N+1} G_t^{(N+1)}$$

Note that $\frac{N}{N+1} = 1 - \frac{1}{N+1}$, so:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \hat{V}_N(s) - \frac{1}{N+1} \hat{V}_N(s) + \frac{1}{N+1} G_t^{(N+1)}$$

Factor out the step size $\alpha_{N+1} = \frac{1}{N+1}$:

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \hat{V}_N(s) + \frac{1}{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} - \hat{V}_N(s) \right]$$

This is the **canonical incremental MC update rule**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Derivation Result:** $\boxed{V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \left[G_t - V(s)\right]}$, with $\alpha = \frac{1}{N(s)}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unbiasedness of First-Visit MC Estimator

Claim: $\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{V}(s)\right] = V_\pi(s)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

Let $I_i = 1$ if state $s$ is *first visited* in episode $i$ at time $t_i$, and $I_i = 0$ otherwise. The return observed at this visit is $G_{t_i}^{(i)}$.

The estimator is:

$$\hat{V}(s) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} I_i \cdot G_{t_i}^{(i)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} I_i}$$

Condition on $\sum I_i = k$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{V}(s) \,\Big|\, \sum I_i = k\right] = \frac{1}{k} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} I_i \cdot G_{t_i}^{(i)} \,\Big|\, \sum I_i = k\right]$$

Using linearity and the fact that when $I_i = 1$, $\mathbb{E}[G_{t_i}^{(i)} \mid S_{t_i} = s] = V_\pi(s)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{V}(s) \,\Big|\, \sum I_i = k\right] = \frac{1}{k} \cdot k \cdot V_\pi(s) = V_\pi(s)$$

By the law of total expectation, $\mathbb{E}\left[\hat{V}(s)\right] = V_\pi(s)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

### Problem Setup

A robot navigates a **3-state episodic MDP**: Start $S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3$ (terminal). Policy $\pi$ is deterministic. Discount factor $\gamma = 0.5$.

**Two episodes observed:**

- **Episode 1:** $S_1 \xrightarrow{R=+2} S_2 \xrightarrow{R=+4} S_3$ (terminal)
- **Episode 2:** $S_1 \xrightarrow{R=+1} S_2 \xrightarrow{R=+2} S_3$ (terminal)

**Compute the First-Visit MC estimate of $V_\pi(S_1)$ and $V_\pi(S_2)$.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute returns for Episode 1:

$$G_0^{(1)} = R_1 + \gamma R_2 = 2 + 0.5 \times 4 = 2 + 2 = 4$$

$$G_1^{(1)} = R_2 = 4 \quad \text{(since terminal follows)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute returns for Episode 2:

$$G_0^{(2)} = R_1 + \gamma R_2 = 1 + 0.5 \times 2 = 1 + 1 = 2$$

$$G_1^{(2)} = R_2 = 2$$

**Step 3:** For $V_\pi(S_1)$ — both episodes visit $S_1$ first at $t = 0$:

$$V_\pi(S_1) = \frac{G_0^{(1)} + G_0^{(2)}}{2} = \frac{4 + 2}{2} = 3$$

**Step 4:** For $V_\pi(S_2)$ — both episodes visit $S_2$ first at $t = 1$:

$$V_\pi(S_2) = \frac{G_1^{(1)} + G_1^{(2)}}{2} = \frac{4 + 2}{2} = 3$$

**Verification using Bellman equation** (with known $V_\pi(S_3) = 0$):

$$V_\pi(S_1) = R_1 + \gamma V_\pi(S_2) \implies 3 = 2 + 0.5(3) = 2 + 1.5 = 3.5$$

Wait — there's a discrepancy! This is **sample noise** from only 2 episodes. With more episodes, the estimate converges to the true value (e.g., with $V_\pi(S_2) = 3$, true $V_\pi(S_1) = 3.5$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The MC estimator is **unbiased but has variance**. Variance shrinks as $1/N(s)$. This is *the* key trade-off distinguishing MC from analytical DP — DP is **zero-variance but biased** (requires model).

## 3.4 Summary of Key Mathematical Results

| Result | Statement | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **LLN Convergence** | $\hat{V}(s) \xrightarrow{P} V_\pi(s)$ as $N(s) \to \infty$ | Theoretically justified |
| **Unbiasedness** | $\mathbb{E}[\hat{V}(s)] = V_\pi(s)$ for FVMC | Sample mean is the gold-standard estimator |
| **Variance Decay** | $\text{Var}(\hat{V}(s)) = \sigma^2 / N(s)$ | More episodes ⇒ tighter confidence |
| **Bias-Variance Trade-off** | Constant $\alpha$ ⇒ biased but tracks non-stationarity | For stationary $\pi$, prefer $\alpha = 1/n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of First-Visit MC Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INIT["INITIALIZATION PHASE"]
        initA["Initialize V of s to 0 for all states s in S"]
        initB["Initialize ReturnsList of s to empty for all s in S"]
    end

    subgraph LOOP["OUTER EPISODE LOOP"]
        loopA["Generate episode following policy pi"]
        loopB["Receive trajectory tau = S0 A0 R1 S1 ... ST"]
    end

    subgraph REVERSE["REVERSE TIME TRAVERSAL"]
        revA["Set G = 0"]
        revB["FOR t = T-1 down to 0"]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE["STATE UPDATE LOGIC"]
        updA["G = gamma times G plus R of t+1"]
        updB["UNLESS St appears in S0 S1 ... St-1"]
        updC["Append G to ReturnsList of St"]
        updD["V of St = Average of ReturnsList of St"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["CONVERGENCE OUTPUT"]
        outA["Return V of pi of s for all s in S"]
    end

    INIT --> LOOP
    LOOP --> REVERSE
    REVERSE --> UPDATE
    UPDATE --> OUTPUT
    loopA --> loopB
    revA --> revB
    updA --> updB
    updB --> updC
    updC --> updD
```

## 4.2 Data Flow — Episode Sampling to Value Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV["ENVIRONMENT"]
        envA["State St"]
        envB["Action At sampled from policy pi"]
        envC["Reward Rt+1 emitted"]
        envD["Next State St+1"]
    end

    subgraph AGENT["LEARNING AGENT"]
        agentA["Trajectory Buffer"]
        agentB["Return Calculator Gt"]
        agentC["First Visit Detector"]
        agentD["Running Mean Estimator V of s"]
    end

    envA --> envB
    envB --> envC
    envC --> envD
    envD --> agentA
    agentA --> agentB
    agentB --> agentC
    agentC --> agentD
    agentD -.feedback.-> envB
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MC vs TD Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MC["MONTE CARLO PREDICTION PATH"]
        mcA["Episode Completion Required"]
        mcB["Actual Return Gt = sum of rewards"]
        mcC["No Bootstrap Step"]
        mcD["High Variance Unbiased"]
    end

    subgraph TD["TEMPORAL DIFFERENCE PATH FOR COMPARISON"]
        tdA["Step by Step Online Update"]
        tdB["Bootstrapped Target Rt+1 plus gamma V of St+1"]
        tdC["Lower Variance But Biased"]
        tdD["Single Step Bootstrapped"]
    end

    mcA --> mcB --> mcC --> mcD
    tdA --> tdB --> tdC --> tdD
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU exams):** When asked to "explain MC prediction," examiners expect a flowchart showing: (1) Episode generation, (2) Reverse time traversal, (3) First-visit detection, (4) Return computation, (5) Running mean update. Memorize this 5-step pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: Define Monte Carlo Prediction. Why is it called a model-free method?**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Tag]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Monte Carlo Prediction is a Reinforcement Learning technique used to estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$ for a given policy $\pi$ by averaging the actual returns $G_t$ observed from complete episodes of interaction, without requiring any prior knowledge of the environment's transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or reward function $R(s, a, s')$.

**Model-free explanation (1 mark):** It is called "model-free" because the algorithm never needs to know or learn the environment's dynamics (the model). It learns purely from *sampled experience tuples* $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ — i.e., from interaction alone.

**Why complete episodes are required (1 mark):** The return $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ requires knowledge of all future rewards until termination. This is why MC methods are restricted to **episodic tasks** where each trial ends in a terminal state $S_T$.

---

### **Question A2: Differentiate between First-Visit MC and Every-Visit MC Prediction.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Tag]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|---|---|---|
| **Visits counted per episode** | Only the **first** time $s$ is visited in an episode | **Every** time $s$ is visited in an episode |
| **Update frequency** | One update per state per episode | Multiple updates possible per state per episode |
| **Unbiased estimator** | **Yes** — rigorous LLN proof | **Yes** — but proof is more complex |
| **Convergence** | Proven convergence to $V_\pi(s)$ | Proven convergence to $V_\pi(s)$ |
| **Mark allocation** | 1 mark for this distinction |

**Key conceptual difference (1 mark):** First-Visit MC treats each episode as contributing *one* sample for state $s$ (the first visit), while Every-Visit MC may contribute *multiple* samples if $s$ is revisited. Both converge, but First-Visit is preferred for theoretical analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): First-Visit MC Algorithm, Derivation, and Worked Example**

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper 2024 Style Tag]**

**(a) Derive the incremental update rule for First-Visit Monte Carlo Prediction starting from the sample mean definition. Explain each step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of the sample mean estimator (1 mark)**

For state $s$ visited $N$ times across episodes, the First-Visit MC estimate is:

$$\hat{V}_N(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)}$$

where $G_t^{(i)}$ is the return observed from the *first visit* of $s$ in episode $i$.

**Step 2: Write the estimate for the $(N+1)$-th visit (1 mark)**

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \sum_{i=1}^{N+1} G_t^{(i)}$$

**Step 3: Separate the new sample (1 mark)**

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)} \right]$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\sum_{i=1}^{N} G_t^{(i)} = N \cdot \hat{V}_N(s)$ (1 mark)**

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{1}{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} + N \cdot \hat{V}_N(s) \right]$$

**Step 5: Distribute and simplify (1 mark)**

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \frac{N}{N+1} \hat{V}_N(s) + \frac{1}{N+1} G_t^{(N+1)}$$

$$\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \hat{V}_N(s) - \frac{1}{N+1} \hat{V}_N(s) + \frac{1}{N+1} G_t^{(N+1)}$$

**Step 6: Final incremental form (2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{N+1}(s) = \hat{V}_N(s) + \alpha_{N+1} \left[ G_t^{(N+1)} - \hat{V}_N(s) \right], \quad \text{where } \alpha_{N+1} = \frac{1}{N+1}}$$

The bracketed term $[G_t - \hat{V}(s)]$ is the **MC error**, and $\alpha$ is the **step-size** (also called learning rate).

---

**(b) A Blackjack-style MDP has 4 observed episodes under policy $\pi$. Using the data below and $\gamma = 0.9$, compute the First-Visit MC estimate of $V_\pi(S_A)$ and $V_\pi(S_B)$. (7 marks)**

**Episode Data:**

| Episode | Trajectory |
|---|---|
| 1 | $S_A \xrightarrow{+2} S_B \xrightarrow{+5} S_C \xrightarrow{+1} S_T$ |
| 2 | $S_B \xrightarrow{+3} S_C \xrightarrow{+2} S_T$ |
| 3 | $S_A \xrightarrow{+1} S_B \xrightarrow{+4} S_C \xrightarrow{+0} S_T$ |
| 4 | $S_A \xrightarrow{+4} S_C \xrightarrow{+3} S_T$ |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute returns for Episode 1 (1 mark)**

$$G_0^{(1)} = 2 + 0.9(5) + 0.9^2(1) = 2 + 4.5 + 0.81 = 7.31$$

$$G_1^{(1)} = 5 + 0.9(1) = 5.9$$

**Step 2: Compute returns for Episode 3 (1 mark)**

$$G_0^{(3)} = 1 + 0.9(4) + 0.9^2(0) = 1 + 3.6 + 0 = 4.6$$

$$G_1^{(3)} = 4 + 0.9(0) = 4$$

**Step 3: Compute returns for Episode 4 (1 mark)**

$$G_0^{(4)} = 4 + 0.9(3) = 4 + 2.7 = 6.7$$

**Step 4: Identify first-visit returns for $S_A$ (1 mark)**

$S_A$ is the first state in Episodes 1, 3, 4.

$$\{G^{(1)}_0, G^{(3)}_0, G^{(4)}_0\} = \{7.31, 4.6, 6.7\}$$

**Step 5: Compute $V_\pi(S_A)$ (1 mark)**

$$V_\pi(S_A) = \frac{7.31 + 4.6 + 6.7}{3} = \frac{18.61}{3} \approx 6.20$$

**Step 6: Identify first-visit returns for $S_B$ (1 mark)**

$S_B$ is first visited at $t=1$ in Episode 1, at $t=0$ in Episode 2, and at $t=1$ in Episode 3.

$$\{G^{(1)}_1, G^{(2)}_0, G^{(3)}_1\} = \{5.9, 3 + 0.9(2), 4\} = \{5.9, 4.8, 4\}$$

**Step 7: Compute $V_\pi(S_B)$ (1 mark)**

$$V_\pi(S_B) = \frac{5.9 + 4.8 + 4}{3} = \frac{14.7}{3} = 4.9$$

**Final Answer:** $V_\pi(S_A) \approx 6.20$ and $V_\pi(S_B) = 4.9$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Convergence Properties and Comparison with DP — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Style Tag]**

**(a) State and explain the convergence theorem for First-Visit MC Prediction. Discuss the role of the Law of Large Numbers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theorem Statement (2 marks):** As the number of first visits to state $s$, $N(s) \to \infty$, the First-Visit MC estimate converges to the true value:

$$\hat{V}(s) \xrightarrow{a.s.} V_\pi(s) \quad \text{as } N(s) \to \infty$$

where $\xrightarrow{a.s.}$ denotes *almost sure convergence* (with probability 1).

**Role of LLN (2 marks):** The Law of Large Numbers guarantees that the *sample mean* of i.i.d. (or stationary-ergodic) random variables converges to the *expected value*. Since $\hat{V}(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum G_t^{(i)}$ is a sample mean, LLN directly applies, with the random variables being the observed returns $G_t^{(i)}$ under the policy $\pi$.

**Two key conditions (2 marks):**
1. **Stationarity under $\pi$**: The returns $G_t$ must be drawn from a fixed distribution, which holds when $\pi$ is fixed and the MDP is stationary.
2. **Infinite visits**: All states in $S$ must be visited infinitely often as $N \to \infty$. This is the **exploration assumption** — easily violated in practice and addressed later in GPI and $\epsilon$-greedy methods (Module 5).

**Practical implication (1 mark):** MC prediction is *asymptotically correct* but has *finite-sample variance*. Engineers must trade off sample size vs. computational cost.

---

**(b) Compare Monte Carlo Prediction with Dynamic Programming (DP) for policy evaluation. List two advantages and two disadvantages of MC over DP. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Monte Carlo | Dynamic Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment model** | Not required (model-free) | Requires full MDP model |
| **Sample type** | Sampled episodes | Full-width backup |
| **Computation** | Empirically, from data | Analytically, using $P, R$ |
| **Bias** | **Unbiased** | **Biased** (uses estimated $V'$) |
| **Variance** | **High** | **Zero** (no sampling) |
| **Task type** | Episodic only | Both episodic and continuing |
| **Convergence speed** | Slow (needs many episodes) | Fast (exploits structure) |

**Two Advantages of MC (4 marks, 2 each):**
1. **Model-free — works in unknown environments.** MC only needs observed transitions, so it can learn in systems where the transition probabilities are too complex to enumerate (e.g., robotics, game playing).
2. **Unbiased estimates.** MC estimates are unbiased under FVMC; DP's bootstrapping introduces propagation of initial bias through iterations.

**Two Disadvantages of MC (3 marks — 1.5 each):**
1. **High variance.** Each return $G_t$ is a noisy sample of the underlying distribution. Variance scales as $1/N$, so many episodes are required for tight estimates.
2. **Restricted to episodic tasks.** MC cannot update mid-episode (it needs the full return), making it unsuitable for continuing tasks without modification. DP, by contrast, works on both.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "first-visit" check** when computing returns — students often count every visit but call it First-Visit MC. This loses 1–2 marks per instance.
> 2. **Not terminating the return properly** — the return at the terminal time step $T$ is just $R_T$ (no further discount). The infinite sum collapses.
> 3. **Confusing $V(s)$ with $Q(s, a)$** — MC Prediction estimates $V_\pi(s)$. Estimating $Q_\pi(s, a)$ requires a separate algorithm (MC Control, Module 5).
> 4. **Using $\gamma$ incorrectly** — if $\gamma = 0$, then $G_t = R_{t+1}$ (immediate reward only). Always expand the discounted sum explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting LLN** — convergence proofs **must** invoke the Law of Large Numbers explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark for this reference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall!)**

- ☐ **MC Prediction = sample-mean of returns to estimate $V_\pi(s)$**
- ☐ **Model-free** — no knowledge of $P$ or $R$ required
- ☐ **Episodic tasks only** — needs terminal state $S_T$ to compute $G_t$
- ☐ **Two variants:** First-Visit (FVMC) and Every-Visit (EVMC) — both converge
- ☐ **Empirical return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ with $\gamma \in [0,1]$
- ☐ **Incremental update:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha [G_t - V(s)]$
- ☐ **Step-size:** $\alpha = 1/N(s)$ for unbiased convergence; constant $\alpha$ for non-stationary
- ☐ **Law of Large Numbers** is the *theoretical foundation* of MC convergence
- ☐ **Unbiased but high-variance** estimator (vs DP: biased, zero-variance)
- ☐ **Exploration assumption:** every state must be visited infinitely often
- ☐ **No bootstrapping** — MC waits for the actual return; TD bootstraps from $V(s')$
- ☐ **Use MC when:** environment model is unknown or too complex; episodic tasks with simulation possible
- ☐ **Avoid MC when:** real-time online updates needed (use TD); continuing (non-terminating) tasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Board Exam Mnemonic — "MC-PEDIA":**
> - **M** = Model-free
> - **C** = Complete episodes required
> - **P** = Prediction = estimating $V_\pi(s)$
> - **E** = Every-Visit & First-Visit variants
> - **D** = Discount factor $\gamma$ weights the return
> - **I** = Incremental update with prediction error $[G_t - V(s)]$
> - **A** = Averaging → Law of Large Numbers → convergence

**Module 3 Takeaway:** MC Prediction is the *empirical workhorse* of model-free RL — simple, unbiased, and powerful when episodes are cheap to simulate, but limited by variance and the episodic constraint. The **incremental form** is the bridge to TD methods you will study in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monte Carlo Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monte Carlo Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Monte Carlo (MC) Control** is a model-free, learning-based approach in Reinforcement Learning (RL) that seeks to discover an *optimal policy* through direct interaction with an unknown environment. Unlike Dynamic Programming (DP), it does **not** require knowledge of the transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or the reward function $R(s, a, s')$. Instead, MC Control estimates the *action-value function* $Q_\pi(s, a)$ by averaging the *empirical returns* observed across many sampled episodes, and then improves the policy greedily (or near-greedily) with respect to these estimates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT755 Module 3):**
> *Monte Carlo Control refers to the iterative procedure of (i) policy evaluation via sampled episode returns, and (ii) policy improvement by acting greedily or $\epsilon$-greedily on the learned action-value estimates $Q(s,a)$.* The goal is $\pi \rightarrow \pi^*$.

The general pseudocode structure as per the KTU-recommended textbook (Sutton & Barto, *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*) is:

> Initialize $\pi$ arbitrarily  
> Repeat:  
>   Generate an episode using $\pi$  
>   For each $(s, a)$ appearing in the episode:  
>     $N(s, a) \leftarrow N(s, a) + 1$  
>     $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \dfrac{1}{N(s,a)}\big(G_t - Q(s, a)\big)$  
>   Improve $\pi$ w.r.t $Q$ (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Slot-Machine Player

Imagine you walk into a casino with **100 different slot machines**, each with an unknown payout rate. You cannot open the machines, peek at their internals, or compute probabilities on paper. The only way to learn the best machine is to *play* them.

- **Episode Analogy**: Each "play session" (you sit, pull levers many times until you walk away) is an *episode*.
- **Return Analogy**: The total money you win in one session is the *return* $G_t$.
- **MC Control Analogy**: You keep a running note: "Machine #7 has averaged me **$4.20 per pull** so far." After many sessions, you always pull the *best-known* machine — but with a small probability you still try a random one (exploration), just in case a worse-looking machine is actually secretly great.
- **Convergence**: As sessions grow to infinity, your average estimate converges to the true expected payout, and you reliably exploit the best machine.

This is precisely what MC Control does in an MDP: the "machines" are the *state-action pairs*, the "money" is the *cumulative discounted reward*, and the "strategy" is the *policy* $\pi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from DP**: MC Control uses *sampling* and *empirical averaging*, whereas DP uses a *known model* and *bootstrapping* (guessing a guess). MC Control requires the task to be *episodic* (finite-horizon rollouts with terminal states).

## 1.3 Fundamental Components

The MC Control loop hinges on three sub-procedures, each formally examined in later sections:

1. **MC Prediction** — estimating $Q_\pi$ from sample returns.
2. **Policy Improvement** — making $\pi$ greedy/$\epsilon$-greedy w.r.t $Q_\pi$.
3. **Policy Evaluation ↔ Improvement Iteration** — the Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI) cycle that drives $\pi \rightarrow \pi^*$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MC Control Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The full MC Control algorithm is grounded in **Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)**. Each iteration performs two phases:

### Phase A — Policy Evaluation (MC Prediction on $Q$)
We need $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ for the *current* policy $\pi$. Because we don't have a model, we estimate this expectation using the *Law of Large Numbers* over observed returns.

- **Why?** The true expectation is unknown; sample averages are unbiased estimators.
- **How?** For every visit to $(s, a)$ in an episode, record the actual return $G_t$ that followed. The running mean converges to $Q_\pi(s,a)$.

### Phase B — Policy Improvement
With $Q_\pi$ approximated, we construct a new policy that is better than (or equal to) $\pi$.

- **Why?** Greedy improvement guarantees monotonic policy advancement by the *Policy Improvement Theorem*.
- **How?** Set $\pi'(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s, a)$. But *pure greedy* traps us — we never revisit under-sampled actions. So we use **$\epsilon$-soft** policies (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy).

### The Cycle
Evaluation and improvement alternate, each making the other more accurate, until convergence to the optimal $\pi^*$.

## 2.2 Two Variants: On-Policy vs Off-Policy MC Control

| Property | On-Policy MC Control | Off-Policy MC Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Behavior Policy** | The same policy $\pi$ being improved | A separate behavior policy $b$ used for exploration |
| **Target Policy** | $\pi$ | $\pi$ (deterministic, greedy) |
| **Exploration Strategy** | Soft (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) | Guaranteed by behavior policy $b$ |
| **Estimator Used** | Direct sample average of $G_t$ | Importance-sampled returns $G_t \cdot \prod \frac{\pi(A_i \mid S_i)}{b(A_i \mid S_i)}$ |
| **Variance** | Low | High (importance weights amplify variance) |
| **KTU Weightage** | **High** — asked in most model solutions | **Medium** — theoretical questions |

## 2.3 The Greedy-in-the-Limit with Infinite Exploration (GLIE) Theorem

For MC Control to converge to the optimal action-value $q_*$, the policy must satisfy the **GLIE conditions**:

1. **Infinite Exploration**: Every state-action pair $(s,a)$ is visited infinitely many times as $N \to \infty$.
2. **Convergence to Greedy**: The policy converges to a greedy policy w.r.t $Q_\pi$, i.e., $\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \mid s) = \mathbf{1}[a = \arg\max_a Q_k(s,a)]$.

A canonical $\epsilon$-greedy decay schedule satisfying GLIE is:

$$
\epsilon_k = \frac{1}{k}
$$

where $k$ is the episode index. This makes the policy increasingly greedy while still exploring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem to Memorize (GLIE MC Control)**:
> *If the policy sequence $\{\pi_k\}$ satisfies GLIE, and the action-value estimates are updated as $Q_{k+1}(s,a) = Q_k(s,a) + \alpha_k \big(G_t - Q_k(s,a)\big)$ with $\sum \alpha_k = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_k^2 < \infty$, then $Q_k \to q_*$ and $\pi_k \to \pi_*$ with probability 1.*

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and must be committed to memory verbatim.

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discounted Return | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$, dimensionless |
| 2 | Action-Value Definition | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\big[G_t \mid S_t=s, A_t=a\big]$ | Expectation over trajectory distribution |
| 3 | Bellman Optimality (for Q) | $Q_*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_*(S_{t+1}, a') \big]$ | Used in DP, not MC directly |
| 4 | Incremental MC Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\big(G_t - Q(s,a)\big)$ | $\alpha = 1/N(s,a)$ for sample mean |
| 5 | $\epsilon$-greedy Action | $\pi(a \mid s) = \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert} + (1-\epsilon)\mathbf{1}[a = a^*]$ | $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ |
| 6 | GLIE Decay | $\epsilon_k = 1/k$ | Ensures convergence to $\pi_*$ |
| 7 | Importance Sampling Ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \dfrac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Off-policy only |
| 8 | Ordinary Importance Sampling | $Q(s,a) = \dfrac{\sum_{t} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} G_t}{\sum_{t} \rho_{t:T(t)-1}}$ | Unbiased, high variance |
| 9 | Weighted Importance Sampling | $Q(s,a) = \dfrac{\sum_{t} \rho_{t:T(t)-1} G_t}{\vert \{\text{visits to }s,a\}\vert}$ | Biased, low variance |
| 10 | Convergence Bound | $P\big(\vert Q_k - Q_*\vert > \epsilon\big) \to 0$ | Almost-sure (w.p. 1) convergence |

> **Engineering Utility**: MC Control underpins many real-world systems including **robotics policy search**, **game-playing AIs** (e.g., early AlphaGo rollouts), **recommender systems** A/B testing, and **adaptive control** in industrial automation where environmental dynamics are not analytically tractable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: From Return Definition to the MC Update Rule

We begin with the formal definition of the return and show how it leads naturally to an *incremental* update suitable for online learning.

### Step 1 — The Return

The discounted return starting from time $t$ in an episode is:

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T
$$

This is simply the sum of future rewards, each discounted by powers of $\gamma$. The recursion is:

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}
$$

with terminal condition $G_T = 0$.

### Step 2 — The Target: $Q_\pi(s,a)$ as an Expectation

The true action-value of $(s,a)$ under policy $\pi$ is the expected return:

$$
Q_\pi(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_t \mid S_t = s,\; A_t = a \,\big]
$$

We cannot compute this expectation in closed form without a model. Hence, we estimate it via the **empirical mean** of returns seen for $(s,a)$ across episodes.

### Step 3 — Sample-Average Estimator

Suppose $(s,a)$ has been visited $n$ times, producing returns $G^{(1)}, G^{(2)}, \dots, G^{(n)}$. The unbiased sample-average estimator is:

$$
\hat{Q}_n(s,a) \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} G^{(i)}
$$

### Step 4 — Incremental Reformulation

Rather than storing all $n$ returns, we can update $\hat{Q}$ in a single-line recursion. Let $\hat{Q}_{n+1}$ be the estimate after the $(n+1)$-th visit:

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G^{(i)}
$$

Separate the contribution of the new sample $G^{(n+1)}$ from the old sum $\sum_{i=1}^n G^{(i)} = n \hat{Q}_n$:

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\Big(\,n\hat{Q}_n + G^{(n+1)}\,\Big)
$$

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{n}{n+1}\hat{Q}_n + \frac{1}{n+1}G^{(n+1)}
$$

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \hat{Q}_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\Big(G^{(n+1)} - \hat{Q}_n\Big)
$$

### Step 5 — Generalize to Step-Size $\alpha$

Replacing $1/(n+1)$ with an arbitrary step-size $\alpha_n \in (0,1]$ yields the *general* incremental MC update:

$$
\boxed{\,Q(s,a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s,a) + \alpha_n \big(\,G_t - Q(s,a)\,\big)\,}
$$

The error term $(G_t - Q(s,a))$ is the **innovation** — the difference between the observed return and the current estimate. By the **Robbins-Monro conditions** ($\sum \alpha_n = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_n^2 < \infty$), this stochastic approximation converges almost surely to $Q_\pi(s,a)$.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Why $\epsilon$-Greedy Guarantees Policy Improvement

We prove the *Policy Improvement Theorem for $\epsilon$-soft policies*, the engine of MC Control.

### Step 1 — Define the $\epsilon$-soft Update

For action set $\mathcal{A}$ with $\vert \mathcal{A}\vert$ actions, the $\epsilon$-greedy policy is:

$$
\pi'(a \mid s) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon + \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}, & a = a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\pi(s,a) \\
\dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}, & a \neq a^*
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the New Value Function

The value of $\pi'$ at state $s$ is, by definition:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi'(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

Split the sum into the greedy action $a^*$ and the rest:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; \pi'(a^* \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a^*) + \sum_{a \neq a^*} \pi'(a \mid s)\, Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the $\epsilon$-greedy Probabilities

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; \left(1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}\right) Q_\pi(s, a^*) + \sum_{a \neq a^*} \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert} Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

Factor $\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}$ out of the non-greedy terms:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; \left(1 - \epsilon + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}\right) Q_\pi(s, a^*) + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert} \sum_{a \neq a^*} Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

### Step 4 — Add and Subtract to Compare

Add and subtract $\frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert} Q_\pi(s, a^*)$ to expose the comparison:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; (1 - \epsilon) Q_\pi(s, a^*) + \frac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert} \sum_{a} Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

### Step 5 — Show $V_{\pi'}(s) \geq V_\pi(s)$

Since $a^* = \arg\max_a Q_\pi(s, a)$, we know $Q_\pi(s, a^*) \geq Q_\pi(s, a)$ for all $a$. Hence the average $\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}\sum_a Q_\pi(s, a) \leq Q_\pi(s, a^*)$ implies:

$$
V_{\pi'}(s) \;=\; (1 - \epsilon) \underbrace{Q_\pi(s, a^*)}_{\geq\, \text{avg}} + \epsilon \cdot \underbrace{\text{avg}}_{\leq\, Q_\pi(s, a^*)}
$$

$$
\geq \; (1 - \epsilon)\,Q_\pi(s, a^*) + \epsilon \cdot Q_\pi(s, a^*) \;=\; Q_\pi(s, a^*) \;\geq\; V_\pi(s)
$$

Thus $\pi' \geq \pi$ pointwise — policy improvement is guaranteed for any $\epsilon \in (0,1]$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Control Strategies

| Criterion | DP Control | MC Control (On-Policy) | TD Control (Sarsa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requires Model? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Needs Episodes? | No | **Yes (episodic only)** | No (works on infinite horizons) |
| Bootstrapping? | Yes | No | Yes |
| Bias | Zero (uses true $P, R$) | Zero (unbiased samples) | Some (uses estimate) |
| Variance | Zero | **High** | Lower than MC |
| Convergence Speed | Fast (analytical) | Slow (needs many samples) | Medium |
| KTU Module 3 Coverage | Module 2 | **Module 3 (focus)** | Module 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: The question *"Compare Monte Carlo Control with Dynamic Programming Control"* appeared in KTU 2023 supplementary exams. The table above is your model answer skeleton.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level MC Control Architecture

The following block diagram shows the functional flow of the Monte Carlo Control algorithm. Each block represents a computational sub-routine, and the arrows represent data dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Initialize pi arbitrarily Q s a for all s a returns history empty] --> B0[Set epsilon exploration rate schedule e.g. 1 over k]
    B0 --> C0[Episode Counter k k 1]
    C0 --> D0[Generate Episode Follow pi and epsilon greedy for t 0 1 ... T]
    D0 --> E0[Observe Trajectory S0 A0 R1 S1 A1 R2 ... ST]
    E0 --> F0[Compute Returns Gt for t T 1 down to 0]
    F0 --> G0{First Visit or Every Visit MC}
    G0 -- First Visit --> H0[Record Gt for first occurrence of s a pair]
    G0 -- Every Visit --> I0[Record Gt for every occurrence of s a pair]
    H0 --> J0[Update Q s a Q s a plus alpha times Gt minus Q s a]
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Decay epsilon epsilon k 1 over k plus 1]
    K0 --> L0[Improve Policy pi becomes epsilon greedy w.r.t. Q]
    L0 --> M0{Convergence Check max delta Q less than theta}
    M0 -- No --> C0
    M0 -- Yes --> N0[Output Optimal Policy pi star and Q star]
    N0 --> O0[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (GPI Perspective)

The diagram below abstracts the **Generalized Policy Iteration (GPI)** view of MC Control — alternating policy evaluation and improvement, each making progress against the other.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EVAL[Policy Evaluation Phase]
        A1[Sample Episodes using current policy pi] --> A2[Compute empirical returns Gt for each visit]
        A2 --> A3[Update Q hat s a by averaging returns]
    end
    subgraph IMP[Policy Improvement Phase]
        B1[Read Q hat s a] --> B2[Compute a star equals argmax a Q hat s a]
        B2 --> B3[Construct epsilon greedy policy pi new]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    B3 --> A1
    A3 --> C1[Convergence to Q star and pi star]
    B3 --> C1
```

## 4.3 On-Policy vs Off-Policy Architecture (Functional Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ONPOL[On Policy MC Control]
        O1[Behavior Policy pi] --> O2[Generate Episode]
        O2 --> O3[Compute Return Gt]
        O3 --> O4[Direct Update of Q under pi]
        O4 --> O5[Make pi epsilon greedy]
        O5 --> O1
    end
    subgraph OFFPOL[Off Policy MC Control]
        F1[Behavior Policy b exploratory] --> F2[Generate Episode]
        F2 --> F3[Compute Return Gt]
        F3 --> F4[Compute Importance Weights rho]
        F4 --> F5[Update Q of target policy pi using weighted returns]
        F5 --> F6[Improve pi greedily w.r.t Q]
        F6 --> F1
    end
```

## 4.4 Schematic: GLIE Convergence Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Episode k equals 1] --> S1[High epsilon large exploration]
    S1 --> S2[Q estimates noisy far from Q star]
    S2 --> S3[Episode k equals k mid]
    S3 --> S4[Moderate epsilon balanced explore exploit]
    S4 --> S5[Q estimates approaching Q star]
    S5 --> S6[Episode k approaches infinity]
    S6 --> S7[epsilon approaches 0 full exploitation]
    S7 --> S8[Q equals Q star pi equals pi star OPTIMAL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: All four schematics are topologically equivalent representations of the same idea — *GPI with stochastic returns*. The architecture is intentionally redundant across views so a student can recognize the algorithm regardless of how the examiner phrases the question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Module 3)**
> Common mistakes students make on Monte Carlo Control questions — *avoid these to secure full marks:*
> 1. **Confusing $V_\pi$ with $Q_\pi$** — MC Control specifically works on *action-values* $Q(s,a)$ because the model-free setting does not let us compute $\arg\max_a$ from $V(s)$ alone. If the question says "MC Control," write $Q$, not $V$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Law of Large Numbers justification** — simply stating "we average returns" is incomplete. You must write that the *sample mean converges to the expectation* by LLN.
> 3. **Skipping the GLIE conditions** — when asked "does MC Control converge?", the answer is **only under GLIE**. Writing "yes, it converges" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing First-Visit and Every-Visit MC** — both converge but have different variance properties. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Mixing up on-policy and off-policy formulas** — ordinary vs weighted importance sampling is a high-value KTU 2024 question.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Differentiate between First-Visit and Every-Visit Monte Carlo methods for estimating $V_\pi(s)$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | First-Visit MC | Every-Visit MC |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Average returns only after the *first* time step $t$ in an episode that state $s$ is visited | Average returns after *every* occurrence of $s$ in the episode |
| Unbiasedness | Unbiased estimator of $V_\pi(s)$ | Unbiased estimator of $V_\pi(s)$ |
| Variance | Higher (fewer samples) | Lower (more samples) |
| Implementation | Needs to track if $s$ was already seen | Tracks all visits |
| Convergence | Converges as $N \to \infty$ | Converges as $N \to \infty$ |

*(3 marks awarded for correct tabulated comparison and stating both are unbiased.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the GLIE conditions for the convergence of on-policy Monte Carlo Control.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration (GLIE)** conditions are:

1. **Infinite Exploration**: Every state-action pair $(s, a)$ must be visited infinitely often as the number of episodes $k \to \infty$.
2. **Convergence to Greedy Policy**: The policy $\pi_k$ must converge to a greedy policy with respect to $Q_k$ in the limit:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} \pi_k(a \mid s) \;=\; \mathbf{1}\big[\,a = \arg\max_{a'} Q_k(s, a')\,\big]
$$

A simple $\epsilon$-greedy schedule satisfying GLIE is $\epsilon_k = 1/k$. Under these conditions, on-policy MC Control converges to the optimal action-value $q_*$ with probability 1.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each condition, 0.5 for the decay schedule / convergence statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Monte Carlo Control algorithm in detail. Discuss how policy evaluation and policy improvement are carried out using sample episodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the incremental update rule for the action-value function $Q(s,a)$ from first principles. Show that under Robbins-Monro conditions, this update converges to $Q_\pi(s,a)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Monte Carlo Control is a model-free, iterative algorithm that discovers an optimal policy $\pi^*$ by interacting with the environment and learning from sampled episodes.

**Step 1 — Initialization**: Initialize an arbitrary stochastic policy $\pi_0$ (e.g., uniform random) and initialize the action-value function $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all $(s, a)$ pairs. Set returns list $Returns(s, a) = \emptyset$.

**Step 2 — Episode Generation**: For each episode $k$, generate a trajectory $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, \dots, S_T$ by following the current $\epsilon$-greedy policy derived from $Q$.

**Step 3 — Policy Evaluation (MC Prediction)**: For each time step $t$ in the episode:
- Compute the return $G_t = \sum_{i=t+1}^{T} \gamma^{i-t-1} R_i$
- Append $G_t$ to $Returns(S_t, A_t)$
- Update the action-value estimate as the sample mean: $Q(S_t, A_t) = \text{average}(Returns(S_t, A_t))$

**Step 4 — Policy Improvement**: Construct a new $\epsilon$-greedy policy:

$$
\pi_k(a \mid s) \;=\; \begin{cases}
1 - \epsilon + \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}, & a = \arg\max_a Q(s,a) \\
\dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}\vert}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Iteration**: Repeat steps 2–4. As $k \to \infty$ and under GLIE, $Q \to Q_*$ and $\pi \to \pi^*$.

The interaction between evaluation and improvement is the **Generalized Policy Iteration** paradigm: each makes the other more accurate. Evaluation is unbiased (sample average = expectation by LLN), and improvement is guaranteed (by the $\epsilon$-soft policy improvement theorem).

*(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–4, 1 for GPI explanation, 2 for theoretical justification.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

We start with the definition of action-value:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \big[\, G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \,\big]
$$

Let the running estimate after the $n$-th visit be $\hat{Q}_n$, and the $(n+1)$-th observed return be $G^{(n+1)}$:

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} G^{(i)}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\hat{Q}_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{n+1}\Big(\,n \hat{Q}_n + G^{(n+1)}\,\Big) \;=\; \hat{Q}_n + \frac{1}{n+1}\Big(G^{(n+1)} - \hat{Q}_n\Big)
$$

Generalize $\frac{1}{n+1}$ to a step-size $\alpha_n$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{n+1}(s, a) \;=\; Q_n(s, a) + \alpha_n \big(G_t - Q_n(s, a)\big)\,}
$$

The error $G_t - Q_n(s,a)$ is the **innovation**. By the **Robbins-Monro theorem** for stochastic approximation, if

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n \;=\; \infty \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha_n^2 \;<\; \infty
$$

then $Q_n(s, a) \to Q_\pi(s, a)$ with probability 1. The first condition ensures the estimate never stops moving (can reach any target), and the second ensures diminishing noise (convergence is stable). The choice $\alpha_n = 1/n$ satisfies both.

*(7 marks: 1 each for setup steps, 2 for incremental algebra, 2 for Robbins-Monro statement, 1 for $\alpha_n = 1/n$ verification.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** What is the difference between on-policy and off-policy Monte Carlo Control? Explain the role of importance sampling in off-policy methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a deterministic off-policy setup, derive the **ordinary importance sampling** estimator of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ using the behavior policy $b$. State its bias and variance properties. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**On-Policy MC Control** uses a *single* policy $\pi$ for both generating episodes and being improved. Exploration is incorporated directly into $\pi$ (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy). The target and behavior policies are identical, so no correction is needed — returns are used as-is.

**Off-Policy MC Control** separates the *behavior policy* $b$ (used to generate exploratory data) from the *target policy* $\pi$ (the deterministic greedy policy being learned). This decoupling allows the agent to learn the optimal policy while exploring with a more aggressive behavior policy. The key challenge: returns observed under $b$ are biased estimators of $Q_\pi$. The solution is **importance sampling**.

**Importance Sampling** corrects the distributional mismatch by weighting each return by the ratio of the probability of the observed trajectory under $\pi$ versus under $b$:

$$
\rho_{t:T-1} \;=\; \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{b(A_k \mid S_k)}
$$

Intuitively, if a trajectory is *more likely* under $\pi$ than under $b$, we *up-weight* its return; if *less likely*, we *down-weight* it. This re-weights the empirical distribution back to the target policy's distribution.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for on-policy definition, 1.5 for off-policy definition, 2 for importance sampling concept, 2 for the weight formula and intuition.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given a set of episodes generated by behavior policy $b$, the **ordinary importance sampling** estimator of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ is:

$$
Q_\pi(s, a) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{t\,:\,S_t=s,\,A_t=a} \rho_{t:T(t)-1}\, G_t}{\sum_{t\,:\,S_t=s,\,A_t=a} \rho_{t:T(t)-1}}
$$

**Derivation of unbiasedness**: Each trajectory $\tau$ has probability $p_b(\tau)$ under $b$ and $p_\pi(\tau)$ under $\pi$. The importance weight is $\rho(\tau) = p_\pi(\tau)/p_b(\tau)$. The estimator computes:

$$
\mathbb{E}_b\!\left[\frac{\rho(\tau) G(\tau)}{\mathbb{E}_b[\rho]}\right] \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{E}_b[\rho(\tau) G(\tau)]}{\mathbb{E}_b[\rho(\tau)]} \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{E}_\pi[G(\tau)]}{1} \;=\; Q_\pi(s, a)
$$

since $\mathbb{E}_b[\rho(\tau) \cdot f(\tau)] = \int p_b(\tau) \cdot \frac{p_\pi(\tau)}{p_b(\tau)} f(\tau)\, d\tau = \mathbb{E}_\pi[f(\tau)]$ and $\mathbb{E}_b[\rho(\tau)] = 1$.

**Properties**:
- **Bias**: **Unbiased** estimator of $Q_\pi(s, a)$ in expectation.
- **Variance**: **High and unbounded** — the importance weights can be extreme (some trajectories under $b$ have very low probability under $\pi$ and vice versa, making $\rho$ vary wildly).
- **Limitation**: The denominator can approach zero, causing numerical instability.

In contrast, **weighted importance sampling** trades unbiasedness for lower variance:

$$
Q_\pi(s,a) \;=\; \frac{\sum_t \rho_{t:T(t)-1} G_t}{N(s, a)}
$$

where $N(s, a)$ is the visit count. This estimator is **biased** (unless $b = \pi$) but has **bounded variance**.

*(7 marks: 2 for the formula, 3 for the unbiasedness derivation using expectation swap, 2 for the bias/variance properties.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **MC Control Definition**: Model-free, sample-based policy optimization using empirical return averages.
- ✅ **Core Update**: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\big(G_t - Q(s,a)\big)$ — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Return Formula**: $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots$
- ✅ **Why $Q$ and not $V$**: Without a model, we cannot compute $\arg\max_a$ from $V(s)$ alone — so MC Control learns $Q(s,a)$.
- ✅ **GLIE Conditions**: (1) All $(s,a)$ visited infinitely often, (2) $\pi_k \to$ greedy policy. Use $\epsilon_k = 1/k$.
- ✅ **$\epsilon$-greedy Policy**: $\pi(a^* \mid s) = 1 - \epsilon + \epsilon/|\mathcal{A}|$, others get $\epsilon/|\mathcal{A}|$.
- ✅ **Convergence Guarantee**: Under GLIE + Robbins-Monro, $Q_k \to Q_*$ and $\pi_k \to \pi_*$ with probability 1.
- ✅ **First-Visit vs Every-Visit**: Both unbiased; Every-Visit has lower variance, First-Visit is simpler.
- ✅ **On-Policy vs Off-Policy**: On-policy uses same $\pi$ for behavior and target; Off-policy separates them and uses importance sampling.
- ✅ **Importance Sampling Ratio**: $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_k \pi(A_k \mid S_k) / b(A_k \mid S_k)$.
- ✅ **Ordinary IS**: Unbiased, high variance. **Weighted IS**: Biased, low variance. *Always quote both.*
- ✅ **MC vs DP**: MC needs episodes, has no model assumption, no bootstrapping. DP needs model, has zero variance, uses bootstrapping.
- ✅ **GLIE Theorem Statement**: Required verbatim for full marks — *"under GLIE, $Q_k$ converges to $q_*$ with probability 1."*
- ✅ **Engineering Apps**: Robotics, game AI, recommender systems, adaptive industrial control.
- ✅ **Robbins-Monro Conditions**: $\sum \alpha_n = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha_n^2 < \infty$ — both must hold for convergence.

> **Final Exam Advice**: When a question says "explain MC Control with proof of convergence," your answer **must** contain four elements: (1) the update rule, (2) sample-average justification via LLN, (3) the $\epsilon$-soft policy improvement theorem statement, and (4) the GLIE convergence theorem. Missing any one loses 2–3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
