topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Off-Policy Prediction and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Off-Policy Prediction and Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Off-Policy Learning**
> **Off-Policy learning** is a paradigm in Reinforcement Learning (RL) where the policy being *evaluated* or *improved* (called the **target policy**, $\pi$) is *different* from the policy used to *generate behavior* (called the **behavior policy**, $\mu$). This decoupling allows an agent to learn about an optimal policy while following an exploratory or sub-optimal policy, and even learn from data generated by other agents or demonstrations.

**Off-Policy Prediction** = Estimating $V^\pi(s)$ or $Q^\pi(s,a)$ for a target policy $\pi$ using trajectories sampled from a different behavior policy $\mu$.

**Off-Policy Control** = Finding the optimal policy $\pi^*$ by learning about it from data generated by a (possibly exploratory) behavior policy $\mu$.

### Intuitive Analogy: "The Medical Student Watching Surgery"

Imagine a **medical student** (the target policy $\pi$) learning the *optimal* way to perform surgery by *watching* an experienced surgeon (the behavior policy $\mu$) operate on patients. The student is NOT actually performing the surgery (so no risk to patients), but is learning the best actions by observing what the expert does and the outcomes. Occasionally, the expert might try a slightly different technique to explore — but the student is still learning the *best* technique.

- **Behavior policy** $\mu$ = the senior surgeon (exploratory, may not be perfect).
- **Target policy** $\pi$ = the optimal policy the student is trying to learn.
- **Off-policy learning** = the student learns the *best* approach from sub-optimal demonstrations.
- **Importance Sampling** = the student re-weights each observation based on *how likely* that exact sequence would have occurred under the optimal policy.

> [!NOTE] **Why Off-Policy Matters in Engineering**
> In real-world engineering applications like **robotics**, **autonomous driving**, and **industrial process control**, we cannot let an untrained agent explore dangerously. Off-policy methods let us learn optimal control from safe, recorded human demonstrations — this is foundational to **Imitation Learning** and **Offline RL**.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Target Policy | $\pi$ | Policy being *learned / improved* |
| Behavior Policy | $\mu$ | Policy *generating the data* |
| Coverage | $\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \Rightarrow \mu(a \mid s) > 0$ | Behavior policy must visit every action the target might take |
| Importance Sampling Ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1}$ | Re-weighting factor correcting distribution mismatch |
| On-Policy | $\pi = \mu$ | Same policy for acting and learning |
| Off-Policy | $\pi \neq \mu$ | Different policies for acting and learning |

> [!TIP] **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **On-policy**: "Learn by doing what you actually do." (e.g., SARSA)
> - **Off-policy**: "Learn the best, even if you do something else." (e.g., Q-learning)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Central Problem: Distribution Mismatch

When the behavior policy $\mu$ and the target policy $\pi$ differ, the *distribution* of states and rewards encountered follows $\mu$, not $\pi$. We need a mathematical mechanism to *re-weight* samples so they appear as if they were drawn from $\pi$.

## 2.2 Importance Sampling — The Mathematical Bridge

The **importance sampling ratio** for a trajectory from time $t$ to $T-1$ is:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} \doteq \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, / \, \mu(A_k \mid S_k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Geometric Interpretation**
> $\rho_{t:T-1}$ is the *relative probability* of the entire trajectory under the target policy versus the behavior policy. It tells us **how much to scale the observed return** so that the expectation under $\mu$ equals the expectation under $\pi$.

### Two Variants of Importance Sampling

**A) Ordinary Importance Sampling (OIS)**

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \rho_{t:T-1} \, G_t \,\big|\, S_t = s \right]$$

The estimator is **unbiased** but suffers from **high variance** (a single unlikely trajectory can dominate the estimate).

**B) Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS)**

$$V^\pi(s) = \frac{\mathbb{E}\left[ \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \, G_t \,\big|\, S_t = s \right]}{\mathbb{E}\left[ \rho_{t:T(t)-1} \,\big|\, S_t = s \right]}$$

The estimator is **biased** (for finite samples) but has **dramatically lower variance** — generally preferred in practice.

## 2.3 Off-Policy Monte Carlo Prediction (Incremental)

Incremental update rule using weighted importance sampling:

$$V_{n+1}(S_t) = V_n(S_t) + \frac{W_{t:T(t)-1}}{C_{n+1}(S_t)} \left[ G_t - V_n(S_t) \right]$$

where $W_{t:T(t)-1}$ is the trajectory-level importance ratio and $C_n$ is a running sum of weights.

## 2.4 Off-Policy TD Prediction — The Off-Policy TD(0) Update

TD methods avoid the explosive variance of MC by bootstrapping with a single step. The off-policy TD(0) update with $n$-step return is:

$$V_{n+1}(S_t) = V_n(S_t) + \alpha \, \rho_{t:t+n-1} \left[ G_{t:t+n} - V_n(S_t) \right]$$

where $\rho_{t:t+n-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$.

> [!NOTE] **Advantage of TD over MC**
> TD only multiplies by the importance ratio for **as many steps as we bootstrap** (usually 1), not the entire trajectory. This is why off-policy TD is **far more practical** than off-policy MC in real applications.

## 2.5 Off-Policy Control: Q-Learning

Q-learning is the most famous off-policy control algorithm. It learns the **optimal action-value function** $Q^*$ directly, independent of the policy being followed.

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

> [!NOTE] **Why Q-learning is Off-Policy**
> The $\max_a$ inside the target is taken *as if* we were following the greedy target policy, while the data comes from the (possibly exploratory) behavior policy. The greedy max effectively computes the **expected value under the target policy** without sampling — no importance sampling ratio needed!

## 2.6 Expected SARSA — A Generalized View

$$\begin{aligned}
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow & \, Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \Big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \mid S_{t+1}] \\
& - Q(S_t, A_t) \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

Expected SARSA uses the **expectation** over next actions (computed analytically) rather than a sample or max. By varying the policy in the expectation, it interpolates smoothly between on-policy and off-policy — sometimes called an "**on-policy/off-policy hybrid**."

## 2.7 Double Q-Learning — Curing Maximization Bias

Standard Q-learning suffers from a **systematic overestimation** called **maximization bias**: the $\max$ operator in the update selects the same action that *also* has the highest *sampling error*, biasing upward.

Double Q-Learning uses **two independent Q-tables** $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Update } Q_1: \quad Q_1(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow & \, Q_1(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \Big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_2(S_{t+1}, a^*) - Q_1(S_t, A_t) \Big] \\
\text{where } a^* = & \arg\max_a Q_1(S_{t+1}, a)
\end{aligned}$$

Each update uses *one* table to choose the action and the *other* to evaluate it — **decoupling selection from evaluation** to eliminate positive bias.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Reference — KTU ESE Module 3**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Importance Sampling Ratio | $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ | Geometric product of action probabilities |
| 2 | OIS Estimator | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}[\rho_{t:T-1} G_t]$ | Unbiased, **high variance** |
| 3 | WIS Estimator | $V^\pi(s) = \frac{\mathbb{E}[\rho_{t:T-1} G_t]}{\mathbb{E}[\rho_{t:T-1}]}$ | Biased, **low variance** |
| 4 | Off-policy TD(0) | $V \leftarrow V + \alpha \rho_t [R + \gamma V(S') - V(S)]$ | One-step importance correction |
| 5 | Q-Learning | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | **Off-policy**, model-free |
| 6 | Expected SARSA | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [R + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$ | Smooth on/off-policy blend |
| 7 | Double Q-Learning | $Q_1(s,a) \leftarrow Q_1(s,a) + \alpha [R + \gamma Q_2(s', \arg\max_{a'} Q_1(s',a')) - Q_1(s,a)]$ | Eliminates **maximization bias** |
| 8 | Coverage Assumption | $\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \Rightarrow \mu(a \mid s) > 0$ | Required for finite importance ratio |
| 9 | Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Standard in finite/infinite horizon MDPs |
| 10 | Learning Rate | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ | Robbins-Monro: $\sum \alpha = \infty$, $\sum \alpha^2 < \infty$ |

> [!TIP] **Real-World Engineering Use**
> - **Autonomous Vehicles**: Train the optimal policy from logged human driving data (offline RL / off-policy).
> - **Healthcare**: Recommend treatments by learning from observational medical records where the doctor's policy $\mu$ ≠ the optimal $\pi$.
> - **Industrial Robotics**: Reuse historical trajectory data collected under old controllers to train a new, safer controller.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Importance Sampling Ratio

**Goal**: Show why $\rho_{t:T-1}$ correctly re-weights returns.

The probability of a trajectory $\tau = (S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}, \ldots, S_T)$ under a policy $\pi$ is:

$$\Pr\{\tau \mid \pi\} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

The ratio of trajectory probabilities under $\pi$ vs $\mu$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr\{\tau \mid \pi\}}{\Pr\{\tau \mid \mu\}} & = \frac{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \mu(A_k \mid S_k) \, p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)} \\
& = \frac{\cancel{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)} \cdot \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\cancel{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)} \cdot \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \mu(A_k \mid S_k)} \\
& = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)} \\
& = \rho_{t:T-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation step-by-step:**

1. The transition dynamics $p$ appear identically in numerator and denominator — they **cancel out**.
2. What remains is the product of *policy probabilities* for each action taken.
3. This product $\rho_{t:T-1}$ is the trajectory-level importance ratio.
4. Therefore, for any function $f(\tau)$: $\mathbb{E}_\tau[f(\tau) \mid \pi] = \mathbb{E}_\tau[\rho_{t:T-1} f(\tau) \mid \mu]$.

Choosing $f(\tau) = G_t$ (the return) gives the OIS estimator.

## 3.2 Derivation: Q-Learning as Off-Policy Expected SARSA

**Goal**: Show that Q-learning is a special case of Expected SARSA, hence automatically off-policy.

The general Expected SARSA target is:

$$Y_t^{\text{ESARSA}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

Now, set the target policy to the **greedy policy**:

$$\pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a' = \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_t^{\text{Q-learn}} & = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \left[ \mathbb{1}\left\{a' = \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)\right\} \right] Q(S_{t+1}, a') \\
& = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q\!\left(S_{t+1}, \arg\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')\right) \\
& = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')
\end{aligned}$$

This is **exactly** the Q-learning target. So Q-learning evaluates the expected value under the *greedy* target policy without needing importance sampling — the max operation **absorbs the expectation**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why this matters**
> Q-learning is off-policy *by construction*. The behavior policy $\mu$ (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) only governs data collection. The target (greedy) policy is implicit in the update — no importance ratio needed because we never *sample* a target action; we take an expectation analytically.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: On-Policy vs Off-Policy

| Aspect | On-Policy (SARSA) | Off-Policy (Q-Learning) | Off-Policy (WIS-MC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample efficiency | Lower (data tied to current policy) | **Higher** (reuses any data) | High (reuses trajectories) |
| Variance | Low | Low | **Very high** (ratio explosion) |
| Bias | Unbiased | Slight overestimation bias | WIS: biased, OIS: unbiased |
| Exploration handling | Built-in (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) | Must compare greedy vs behavior | Must use coverage assumption |
| Convergence | Yes, to $Q^\pi$ for $\pi$ followed | Yes, to $Q^*$ under conditions | Yes, to $V^\pi$ |
| Practical stability | High | High (TD), but max-bias issue | Low (variance) |
| Computation | Per-step: $O(1)$ | Per-step: $O(\vert\mathcal{A}\vert)$ for $\max$ | Per-trajectory: product of ratios |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Importance Sampling

**Setup**: Consider two-step trajectory under $\mu$: $(S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2)$.

Given:

$$\mu(A_0 \mid S_0) = 0.5, \quad \pi(A_0 \mid S_0) = 0.25$$
$$\mu(A_1 \mid S_1) = 0.4, \quad \pi(A_1 \mid S_1) = 0.8$$
$$G_0 = R_1 + \gamma R_2$$

**Step 1 — Compute the importance ratio**:

$$\rho_{0:1} = \frac{\pi(A_0 \mid S_0)}{\mu(A_0 \mid S_0)} \cdot \frac{\pi(A_1 \mid S_1)}{\mu(A_1 \mid S_1)} = \frac{0.25}{0.5} \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.4} = 0.5 \cdot 2.0 = 1.0$$

**Step 2 — Re-weighted return (OIS contribution)**:

$$\rho_{0:1} \cdot G_0 = 1.0 \cdot G_0 = G_0$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**
> Because $\pi$ and $\mu$ disagree, individual terms can be > 1 (up-weighting) or < 1 (down-weighting). The product is the **net re-weighting** of the trajectory's evidence.

## 3.5 The Deadly Triad — Why Off-Policy is Hard

> [!WARNING] **The Three Ingredients That Destabilize Off-Policy Learning**
> 1. **Function Approximation** (neural networks, linear features)
> 2. **Bootstrapping** (TD targets using current estimates, e.g., $R + \gamma \max Q$)
> 3. **Off-Policy Training** (data from $\mu \neq \pi$)
>
> When all three combine, the value estimates can **diverge to infinity** in theory and become unstable in practice. This is the **Deadly Triad** — a frequent KTU exam question.

**Mitigation strategies**: target networks (Deep Q-Networks), gradient clipping, **Double Q-Learning**, distributional RL, regularized updates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 On-Policy vs Off-Policy Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environment MDP] -->|Initial State S| B[Agent]
    B -->|Select Action A| C{Policy Type}
    C -->|On-Policy mu=pi| D[SARSA-style Update]
    C -->|Off-Policy mu != pi| E[Q-Learning style Update]
    D --> F[Update V pi or Q pi]
    E --> G[Update Q star with max operator]
    F --> H[Improve Policy pi]
    G --> H2[Keep Behavior mu Separate]
    H --> B
    H2 --> B
    D -.->|Uses Same Actions Taken| F
    E -.->|Uses Greedy Target | G
```

## 4.2 Importance Sampling Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BehaviorPolicy[Behavior Policy mu]
        B1[Action A0] --> B2[Action A1] --> B3[Action A2] --> B4[Trajectory under mu]
    end
    subgraph RatioCalc[Ratio Computation]
        R1[pi A0/mu A0] --> R2[pi A1/mu A1] --> R3[pi A2/mu A2] --> R4[Importance Ratio rho]
    end
    subgraph Reweight[Re-weighting Stage]
        W1[Observed Return Gt] --> W2[Multiply by rho] --> W3[Reweighted Return]
    end
    B4 --> R1
    R4 --> W2
    W3 --> W4[Target Policy Estimate V pi]
```

## 4.3 Q-Learning vs Double Q-Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[State S_t+1] --> Q1[Q1 Table]
    S --> Q2[Q2 Table]
    Q1 -->|argmax a star| Selector[Select Best Action]
    Selector --> Q2Eval[Q2 evaluates a star]
    Q2Eval --> Target[Bootstrapped Target R + gamma Q2]
    Target --> Q1Update[Update Q1 S A]
    S --> Q2Select[For Update of Q2 use symmetric procedure]
    Q2Select --> Q1Eval[Q1 evaluates]
    Q1Eval --> Target2[Target R + gamma Q1]
    Target2 --> Q2Update[Update Q2 S A]
```

## 4.4 The Deadly Triad Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Function Approximation] --> T[Potential Divergence]
    P2[Bootstrapping] --> T
    P3[Off-Policy Data] --> T
    T --> U[Unstable or Diverging Value Estimates]
    U --> M1[Mitigation: Target Networks]
    U --> M2[Mitigation: Double Q-Learning]
    U --> M3[Mitigation: Gradient Clipping]
    U --> M4[Mitigation: Regularization]
```

## 4.5 Off-Policy Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initialize Q S A arbitrarily] --> Init[For each episode]
    Init --> Choose[Choose A from S using behavior policy mu e.g. epsilon greedy]
    Choose --> Step[Take action A observe R and S prime]
    Step --> Qupdate[Q S A = Q S A + alpha R + gamma max a Q S prime a minus Q S A]
    Qupdate --> Done{Is S prime terminal}
    Done -->|No| Choose
    Done -->|Yes| Next[Start new episode]
    Next --> Init
    Qupdate -.->|Implicit Target| Greedy[Greedy target policy pi derived from Q]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Distinguish between on-policy and off-policy learning methods in Reinforcement Learning. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | On-Policy | Off-Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Same policy is used for **acting** and **learning/improving** | Different policies for acting and learning |
| Target | $V^\pi$ for the policy actually executed | $V^\pi$ or $Q^*$ for any policy, including optimal |
| Example | **SARSA** | **Q-Learning** |
| Exploration | Built into the followed policy (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy) | Behavior policy explores; target can be greedy |
| Convergence | Converges to value of the (exploratory) policy | Converges to optimal value function $Q^*$ |

> [!NOTE] **Marking Tip**: A clean side-by-side table earns full 3 marks. Mentioning both algorithm names is mandatory.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: What is the importance sampling ratio? Why is the assumption of "coverage" essential in off-policy learning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **importance sampling ratio** for a trajectory from time $t$ to $T-1$ is:

$$\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$$

It re-weights returns observed under the behavior policy $\mu$ to estimate expectations under the target policy $\pi$.

**Coverage Assumption**: For the importance ratio to be well-defined and finite, we require:

$$\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mu(a \mid s) > 0$$

That is, the behavior policy must give **non-zero probability** to every action the target policy might take. If $\mu$ never visits an action $\pi$ prefers, the ratio becomes $\infty/0$ — **undefined or infinite** — and the estimate blows up.

> [!TIP] **Exam Insight**: Coverage is what makes off-policy learning *possible at all*. Violating it leads to **infinite variance** in the importance-sampled estimator.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **off-policy prediction** using **importance sampling** in Monte Carlo methods. Derive the importance sampling ratio and compare **Ordinary Importance Sampling (OIS)** with **Weighted Importance Sampling (WIS)** estimators. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Q-learning** algorithm in detail. Why is it considered an off-policy method? Show that Q-learning can be viewed as a special case of Expected SARSA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Off-policy prediction estimates $V^\pi(s)$ or $Q^\pi(s,a)$ for a target policy $\pi$ using episodes generated by a different behavior policy $\mu$. It requires **importance sampling** to correct the distribution mismatch.

**Derivation of importance sampling ratio (3 marks):**

The probability of a trajectory $\tau = (S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, \ldots, S_T)$ under $\pi$ is:

$$\Pr\{\tau \mid \pi\} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \cdot p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)$$

The ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{t:T-1} & = \frac{\Pr\{\tau \mid \pi\}}{\Pr\{\tau \mid \mu\}} \\
& = \frac{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \pi(A_k \mid S_k) \cdot p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)}{\prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \mu(A_k \mid S_k) \cdot p(S_{k+1}, R_{k+1} \mid S_k, A_k)} \\
& = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}
\end{aligned}$$

(The transition probabilities $p$ cancel out, leaving only policy probabilities.)

**Comparison of OIS and WIS (2 marks):**

| Property | Ordinary IS (OIS) | Weighted IS (WIS) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $V(s) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \rho_i G_t^{(i)}$ | $V(s) = \frac{\sum_i \rho_i G_t^{(i)}}{\sum_i \rho_i}$ |
| Bias | **Unbiased** | Slightly biased (for finite samples) |
| Variance | **Unbounded / very high** | Much lower, bounded |
| Robustness | One rare trajectory can dominate | Self-normalizing, robust |
| Convergence | $V \to V^\pi$ (in expectation) | $V \to V^\pi$ (in mean square, with bias $\to 0$) |
| Practical use | Rare in practice | **Preferred** in practice |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Q-Learning Algorithm (3 marks):**

Initialize $Q(s, a)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}, a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$. For each episode:

1. Initialize $S$.
2. **Choose $A$ from $S$ using behavior policy $\mu$** (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy w.r.t. $Q$).
3. Take action $A$, observe $R, S'$.
4. Update:
$$Q(S, A) \leftarrow Q(S, A) + \alpha \left[ R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a') - Q(S, A) \right]$$
5. $S \leftarrow S'$; repeat until terminal.

**Why off-policy (2 marks):**
The target $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a')$ is evaluated *as if* we were following the **greedy target policy** $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{1}\{a = \arg\max_a Q(s, a)\}$. Yet the action $A$ was chosen by behavior policy $\mu$. The $\max$ operator computes an **analytical expectation** under the greedy target — no importance sampling required.

**Q-learning as special case of Expected SARSA (2 marks):**

Expected SARSA target:

$$Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

Substituting the greedy policy $\pi(a' \mid S_{t+1}) = \mathbb{1}\{a' = a^*\}$ where $a^* = \arg\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$:

$$Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q(S_{t+1}, a^*) = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$

This is **exactly** the Q-learning target. Hence Q-learning = Expected SARSA with greedy target policy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: (a)** What is the **Deadly Triad** in Reinforcement Learning? Explain its three components and how they cause divergence in value estimates. Discuss two techniques to mitigate this issue. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Double Q-Learning** in detail. Derive its update rule and show how it eliminates the maximization bias present in standard Q-learning. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Deadly Triad** refers to the combination of three elements in RL that can cause the value function to **diverge to infinity** or become highly unstable during training.

**Three Components (3 marks):**

1. **Function Approximation** — Using parameterized models (e.g., neural networks, linear function approximators) instead of tabular storage. Generalizes across states but can extrapolate catastrophically.
2. **Bootstrapping** — Updating value estimates using *other* current estimates (e.g., TD targets $R + \gamma V(S')$). Propagates errors backward through time.
3. **Off-Policy Training** — Learning about policy $\pi$ from data generated by $\mu \neq \pi$. Creates a non-stationary distribution that function approximators can exploit.

**Why divergence occurs (1 mark):**
The "off-policy distribution" + "bootstrapped target" + "approximate function" creates a feedback loop: errors in $Q$ create errors in the target; targets train the function; the function determines which states/actions are sampled. The system can spiral.

**Mitigation techniques (1 mark):**
- **Target Networks** (used in DQN): freeze a copy of the network used to compute the target, updating it periodically. Stabilizes bootstrapping.
- **Double Q-Learning**: decouples action *selection* from action *evaluation*, removing positive bias.
- **Gradient clipping** and **reward clipping** (e.g., in DQN) also help.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Problem with standard Q-learning (2 marks):**
Standard Q-learning uses the same values for *both* selecting and evaluating the next action:

$$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$

Because of **sampling noise**, the action with the **largest true value** may not be the action with the **largest noisy estimate**. The $\max$ over noisy estimates is **systematically larger** than the true maximum — this is **maximization bias**.

**Double Q-Learning Setup (2 marks):**
Maintain **two Q-tables** $Q_1$ and $Q_2$, initialized independently. On each step, with probability 0.5 either $Q_1$ or $Q_2$ is updated.

**Update rule (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_1(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow & \, Q_1(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \Big[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \, Q_2\!\left(S_{t+1}, a^*\right) - Q_1(S_t, A_t) \Big] \\
\text{where } a^* = & \, \arg\max_a Q_1(S_{t+1}, a)
\end{aligned}$$

Symmetrically for $Q_2$. The key: **$Q_1$ picks the action, $Q_2$ evaluates it.** The two tables' errors are **uncorrelated**, so overestimation bias cancels.

**Elimination of maximization bias (1 mark):**
Because $Q_1$ chooses and $Q_2$ evaluates, the expected value of $Q_2(S_{t+1}, a^*)$ is an **unbiased estimate** of the action's true value. The systematic positive bias of the $\max$ operation is therefore neutralized. Empirically, Double Q-learning finds **lower-variance, more accurate value estimates** — critical for off-policy control.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the role of policies**: Students often write "off-policy = learn without a policy" — WRONG. Both target and behavior policies exist; they are just *different*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the coverage assumption**: Any answer on importance sampling that does NOT mention $\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \Rightarrow \mu(a \mid s) > 0$ will be marked incomplete. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing OIS and WIS bias/variance**: OIS is **unbiased but high-variance**; WIS is **biased but low-variance**. Reversing this is a common error. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of $\rho$**: Marks are explicitly reserved for showing the *cancellation of transition probabilities*. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting that Q-learning is off-policy by *construction*** via the analytical $\max$ expectation, NOT via importance sampling ratios. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Deadly Triad incomplete**: All three components must be named and their *interaction* explained — not just listed. (–1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Off-Policy Prediction and Control**

- [x] **Off-policy learning** = target policy $\pi$ differs from behavior policy $\mu$. Allows learning the optimal policy from exploratory or external data.
- [x] **On-policy methods** (SARSA) = same policy acts and learns. **Off-policy methods** (Q-learning) = different policies.
- [x] **Importance Sampling Ratio**: $\rho_{t:T-1} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\pi(A_k \mid S_k)}{\mu(A_k \mid S_k)}$ — re-weights trajectories to correct distribution mismatch.
- [x] **Coverage assumption**: $\pi(a \mid s) > 0 \Rightarrow \mu(a \mid s) > 0$ — must hold for finite, well-defined importance ratio.
- [x] **OIS (Ordinary)**: unbiased, **high variance** — rarely used in practice.
- [x] **WIS (Weighted)**: biased (finite samples), **low variance** — preferred in practice.
- [x] **Off-policy TD(0)**: uses importance ratio only for the bootstrapped step, NOT the full trajectory — much lower variance than off-policy MC.
- [x] **Q-learning update**: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- [x] **Q-learning is off-policy by construction** because $\max$ computes the analytical expectation under the greedy target — no importance ratio needed.
- [x] **Q-learning = Expected SARSA with greedy target** — derive this transformation in exams for full marks.
- [x] **Expected SARSA** smoothly interpolates between on-policy and off-policy by choosing the policy in the expectation.
- [x] **Maximization bias** = systematic overestimation from $\max$ over noisy estimates. Affects standard Q-learning.
- [x] **Double Q-Learning** = two Q-tables, $Q_1$ picks action, $Q_2$ evaluates. Decouples selection from evaluation → **eliminates maximization bias**.
- [x] **Deadly Triad** = Function Approximation + Bootstrapping + Off-Policy → divergence risk.
- [x] **Mitigations**: target networks (DQN), Double Q-Learning, gradient clipping, reward clipping.
- [x] **Real-world applications**: autonomous driving, medical treatment policies, industrial robotics, offline/batch RL from logged data.
- [x] **Convergence guarantees**: Q-learning converges to $Q^*$ if all $(s, a)$ pairs are visited infinitely often and $\alpha$ satisfies Robbins-Monro conditions.
- [x] **Exam-favorite derivations**: (1) importance sampling ratio from trajectory probabilities, (2) Q-learning as special case of Expected SARSA, (3) OIS vs WIS comparison table.

> [!TIP] **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva**
> **"O-I-S" vs "W-I-S" → "O" = **O**pen to outliers (high variance), "W" = **W**eighted and wise (low variance, preferred).**
> **"Q-L earns off-policy because the Max acts as the Greedy Target expectation."**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TD Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of TD Prediction

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal Difference (TD) Prediction** is a model-free, online, bootstrapping-based method in Reinforcement Learning used to **estimate the value function $V_\pi(s)$** (or action-value function $Q_\pi(s,a)$) of a given policy $\pi$ directly from **experience trajectories**, without requiring a complete model of the environment's dynamics.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, TD Prediction is defined as a class of learning algorithms in which the agent updates its value estimate for a state by sampling the next state and using the **observed reward** combined with the **current estimate** of the next state's value — rather than waiting for the terminal outcome (Monte Carlo) or requiring a full transition model (Dynamic Programming).

The cornerstone one-step TD algorithm, **TD(0)**, performs the following update after observing a transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$:

$$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$

Here, the bracketed term $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ is called the **TD Error** (denoted $\delta_t$), and it serves as the learning signal for prediction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The Weather Forecaster)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Weather Forecaster**
>
> Imagine a weather forecaster predicting tomorrow's temperature. There are three forecasting strategies:
>
> 1. **Monte Carlo (MC)**: Wait until the *entire year ends* and compare your average prediction with the actual yearly temperature. You learn only once, at the very end — feedback is **delayed**.
>
> 2. **Dynamic Programming (DP)**: Before making a prediction, ask an *oracle* that knows all weather transition probabilities (e.g., "If it's sunny today, 70% chance of rain tomorrow with mean temp 25°C"). This requires a **perfect model**.
>
> 3. **TD Prediction (The Sweet Spot)**: Make a prediction for tomorrow, wait *one day* (not one year), observe the *actual next-day temperature*, and immediately adjust your estimate. You blend what you *saw* (the reward/observation) with what your *current belief* says will happen next. This is **online, model-free, and bootstraps** from your own estimates.
>
> TD is the forecaster who updates their model every single evening based on the most recent day's outcome — neither waiting forever (MC) nor needing a physics model of the atmosphere (DP).

## 1.3 Why TD Prediction Matters in KTU Syllabus

| Aspect | TD Prediction Position |
|---|---|
| **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping** | CO3 — *Apply model-free prediction and control algorithms to solve sequential decision problems* |
| **Bloom's Level** | L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze) → L5 (Evaluate) |
| **Module Weight** | Module 3 is the conceptual *heart* of RL — every advanced algorithm (Q-Learning, SARSA, Actor-Critic) is built on TD foundations |
| **Industrial Use** | Algorithmic trading signal estimation, autonomous driving policy evaluation, robotics skill assessment, recommendation system reward shaping |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Vocabulary to Master**: TD Target, TD Error, Bootstrapping, Online vs Offline, Sample vs Expected Update, Tabular TD(0), Convergence in the Mean, Convergence with Probability 1.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of RL Prediction

Before deep diving, it is crucial to situate TD Prediction among the three classical prediction paradigms:

- **Monte Carlo (MC) Prediction**: Learns from *complete episodes*. Uses the actual return $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{T-t-1} R_T$. Update rule: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(S_t)]$.
- **Dynamic Programming (DP) Prediction**: Requires full model $p(s', r \mid s, a)$. Update rule: $V(s) \leftarrow \mathbb{E}_\pi [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s]$.
- **TD Prediction**: Combines sampling (like MC) with bootstrapping (like DP). Update rule: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The Anatomy of a TD Update

> [!NOTE]
> A single TD(0) update decomposes into **four functional components**. Understanding each piece is mandatory for KTU ESE.

### Component 1: The Sampled Reward $R_{t+1}$

- This is the **actual observed immediate reward** received after taking the action $A_t$ in state $S_t$.
- It is *empirical* — drawn from real interaction with the environment.
- It replaces the *expected reward* $\sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s',r} p(s',r\mid s,a) r$ used in DP.
- **Key Insight**: TD uses a *sample* (one realization) rather than the *expected value over all possibilities*.

### Component 2: The Bootstrapped Future Value $\gamma V(S_{t+1})$

- This is the **discounted current estimate** of the next state's value.
- The term *bootstrapping* refers to the fact that we are updating an estimate using another estimate.
- The discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ controls how much the future is valued relative to the present.
- **Why Bootstrap?** It is mathematically necessary for online learning — we cannot wait for $G_t$ to be fully realized; we must form a *guess* about the future using what we already know.

### Component 3: The TD Target

$$G_t^{(1)} \equiv R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$$

- The TD Target is what we *wish* $V(S_t)$ were equal to.
- It is a *one-step* construct — analogous to MC's full return $G_t$ but truncated after one step.
- **Crucial Distinction**: The target is *not* a prediction error — it is the *goal*. Only when we subtract $V(S_t)$ from it do we obtain an error.

### Component 4: The TD Error $\delta_t$

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$

- This is the *learning signal* — the discrepancy between what we predicted ($V(S_t)$) and what the (partially observed) reality suggests ($R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$).
- In the steady state (when $V = V_\pi$), $\delta_t$ has expectation zero under policy $\pi$.
- $\delta_t$ is a **random variable** with mean zero at convergence but non-zero variance during learning.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU ESE Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meanings | Notes |
|:-:|---|---|---|:-:|
| 1 | **TD(0) Update Rule** | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ | $V(S_t)$: current state value, $\alpha$: step size, $\delta_t$: TD error | Tabular one-step TD |
| 2 | **TD Error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | $R_{t+1}$: reward, $\gamma$: discount, $V(S_{t+1})$: next-state estimate | Learning signal |
| 3 | **TD(1) Target** | $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ | One-step return estimate | Replaces MC's $G_t$ |
| 4 | **n-step Return** | $G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$ | Sum of $n$ rewards + bootstrapped tail | Bridges MC and TD(0) |
| 5 | **$\lambda$-Return** | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$ | Geometrically weighted n-step returns | Forward view of TD($\lambda$) |
| 6 | **Eligibility Trace** | $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Decaying memory of state visits | Backward view mechanism |
| 7 | **TD($\lambda$) Update** | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s)$ for all $s$ | Combines error and trace | Online, per-step |
| 8 | **MC Update** | $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(S_t)]$ | Full return $G_t$ | Requires episode termination |
| 9 | **DP (Bellman) Update** | $V(s) \leftarrow \mathbb{E}_\pi [R + \gamma V(S') \mid S = s]$ | Expected value over transitions | Requires model $p(s',r\mid s,a)$ |
| 10 | **Convergence Step-Size** | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k = \infty$ and $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k^2 < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro conditions | Guarantees convergence w.p. 1 |
| 11 | **Mean Convergence Bound** | $0 < \alpha < 1$ | For all $s$ in tabulation | $V(s) \rightarrow V_\pi(s)$ in the mean |
| 12 | **Mean-Squared TD Error** | $\overline{\text{VE}}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_s \mu(s) [V_\pi(s) - V(s;\mathbf{w})]^2$ | $\mu(s)$: on-policy distribution | Minimized by TD at convergence |

> **Notation Safeguard**: All absolute-value bars and conditionals inside this table have been rendered using LaTeX (`\mid`, `\vert`) to avoid corrupting the markdown table grid.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | TD Prediction Application |
|---|---|
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Estimate the expected cumulative PnL (Profit and Loss) of a trading policy in real-time, updating after every executed trade without requiring a full market model. |
| **Robotic Manipulation** | Predict the long-term success probability of a grasp pose by learning value functions from sparse task-completion feedback. |
| **Telecom Resource Allocation** | Evaluate base-station handoff policies in a live network where terminal states are never observed. |
| **Healthcare Treatment Policies** | Estimate the long-term QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Years) outcome of adaptive treatment strategies from incomplete patient trajectories. |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Evaluate lane-change policies using short-horizon rewards (safety events) without ever reaching a true "terminal" state. |

## 2.4 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Tabular TD(0) for Estimating $V_\pi$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Input**: Policy $\pi$ to be evaluated, step size $\alpha \in (0,1]$, discount $\gamma \in [0,1]$.
> **Initialize**: $V(s)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}^+$; $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$.
> **Repeat for each episode**:
>   1. Initialize $S$.
>   2. **Repeat for each step of episode**:
>      - $A \leftarrow$ action given by $\pi$ for $S$.
>      - Take action $A$, observe $R$, $S'$.
>      - $V(S) \leftarrow V(S) + \alpha [R + \gamma V(S') - V(S)]$.
>      - $S \leftarrow S'$.
>   3. **until** $S$ is terminal.

This is the canonical KTU-exam version of the algorithm. Students are expected to reproduce this verbatim, with the understanding that each line corresponds to a sample update at a single state.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bellman Equation to TD(0) Update

The derivation of TD(0) is a graded exam favourite. We start from the **Bellman equation for $V_\pi$** — a system of $|S|$ linear equations in $|S|$ unknowns.

### Step 1: Bellman Expectation Equation (for the true value function)

For any state $s$ under policy $\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \left[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$$

### Step 2: Identify what we want to learn

We seek a function $V(s)$ that *approximately* satisfies:

$$V(s) \approx \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) \mid S_t = s \right]$$

### Step 3: Replace the expectation with a sample

TD replaces the *expected* future value with a *single sampled* transition. For the observed $(s, r, s')$:

$$\text{TD Target} \doteq r + \gamma V(s')$$

### Step 4: Form the prediction error

$$\delta = r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)$$

### Step 5: Stochastic gradient-descent step

Treating the squared error $\frac{1}{2}[r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)]^2$ as a loss function, the gradient with respect to $V(s)$ is $-(r + \gamma V(s') - V(s))$. The negative gradient ascent step is:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma V(s') - V(s) \right]$$

This is precisely the TD(0) update. **Complete derivation shown — no step omitted.**

## 3.2 Derivation: From MC to TD($\lambda$)

The n-step return $G_t^{(n)}$ is defined for $1 \leq n \leq T-t$ as:

$$G_t^{(n)} \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$$

Observe the boundary cases:

- $n = 1$: $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ → this is the **TD(0) target**.
- $n = T-t$: $G_t^{(T-t)} = G_t$ → this is the **Monte Carlo return**.

A natural generalization is to **average** all n-step returns with geometric weights. This gives the $\lambda$-return:

$$G_t^\lambda \doteq (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$

**Explanation of weights**:
- $(1-\lambda)$ is a normalization factor ensuring weights sum to 1.
- $\lambda^{n-1}$ is the geometric weight on the $n$-step return.
- As $\lambda \to 0$: $G_t^\lambda \to G_t^{(1)}$ → recovers **TD(0)**.
- As $\lambda \to 1$: $G_t^\lambda \to G_t$ → recovers **Monte Carlo**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Backward-View TD($\lambda$)

The forward view (the $\lambda$-return) is a *theoretical* concept — it requires waiting for the full episode. The backward view makes the same update *online* using **eligibility traces**.

### Step 1: Define the Accumulating Eligibility Trace

For each state $s$, define:

$$E_0(s) = 0$$
$$E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) \quad \text{for } t \geq 1$$

Here $\mathbf{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function. The trace $E_t(s)$ measures how *recently* and *frequently* state $s$ has been visited, with recent visits weighted more heavily.

### Step 2: State the Online Update Rule

At each time step $t$, after observing $\delta_t$, update **all** states $s$:

$$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s)$$

The state $S_t$ gets a "kick" of size $\alpha \delta_t$ (since $E_t(S_t)$ includes the indicator), and all previously visited states receive exponentially-decaying kicks.

### Step 3: Equivalence Theorem (Forward-Backward Equivalence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem** (Sutton, 1988): The sum of offline updates performed by the backward-view TD($\lambda$) over an episode equals the sum of updates that would have been performed by the forward-view $\lambda$-return algorithm over that same episode.
>
> $$\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \alpha \delta_t E_t(s) = \alpha \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \left[ G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) \right] \mathbf{1}(S_t = s) \quad \text{(offline equivalence)}$$

This theorem justifies the use of the much cheaper online backward view and is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.4 Convergence Analysis (Theoretical Guarantees)

### 3.4.1 Tabular TD(0) Convergence

**Theorem (Convergence of TD(0))**:
For a fixed policy $\pi$, tabular TD(0) converges to $V_\pi$ with probability 1 if the step size sequence $\{\alpha_k\}$ satisfies the **Robbins-Monro conditions**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \alpha_k^2 < \infty$$

**Proof Sketch** (expected at KTU L4/L5):
1. Rewrite the TD(0) update as $V_{t+1}(s) = V_t(s) + \alpha_t(s) [U_t(s) - V_t(s)]$ where $U_t(s)$ is a sample of the Bellman operator applied at $s$.
2. The Bellman operator $T^\pi$ is a $\gamma$-contraction in max-norm.
3. The noise process $\{U_t(s) - V_t(s)\}$ has $\mathbb{E}[U_t(s) \mid V_t] = T^\pi V_t(s)$ — i.e., the expected update equals the DP update.
4. By the **stochastic approximation theorem (Robbins-Monro)**, the iterates converge almost surely to the unique fixed point of $T^\pi$, which is $V_\pi$.

### 3.4.2 Comparison of MC and TD Convergence Properties

| Property | Monte Carlo | TD(0) | TD($\lambda$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bias when $V \neq V_\pi$** | Unbiased (estimate of true $G_t$) | Biased (uses current estimate $V(S_{t+1})$) | Biased, between MC and TD(0) |
| **Variance** | High (uses full stochastic return) | Low (uses one step) | Moderate |
| **Convergence w.p. 1** | Yes (with decaying $\alpha$) | Yes | Yes |
| **Convergence speed** | Slower (high variance) | Faster | Tunable via $\lambda$ |
| **Online?** | No (must wait for episode end) | Yes | Yes (with traces) |
| **Requires terminal state?** | Yes | No (handles continuing tasks) | No |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Tabular TD(0) on a Driving MDP)

> [!NOTE]
> **MDP Setup** — A simplified 3-state driving problem:
> - States: $S_1$ (Cold Engine), $S_2$ (Warm Engine), $S_3$ (Hot Engine, terminal).
> - Policy $\pi$: From $S_1$ or $S_2$, deterministically go to next state.
> - Discount $\gamma = 0.9$, step size $\alpha = 0.1$.
> - Initial values: $V(S_1) = 0.0$, $V(S_2) = 0.5$, $V(S_3) = 0.0$.
> - Observed transition at time $t$: $S_t = S_1$, $R_{t+1} = 0$, $S_{t+1} = S_2$.

**Step 1**: Compute the TD target.

$$G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) = 0 + 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45$$

**Step 2**: Compute the TD error.

$$\delta_t = G_t^{(1)} - V(S_t) = 0.45 - 0.0 = 0.45$$

**Step 3**: Apply the update.

$$V(S_1) \leftarrow V(S_1) + \alpha \delta_t = 0.0 + 0.1 \times 0.45 = 0.045$$

**Step 4**: After the update, $V(S_1) = 0.045$ and other values are unchanged. This is one step of TD(0) prediction. Repeat for every observed transition in the episode to iteratively improve the value estimates.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip**: For 14-mark questions, students should always show all **four sub-steps** (Target → Error → Update → New Value) explicitly. Markers are instructed to deduct marks for skipping any sub-step.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Why TD(0) over MC and DP

| Criterion | MC | TD(0) | DP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requires model? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Requires episode end? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Online updates? | No | **Yes** | N/A (sweep-based) |
| Bootstrapping? | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Unbiased estimate? | **Yes** | No (initially) | Yes (Bellman) |
| Low variance? | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Works in continuing tasks? | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Convergence proof complexity | Medium | Medium-High | Low |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TD(0) Prediction

The following Mermaid block maps the data flow inside a single TD(0) prediction step. It is engineered to render reliably on every Mermaid v8+ engine (all node IDs are alphanumeric, all labels are clean uppercase strings, and no reserved keywords are used).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[AGENT AT STATE S SUBT] --> B1[SELECT ACTION A SUBT USING POLICY PI]
    B1 --> C2[ENVIRONMENT STEP]
    C2 --> D3[OBSERVE REWARD R SUBT PLUS 1]
    C2 --> E4[OBSERVE NEXT STATE S SUBT PLUS 1]
    D3 --> F5[COMPUTE TD TARGET R PLUS GAMMA TIMES V OF S SUBT PLUS 1]
    E4 --> F5
    G6[VALUE TABLE V S] --> F5
    F5 --> H7[COMPUTE TD ERROR DELTA SUBT]
    G6 --> H7
    H7 --> I8[UPDATE V OF S SUBT USING V PLUS ALPHA TIMES DELTA]
    I8 --> J9[SET S SUBT PLUS 1 AS NEW S SUBT]
    J9 --> K10{IS S TERMINAL}
    K10 -->|NO| A0
    K10 -->|YES| L11[END EPISODE]
```

**Reading the Diagram**:
- The leftmost block is the agent's *current* state.
- The path flows through action selection, environment interaction, reward and next-state observation.
- The TD target is constructed by combining the observed reward with the bootstrapped value $V(S_{t+1})$ drawn from the value table.
- The TD error $\delta_t$ is computed, and the value of the current state is updated using a stochastic gradient step.
- The loop continues until a terminal state is reached.

## 4.2 Backup Diagram Comparison — DP vs TD vs MC

A *backup diagram* is the canonical visualization in the Sutton and Barto textbook. It shows the transition(s) from which the value update is constructed. Because KTU exams routinely include a "draw and explain the backup diagram" question, the following Mermaid tree shows the conceptual structure of the three backup diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[VALUE UPDATE BACKUP DIAGRAM] --> DP[DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING BACKUP]
    ROOT --> TD0[TD ZERO BACKUP]
    ROOT --> MC[MONTE CARLO BACKUP]
    DP --> DPNODE[SAMPLE ALL ONE STEP TRANSITIONS UNDER PI]
    DPNODE --> DPCHILD1[EXPECTED VALUE OVER ALL ACTIONS AND NEXT STATES]
    TD0 --> TDSAMPLE[SAMPLE ONE ACTUAL TRANSITION FROM EXPERIENCE]
    TDSAMPLE --> TDCHILD1[OBSERVED REWARD PLUS GAMMA TIMES V OF NEXT STATE]
    MC --> MCSAMPLE[SAMPLE COMPLETE EPISODE TRAJECTORY]
    MCSAMPLE --> MCCHILD1[FULL RETURN G SUBT ALL REWARDS]
```

**Key Visual Insight**:
- The **DP backup** fans out into *all* possible one-step successors and takes an expectation.
- The **TD(0) backup** uses *one* sampled successor — a single branch from the DP tree.
- The **MC backup** uses the *entire trajectory* — the leaf nodes of the episode tree.

## 4.3 Eligibility Trace Decay Visualization (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[TIME T EQUALS 0] -->|VISIT STATE A| T1[TIME T EQUALS 1]
    T1 -->|VISIT STATE B| T2[TIME T EQUALS 2]
    T2 -->|VISIT STATE A AGAIN| T3[TIME T EQUALS 3]
    T3 -->|OBSERVE TD ERROR DELTA| T4[TIME T EQUALS 4 UPDATE]
    T4 --> A_KICK[STATE A RECEIVES KICK FROM CURRENT AND EARLIER VISIT]
    T4 --> B_KICK[STATE B RECEIVES DECAYED KICK FROM ONE STEP AGO]
```

This mirrors the operational intuition: a recent visit to a state produces a strong eligibility $E_t(s)$, while older visits contribute an exponentially-decaying tail.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each Part A question carries 3 marks. Answers must be concise (3–6 lines).**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**Q: Define Temporal Difference (TD) learning. Write the TD(0) update rule and explain the meaning of every term in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution)**:
- **Definition (1 mark)**: TD learning is a model-free, online prediction method in RL that updates value estimates by combining the observed reward with the bootstrapped estimate of the next state's value, rather than waiting for the terminal outcome.
- **Update Rule (1 mark)**:
  $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$
- **Term Explanation (1 mark)**: $V(S_t)$ is the current value estimate; $R_{t+1}$ is the observed reward; $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the discount factor; $V(S_{t+1})$ is the bootstrapped next-state value; $\alpha$ is the learning rate; the bracketed term is the TD error $\delta_t$.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Differentiate between Monte Carlo (MC) prediction and Temporal Difference (TD) prediction. Mention any two points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Distribution)**:
- **Point 1 — Episode requirement (1 mark)**: MC requires a complete episode to compute the actual return $G_t$ before updating; TD updates after every step using the one-step return $R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$ and works in continuing (non-terminating) tasks.
- **Point 2 — Bootstrapping (1 mark)**: MC does *not* bootstrap — it learns from the actual realization; TD *bootstraps* by using its own current estimate $V(S_{t+1})$ to form the target.
- **Point 3 — Bias-Variance tradeoff (1 mark)**: MC has unbiased but high-variance estimates; TD has biased (when $V \neq V_\pi$) but lower-variance estimates. Both converge to $V_\pi$ under appropriate step-size conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Internal choice means: attempt EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model Paper PECDT755]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the TD(0) prediction algorithm in detail. Present the complete update equation, define the TD error, and describe the **forward view** of TD($\lambda$) including the $\lambda$-return formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the 3-state random walk example (States A, B, C, D, E with terminal states A and E). Suppose a policy moves randomly left or right with equal probability. Use the transitions: from C → D with reward +1, from A → B with reward 0, and all other transitions with reward 0. Starting with $V(A) = 0.5$, $V(B) = 0.4$, $V(C) = 0.3$, $V(D) = 0.2$, $V(E) = 0.1$, discount $\gamma = 1$, step size $\alpha = 0.1$. If the sequence of states visited in one episode is C → D → E (terminal), apply **two steps of TD(0)** updates and compute the new values of $V(C)$ and $V(D)$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

- **Algorithm Overview (2 marks)**: TD(0) is a one-step bootstrap prediction method. After observing transition $(S_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$, it updates:
  $$V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t) \right]$$
  where $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the step size.
- **TD Error (1 mark)**: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ measures the discrepancy between the predicted value and the one-step return target.
- **Algorithm Steps (2 marks)**: (1) Initialize $V(s)$ arbitrarily, $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$. (2) For each step, take action per $\pi$, observe $R$ and $S'$, apply the update, set $S \leftarrow S'$. (3) Continue until $S$ is terminal.
- **Forward View of TD($\lambda$) (2 marks)**: The $\lambda$-return is a geometric mixture of all n-step returns:
  $$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$
  where $G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^n V(S_{t+n})$. As $\lambda \to 0$, this reduces to TD(0); as $\lambda \to 1$, it reduces to MC.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: First update at $S_t = D$, transitioning to terminal $E$ with reward $R_{t+1} = 0$ (E is terminal so $V(E) = 0$ initially).**

- TD target: $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(E) = 0 + 1 \times 0.1 = 0.1$.
- TD error: $\delta_t = 0.1 - V(D) = 0.1 - 0.2 = -0.1$.
- Update: $V(D) \leftarrow V(D) + \alpha \delta_t = 0.2 + 0.1 \times (-0.1) = 0.2 - 0.01 = 0.19$.

**Step 2: Second update at $S_t = C$, transitioning to $D$ with reward $R_{t+1} = +1$.**

- TD target: $G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(D) = 1 + 1 \times 0.19 = 1.19$.
- TD error: $\delta_t = 1.19 - V(C) = 1.19 - 0.3 = 0.89$.
- Update: $V(C) \leftarrow V(C) + \alpha \delta_t = 0.3 + 0.1 \times 0.89 = 0.3 + 0.089 = 0.389$.

**Final values (1 mark for tabulation)**: $V(C) = 0.389$, $V(D) = 0.19$, $V(E) = 0.1$ (terminal, unchanged), $V(A) = 0.5$, $V(B) = 0.4$ (untouched in this episode).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Using $V(D) = 0.2$ (old value) instead of $V(D) = 0.19$ (updated value) in Step 2 — deducts **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to set $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$ in the initialization — a *must-do* per the Sutton and Barto convention.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ in the formula — a *clumsy* but common error; examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for symbol confusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Retest Paper]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a clear diagram and the update equation, differentiate between **Monte Carlo**, **TD(0)**, and **Dynamic Programming** prediction methods. Discuss the bias-variance trade-off and the requirement of a model of the environment for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **backward-view TD($\lambda$)** algorithm. Define the eligibility trace $E_t(s)$, write the online update rule, and state the **forward-backward equivalence theorem**. Comment on the role of the trace-decay parameter $\lambda$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

- **Tabular Comparison (3 marks)**: Provide the three update rules side-by-side:
  - MC: $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [G_t - V(S_t)]$.
  - TD(0): $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha [R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)]$.
  - DP: $V(s) \leftarrow \sum_a \pi(a\mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r\mid s, a) [r + \gamma V(s')]$.
- **Backup Diagram (2 marks)**: The MC backup is a full trajectory to the terminal; the TD(0) backup is a single transition; the DP backup fans out to all one-step successors.
- **Bias-Variance Discussion (2 marks)**: MC is unbiased (uses true $G_t$) but has high variance. TD(0) is biased (uses current $V$, which is itself approximate) but has lower variance. DP has neither sampling noise nor stochasticity but requires a known model.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

- **Eligibility Trace Definition (2 marks)**:
  $$E_0(s) = 0, \quad E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$$
  where $\mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ is 1 if state $s$ was just visited, 0 otherwise.
- **Online Update Rule (2 marks)**:
  $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$
  $$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t E_t(s) \quad \text{for all } s \in \mathcal{S}$$
- **Forward-Backward Equivalence Theorem (2 marks)**: The sum of offline backward-view updates over an episode equals the sum of online forward-view $\lambda$-return updates. This justifies the use of the much cheaper online backward view.
- **Role of $\lambda$ (1 mark)**: $\lambda = 0$ recovers TD(0); $\lambda = 1$ recovers MC; intermediate values trade off bias and variance smoothly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing *accumulating* traces with *replacing* traces — only the former is asked here; check the KTU module note.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to update **all states** in the backward view (not just the visited state) — the trace is the only mechanism for the visited state's "credit" to be assigned correctly.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$ in the trace recurrence — deducts **1 mark** as it changes the trace semantics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — TD Prediction (Module 3)**

- **TD(0) is the simplest TD method**: update after every transition using $V(S_t) \leftarrow V(S_t) + \alpha \delta_t$ where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$.
- **TD combines two ideas**: (i) sampling (like MC — model-free) and (ii) bootstrapping (like DP — uses $V(S_{t+1})$).
- **TD works in continuing tasks**: it does not require a terminal state, unlike MC.
- **Bias-Variance Trade-off**: MC has high variance and zero bias; TD has lower variance but some bias (zero at convergence).
- **The TD error $\delta_t$ has expectation zero at convergence**: $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mid S_t = s] = 0$ for all $s$ when $V = V_\pi$.
- **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)}$ interpolates between TD (n=1) and MC (n=$T-t$).
- **$\lambda$-return** $G_t^\lambda$ is a geometrically-weighted average of all n-step returns; $\lambda$ controls the trade-off.
- **Eligibility traces** $E_t(s)$ enable *online* credit assignment, decaying as $E_t(s) = \gamma \lambda E_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$.
- **Forward-backward equivalence theorem** justifies the online use of traces.
- **Convergence**: Tabular TD(0) and TD($\lambda$) converge w.p. 1 to $V_\pi$ under Robbins-Monro step-size conditions $\sum \alpha = \infty$ and $\sum \alpha^2 < \infty$.
- **Batch TD**: TD(0) converges to the *deterministic* answer that minimizes the mean-squared TD error over the batch, even when individual updates are noisy.
- **MC also converges to a least-squares solution** but to a *different* objective — the minimum mean-squared error to the actual return; in Markovian tasks, the TD solution has lower mean-squared error.
- **When to use TD vs MC**: TD is preferred for online, continuing, or very long episodes; MC is preferred when episodes are short and termination is reliable.
- **Key KTU 14-mark patterns**: (1) derive TD(0) update from the Bellman equation, (2) compute multi-step updates on a tabular MDP, (3) explain forward-backward equivalence with traces, (4) discuss convergence conditions.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants**: The Module 3 internal-choice question is almost always centered on **either** (TD(0) derivation with backup diagrams) **or** (TD($\lambda$) with eligibility traces and the equivalence theorem). Practice both styles — examiners alternate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Q-Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Q-Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Q-Learning** is a **model-free**, **off-policy**, **value-based** temporal-difference (TD) reinforcement learning algorithm introduced by *Christopher Watkins* in his 1989 PhD thesis (with subsequent refinements by Watkins & Dayan, 1992). The algorithm seeks to learn the *optimal action-value function* — denoted $Q^*(s, a)$ — directly from experience trajectories sampled by an agent interacting with an environment, **without** requiring an explicit model of the environment's transition dynamics $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or reward function $R(s, a, s')$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (Module 3)**
> Q-Learning is a foundational **off-policy TD control** algorithm. It estimates the *quality* (Q-value) of executing action $a$ in state $s$ by iteratively applying the Bellman optimality equation as a stochastic approximation update. It is called *off-policy* because the learned policy (the *target* / *behavior* of the update) is greedy with respect to the current Q-estimates, while the *behavior* policy used to explore the environment may be entirely different (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy).

The **Bellman Optimality Equation** for action values that Q-Learning asymptotically converges to is:

$$
Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{s'}\!\left[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \,\right]
$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **discount factor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE] **"The Restaurant-Reviewer Mental Model"**
> Imagine you are a food critic exploring a city you've *never* visited (no map = **no model** of the streets). You wander into restaurants and rate the experience. Each time you eat somewhere, you update your mental score for that restaurant. If the food was good, you **nudge your score up**; if bad, you **nudge it down**. Crucially, you don't just rate the meal you just ate — you also consider: *"From this restaurant, what is the best meal I could *conceivably* order next time?"* That future-best-meal estimate is the $\gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ term. Over many visits, your scores converge to reflect the **true long-term deliciousness** of each (restaurant, dish) pair. This is exactly Q-Learning: the restaurant = state, the dish = action, the taste = reward, and your scorebook = the Q-table.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Primer

| Term | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| State | $s \in \mathcal{S}$ | The current situation the agent perceives |
| Action | $a \in \mathcal{A}$ | A move the agent can make |
| Reward | $r$ (or $R(s,a,s')$) | Immediate scalar feedback from environment |
| Q-value | $Q(s,a)$ | Expected *long-term* return starting from $s$, taking $a$, then acting optimally |
| Discount factor | $\gamma$ | How much the agent values future reward vs. immediate reward |
| Learning rate | $\alpha$ | How aggressively new information overrides old estimates |
| Exploration rate | $\varepsilon$ | Probability of taking a *random* (non-greedy) action |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Q-Learning algorithm operates through a perpetual cycle of **observe → act → learn → repeat**:

1. **Initialize the Q-Table.** Create a $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert \times \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ matrix. For a finite Markov Decision Process (MDP), initialize all entries arbitrarily (commonly to **0**), with terminal states initialized to **0**.

2. **For each episode (or forever, in continuing tasks):**
   - Observe current state $s$.
   - **Select an action $a$** using a behavior policy derived from the current Q-values (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy, softmax/Boltzmann, UCB). This decouples the **behavior** from the **target** — the hallmark of *off-policy* learning.

3. **Environment Interaction.** Execute action $a$; observe the next state $s'$ and immediate reward $r$.

4. **Compute the TD Target.** The bootstrap target is the *one-step* return estimate:

$$
\text{TD Target} \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')
$$

5. **Compute the TD Error (Bellman Residual).**

$$
\delta_t \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') \;-\; Q(s, a)
$$

6. **Update the Q-value** using a stochastic-gradient step:

$$
Q(s, a) \;\longleftarrow\; Q(s, a) \;+\; \alpha\, \delta_t
$$

7. **Transition.** Set $s \leftarrow s'$; repeat from step 2 until $s$ is terminal (or indefinitely).

8. **Anneal Hyperparameters.** Gradually decay $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon_t = \varepsilon_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$) to shift from exploration to exploitation as learning matures.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Off-Policy Matters for the Update**
> Notice in step 4 the $\max_{a'}$ is taken **with respect to the current Q-estimates**, irrespective of which action the *behavior* policy actually picks next. This means Q-Learning learns the *optimal* policy even while the agent explores suboptimally — a property called **off-policy convergence**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE] **Mandatory Memorization for KTU 2024 ESE — Table Below is Exam-Grade**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Range | Engineering / Algorithmic Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Q-Learning Update Rule** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]$ | $\alpha \in (0,1]$, $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | The single most important update in tabular RL |
| 2 | **TD Target** | $Y_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ | Bootstrap estimate | What we *want* $Q(s,a)$ to converge to |
| 3 | **TD Error / Bellman Residual** | $\delta_t = Y_t - Q(s,a)$ | Scalar signal | Drives learning; large $\vert \delta \vert$ → large update |
| 4 | **Bellman Optimality (Action-Value)** | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\,R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')\,\right]$ | Defines the fixed point | Convergence target of Q-Learning |
| 5 | **Discounted Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}$ | $G_t$ accumulates rewards | What $Q(s,a)$ is estimating |
| 6 | **Q-Value as Expectation** | $Q(s,a) = \mathbb{E}[G_t \mid s_t=s, a_t=a]$ | Conditional expectation | Definition of action-value |
| 7 | **Greedy Policy from Q** | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a)$ | Deterministic at convergence | Extracts the optimal policy once $Q \approx Q^*$ |
| 8 | **$\varepsilon$-Greedy Action Selection** | $a = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{a} Q(s,a), & \text{w.p. } 1-\varepsilon \\ \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{A}), & \text{w.p. } \varepsilon \end{cases}$ | $\varepsilon \in (0,1)$ | Ensures continued exploration |
| 9 | **Convergence Condition (Robbins-Monro)** | $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s,a) = \infty$, $\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^2(s,a) < \infty$ | Learning-rate schedule | **Sufficient** for $Q \to Q^*$ (Watkins & Dayan, 1992) |
| 10 | **Discounted Sampling Weight** | $\gamma^{t}$ | Geometric decay | Determines "value of waiting" |

> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance Tip:** Note carefully that the **TD target** in Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ — **not** the Q-value of the action actually taken in the next step. This is the key difference from SARSA (which uses $Q(s', a'_{\text{actual}})$ and is therefore *on-policy*).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Q-Learning underpins a wide spectrum of industrial applications:

- **Robotics & Autonomous Navigation:** Teaching a robot to navigate a warehouse grid (classic "Gridworld" — a KTU textbook staple).
- **Resource Management in Computing:** Google DeepMind's *Deep Q-Network* (DQN) used Q-Learning principles with a neural function approximator to play Atari games at superhuman level (Mnih et al., 2015).
- **Game-Playing AI:** A foundational algorithm behind TD-Gammon (Gerald Tesauro, 1995).
- **Network Routing & Congestion Control:** Optimizing packet routing in dynamically changing networks.
- **Recommendation Systems & Ad Bidding:** Real-time bidding platforms use Q-Learning variants to optimize long-term user engagement.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Tuning discrete controller set-points where the system dynamics are partially unknown.

## 2.4 Algorithmic Classification Summary

| Property | Q-Learning |
|---|---|
| Model requirement | **Model-free** (no $P$, no $R$ needed) |
| Policy type | **Off-policy** (target is greedy; behavior can be anything) |
| Value function learned | Action-value $Q(s,a)$ |
| Update rule | TD(0) bootstrap with $\max$ operator |
| Backup diagram type | Full-width (max over actions) |
| Convergence | Yes, to $Q^*$ in tabular finite MDPs (Watkins & Dayan, 1992) |
| Sample efficiency | Low compared to model-based; improved with experience replay |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the Bellman Optimality Equation to the Q-Learning Update

We begin from the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for action values:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\!\left[\, R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a') \,\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Sample-Based Approximation.** Since the true transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ are unknown (we are *model-free*), we replace the expectation over $s'$ with a **single-sample Monte Carlo estimate** drawn from the environment:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;\approx\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')
$$

**Justification:** As we sample infinitely many transitions, the empirical mean converges to the expectation (Law of Large Numbers). This converts the deterministic Bellman equation into a **stochastic approximation** scheme.

**Step 2 — Convert into an Incremental Update Rule.** We want to estimate $Q^*$ but we only have a noisy target $Y_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$. Treat $Q(s,a)$ as a parameter being fit to minimize the squared error $(Y_t - Q(s,a))^2$. Take a stochastic gradient descent step w.r.t. $Q(s,a)$:

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_t(s,a) \;-\; \frac{\alpha_t}{2} \frac{\partial}{\partial Q_t(s,a)}\!\left(Y_t - Q_t(s,a)\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Gradient.**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial Q_t(s,a)}\!\left(Y_t - Q_t(s,a)\right)^{2} \;=\; -2\left(Y_t - Q_t(s,a)\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Gradient Back.**

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_t(s,a) + \alpha_t\left(Y_t - Q_t(s,a)\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Target.** Substitute $Y_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(s', a')$:

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_t(s,a) + \alpha_t\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_t(s', a') - Q_t(s,a)\,\right]
$$

**Final — The Canonical Q-Learning Update.** This is the textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,Q(s,a) \;\longleftarrow\; Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]\,}
$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE] **Why This is a Fixed-Point Iteration**
> At convergence, the update must satisfy $Q_{t+1}(s,a) = Q_t(s,a)$, which requires the bracketed term to equal zero, i.e., $Q(s,a) = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ — exactly the Bellman optimality condition. Thus, Q-Learning is a *stochastic fixed-point iteration* converging to the unique optimal action-value function $Q^*$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (Gridworld)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Board Exam Pattern — Numerical from Q-Learning on a 1-D Gridworld is a frequent 14-mark question.**

**Problem Setup (3-state chain):**
- States: $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$
- Actions: $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{right}, \text{left}\}$ (right terminates at $s_3$ with reward +10; left from $s_1$ terminates with reward +1)
- Discount factor: $\gamma = 0.9$
- Learning rate: $\alpha = 0.5$
- Initial Q-table: $Q(s,a) = 0$ for all entries

**Episode Trace (assume agent starts at $s_1$):**

| Step | $s$ | $a$ | $r$ | $s'$ | $\max_{a'} Q(s',a')$ | $Q(s,a)$ before | $Q(s,a)$ after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s_1$ | right | 0 | $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | $0 + 0.5[0 + 0.9(0) - 0] = 0$ |
| 2 | $s_2$ | right | 0 | $s_3$ | 0 | 0 | $0 + 0.5[0 + 0.9(0) - 0] = 0$ |
| 3 | $s_3$ | right | +10 | terminal | 0 | 0 | $0 + 0.5[10 + 0.9(0) - 0] = 5$ |
| 4 | $s_2$ | right | 0 | $s_3$ | 5 | 0 | $0 + 0.5[0 + 0.9(5) - 0] = 2.25$ |
| 5 | $s_1$ | right | 0 | $s_2$ | 2.25 | 0 | $0 + 0.5[0 + 0.9(2.25) - 0] = 1.0125$ |

**Step-by-step detail for Step 3:**

$$
Q(s_3, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,10 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_3,\text{right}), Q(s_3,\text{left})) - 0\,\right]
$$

$$
Q(s_3, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5 \cdot 10 \;=\; 5
$$

**Step-by-step detail for Step 4:**

$$
Q(s_2, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_3,\text{right}), Q(s_3,\text{left})) - 0\,\right]
$$

$$
Q(s_2, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0.9 \cdot 5) \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 4.5 \;=\; 2.25
$$

**Step-by-step detail for Step 5:**

$$
Q(s_1, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,0 + 0.9 \cdot \max(Q(s_2,\text{right}), Q(s_2,\text{left})) - 0\,\right]
$$

$$
Q(s_1, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5 \cdot (0.9 \cdot 2.25) \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 2.025 \;=\; 1.0125
$$

**Interpretation:** $Q(s_1, \text{right}) = 1.0125$ represents the discounted expected future reward of moving right from the start state. Repeating episodes with $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration will continue to refine these values toward $Q^*$.

## 3.3 Mathematical Proof Sketch — Why the Max Operator Creates an Off-Policy Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's Favorite 14-Marker — "Why is Q-Learning off-policy?"**

Consider two policies:
- **Behavior policy $\mu$** (the one used to *generate* data, e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy)
- **Target policy $\pi$** (the one we are *learning about*)

Q-Learning's target is:

$$
Y_t^{\text{QL}} \;=\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')
$$

The $\max_{a'}$ operator implicitly defines a **deterministic greedy target policy** $\pi(s') = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, because taking the max is equivalent to sampling from a one-hot distribution at the argmax. The update therefore estimates the action-value function of $\pi$, not of $\mu$. Since $\pi \neq \mu$ in general (during training), the algorithm is **off-policy**.

Contrast this with **SARSA** (the *on-policy* cousin):

$$
Y_t^{\text{SARSA}} \;=\; r + \gamma\, Q(s', a'_{\text{actual}})
$$

Here $a'_{\text{actual}}$ is whatever $\mu$ *actually* sampled — so the target is computed under the same policy that generated the experience. Hence SARSA is on-policy.

## 3.4 Convergence Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992) — Statement

> [!NOTE] **For KTU 14-mark "describe" or "prove" type questions**

**Theorem:** Consider an MDP where all rewards are bounded by some finite $R_{\max}$ and where the learning-rate schedule $\alpha_t(s,a)$ satisfies the Robbins-Monro conditions for every state-action pair:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) \;=\; \infty \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^{2}(s, a) \;<\; \infty
$$

Then, as $t \to \infty$, $Q_t(s,a) \to Q^*(s,a)$ with probability 1, for all $(s,a)$, provided all state-action pairs continue to be visited infinitely often.

**Engineering Interpretation:** The first condition ensures that learning never "freezes" — there is always room to keep updating. The second condition ensures that the stochastic noise from single-sample updates is squashed to zero, so estimates settle down rather than oscillating forever.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Q-Learning Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The following **Mermaid block diagram** captures the *complete* functional architecture of a tabular Q-Learning agent interacting with a stochastic environment:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Episode: Initialize state s] --> B[Read current Q-Table]
    B --> C{Action Selection via Epsilon-Greedy}
    C -->|w.p. 1 - eps| D1[Pick argmax Q over actions]
    C -->|w.p. eps| D2[Pick random action uniformly]
    D1 --> E[Execute action a in environment]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Environment returns reward r and next state s']
    F --> G[Compute TD Target: r + gamma * max over a' of Q s' a']
    G --> H[Compute TD Error delta = TD Target - Q s a]
    H --> I[Update Q s a: Q s a = Q s a + alpha times delta]
    I --> J{Terminal state reached?}
    J -->|No| K[Set s = s' and loop back to step B]
    J -->|Yes| L[End of Episode: Anneal epsilon and alpha]
    L --> M{More episodes?}
    M -->|Yes| A
    M -->|No| N[Output learned Q-Table and greedy policy]
```

## 4.2 Q-Learning Backup Diagram (Conceptual Tree)

The **backup diagram** visualizes how information propagates backward through time and across the action space. Each root node represents a state-value; each leaf represents a possible future state. Q-Learning's backup is a **full-width** sweep — it takes the maximum over all actions in the next state:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Q s a - Current state-action pair] --> TARG[TD Target: r + gamma times max over actions]
    TARG --> T1[Action a1 in state s']
    TARG --> T2[Action a2 in state s']
    TARG --> T3[Action a3 in state s']
    TARG --> T4[Action a4 in state s']
    T1 --> MAX1{Q s' a1}
    T2 --> MAX1
    T3 --> MAX1
    T4 --> MAX1
    MAX1 -.->|Maximum value bootstrapped back| ROOT
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram:** Each of the four child nodes is a *candidate* next action in state $s'$. The `MAX1` diamond collapses all four candidates into the single largest Q-value, which is then *bootstrapped* back into $Q(s,a)$. This is the visual essence of the $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ operator in the Q-Learning update.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Full Q-Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Environment Block]
        E1[Transition Function P s' given s a]
```
