```mermaid
        E2[Reward Function R s a s']
    end
    subgraph AGENT[Agent Block]
        A1[Behavior Policy: Epsilon-Greedy over Q]
        A2[Action Sampler: a from A1]
        A3[Q-Table Storage: states times actions]
        A4[TD Target Calculator: r + gamma times max]
        A5[Update Rule: Q s a = Q s a + alpha times delta]
    end
    subgraph MON[Monitoring Block]
        M1[Episode Logger]
        M2[Hyperparameter Scheduler: decay epsilon and alpha]
        M3[Convergence Detector]
    end
    A2 -->|action a| E1
    E1 -->|next state s'| A1
    E2 -->|reward r| A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A3
    A1 --> MON
    A5 --> MON
    A2 --> MON
    M2 --> A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight — Component Roles**
> - **Environment block** is treated as a black box; we only *sample* from it, never inspect its internals (this is the *model-free* assumption).
> - **Behavior policy** is decoupled from the **target** of the update (the $\max$). This physical separation in the architecture is what embodies the *off-policy* property.
> - **Monitoring block** is optional in pure research code but **essential** in production RL deployments for stability, reproducibility, and KTU lab-records evidence.

## 4.4 Comparative Algorithm Flow: Q-Learning vs. SARSA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QL[Q-Learning: off-policy] --> Q1[Behavior: epsilon-greedy]
    Q1 --> Q2[TD Target: r + gamma * max a prime Q s prime a prime]
    Q2 --> Q3[Target Policy: greedy w.r.t. Q]
    SA[SARSA: on-policy] --> S1[Behavior: epsilon-greedy]
    S1 --> S2[Sample next action a prime from epsilon-greedy]
    S2 --> S3[TD Target: r + gamma * Q s prime a prime]
    S3 --> S4[Target Policy: same as behavior policy]
    Q3 -.->|Differs in target| QL
    S4 -.->|Matches behavior| SA
```

> [!TIP] **KTU Quick Recall Hook:** *"Q-Learning **max**es, SARSA **samples**"* — a single-sentence mnemonic to remember which operator each algorithm uses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q1. Define Q-Learning. Why is it classified as a model-free and off-policy algorithm? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Q-Learning is a **model-free, off-policy, value-based reinforcement learning algorithm** that learns the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ by iteratively applying a temporal-difference (TD) update rule. It is called **model-free** because it does not require prior knowledge of the environment's transition probabilities $P(s' \mid s, a)$ or reward function $R(s, a, s')$ — learning happens purely from sampled experience tuples $(s, a, r, s')$. It is called **off-policy** because the target of its update, $r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, is computed using a **greedy target policy** (the $\max$ implicitly selects the best action), while the data is generated by a different **behavior policy** (typically $\varepsilon$-greedy). This decoupling allows the algorithm to learn the optimal policy even while exploring suboptimally.

---

### Q2. State the Q-Learning update rule and explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Q-Learning update rule is:

$$
Q(s, a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s, a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)\,\right]
$$

The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ quantifies how much the agent values **future rewards** relative to immediate ones. A value of $\gamma$ close to 1 makes the agent far-sighted (cares about long-term cumulative return); a value close to 0 makes it myopic (cares only about the immediate reward $r$). It is mathematically necessary to ensure the infinite-horizon return $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ remains bounded, and it appears inside the bootstrap term $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ to propagate long-term value information back through the Q-table.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a).** Explain the Bellman Optimality Equation for action values and derive the Q-Learning update rule from it using stochastic approximation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the action-value function is:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;=\; \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)\!\left[\,R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')\,\right]
$$

This equation states that the optimal Q-value of taking action $a$ in state $s$ equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted optimal future value.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Sample-Based Approximation.** Since $P$ is unknown (model-free), replace the expectation with a single sample:

$$
Q^{*}(s, a) \;\approx\; r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^{*}(s', a')
$$

**Step 2 — Stochastic Gradient Descent on Squared Error.** Define the loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(Y_t - Q(s,a))^2$ where $Y_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$.

**Step 3 — Gradient Computation.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(s,a)} \;=\; -(Y_t - Q(s,a))
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient Descent Update.**

$$
Q_{t+1}(s,a) \;=\; Q_t(s,a) - \alpha_t \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_t(s,a)} \;=\; Q_t(s,a) + \alpha_t(Y_t - Q_t(s,a))
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Target.**

$$
\boxed{\,Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)\,\right]\,}
$$

This is the canonical Q-Learning update, a stochastic fixed-point iteration converging to $Q^*$.

---

**Q3(b).** With reference to the convergence theorem by Watkins and Dayan (1992), list the two sufficient conditions on the learning rate $\alpha_t(s, a)$. Why are both required? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The two **Robbins-Monro conditions** on the learning-rate schedule $\alpha_t(s,a)$ are:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) \;=\; \infty \qquad \text{(Condition 1: divergence)}
$$

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \alpha_t^{2}(s, a) \;<\; \infty \qquad \text{(Condition 2: square-summability)}
$$

**Why both are required:**

- **Condition 1** guarantees that the cumulative magnitude of updates is infinite, meaning the algorithm never *freezes* prematurely before reaching the optimum. If the sum converged to a finite value, the algorithm would stop adjusting and could leave $Q$ far from $Q^*$.

- **Condition 2** guarantees that the *squared* step sizes are summable, which by comparison-test arguments ensures that the stochastic noise introduced by single-sample Monte Carlo updates is squashed to zero (i.e., the variance of the estimate vanishes). Without this, $Q_t$ would oscillate around $Q^*$ forever due to persistent gradient noise.

**Common schedules that satisfy both:** $\alpha_t = 1/t$, $\alpha_t = 1/t^{0.6}$ (since $0.6 \in (0.5, 1]$).

**Practical caveat:** In KTU exam answers, also mention that the theorem additionally requires *all state-action pairs to be visited infinitely often* — this is why persistent exploration (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy with $\varepsilon > 0$ always) is mandatory for guaranteed convergence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a).** Consider a 4-state Gridworld with states $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4$ (where $s_4$ is terminal with reward +10 and all other transitions give reward 0). The discount factor is $\gamma = 0.8$ and the learning rate is $\alpha = 0.5$. All Q-values are initially 0. An agent runs one episode with the trajectory:

$$
s_1 \xrightarrow{r=0} s_2 \xrightarrow{r=0} s_3 \xrightarrow{r=+10} s_4 \;(\text{terminal})
$$

Compute the Q-values after this single episode using the Q-Learning update rule. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Initialization:** $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all $(s, a)$. Assume the actions taken were the "right" action (i.e., the only meaningful action in this chain).

**Step 1 — Update $Q(s_3, \text{right})$** using the transition to terminal $s_4$:

$$
Q(s_3, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s_3, \text{right}) + \alpha\!\left[\,r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_4, a') - Q(s_3, \text{right})\,\right]
$$

Since $s_4$ is terminal, $Q(s_4, a') = 0$ for all $a'$:

$$
Q(s_3, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,10 + 0.8(0) - 0\,\right] \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 10 \;=\; 5.0
$$

**Step 2 — Update $Q(s_2, \text{right})$** using transition to $s_3$:

$$
Q(s_2, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,0 + 0.8 \cdot \max_{a'} Q(s_3, a') - 0\,\right]
$$

$$
Q(s_2, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0.5 \cdot (0.8 \cdot 5.0) \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 4.0 \;=\; 2.0
$$

**Step 3 — Update $Q(s_1, \text{right})$** using transition to $s_2$:

$$
Q(s_1, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.5\!\left[\,0 + 0.8 \cdot \max_{a'} Q(s_2, a') - 0\,\right]
$$

$$
Q(s_1, \text{right}) \;\leftarrow\; 0.5 \cdot (0.8 \cdot 2.0) \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 1.6 \;=\; 0.8
$$

**Final Q-Table (post-episode):**

| State | $Q(s, \text{right})$ | $Q(s, \text{left})$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0.8 | 0 |
| $s_2$ | 2.0 | 0 |
| $s_3$ | 5.0 | 0 |
| $s_4$ | 0 (terminal) | 0 (terminal) |

**Greedy policy extracted:** $\pi^*(s_1) = \pi^*(s_2) = \pi^*(s_3) = \text{right}$, which intuitively moves the agent toward the +10 terminal reward.

---

**Q4(b).** Compare Q-Learning with SARSA in terms of (i) on-policy vs. off-policy nature, (ii) update rule, (iii) exploration behavior on the "Cliff Walking" environment, and (iv) optimality vs. safety trade-off. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) On-Policy vs. Off-Policy:**

- **Q-Learning** is **off-policy**: its target uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, which corresponds to a *greedy* target policy that may differ from the $\varepsilon$-greedy behavior policy generating data.
- **SARSA** (State-Action-Reward-State-Action) is **on-policy**: its target uses $Q(s', a'_{\text{actual}})$, where $a'_{\text{actual}}$ is the action actually taken by the behavior policy, so target and behavior coincide.

**(ii) Update Rules Side by Side:**

| Algorithm | Update |
|---|---|
| **Q-Learning** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$ |
| **SARSA** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma Q(s', a') - Q(s,a)]$ |

The only difference is the **$\max$** vs. the **Q-value of the actually-sampled next action**.

**(iii) Cliff Walking Behavior:**

In the classical Cliff Walking gridworld (Sutton & Barto, Example 6.6), the agent must reach a goal while avoiding a cliff that yields a large negative reward. With $\varepsilon$-greedy exploration:

- **Q-Learning** learns the **optimal path** (shortest, right along the cliff edge) because its max-operator is optimistic — it pretends the *best* next action will be taken, ignoring exploratory missteps.
- **SARSA** learns a **safer, longer path** (one row away from the cliff) because it accounts for the real chance that $\varepsilon$-exploration will accidentally step into the cliff, and thus penalizes the risky optimal path.

**(iv) Optimality vs. Safety Trade-off:**

- **Q-Learning** converges to the **optimal policy** in tabular finite MDPs (per Watkins & Dayan, 1992) but may take **risky exploratory actions** during training that hurt cumulative return.
- **SARSA** converges to a **near-optimal but safe policy** that accounts for exploration noise — its asymptotic performance is often better in environments with high penalty for unsafe actions.

**Mnemonic for exams:** *"Q-Learning asks: what's the best I could do? SARSA asks: what will I actually do?"*

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up max vs. sampled next action** — Writing $Q(s', a')$ instead of $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ in Q-Learning costs full marks. Always verify: Q-Learning uses **$\max$**, SARSA uses **sampled** $a'$.
> 2. **Forgetting to terminate the TD target at terminal states** — When $s'$ is terminal, the bootstrap term $\gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ must be set to **0**, not to the last known $Q$. This is a one-mark deduction trap.
> 3. **Mis-stating off-policy** — Saying "Q-Learning does not explore" is wrong. It explores via the *behavior* policy; it is *off-policy* because the *target* differs.
> 4. **Omitting Robbins-Monro conditions in convergence proofs** — A 14-marker "prove/discuss convergence" question without these two conditions is incomplete.
> 5. **Sign errors in numerical problems** — Be careful with the $\delta_t = Y_t - Q_t$ substitution; many students get the sign of $\alpha \delta$ reversed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] **Print this section for last-minute exam revision**

- ✅ **Q-Learning is model-free, off-policy, value-based, TD(0).**
- ✅ **Update Rule:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$
- ✅ **The $\max$ operator is what makes Q-Learning off-policy** — it implicitly assumes a greedy target policy.
- ✅ **Exploration is handled separately** by the *behavior* policy, most commonly $\varepsilon$-greedy.
- ✅ **Hyperparameters to remember:** $\alpha$ (learning rate), $\gamma$ (discount factor), $\varepsilon$ (exploration rate).
- ✅ **Convergence Theorem (Watkins & Dayan, 1992):** $Q_t \to Q^*$ w.p. 1 if (a) all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often, (b) Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$ hold, and (c) rewards are bounded.
- ✅ **Comparison with SARSA:** SARSA uses the actual next action $a'$ in its target → on-policy. Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'}$ → off-policy.
- ✅ **Optimal policy extraction:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ once learning has converged.
- ✅ **Common real-world use cases:** Game AI (Atari via DQN, Backgammon via TD-Gammon), robotics navigation, network routing, recommendation engines.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Tabular Q-Learning is infeasible for huge state spaces; this motivates *function approximation* (e.g., Deep Q-Networks, Module 4 / 5 in your KTU syllabus).
- ✅ **Exploration vs. Exploitation dilemma:** $\varepsilon$-greedy, Boltzmann/softmax, UCB, and optimistic initialization are standard remedies.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Q-Learning **max**es, SARSA **samples**."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SARSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SARSA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **SARSA (State-Action-Reward-State-Action)** is an **on-policy Temporal Difference (TD) learning** control algorithm used in Reinforcement Learning (RL) to estimate the optimal action-value function $Q^\pi(s,a)$. The name is derived from the quintuple of elements required for a single update: $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.

The algorithm updates the value of a state-action pair based on the **actual action taken in the next state** (according to the agent's current behaviour policy), rather than the maximum over all possible actions. This fundamental distinction makes SARSA an **on-policy** method, as it learns the value of the policy it is actually executing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cautious Taxi Driver**
> Imagine a self-learning taxi driver navigating a city:
> - **(S)** The current intersection.
> - **(A)** The actual turn the driver makes (left/right/straight).
> - **(R)** The passenger's satisfaction score for that segment.
> - **(S')** The next intersection reached.
> - **(A')** The actual turn the driver *plans* to make at the next intersection (based on current habit, not the "best" theoretical turn).
>
> The driver updates his mental map using the turn he *will really take*, not the turn he *wishes* he could take in hindsight. This is exactly the **on-policy** philosophy of SARSA — learning from the policy that is actually being followed.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **On-Policy**: Learns the value of the policy currently being executed (SARSA).
> - **Off-Policy**: Learns the optimal policy while following a different behaviour policy (Q-Learning).
> - **TD Target**: $R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.
> - **TD Error**: $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)$.
> - **Convergence Guarantee**: Under standard stochastic approximation assumptions, SARSA converges to the optimal action-value function $Q^*(s,a)$ with probability 1.

### 1.4 Why SARSA Matters

| Aspect | Significance in Engineering |
|---|---|
| **Sample Efficiency** | Updates after every step, no need to wait for episode termination |
| **Online Learning** | Suitable for real-time control systems (robotics, autonomous vehicles) |
| **Safety** | On-policy nature avoids overestimation bias, critical in safety-sensitive RL applications |
| **Convergence** | Proven convergence under Robbins-Monro learning-rate conditions |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How SARSA Works

SARSA follows a strict, repeating cycle that directly mirrors the quintuple in its name:

1. **Initialize** the Q-table $Q(s,a)$ arbitrarily for all $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and $a \in \mathcal{A}(s)$.
2. **Observe** the current state $S_t$ at time $t$.
3. **Select** action $A_t$ from $S_t$ using a policy derived from Q (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy).
4. **Execute** $A_t$ in the environment and **observe** the immediate reward $R_{t+1}$ and the next state $S_{t+1}$.
5. **Select** the next action $A_{t+1}$ from $S_{t+1}$ using the same $\varepsilon$-greedy policy.
6. **Update** the Q-value using the SARSA update rule.
7. **Set** $S_t \leftarrow S_{t+1}$, $A_t \leftarrow A_{t+1}$ and repeat from step 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> - Steps 3 and 5 are *symmetric*: both select actions via the **same** policy. This is what makes SARSA on-policy.
> - Step 5 is the defining difference from Q-Learning (which would take $\max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a)$).
> - Step 7 is the *bootstrap loop*: the agent's current action becomes the "next action" in the next iteration.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value symbols to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SARSA Update Rule** | $Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\bigr]$ | $\alpha \in (0,1]$, $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 2 | **TD Target** | $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1})$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| 3 | **TD Error** | $\delta_t = Y_t - Q(S_t,A_t) = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| 4 | **Bellman Optimality (Action-Value)** | $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1},a') \,\big\vert\, S_t=s, A_t=a\bigr]$ | Expectation operator |
| 5 | **$\varepsilon$-Greedy Policy** | $\pi(a\vert s) = \begin{cases} 1-\varepsilon+\frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert} & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s,a') \\ \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\varepsilon \in [0,1]$ |
| 6 | **GLIE Condition (Convergence)** | $\sum_t \alpha_t(s,a) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2(s,a) < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro |
| 7 | **Convergence Theorem** | $Q_t \rightarrow Q^*$ w.p. 1 as $t \rightarrow \infty$ under GLIE | Probability statement |
| 8 | **Q-Learning vs SARSA Target** | SARSA: $R+\gamma Q(S',A')$; Q-Learning: $R+\gamma \max_{a'} Q(S',a')$ | Comparative |
| 9 | **Discounted Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative reward |
| 10 | **Action-Value Definition** | $Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[G_t \vert S_t=s, A_t=a\bigr]$ | Expected return |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Driving**: On-policy methods like SARSA are preferred in pedestrian-rich urban environments because they account for the *actual* exploratory actions the agent will take, avoiding catastrophic "maximum-overestimation" failures.
- **Robotic Manipulation**: Safe exploration under constrained action spaces.
- **Process Control**: Industrial controllers where the policy in deployment must match the policy used for learning.
- **Game AI**: Cliff-walking problem — a classic KTU illustration where SARSA learns the *safer* longer path, while Q-Learning learns the *riskier* optimal path.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the SARSA Update Rule from First Principles

We start from the **action-value Bellman equation** for policy $\pi$:

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \,\big\vert\, S_t=s, A_t=a\bigr]
$$

This equation states that the value of taking action $a$ in state $s$ equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state-action pair.

**Step 1 — Replace expectation with a sample-based unbiased estimate.** Because we are operating in a model-free setting (we do not know the transition probabilities $P(s'\vert s,a)$), we substitute the stochastic expectation with the *single-sample realization* that the agent actually experiences during interaction:

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) \;\approx\; R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Temporal Difference (TD) Target.** The right-hand side of Step 1 is called the **TD target**, denoted $Y_t$:

$$
Y_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})
$$

**Step 3 — Define the TD Error.** The discrepancy between the current estimate and the TD target is the **TD error** $\delta_t$:

$$
\delta_t \;=\; Y_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Stochastic Gradient Descent on the squared TD error.** We minimize the loss $\mathcal{L}_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^2$ with respect to $Q(S_t,A_t)$. The negative gradient with respect to $Q(S_t,A_t)$ is $-\delta_t$. A step of gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha$ yields:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial Q(S_t,A_t)} \;=\; Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\delta_t$ back into the update equation:**

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \bigl[ R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t, A_t) \bigr]
$$

This is the **canonical SARSA update rule**, fully derived.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style, 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Consider a 3-state environment with the following transition structure. Actions: $\uparrow$ (up) and $\rightarrow$ (right). Discount factor $\gamma = 0.9$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$. All Q-values are initially **0**.

**Episode trajectory** (following the current $\varepsilon$-greedy policy):

| Time step $t$ | $S_t$ | $A_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $S_{t+1}$ | $A_{t+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A | $\rightarrow$ | +5 | B | $\rightarrow$ |
| 1 | B | $\rightarrow$ | +3 | C | $\uparrow$ |
| 2 | C | $\uparrow$ | +10 | Terminal | — |

**Update 1 — at $t = 0$:**

$$
Q(A, \rightarrow) \;\leftarrow\; Q(A,\rightarrow) + 0.1\bigl[5 + 0.9 \cdot Q(B,\rightarrow) - Q(A,\rightarrow)\bigr]
$$

Since all Q-values are initially 0:

$$
Q(A,\rightarrow) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.1\bigl[5 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0\bigr] \;=\; 0.5
$$

**Update 2 — at $t = 1$:**

$$
Q(B, \rightarrow) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.1\bigl[3 + 0.9 \cdot Q(C,\uparrow) - 0\bigr] \;=\; 0.1 \cdot 3 \;=\; 0.3
$$

**Update 3 — at $t = 2$:** (Terminal state; $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) = 0$)

$$
Q(C, \uparrow) \;\leftarrow\; 0 + 0.1\bigl[10 + 0.9 \cdot 0 - 0\bigr] \;=\; 1.0
$$

**Final Q-table after this single episode:**

| State | $\uparrow$ | $\rightarrow$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | **0.5** |
| B | 0 | **0.3** |
| C | **1.0** | 0 |

### 3.3 Convergence Theorem (Stochastic Approximation Basis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Singh et al., 2000):** SARSA converges to the optimal action-value function $Q^*$ with probability 1 if:
> 1. The policy sequence is **GLIE** (Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration).
> 2. The step-sizes satisfy the Robbins-Monro conditions: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$.
> 3. All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SARSA Algorithm Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the iterative control flow of the SARSA algorithm. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> init1["Initialize Q(s,a) arbitrarily for all s, a"]
    init1 --> init2["Initialize state S to starting state"]
    init2 --> selA1["Select A from S using policy derived from Q (e.g. epsilon-greedy)"]
    selA1 --> loopStart{For each time step}
    loopStart --> takeA1["Take action A, observe R and S prime"]
    takeA1 --> selA2["Select A prime from S prime using same policy"]
    selA2 --> updateQ["Q(S,A) <-- Q(S,A) + alpha * ( R + gamma * Q(S prime, A prime) - Q(S,A) )"]
    updateQ --> assign1["S <-- S prime"]
    assign1 --> assign2["A <-- A prime"]
    assign2 --> loopStart
    loopStart --> check1{Is S terminal?}
    check1 -- No --> selA1
    check1 -- Yes --> epEnd1(["End Episode"])
    epEnd1 --> nextEp1{More episodes?}
    nextEp1 -- Yes --> init2
    nextEp1 -- No --> stop1([Stop])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SARSA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envBlock["Environment"] -- Reward R and Next State S prime --> agentBlock["RL Agent Core"]
    policyBlock["Behavior Policy (epsilon-greedy)"] -- Action A prime --> agentBlock
    agentBlock -- Action A --> envBlock
    agentBlock --> qtableBlock["Q-Table Storage"]
    qtableBlock -- Q(S,A) value --> agentBlock
    agentBlock --> updateBlock["SARSA Update Module"]
    updateBlock -- Updated Q-value --> qtableBlock
```

### 4.3 SARSA vs Q-Learning — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startS["Current State S and Action A"] --> rewardR["Observe Reward R and Next State S prime"]
    rewardR --> branchDecide{On-Policy or Off-Policy Decision}
    branchDecide -- On-Policy SARSA --> selAprime1["Select A prime using current policy"]
    branchDecide -- Off-Policy Q-Learning --> maxA["Take max over all actions in S prime"]
    selAprime1 --> targetSarsa["TD Target = R + gamma * Q(S prime, A prime)"]
    maxA --> targetQ["TD Target = R + gamma * max_a Q(S prime, a)"]
    targetSarsa --> updateSarsa["Update Q(S,A)"]
    targetQ --> updateQ["Update Q(S,A)"]
    updateSarsa --> endNode(["End"])
    updateQ --> endNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is SARSA in Reinforcement Learning? Why is it called an on-policy algorithm?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> SARSA stands for **State-Action-Reward-State-Action**. It is an on-policy Temporal Difference (TD) learning algorithm used to estimate the action-value function $Q^\pi(s,a)$. It is called *on-policy* because the TD target uses the action $A_{t+1}$ selected by the *current behaviour policy* (e.g., $\varepsilon$-greedy) rather than the greedy maximum over all actions. Hence, SARSA learns the value of the policy it is actually executing. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the SARSA update rule and explain each term.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The SARSA update rule is:
>
> $$Q(S_t,A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t,A_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\bigr]$$
>
> where $\alpha$ is the learning rate, $\gamma$ is the discount factor, $R_{t+1}$ is the observed reward, and $Q(S_{t+1},A_{t+1})$ is the value of the *next state-action pair* as selected by the current policy. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
> **(a)** Derive the SARSA update rule from the Bellman optimality equation. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare SARSA with Q-Learning in terms of (i) policy type, (ii) TD target, (iii) convergence guarantees, and (iv) suitability for the cliff-walking problem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Write the action-value Bellman equation for any policy $\pi$:

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \,\big\vert\, S_t=s, A_t=a\bigr] \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Step 2: Drop the expectation (model-free, single sample) and define the TD target:

$$
Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

Step 3: Define the TD error $\delta_t = Y_t - Q(S_t, A_t)$. **(1 mark)**

Step 4: Apply one step of stochastic gradient descent on the squared error $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta_t^2$ with step-size $\alpha$:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \delta_t \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

Step 5: Substitute $\delta_t$ to obtain the final rule:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha\bigl[R_{t+1} + \gamma Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) - Q(S_t,A_t)\bigr] \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

Assumptions: model-free setting, all states and actions discretized, Robbins-Monro step-size conditions hold, GLIE policy.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | SARSA | Q-Learning |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Policy type | On-policy | Off-policy |
| (ii) TD target | $R + \gamma Q(S', A')$ | $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S', a')$ |
| (iii) Convergence | Converges w.p.1 to $Q^*$ under GLIE | Converges w.p.1 as long as all pairs explored |
| (iv) Cliff-walking | Learns **safer longer path** | Learns **risky optimal path** through cliff edge |

**(1.5 marks per aspect × 4 + 1 mark for any one extra correct technical detail.)**

---

> **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the SARSA algorithm. Include a flowchart in your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A reinforcement learning agent operates in a 3-state environment (A, B, C) with two actions per state ($\uparrow$ and $\rightarrow$). Let $\alpha = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.9$. The Q-table is initialized to all zeros. Given the trajectory shown below, compute the updated Q-values using SARSA. **(7 marks)**
>
> | $t$ | $S_t$ | $A_t$ | $R_{t+1}$ | $S_{t+1}$ | $A_{t+1}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | A | $\rightarrow$ | +5 | B | $\rightarrow$ |
> | 1 | B | $\rightarrow$ | +3 | C | $\uparrow$ |
> | 2 | C | $\uparrow$ | +10 | Terminal | — |

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Algorithm Procedure (7 marks):**

1. Initialize $Q(s,a)$ arbitrarily; set $Q(\text{terminal},\cdot) = 0$. **(1 mark)**
2. For each episode: initialize $S$; choose $A$ from $S$ using $\varepsilon$-greedy. **(1.5 marks)**
3. Repeat for each time step: take action $A$, observe $R$ and $S'$. **(1 mark)**
4. Choose $A'$ from $S'$ using $\varepsilon$-greedy. **(1 mark)**
5. Apply update: $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha[R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A)]$. **(1.5 marks)**
6. Set $S \leftarrow S'$, $A \leftarrow A'$; continue until $S$ is terminal. **(0.5 marks)**
7. Include a labelled flowchart showing the loop. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Update at $t=0$** (using $Q(B,\rightarrow)=0$ initially):

$$
Q(A,\rightarrow) = 0 + 0.1 \times [5 + 0.9 \times 0 - 0] = 0.5 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Update at $t=1$** (using $Q(C,\uparrow)=0$ initially):

$$
Q(B,\rightarrow) = 0 + 0.1 \times [3 + 0.9 \times 0 - 0] = 0.3 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Update at $t=2$** (terminal state, $Q(\text{terminal}, \cdot) = 0$):

$$
Q(C,\uparrow) = 0 + 0.1 \times [10 + 0.9 \times 0 - 0] = 1.0 \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Final Q-table (1 mark):**

| State | $\uparrow$ | $\rightarrow$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 0.5 |
| B | 0 | 0.3 |
| C | 1.0 | 0 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently write the Q-Learning update ($R + \gamma \max Q$) in SARSA derivations, losing 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to set $A \leftarrow A'$ after the update — this breaks the on-policy loop and loses 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical problems, students often forget the terminal-state condition $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1}) = 0$ when $S_{t+1}$ is terminal.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing "SARSA is off-policy" — this is the most frequent conceptual error and attracts a full 3-mark deduction in Part A.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SARSA = State-Action-Reward-State-Action**; an **on-policy** TD(0) control algorithm.
- ✅ Update rule: $Q(S,A) \leftarrow Q(S,A) + \alpha[R + \gamma Q(S',A') - Q(S,A)]$.
- ✅ TD target is computed using the **action actually selected** by the current policy, not the greedy max.
- ✅ Converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 under **GLIE** + **Robbins-Monro** step-size conditions.
- ✅ In the **cliff-walking** problem, SARSA learns the safer path; Q-Learning learns the risky optimal path.
- ✅ Two convergence requirements: (1) infinite exploration, (2) step-sizes decay to zero but not too fast.
- ✅ The update uses five elements — missing any one breaks the on-policy chain.
- ✅ Differentiator from Q-Learning: presence of $\max$ operator in the TD target (absent in SARSA, present in Q-Learning).
- ✅ Engineering value: preferred in **safety-critical** and **real-time online** learning applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eligibility Traces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Eligibility Traces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Eligibility Traces** are one of the basic mechanisms of reinforcement learning (RL). They are *temporary records* of the occurrence of a state (or state–action pair), which *mark* the parameters associated with that state (or pair) as *eligible* for undergoing learning changes. When a temporal-difference (TD) error occurs, only the *eligible* states or actions are assigned credit or blame for the error. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Eligibility traces unify and generalize **Temporal Difference (TD)** and **Monte Carlo (MC)** methods. They provide an efficient, incremental way to blend multi-step returns without waiting until the end of an episode. Mathematically, an eligibility trace $e_t(s) \in \mathbb{R}^+$ is a scalar value assigned to every state $s$ at each time step $t$, decaying over time unless reinforced by visitation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **teacher grading a class assignment**: 1. A student submits a paper today. You have not yet graded it. 2. As the grading team circulates, the paper gets an *eligibility stamp* the moment it lands on the desk — the longer it sits, the fainter the stamp becomes (decay). 3. When you finally reveal the grade (the **TD error**), you apply the grade to *every paper* still bearing a visible stamp, in proportion to how visible (eligible) the stamp still is. In this analogy: - The **paper** = a state visited. - The **stamp's fading** = the trace decay factor $\gamma \lambda$. - The **grade** = the TD error $\delta_t$. - The **correction** = the weight update on the value function. ## 1.3 Key Parameters (Syllabus-Defined Constants) | Symbol | Name | Range | Role | |:------:|:----:|:-----:|:----| | $\lambda$ | Trace-decay / *lambda* parameter | $[0, 1]$ | Controls the horizon of credit assignment | | $\gamma$ | Discount factor | $[0, 1]$ | Standard RL discount for future rewards | | $\alpha$ | Step-size / learning rate | $(0, 1]$ | Magnitude of the value update | | $\delta_t$ | TD error | $\mathbb{R}$ | One-step bootstrapped error signal | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Behaviors:** > - $\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow$ Only the immediately preceding state learns (one-step TD). > - $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ Equivalent to the Monte Carlo return for offline updating. > - $\gamma = 0 \Rightarrow$ No eligibility carries forward; only the current state updates. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Views of Eligibility Traces Eligibility traces can be understood through **two equivalent, complementary perspectives**: ### (A) Forward View — *Theoretical* - Looks ahead from each visited state to *future* rewards. - Defines the **$\lambda$-return** $G_t^\lambda$, a geometric blend of all $n$-step returns: $$G_t^\lambda = (1 - \lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$$ - Each $G_t^{(n)}$ is the $n$-step return from time $t$: $$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$ - Learning with the $\lambda$-return is **theoretical** because it requires waiting $n$ steps to know $G_t^{(n)}$, so it cannot be updated online. ### (B) Backward View — *Mechanistic / Implementable* - Looks *backward* in time from the current TD error to recently visited states. - Maintains an eligibility trace $e_t(s)$ for every state and updates all eligible states *incrementally*: $$V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$$ where $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$$ ## 2.2 Trace Update Rules | Trace Type | Update Rule | Behavior | |:----------:|:-----------:|:--------| | **Accumulating** | $e_t(s) = \gamma \lambda \, e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Adds 1 every visit; can grow unbounded for frequently visited states. | | **Replacing** | $e_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Resets trace to 1 on visit; cap avoids trace blow-up. | | **Dutch** | $e_t(s) = (1-\alpha)\,\gamma\lambda\,e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Hybrid; better theoretical properties; reduces bias accumulation. | Where $\mathbf{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Replacing traces** are widely used in practice (e.g., Sarsa($\lambda$) in control) because they prevent long trajectories through a single state from overwhelming the update. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Domain | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------| | 1 | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda\,e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Accumulating trace recursion | Scalar $\geq 0$ | | 2 | $e_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Replacing trace recursion | Scalar $\in \{0, 1\}$ | | 3 | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | One-step TD error | Reward units | | 4 | $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \, \delta_t \, e_t(s)$ | TD($\lambda$) value update | Scalar | | 5 | $G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)}$ | $\lambda$-return (forward view) | Reward units | | 6 | $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\gamma^{k-1}R_{t+k} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$ | $n$-step return | Reward units | | 7 | $G_t^{\lambda s} = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1}G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ | Truncated $\lambda$-return (episodic) | Reward units | | 8 | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1} = 1$ | Normalization of $\lambda$-weights | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical:** Use `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., $\vert V(s) \vert$) to preserve the markdown table structure. The pipe symbol `\vert` will *not* break the table. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Robotics**: Assigning credit for delayed rewards (e.g., a biped robot's stable walking depends on a balance correction made 10 steps earlier). - **Autonomous Driving**: Late reward signals (e.g., completing a safe lane change) need to be traced back to many earlier steering decisions. - **Game AI (AlphaGo, Atari)**: Speeds up learning dramatically vs. pure Monte Carlo by giving intermediate bootstrapped credit. - **Recommender Systems (Industrial RL)**: Traces help attribute long-horizon engagement to early exploratory recommendations. - **Network Routing & Control**: Multi-step delayed cost signals can be propagated backwards efficiently using traces. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Forward (λ-return) and Backward (TD(λ)) Views We prove that **offline updating** using the $\lambda$-return yields the *same* total update to $V(s)$ as a full episode of TD($\lambda$) with accumulating traces. ### Step 1: Define the offline update using the λ-return The total change to state $s$ at the end of an episode of length $T$, visited at time $t$, is: $$\Delta V_{t}(s) = \alpha \left[ G_t^\lambda - V(s) \right]$$ (only the *first* time $s$ is visited in the episode is counted in the offline, *every-visit* form). ### Step 2: Expand the λ-return $$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1} G_t$$ Here $G_t$ is the complete Monte Carlo return, used as the tail term. ### Step 3: Express the n-step return in terms of TD errors It is a standard identity (Sutton & Barto, Eq. 12.2) that: $$G_t^{(n)} - V(S_t) = \sum_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \gamma^{k-t} \delta_k$$ where each $\delta_k$ is the one-step TD error at time $k$. ### Step 4: Substitute into the λ-return expression $$G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) = (1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1} \lambda^{n-1} \sum_{k=t}^{t+n-1} \gamma^{k-t} \delta_k + \lambda^{T-t-1}\sum_{k=t}^{T-1}\gamma^{k-t}\delta_k$$ ### Step 5: Swap the order of summation Swap $\sum_n \sum_k \to \sum_k \sum_n$. For a given TD error $\delta_k$ occurring at time $k \geq t$, the inner coefficient becomes: $$(1-\lambda) \sum_{n=1}^{T-k-1} \lambda^{n-1} \lambda^{k-t} = \lambda^{k-t}(1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-k-1}\lambda^{n-1}$$ ### Step 6: Simplify the inner geometric series Using the standard geometric series identity: $$(1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-k-1}\lambda^{n-1} = 1 - \lambda^{T-k-1}$$ Multiplying by $\lambda^{k-t}$: $$\text{coefficient of }\delta_k = \lambda^{k-t}\bigl(1 - \lambda^{T-k-1}\bigr)$$ ### Step 7: Identify this coefficient as the accumulating trace In the backward TD($\lambda$) algorithm, the eligibility of state $s$ (visited at time $t$) at time $k \geq t$ is: $$e_k(s) = \gamma^{k-t}\lambda^{k-t} \mathbf{1}(s \text{ visited at } t)$$ This matches the coefficient computed above (offline equivalence). Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\Delta V(s) = \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \delta_k \, e_k(s)\;}$$ which is **exactly** the sum of incremental updates performed online by the backward TD($\lambda$) algorithm. ### Conclusion For **offline updating**, the $\lambda$-return algorithm and TD($\lambda$) with accumulating traces are **mathematically identical** — they produce the same final value function update. This is the celebrated **forward–backward equivalence** of eligibility traces. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How λ Shapes the Backup Diagram | $\lambda$ value | Backup Diagram Geometry | Effective Horizon | Update Behavior | |:--------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------------:|:----------------| | $\lambda = 0$ | One-step TD, single edge | 1 step | Pure bootstrapping; fast but high bias | | $\lambda = 0.5$ | Geometric mixture | $\sim \frac{1}{1-\lambda}$ steps | Balanced bias/variance | | $\lambda \to 1$ | Truncated MC return | Episode length | Unbiased; high variance | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Setup:** 4-state episodic trajectory with $\gamma = 1$, rewards $R = [0, 0, 0, 1]$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$. Suppose states $S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3$ are visited in order and the agent updates *all four* states' values using one step of the backward TD($\lambda$). - Initial values: $V(S_0) = 0.2, V(S_1) = 0.3, V(S_2) = 0.4, V(S_3) = 0.0$. - Terminal step: $R_4 = 1$ (assume $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$). **Compute TD errors backward:** $$\delta_3 = R_4 + \gamma V(\text{terminal}) - V(S_3) = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1$$ $$\delta_2 = R_3 + \gamma V(S_3) - V(S_2) = 0 + 1\cdot 0 - 0.4 = -0.4$$ $$\delta_1 = R_2 + \gamma V(S_2) - V(S_1) = 0 + 0.4 - 0.3 = 0.1$$ $$\delta_0 = R_1 + \gamma V(S_1) - V(S_0) = 0 + 0.3 - 0.2 = 0.1$$ **Trace evolution (accumulating, $\gamma \lambda = 0.5$):** $$e_0(S_0) = 1,\; e_0(\text{others}) = 0$$ $$e_1(S_0) = 0.5,\; e_1(S_1) = 1$$ $$e_2(S_0) = 0.25,\; e_2(S_1) = 0.5,\; e_2(S_2) = 1$$ $$e_3(S_0) = 0.125,\; e_3(S_1) = 0.25,\; e_3(S_2) = 0.5,\; e_3(S_3) = 1$$ **Apply updates** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$ for each $t$: - At $t=3$: $V(S_3) \leftarrow 0 + 0.1\cdot 1\cdot 1 = 0.1$ - At $t=2$: $V(S_2) \leftarrow 0.4 + 0.1\cdot(-0.4)\cdot 1 = 0.36$ - At $t=1$: $V(S_1) \leftarrow 0.3 + 0.1\cdot(0.1)\cdot 1 = 0.31$ - At $t=0$: $V(S_0) \leftarrow 0.2 + 0.1\cdot(0.1)\cdot 1 = 0.21$ > [!NOTE] > Note how *every* state in the trajectory received a graded update on each step, scaled by its current eligibility — this is the power of the backward view. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Backward-View Update Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Agent in State St"] --> B["Take Action At"] B --> C["Observe Reward R_t+1 and Next State S_t+1"] C --> D["Compute TD Error delta_t = R_t+1 + gamma*V(S_t+1) - V(S_t)"] D --> E["Update Eligibility Traces: e_t(s) = gamma*lambda*e_t-1(s) + 1 if s==St else 0"] E --> F["For ALL states s: V(s) += alpha*delta_t*e_t(s)"] F --> G["Decay Traces: e_t(s) *= gamma*lambda"] G --> H{"Episode Done?"} H -- "No" --> A H -- "Yes" --> I["Reset Traces to Zero"] I --> J["Start New Episode"] ``` ## 4.2 Forward View: λ-Return as a Geometric Mixture of n-step Returns ```mermaid flowchart LR S_t["State at time t"] --> N1["n=1 step Return
Weight (1-lambda)"] S_t --> N2["n=2 step Return
Weight (1-lambda)*lambda"] S_t --> N3["n=3 step Return
Weight (1-lambda)*lambda^2"] S_t --> N4["n=... step Return
Weight (1-lambda)*lambda^(n-1)"] N1 --> MIX["Weighted Sum"] N2 --> MIX N3 --> MIX N4 --> MIX MIX --> OUT["G_t lambda = lambda-return
Used for offline update"] ``` ## 4.3 Trace Decay Schematic ```mermaid graph LR V0["Visit at t=0
Trace = 1.0"] -- "decay by gamma*lambda" --> V1["t=1
Trace = 0.50"] V1 -- "decay" --> V2["t=2
Trace = 0.25"] V2 -- "decay" --> V3["t=3
Trace = 0.125"] V3 -- "decay" --> V4["t=4
Trace ~ 0.06"] V1 -. "Second visit: add 1" .-> V1b["Trace resets to 1.5 (accumulating)"] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Algorithm Components) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ENV["Environment Interface"] OBS["Observation S_t"] R["Reward R_t+1"] end subgraph MEM["Trace Memory"] E["Eligibility Vector e_t"] V["Value Function V_t"] end subgraph CORE["TD(lambda) Core"] ERR["TD Error Calculator"] UPD["Value Updater"] TDEC["Trace Decayer"] TSET["Trace Setter (visit)"] end subgraph POL["Policy"] PI["Action Selector"] end OBS --> ERR R --> ERR V --> ERR ERR -- "delta_t" --> UPD UPD --> V E --> UPD OBS --> TSET TSET --> E TDEC --> E V --> PI PI --> OBS ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define eligibility traces in reinforcement learning. How do they unify TD and Monte Carlo methods?** **Model Answer:** Eligibility traces are temporary, decaying scalar values $e_t(s)$ associated with each state $s$ at time $t$. They are incremented by 1 whenever $s$ is visited, and decayed by the factor $\gamma \lambda$ at every step. They unify TD and Monte Carlo by acting as a *bridge*: - When $\lambda = 0$, only one-step TD errors update values (pure bootstrapping). - When $\lambda = 1$ (and offline updates are used), the algorithm effectively performs an MC update by summing all future TD errors with full weight. - For $0 < \lambda < 1$, eligibility traces produce a geometrically weighted mixture of $n$-step returns, achieving a controllable bias–variance trade-off. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between accumulating and replacing eligibility traces. When is each preferred?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Accumulating Traces | Replacing Traces | |:-------|:-------------------|:----------------| | Update | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | $e_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | | Re-visit effect | Adds 1 on top of existing trace | Resets trace to 1 | | Boundedness | Can grow > 1 for repeated visits | Bounded by 1 | | Preferred use | Theoretical analyses, episodic tasks | Control (Sarsa-$\lambda$), tasks with long trajectories | Replacing traces are preferred in **control** because they prevent a single frequently visited state from dominating the update. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Theoretical / Derivation Focus* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the $\lambda$-return $G_t^\lambda$ as a forward-view quantity and explain how it reduces to one-step TD and to the Monte Carlo return as special cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Starting from the backward TD($\lambda$) update rule $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$ with accumulating traces, show step-by-step that **offline** updating by TD($\lambda$) is mathematically equivalent to the $\lambda$-return algorithm. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) The $\lambda$-return is defined as a geometric blend of all $n$-step returns: $$G_t^\lambda = (1-\lambda)\sum_{n=1}^{T-t-1}\lambda^{n-1} G_t^{(n)} + \lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$$ where the $n$-step return is: $$G_t^{(n)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \dots + \gamma^{n-1}R_{t+n} + \gamma^{n} V(S_{t+n})$$ **Special cases:** - **$\lambda = 0$:** Only the $n=1$ term survives because $\lambda^0 = 1$ and all higher powers of $\lambda$ vanish. Thus $G_t^{0} = G_t^{(1)} = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1})$, which is the **one-step TD return**. **(3 marks)** - **$\lambda = 1$:** The weights $(1-\lambda)\lambda^{n-1}$ are all zero for finite $n$, but the tail term $\lambda^{T-t-1}G_t$ tends to $G_t$ as the episode terminates. Hence $G_t^{1} = G_t$, the **Monte Carlo return**. **(2 marks)** - **$0 < \lambda < 1$:** A weighted mixture, smoothly interpolating between the two extremes. **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution (b) Following the derivation in **Section 3.1**, the offline total update to $V(s)$ for an episode visit at time $t$ is: $$\Delta V(s) = \alpha \bigl[G_t^\lambda - V(s)\bigr]$$ Using the telescoping identity $G_t^\lambda - V(S_t) = \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{k-t}\lambda^{k-t} \delta_k$ (proved in Steps 3–6 of Section 3.1 by swapping summation and using geometric series): $$\Delta V(s) = \alpha \sum_{k=t}^{T-1} \delta_k \, e_k(s)$$ where the eligibility trace at time $k$ satisfies the recursion $e_k(s) = \gamma\lambda\, e_{k-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$. This is *exactly* the sum of incremental updates performed online by the backward TD($\lambda$) algorithm — confirming forward–backward equivalence offline. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Numerical / Applied Focus* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the difference between **accumulating**, **replacing**, and **Dutch** eligibility traces with their update equations. State one practical advantage of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the trajectory $S_0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to \text{Terminal}$ with rewards $R_1 = 0, R_2 = 0, R_3 = 1$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.2$, discount $\gamma = 1$, and $\lambda = 0.5$. Initial values are $V(S_0) = 0.5, V(S_1) = 0.5, V(S_2) = 0.5$. Compute the updated values of all states using one pass of offline backward TD($\lambda$) with **accumulating** traces. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) | Trace Type | Update Equation | Practical Advantage | |:----------:|:---------------|:--------------------| | **Accumulating** | $e_t(s) = \gamma\lambda\,e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Strongest signal for frequently visited states; theoretically clean. | | **Replacing** | $e_t(s) = \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Bounded by 1; avoids trace blow-up; standard in Sarsa($\lambda$). | | **Dutch** | $e_t(s) = (1-\alpha)\gamma\lambda\,e_{t-1}(s) + \mathbf{1}(S_t = s)$ | Reduces bias accumulation in continuing tasks; better with function approximation. | **(7 marks distributed: 2 for equations, 2 for differences, 3 for practical use-cases)** #### Model Solution (b) **Step 1: Compute TD errors backward (γ = 1, V(terminal) = 0)** $$\delta_2 = R_3 + \gamma V(\text{terminal}) - V(S_2) = 1 + 0 - 0.5 = 0.5$$ $$\delta_1 = R_2 + \gamma V(S_2) - V(S_1) = 0 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 0.0$$ $$\delta_0 = R_1 + \gamma V(S_1) - V(S_0) = 0 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 0.0$$ **Step 2: Trace evolution (accumulating, γλ = 0.5)** | Time $t$ | $e_t(S_0)$ | $e_t(S_1)$ | $e_t(S_2)$ | |:--------:|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:| | 0 | 1.00 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 0.50 | 1.00 | 0 | | 2 | 0.25 | 0.50 | 1.00 | **Step 3: Apply offline cumulative update** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \sum_{t=0}^{2} \delta_t e_t(s)$ For $S_0$: $$\Delta V(S_0) = 0.2 \cdot \bigl[0.0\cdot 1.0 + 0.0\cdot 0.5 + 0.5\cdot 0.25\bigr] = 0.2 \cdot 0.125 = 0.025$$ $$V(S_0)_{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.025 = \mathbf{0.525}$$ For $S_1$: $$\Delta V(S_1) = 0.2 \cdot \bigl[0.0\cdot 0 + 0.0\cdot 1.0 + 0.5\cdot 0.5\bigr] = 0.2 \cdot 0.25 = 0.05$$ $$V(S_1)_{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.05 = \mathbf{0.55}$$ For $S_2$: $$\Delta V(S_2) = 0.2 \cdot \bigl[0.0\cdot 0 + 0.0\cdot 0 + 0.5\cdot 1.0\bigr] = 0.2 \cdot 0.5 = 0.1$$ $$V(S_2)_{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.1 = \mathbf{0.6}$$ **Final updated values:** $V(S_0) = 0.525,\; V(S_1) = 0.55,\; V(S_2) = 0.6$ **(7 marks: 2 trace table + 2 error compute + 3 update computation)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Confusing $\lambda$ with the discount $\gamma$** in trace decay: the trace decays by $\gamma\lambda$, *not* $\lambda$ alone. > 2. **Forgetting to decay traces for non-visited states** — every trace decays at every step, not just for the visited state. > 3. **Using replacing-trace update inside an accumulating-trace question** (or vice-versa). Always read carefully. > 4. **Mixing forward and backward views in derivation** — clearly state which is which. > 5. **In the numerical, forgetting to set $V(\text{terminal}) = 0$** when computing the last TD error. > 6. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid`. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Eligibility trace $e_t(s)$** = decaying memory of state visits; incremented on visit, decayed by $\gamma\lambda$. - [x] **Two views:** *Forward* ($\lambda$-return, theoretical) and *Backward* (TD($\lambda$), implementable). - [x] **Three trace variants:** Accumulating, Replacing, Dutch — know the update rules and trade-offs. - [x] **$\lambda = 0$** $\Rightarrow$ one-step TD. **$\lambda = 1$** $\Rightarrow$ Monte Carlo (offline). - [x] **TD($\lambda$) update:** $V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha \delta_t e_t(s)$. - [x] **TD error:** $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$. - [x] **Equivalence theorem:** Offline $\lambda$-return $\equiv$ offline TD($\lambda$) with accumulating traces. - [x] **Replacing traces** preferred in control (Sarsa($\lambda$)) to prevent trace blow-up. - [x] **Dutch traces** preferred with function approximation and continuing tasks. - [x] **n-step return** $G_t^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\gamma^{k-1}R_{t+k} + \gamma^{n}V(S_{t+n})$ — building block of $\lambda$-return. - [x] **True Online TD($\lambda$)** extends the algorithm to be *exactly* online (not just offline-equivalent). - [x] Always use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`) for absolute value inside markdown tables. - [x] Eligibility traces drastically speed up credit assignment in long-horizon tasks. - [x] **Applications:** robotics, autonomous driving, game AI, recommender systems, network control.

topics.title: Linear Function Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT755 - REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Function Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Linear Function Approximation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Function Approximation (LFA)** is a parametric method used in Reinforcement Learning (RL) to estimate large, high-dimensional value functions (or action-value functions) by representing them as a **linear combination of a fixed set of features**, with the coefficients being a tunable weight vector.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this technique directly addresses the **Curse of Dimensionality** — a phenomenon introduced by Bellman & Dreyfus (1959) and formalized by Sutton & Barto — where tabular storage of Q(s,a) or V(s) becomes infeasible when the state space is large or continuous.

Mathematically, the approximate value function is written as:

$$\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \theta_i \, x_i(s)$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **learnable weight vector** (parameter vector).
- $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the **feature vector** representing state $s$ (a hand-crafted or learned embedding).
- $d$ is the **dimensionality** of the feature space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The course explicitly asks for treatment of *Linear Function Approximation* covering gradient methods (Gradient MC, Semi-gradient TD(0), Semi-gradient TD($\lambda$)), **convergence guarantees**, and **least-squares based methods** (LSTD, LSPI conceptual idea).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are a **real-estate pricing expert** in Kerala. You cannot memorize the price of *every individual house* in Kochi (that would be the **tabular** approach — one entry per house). Instead, you learn a simple rule:

> *"Price ≈ 4500 × (Area in sq.ft) + 800000 × (Bedrooms) − 200000 × (Distance from city center in km)"*

This rule is a **linear function of features**. The coefficients `4500`, `800000`, `−200000` are your **weights** ($\theta_i$). Each house is described by a **feature vector** $\mathbf{x}(s) = [\text{Area}, \text{Bedrooms}, \text{Distance}]^{\top}$.

**Key Insight:** When a new house (state) appears, you don't need to have seen it before. You just plug its features into the linear rule. The model **generalizes** from a finite set of weights across an infinite state space. This is *exactly* what Linear Function Approximation does for an RL agent estimating $V_\pi(s)$ or $Q_\pi(s,a)$.

> [!TIP]
> Think of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ as the "**policy memory**" of the agent and $\mathbf{x}(s)$ as the "**eyes**" through which the agent perceives the world.

---

## 1.3 Why Linear? — The Role of the Approximation Architecture

| Architecture | Functional Form | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\hat{v} = \theta^{\top} x(s)$ | Convex loss surface, **guaranteed convergence** for many algorithms |
| **Non-linear (Neural Net)** | $\hat{v} = f_\theta(s)$ | Highly expressive, but **non-convex**, may diverge |
| **Tabular** | One parameter per state | No generalization, infinite storage for large spaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Learning Outcome (implied CO):** *The student should be able to design value-function approximators and reason about their convergence properties.* Linear methods are the **only** function class where semi-gradient TD methods are guaranteed to converge to a bounded neighborhood of the TD fixed point.

---

## 1.4 Feature Construction (Feature Vectors $\mathbf{x}(s)$)

Before approximation, every state $s$ must be mapped to a feature vector. The KTU syllabus recognizes the following common choices:

1. **Polynomial Features**: For state $s \in \mathbb{R}^k$, $\mathbf{x}(s) = [1, s_1, s_2, s_1 s_2, s_1^2, s_2^2, \dots]^{\top}$.
2. **Radial Basis Functions (RBFs)**: $\mathbf{x}_i(s) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|s - c_i\|^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$, centered at $c_i$.
3. **Coarse Coding (Tile Coding)**: Binary indicator features for overlapping tilings.
4. **Fourier Basis**: $\mathbf{x}_i(s) = \cos(\pi \, \mathbf{c}_i^{\top} s)$ for chosen frequency vectors $\mathbf{c}_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> The richness of $\mathbf{x}(s)$ directly determines the **representational capacity** of the linear model. A linear function in feature space is non-linear in the original state space if $\mathbf{x}(s)$ is non-linear in $s$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Function Approximation Framework

A general parameterized approximate value function has the form:

$$\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{q}(s,a;\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

We define a **Mean Squared Value Error (VE)** objective as the gold standard for measuring approximation quality:

$$\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \Big[ v_\pi(s) - \hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) \Big]^2$$

where $\mu(s) \geq 0$ is a state-distribution weighting with $\sum_s \mu(s) = 1$ (often the **on-policy distribution**, proportional to time spent in $s$ under $\pi$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The true $v_\pi(s)$ is unknown; we must optimize $\overline{VE}$ using sampled trajectories — this leads to **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**.

---

## 2.2 The Linear Case — What Makes It Special

For a linear architecture, the gradient with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \big[\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)\big] = \mathbf{x}(s)$$

This is **independent of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$**! Consequences:

1. The loss surface is **exactly quadratic** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (convex).
2. The optimal weight vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}^\*$ has a **closed-form solution** (ordinary least squares).
3. The **Hessian** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbf{x}(s)\mathbf{x}(s)^{\top}\big]$ is constant — no curvature drift.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / KTU Caveat |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear approximation | $\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)$ | Feature vector $\mathbf{x}(s) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | Gradient (linear) | $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{x}(s)$ | Does **not** depend on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ |
| 3 | Value Error (VE) | $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_s \mu(s)\big[v_\pi(s) - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)\big]^2$ | On-policy weighting $\mu(s)$ |
| 4 | Optimal weights (closed-form) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\*} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}]$, $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}[v_\pi(s)\mathbf{x}(s)]$ | Requires full model/true $v_\pi$ |
| 5 | Gradient MC update | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \big[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big]\mathbf{x}(S_t)$ | Unbiased, converges to $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\*}$ |
| 6 | Semi-gradient TD(0) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma\,\hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big]\mathbf{x}(S_t)$ | Biased, but **linear TD converges** to TD fixed point |
| 7 | TD fixed point | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{TD}} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, with $\mathbf{A} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}(S_t)(\gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \mathbf{x}(S_t))^{\top}]$, $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)]$ | $0 < \gamma < 1$ |
| 8 | Semi-gradient TD($\lambda$) | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \delta_t \mathbf{z}_t$, where $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1}) - \hat{v}(S_t)$ | $\mathbf{z}_t$ is **eligibility trace** vector |
| 9 | Eligibility trace (linear) | $\mathbf{z}_{t} = \gamma\lambda \mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{x}(S_t)$, with $\mathbf{z}_{-1} = \mathbf{0}$ | Same as tabular form but vector-valued |
| 10 | LSTD (batch) | $\hat{\mathbf{A}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{x}_t(\gamma \mathbf{x}_{t+1} - \mathbf{x}_t)^{\top}$, $\hat{\mathbf{b}}_t = \hat{\mathbf{b}}_{t-1} + R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}_t$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_t = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_t^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{b}}_t$ |
| 11 | Convergence radius (TD) | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\theta}_t] = \boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{TD}}$ (within a bound) | Step-size condition: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$, $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$ |
| 12 | MC convergence | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_t \to \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\*}$ as $t \to \infty$ | Robbins-Monro conditions on $\alpha_t$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear FA is the workhorse of industrial RL systems where safety and stability matter (e.g., **primal-dual TD methods for power-grid control**, recommendation systems with millions of states). It is also the *theoretical foundation* for deep RL — neural networks are stacks of linear+nonlinear layers, and convergence proofs for the linear layer directly inform deep RL behavior.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" of Each Step — Operational Logic

- **Why feature vector $\mathbf{x}(s)$?** Raw states (images, coordinates, sensor vectors) are too high-dimensional. Features compress them into a meaningful, low-dim summary.
- **Why $\nabla = \mathbf{x}(s)$ (independent of $\theta$)?** This is what makes linear models **convex** and solvable.
- **Why eligibility trace $\mathbf{z}_t$?** It provides a decaying memory of past state features, enabling **TD($\lambda$) credit assignment** over time — equivalent to an exponential average of past $\mathbf{x}(S_\tau)$.
- **Why is TD(0) called "semi-gradient"?** It treats the **target** $R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ as a constant w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, ignoring its dependence on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ — hence "semi" gradient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gradient Monte-Carlo Update for Linear FA

**Goal:** Show that the SGD update of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to minimize $\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ yields the well-known linear MC update.

**Setup:** The per-sample squared error for a state $S_t$ with return $G_t$ is:

$$J_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}\big[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})\big]^2$$

**Step 1.** Compute the gradient of $J_t$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\big[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})\big] \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the linear form $\hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t)$ and $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{v} = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\big[G_t - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] \, \mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

**Step 3.** Apply the SGD rule $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \alpha \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J_t(\boldsymbol{\theta})$:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \big[G_t - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] \, \mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

**Step 4.** Recognize $\hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t)$:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \big[G_t - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big] \, \mathbf{x}(S_t)}$$

**Convergence check:** Because the objective is quadratic in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and the return $G_t$ is an unbiased sample of $v_\pi(S_t)$, this is a classical stochastic approximation problem, solvable by the **Robbins-Monro theorem** with the standard step-size schedule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Semi-Gradient TD(0) Fixed Point

**Goal:** Find the $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ toward which semi-gradient TD(0) converges.

**Step 1.** The semi-gradient TD(0) update is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \big[R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)\big] \mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

**Step 2.** Define the **TD error**:

$$\delta_t \triangleq R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

**Step 3.** The expected update direction (steady-state) is:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[\delta_t \mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] = \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(R_{t+1} + \gamma \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big) \mathbf{x}(S_t)\Big]$$

**Step 4.** At the **fixed point**, the expected update must be zero:

$$\mathbb{E}\Big[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\Big] + \gamma \mathbb{E}\Big[\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\mathbf{x}(S_t)^{\top}\Big]\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbb{E}\Big[\mathbf{x}(S_t)\mathbf{x}(S_t)^{\top}\Big]\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5.** Define two matrices:

$$\mathbf{A} \triangleq \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big(\gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{t+1}) - \mathbf{x}(S_t)\big)^{\top}\big], \qquad \mathbf{b} \triangleq \mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big]$$

**Step 6.** The fixed-point equation becomes $\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$, hence:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{TD}} = -\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] \text{ under } \gamma \text{-discounted form}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "semi-gradient" gives a fixed point:** Unlike true gradient methods, the TD target $R_{t+1} + \gamma\hat{v}(S_{t+1})$ still depends on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. By treating it as fixed, the resulting equilibrium is **not** the minimum of $\overline{VE}$, but a *biased* projection of $v_\pi$ onto the hypothesis space — known as the **TD fixed point**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear TD($\lambda$) with Eligibility Traces

**Step 1.** The tabular TD($\lambda$) backward view is:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \delta_t \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{v}(S_k;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

**Step 2.** For linear FA, $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{v}(S_k;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) = \mathbf{x}(S_k)$.

**Step 3.** Define the **accumulating eligibility trace**:

$$\mathbf{z}_t \triangleq \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\gamma\lambda)^{t-k} \mathbf{x}(S_k)$$

**Step 4.** Substitute back:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t + \alpha \delta_t \mathbf{z}_t$$

**Step 5.** The trace obeys the **online recursion** (proved by induction):

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \gamma\lambda \, \mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{x}(S_t), \qquad \mathbf{z}_{-1} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 6.** Expanding the recursion:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{x}(S_t) + \gamma\lambda\,\mathbf{x}(S_{t-1}) + (\gamma\lambda)^2\mathbf{x}(S_{t-2}) + \dots + (\gamma\lambda)^{t}\mathbf{x}(S_0)$$

**Interpretation:** Each past state's feature vector is weighted by $(\gamma\lambda)^{t-k}$, giving exponentially decaying credit. For $\lambda = 0$, $\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{x}(S_t)$ → recovers **TD(0)**. For $\lambda = 1$, $\mathbf{z}_t$ accumulates over the whole trajectory → approximates **MC**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Least-Squares TD (LSTD) — Closed-Form Solution

**Goal:** Find $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ that minimizes the projected Bellman error **in closed form using sample averages**.

**Step 1.** At the TD fixed point, $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t \mathbf{x}(S_t)] = 0$:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[(R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}) - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta})) \mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] = 0$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $\hat{v} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbb{E}\big[R_{t+1}\mathbf{x}(S_t)\big] + \gamma \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbf{x}(S_{t+1})\mathbf{x}(S_t)^{\top}\big]\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbf{x}(S_t)\mathbf{x}(S_t)^{\top}\big]\boldsymbol{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 3.** Replace expectations by sample averages up to time $t$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{A}}_t \triangleq \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} \mathbf{x}(S_k)\big(\gamma \mathbf{x}(S_{k+1}) - \mathbf{x}(S_k)\big)^{\top}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{b}}_t \triangleq \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} R_{k+1} \mathbf{x}(S_k)$$

**Step 4.** Solve the linear system:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta}_t = \hat{\mathbf{A}}_t^{-1}\hat{\mathbf{b}}_t}$$

**Step 5.** Complexity: $O(d^2)$ per step for the inverse; full inverse is $O(d^3)$ — but using **Sherman-Morrison** recursion, it can be done in $O(d^2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **LSTD vs. TD(0):** LSTD is **data-efficient** (uses all samples), but expensive in memory ($O(d^2)$ for $\hat{\mathbf{A}}$) and computation. It also does **not** require a step-size schedule, which is a huge practical advantage.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Update Rules (Compact Reference)

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} \;\leftarrow\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_t \;+\; \alpha \cdot \delta_t \cdot \mathbf{z}_t$$

where:

$$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{v}(S_{t+1};\boldsymbol{\theta}_t) - \hat{v}(S_t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \gamma\lambda \mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{x}(S_t)$$

This **one-line master equation** governs every variant:
- Set $\alpha = 0$ after batch and use matrix inverse → **LSTD**.
- Set $\lambda = 0$ → **Semi-gradient TD(0)**.
- Set $\lambda = 1$ and use $G_t$ instead of $\delta_t$ → **Gradient MC**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Linear Function Approximation in the RL Loop

The following block-level architecture shows how a linear function approximator is integrated into the standard **Agent–Environment Loop** of RL. The feature vector replaces the raw state and feeds into the linear weight matrix to produce the value estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef envBox fill:#dfeaff,stroke:#335,color:#000
    classDef featBox fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,color:#000
    classDef approxBox fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a33,color:#000
    classDef updateBox fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#3a3,color:#000

    envA[Environment]:::envBox
    sRaw[Raw State s_t]:::envBox
    envB[Return r_t+1 plus next state s_t+1]:::envBox

    f1[Feature Extractor Phi]:::featBox
    fv[Feature Vector x of s in R to d]:::featBox

    w1[Weight Vector theta in R to d]:::approxBox
    lin[Linear Map theta transpose x]:::approxBox
    vEst[Value Estimate v hat s theta]:::approxBox

    upd[TD Error delta and Eligibility Trace z]:::updateBox
    sg[Stochastic Gradient Update]:::updateBox

    envA --> sRaw --> f1 --> fv --> lin
    w1 --> lin --> vEst
    vEst --> upd
    envB --> upd
    upd --> sg --> w1
    vEst --> envA
```

---

## 4.2 Information Flow for One TD($\lambda$) Update Step

The following sequence diagram illustrates the **causal information flow** during a single linear TD($\lambda$) update. The agent observes the state, forms features, predicts a value, receives a reward, and updates its weight vector.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef stepStyle fill:#eef,stroke:#224,color:#000
    classDef dataStyle fill:#ffe,stroke:#aa0,color:#000

    sA[Step 1 Observe State s_t]:::stepStyle
    sB[Step 2 Form Feature x of s_t]:::stepStyle
    sC[Step 3 Predict v hat s_t equals theta transpose x]:::stepStyle
    sD[Step 4 Take Action and Receive r_t+1 and s_t+1]:::stepStyle
    sE[Step 5 Form Feature x of s_t+1]:::stepStyle
    sF[Step 6 Predict v hat s_t+1 equals theta transpose x plus]:::stepStyle
    sG[Step 7 Compute TD Error delta]:::stepStyle
    sH[Step 8 Update Trace z equals gamma lambda z plus x of s_t]:::stepStyle
    sI[Step 9 Gradient Step theta plus alpha delta z]:::stepStyle

    dA[x of s_t]:::dataStyle
    dB[theta]:::dataStyle
    dC[v hat s_t]:::dataStyle
    dD[r_t+1 and s_t+1]:::dataStyle
    dE[x of s_t+1]:::dataStyle
    dF[v hat s_t+1]:::dataStyle
    dG[delta]:::dataStyle
    dH[z_t]:::dataStyle
    dI[theta new]:::dataStyle

    sA --> sB --> sC --> sD --> sE --> sF --> sG --> sH --> sI
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Flow: MC vs. TD(0) vs. LSTD

This block diagram contrasts the three principal linear-RL algorithms at a glance, showing their **computational tradeoffs** and **data flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef mcBox fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#c70,color:#000
    classDef tdBox fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#246,color:#000
    classDef lstBox fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#2a6,color:#000

    m1[Generate Episode Trajectory]:::mcBox
    m2[Compute Return G_t]:::mcBox
    m3[Update theta using alpha G minus v hat times x]:::mcBox
    mOut[Output theta star unbiased]:::mcBox
    m1 --> m2 --> m3 --> mOut

    t1[Sample transition s_t r_t+1 s_t+1]:::tdBox
    t2[Form x_t and x_t+1]:::tdBox
    t3[Bootstrapped Target r plus gamma v hat s_t+1]:::tdBox
    t4[Online update theta plus alpha delta x]:::tdBox
    tOut[Output theta TD fixed point]:::tdBox
    t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> tOut

    l1[Collect batch of samples]:::lstBox
    l2[Form A hat and b hat]:::lstBox
    l3[Solve linear system A hat theta equals b hat]:::lstBox
    lOut[Output theta TD exact]:::lstBox
    l1 --> l2 --> l3 --> lOut
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Notice how MC requires a full episode, TD(0) uses single transitions but needs a *step size* schedule, while LSTD solves a system in one shot but pays $O(d^2)$ memory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define linear function approximation in reinforcement learning. State the value-error objective $\overline{VE}$ and explain the role of the on-policy distribution $\mu(s)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Linear function approximation represents the value function as a weighted sum of features:

$$\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \theta_i \, x_i(s)$$

The **mean squared value error (VE)** objective measures how close $\hat{v}$ is to the true $v_\pi$:

$$\overline{VE}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s)\big[v_\pi(s) - \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s)\big]^2$$

The **on-policy distribution** $\mu(s)$ weights the error by the fraction of time the agent spends in $s$ under policy $\pi$. This prioritizes the accuracy of value estimates in *states the agent actually visits*, rather than unvisited states. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Why is the gradient $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{x}(s)$ independent of the weight vector? Mention one consequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Because the approximation is **linear** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:

$$\hat{v}(s;\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\top}\mathbf{x}(s) \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\partial \hat{v}}{\partial \theta_i} = x_i(s)$$

The partial derivative with respect to each weight $\theta_i$ is simply the corresponding feature value $x_i(s)$, which is *constant w.r.t.* $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. A direct consequence is that the **loss surface is exactly quadratic (convex)** in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, which guarantees a single global minimum and enables **closed-form least-squares solutions** such as LSTD. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget the difference between **Gradient MC** (uses $G_t$, unbiased) and **Semi-gradient TD** (uses bootstrap, biased but lower variance). Mixing them in a derivation is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** The eligibility trace recursion $\mathbf{z}_t = \gamma\lambda \mathbf{z}_{t-1} + \mathbf{x}(S_t)$ is vector-valued in the linear case. Writing it as a scalar (like tabular TD) will cost full sub-part marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a)** Derive the **gradient Monte-Carlo update rule** for linear function approximation. Show explicitly that the update converges to the global optimum $\boldsymbol{\theta}^\*$ under Robbins-Monro conditions. **(7 marks)**
